item.title: UK police say Russia military intelligence behind European cargo fires
item.description:
LONDON, March 6 (Reuters) - British police said on Friday ‚Äãan international investigation had ‚Äåidentified suspected Russian military intelligence participation in a series of fires involving air ‚Äãfreight cargo across Europe dating ‚Äãback to 2024.
The conclusion came ‚Å†after work carried out ‚Äãby a team of detectives from ‚ÄãBritain, Lithuania, Poland, Germany and The Netherlands, the London police ‚Äãstatement said.
"The work of ‚Äãour European counterparts has been truly ‚Äåremarkable ‚Å†and demonstrates the critical need for us to work across borders, as ‚Äãwell ‚Äãas ‚Å†the expertise of our colleagues overseas," ‚Äãsaid Deputy Assistant Commissioner ‚ÄãVicki ‚Å†Evans, Senior National Coordinator for Counter Terrorism Policing.

item.title: Swiss expect to purchase 30 F-35 jets after cost increases
item.description:
ZURICH, March 6 (Reuters) - Switzerland now expects to purchase ‚Äãaround 30 Lockheed Martin (LMT.N) , opens new tab F-35A Lightning II ‚Äåfighter jets, six fewer than initially projected, because of cost increases in the order, the ‚Äãgovernment said on Friday. In December, Switzerland ‚Äãsaid it would no longer buy 36 ‚Å†of the jets as planned, and ‚ÄãDefence Minister Martin Pfister said rising costs meant ‚Äãthe government was requesting an additional credit worth 394 million Swiss francs ($505 million) from parliament .
With that credit, ‚ÄãSwitzerland now expected to be able to ‚Äãbuy around 30 F-35A jets, Pfister told a news ‚Äåconference.
The ‚Å†Swiss government last year announced that the United States was delaying delivery of Patriot air defence systems to Switzerland in order ‚Äãto support Ukraine. ‚ÄãPfister ‚Å†said on Friday he expected that delay to be around four ‚Äãto five years.
As a result, the ‚Äãgovernment ‚Å†is considering acquiring a second air defence system, Pfister said. Preference would be given ‚Å†to ‚Äãa system made in ‚ÄãEurope, the government said.
($1 = 0.7807 Swiss francs)

item.title: Croatia to re-launch mandatory military service after 17 years
item.description:
KARLOVAC, Croatia March 6 (Reuters) - Croatia is set to resume mandatory military service for the ‚Äåfirst time in 17 years as part of the European Union member's broader strategy to address security concerns in Europe and the Western Balkans.
On Monday, 800 recruits will start two-month basic training in military ‚Äãcompounds across the country.
Croatia amended its defence law last October to introduce mandatory ‚Äãservice in line with European trends and as tensions with Russia spurred ‚Å†calls for stronger defence capabilities across Europe.
Despite initial public division, more than half of the ‚Äãfirst batch of recruits were volunteers, 10% of them women. Only 10 people have filed ‚Äãconscientious objections and applied to serve in the civilian service instead, which is the lowest percentage in Europe, according to academics.
When Leon Dejanovic, 18, starts his training in May, he will be one of ‚Äã4,000 conscripts that the government plans to call on an annual basis.
Even though receiving a ‚Äãdraft notice in January felt "a little scary and surprising," the internet influencer with thousands of followers said ‚Äåhe ‚Å†views the upcoming training as a chance to take a break from his digital lifestyle.
"Honestly, I cannot wait to be without my cell phone for a while because I am on my cell phone all day for my job," Dejanovic told Reuters in his hometown of ‚ÄãKarlovac, 50 km (31 miles) ‚Äãsouthwest of the ‚Å†capital Zagreb.
New recruits will be trained in basic survival skills, self-defence, first aid and FPV drone operation. The program also offers incentives such ‚Äãas a 1,100 euro ($1,272.04) monthly allowance, work record credit and preferential ‚Äãaccess to ‚Å†public-sector employment.
Dejanovic's friend Josip Franjo Cvitesic does not share his enthusiasm.
"I haven't received a call-up yet, but honestly, I'm against it," said Cvitesic, a 19-year-old factory worker, adding that he was concerned ‚Å†that ‚Äãthe service could disrupt his job and income.
Including Croatia, ‚Äãthere will now be 10 NATO countries with mandatory military service, joining Greece, Turkey, Finland, Sweden, Norway, Denmark, Estonia, ‚ÄãLatvia, and Lithuania.
($1 = 0.8648 euros)

item.title: Trump says there will be no deal with Iran except 'unconditional surrender'
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. President ‚ÄåDonald Trump said on Friday there would be no ‚Äãdeal struck with ‚ÄãIran except "unconditional surrender," a week after ‚Å†launching war with Israel ‚Äãagainst Tehran.
"After that, and the ‚Äãselection of a GREAT & ACCEPTABLE Leader(s), we, and many of ‚Äãour wonderful and very ‚Äãbrave allies and partners, will work ‚Äåtirelessly ‚Å†to bring Iran back from the brink of destruction," Trump said in ‚Äãa social ‚Äãmedia ‚Å†post, adding that they would work to ‚Äãboost Iran's economy.
Trump ‚Äãtold ‚Å†Reuters on Thursday that he wants to be ‚Å†involved ‚Äãin choosing the ‚Äãnext leader of Iran.

item.title: EU's Costa, von der Leyen talk to Middle Eastern leaders on Monday, spokesperson says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 6 (Reuters) - EU leaders Antonio Costa and Ursula von der Leyen ‚Äãwill hold a video conference with ‚ÄåMiddle Eastern leaders on Monday to discuss the situation in the region, a ‚Äãspokesperson for Costa said on ‚ÄãFriday.
"This exchange will provide an opportunity ‚Å†to hear leaders' assessments of ‚Äãthe situation and to discuss further ‚Äãsupport from the EU and its member states to countries in the region, as ‚Äãwell as ways to bring ‚Äãthe current conflict to an end," the spokesperson ‚Äåsaid.
The ‚Å†foreign ministers of the 27 EU members discussed the situation in Iran and the broader Middle East ‚Äãwith their ‚Äãcounterparts from ‚Å†the Gulf Cooperation Council in a video call on ‚ÄãThursday.
During the meeting, they all ‚Äãcalled ‚Å†for dialogue and diplomacy to resolve the crisis, while reiterating that Gulf ‚Å†countries ‚Äãhave the right to ‚Äãdefend themselves against Iran.

item.title: Rip up the glut forecasts - Mideast oil shock signals supply crunch
item.description:
LONDON, March 6 (Reuters) - The sudden and acute disruption to Middle East oil supplies caused by the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran is forcing buyers to tap every available barrel, rapidly dismantling forecasts of an oil glut this year.
In February, the International Energy Agency forecast that global oil supply would exceed demand by around 3.7 million barrels per day (bpd) in 2026, with the surplus extending into the following year. A month later, that projection ‚Äãappears redundant.
Nearly 15 million bpd of crude production, plus another 4.5 million bpd of refined fuels, remain effectively stranded in the Gulf after the near‚Äëcomplete closure of the Strait of Hormuz . The chokepoint was shut ‚Äåshortly after the launch of the joint U.S.‚ÄìIsrael aerial bombing campaign against Iran on February 28, which Tehran answered by targeting Gulf states and regional energy infrastructure.
The loss of such a vast volume of supply ‚Äì equivalent to almost a fifth of global daily consumption ‚Äì has sent shockwaves through oil markets and the wider economy. Global benchmark Brent crude surged above $90 a barrel on Friday, gaining nearly 30% over the past week since the conflict started.
Asia, which sources around 60% of its crude imports from the Middle East, is bearing the brunt . Refineries and petrochemical plants across the region have cut production or shut ‚Äãaltogether to conserve feedstock, while other energy‚Äëintensive industries, from ceramics to car manufacturing, are facing acute shortages.
How long the conflict ‚Äì and the Hormuz shutdown ‚Äì will last is impossible to predict. But with each passing day, pressure on ‚Äãthe oil supply chain is compounding, not easing.
Inside the Gulf, producers are running out of options. With exports blocked, crude is being pushed into onshore ‚Å†storage tanks and offshore tankers.
Iraq, which has limited storage options, has already shut off at least a quarter of its 4.3 million bpd of production. Kuwait, the United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia, the world‚Äôs largest exporter, have some remaining storage ‚Äãcapacity available ‚Äì but it‚Äôs measured in days, not weeks.
Saudi Arabia and the UAE can divert some crude through alternative export routes, but these only partially offset the loss of Hormuz. As storage fills, more producers will be forced to cut output and idle ‚Äãrefineries.
Shutting oil fields safely is a complex process. Restarting them can take days or even weeks before production returns to full capacity, extending the market impact well beyond any eventual reopening of the Strait.
Meanwhile, refiners ‚Äì particularly in Asia ‚Äì are scrambling for barrels .
The good news is that inventories had been building in recent months, thanks to rising output from producers including OPEC . Global observed oil inventories rose by 1.3 million bpd, or 477 million barrels, in 2025, reaching their highest level since March 2021, according to the IEA.
Around 80 million barrels of oil are currently ‚Äãstored on tankers at sea, data from analytics firm Kpler show, with nearly two‚Äëthirds in Asia.
About three‚Äëquarters of that ‚Äúfloating storage‚Äù originated from Iran, Venezuela and Russia, all subject to Western sanctions, meaning much of it is inaccessible to most buyers. Iranian ‚Äãcrude alone accounts for roughly 50 million barrels.
Some of that oil is starting to move, however. The U.S. on Thursday granted India a waiver to buy Russian crude to help refiners with the supply squeeze. New Delhi had sharply cut imports last month as part ‚Äåof a trade ‚Å†deal with Washington.
Russian crude in floating storage has already fallen from 7.7 million barrels just before the strikes on Iran to 4.5 million barrels by March 6.
Independent Chinese refiners, which have absorbed the bulk of Iran‚Äôs crude exports in recent years, are likely to pick up the lion‚Äôs share of Iranian barrels that are either located outside the Gulf or able to get through the Strait.
At the same time, Middle East producers are almost certainly drawing on their overseas storage to meet contractual obligations with buyers.
If the disruption persists, pressure will grow on governments to tap their own reserves. OECD members hold strategic petroleum reserves created in the 1970s specifically to deal with supply shocks. Under IEA rules, net oil‚Äëimporting countries must hold stocks equivalent to ‚Äãat least 90 days of imports.
The U.S., the world‚Äôs largest ‚Äãoil producer and consumer, currently holds more than 400 ‚Å†million barrels in its SPR. That‚Äôs well below its capacity of roughly 700 million barrels, but given it is no longer a net importer of energy, the risk of cutting into its reserves is limited.
The most critical unknown is China. Beijing has quietly accumulated vast oil reserves in recent years, adding an average of around 300,000 bpd last year alone, according to the IEA. So far, ‚Äãit has not signalled any intention to release stocks, though it has instructed refiners to curb fuel exports.
Global reserves, though ample at the outset of this crisis, are finite. This shock ‚Äãis unprecedented: the Strait of Hormuz ‚Å†has never before been fully blocked. And even if it were reopened tomorrow, it would still take weeks for markets to rebalance and for finely calibrated supply chains to return to normal.
Assuming some of the Gulf oil production is diverted, offsetting a supply disruption of 15 million bpd would require more than 100 million barrels of oil from storage in a week. At that pace, an extended outage would rapidly erode global inventories.
‚ÄúIt is very difficult for stocks to compensate for flows, particularly when the reduction in flows ‚Å†is so large,‚Äù ‚Äãsaid Paul Horsnell, an independent oil analyst.
If stocks are depleted, governments and traders would obviously need to rebuild them, implying higher demand for crude, as ‚Äãwell as higher prices, in the coming year. The Middle East supply shock is already flipping expectations of a glut into a far more plausible scenario of undersupply.
(The opinions expressed here are those of the author , a columnist for Reuters)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial ‚Äãcommentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Morning Bid: No quiet on the eastern front
item.description:
March 6 - Everything Mike Dolan and the ROI team are excited to read, watch and listen to over the weekend.
From the Editor
Hello Morning Bid readers!
The war in Iran could prove to be one of the most momentous geopolitical events in decades, with long-term ramifications for global alliances and energy markets . It also represents the biggest risk to global supply chains since the Covid-19 pandemic.
The joint U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran that began last Saturday , killing Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, were met with fierce retaliation by Tehran, which launched ‚Äãstrikes across the region.
Yet as events unfolded this week, investors often appeared like the proverbial deer caught in the headlights. With some notable exceptions, mostly in Asia, prices in many asset classes moved far less than one would have expected ‚Äì and ‚Äåsometimes in unexpected directions .
Today's spike in energy prices may change that, however.
Major oil benchmarks are on pace for the largest weekly jumps since Russia‚Äôs invasion of Ukraine in 2022. Brent crude has topped $87 per barrel with WTI eclipsing $84, following big jumps on Friday morning. These represent increases of over 20% and 25%, respectively, on the week, and spikes of over 40% in the year to date.
The scale of the current disruption is enormous: the vital Strait of Hormuz has essentially been closed , hundreds of oil tankers are stranded , Qatar has shut its liquefied natural gas production, Saudi Arabia has suspended production at a key refinery and Iraq has shut its crude production.
One might have expected prices to already be closer to ‚Äãthree digits. But this morning's big moves suggest the market's optimism about a quick resolution to the crisis is now being tested .
Certain regions are struggling more than others. Asian refineries that depend on the Middle East for nearly 60% of their crude supplies are struggling to ‚Äãfind alternatives. Some plants have already reduced operations. Asian jet fuel prices skyrocketed over 70% on Wednesday , hitting a record high. European gas prices have also shot up.
(For a look at how the conflict is impacting various ‚Å†energy markets, check out ROI columnist Gavin Maguire‚Äôs chart-based overview .)
President Donald Trump‚Äôs response to the energy situation has been mixed. In reference to increasing gasoline prices, he said on Thursday , ‚ÄúThey'll drop very rapidly when this is over, and if they rise, they rise."
Yet the U.S. is also now temporary loosening restrictions ‚Äãon Indian purchases of Russian oil to ease the squeeze on India‚Äôs refiners, and the U.S. Treasury also considered taking action in the oil futures markets to tamp down prices, though it has reportedly ruled that out for now .
Additionally, the president said earlier in the week that the U.S. would offer insurance coverage and ‚Äãnavy escorts to help open the blocked Strait of Hormuz ‚Äì but this plan is likely to be too little, too late .
The impact on other asset classes has been notably varied.
Asian stocks markets ‚Äì home to many countries that import the majority of their energy from the Middle East ‚Äì have been rocked. In particular, South Korea's Kospi ‚Äì one of the best-performing equity indices in the first two months of the year ‚Äì was headed for its largest weekly fall in six years , around 10.5%.
European equities have also weakened since the conflict began , though far less significantly, and U.S. equities performed much better than their global peers, as the world‚Äôs biggest energy producer is well-insulated from shortage concerns and benefitted from ‚Äãa bounce in technology stocks .
The dollar, one of the bigger winners during the conflict, is on track to rise roughly 1.5% this week ‚Äì and this may not be down entirely to safe haven demand .
Meanwhile, Treasury yields rose as fears about the potentially meaningful inflationary impact of an energy crisis offset investors‚Äô flight to ‚Äãsafety.
While gold prices were up on Friday, the supposed safe haven is actually down on the week. This could represent investors selling to cover losses in other areas but could also highlight the speculation that has helped fuel bullion‚Äôs massive runup in the past year.
Later today, markets may briefly turn their focus away from fear ‚Äåof war only to ‚Å†run back into the fear of a potential AI jobs apocalypse , with the release of U.S. February payrolls.
But all eyes will eventually return to the Middle East. No matter how ‚Äì or when ‚Äì this conflict ends, it has the potential to shift how the world thinks about defense, energy security, and resource nationalism. At a time when the AI revolution is demanding ever-greater amounts of energy, metals and minerals, that‚Äôs a huge deal.
For more stories on how the Middle East conflict may impact financial markets and commodities, check out Reuters Open Interest . You can learn:
As we head into the weekend, check out the ROI team‚Äôs ‚Äãrecommendations for what you should read, listen to, and watch to stay ‚Äãinformed and ready for the week ahead.
I‚Äôd love to hear from ‚Å†you, so please reach out to me at anna.szymanski@thomsonreuters.com , opens new tab .
This weekend, we're reading...
JAMIE MCGEEVER, ROI Markets Columnist: Evan Feigenbaum, at the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, dissects China's response ‚Äì or, more accurately, non-response ‚Äì to U.S. attacks on Venezuela and Iran, two of China‚Äôs supposed allies. Confirmation, as if any were needed, that Beijing Doesn‚Äôt Think Like Washington , opens new tab .
RON BOUSSO, ROI Energy Columnist: This excellent U.S. congressional report , opens new tab , drafted after last year's 12-day Israel‚ÄìIran war, clearly lays out the history and ‚Äãstrategic importance of the Strait of Hormuz.
ANDY HOME, ROI Metals Columnist: Amanda van Dyke, founder of the Critical Minerals Hub, offers insightful analysis , opens new tab of how the Iran war is burning through not only Western missile stockpiles but ‚Äãalso the critical metals that make them ‚Å†function.
MIKE DOLAN, ROI Finance & Markets Columnist: This VoxEU column , opens new tab from the Centre for Economic Policy Research uses the 2021‚Äì2022 boom and bust of non-fungible tokens (NFTs) as an "unprecedented real-time laboratory for bubble economics." The authors show how hard it is to measure or realize returns in illiquid markets for unique assets and how transaction prices can mislead, with clear lessons for regulators of digital and private assets.
GAVIN MAGUIRE, ROI Global Energy Transition Columnist: This excellent Reuters explainer summarises what's in China's latest five-year plan (2026‚Äì2030). Easy to miss amid the chaos surrounding the Iran war, the plan is likely to have a material impact on global markets and key technology trends ‚Å†for the rest of ‚Äãthe 2020s.
We're listening to...
CLYDE RUSSELL, ROI Asia Commodities and Energy Columnist: Gulf Intelligence's Daily Energy Markets podcast , opens new tab provides essential context on the current Gulf oil situation and China's decision to order ‚Äãits major refiners to suspend fuel imports.
And we're watching...
ANNA SZYMANSKI, ROI Editor-in-Charge: The Morning Bid podcast ‚Äì where you can catch Mike Dolan five days a week ‚Äì is now available on YouTube , opens new tab . Check out the Sunday edition each week where I join Mike to discuss what‚Äôs in store for the week ahead in markets.
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every ‚Äãweekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the authors. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Exclusive: Israel backing Iranian Kurdish plans to seize Iran border areas, sources say
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 6 (Reuters) - Israel has been bombing parts of western Iran to support Iranian Kurdish militias who hope to exploit the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran to seize towns near the frontier, ‚Äãaccording to three sources familiar with Israel's talks with the factions.
The notion of an offensive by Iranian Kurdish forces based in Iraq gained attention on Friday when U.S. ‚ÄåPresident Donald Trump told Reuters it would be "wonderful" if they crossed the border.
A Kurdish insurgency could have serious consequences for Iran as it defends itself against the air campaign. The militias have consulted with the U.S. about how and whether to attack Iran's security forces, Reuters has reported.
Israel has been holding its own talks with Iranian Kurdish insurgent groups based in the semi-autonomous region of Iraqi Kurdistan for around a year, two Iranian Kurdish sources ‚Äãsaid, while an Israeli source said talks had been "long-term".
The two Iranian Kurdish sources have direct knowledge of the armed dissident groups and the source from Israel has direct knowledge of its engagement ‚Äãwith them. All spoke on condition of anonymity.
Israel's government and the Israeli military did not immediately respond to requests for comment, and Israel has not commented ‚Å†publicly on such engagement during the current war.
An initial goal of the Kurdish factions would be seizing Iranian territory along the border, the three sources said. One of the Kurdish sources said their aim ‚Äãwas to seize the towns of Oshnavieh and Piranshahr, among others.
These sources said thousands of fighters were gathering on the Iraqi side of the border and preparing to launch an offensive within a week, something Reuters ‚Äãwas not able to confirm.
Independent estimates put the militias' combined strength at 5,000-8,000.
They possess only light arms, according to the Kurdish sources. But while they might not have the firepower to mount a significant bid for self-rule, with U.S. and Israeli help they could cause trouble on the border.
The Israeli source said Israel did not expect them to be able to overthrow their government, but that backing them could erode Iran's control over its hinterlands and distract its Islamic Revolutionary Guard ‚ÄãCorps.
Five long-standing Iranian dissident groups announced an alliance just at the end of last month.
It includes the Kurdistan Free Life Party (PJAK), the Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan (PDKI) and the Kurdistan Freedom Party (PAK), which have all participated in ‚Äãinsurgencies and maintain fighters in Iraq.
It is not clear that they will get any support from their ethnic brethren in Iraq, however; Iraqi Kurdistan's political leadership has publicly denied any plan to send fighters or get involved in Iran, ‚Äådespite reports of ‚Å†outside pressure to do so.
The Israeli source cautioned that there was pushback from the Iraqi Kurds, and without their practical support it would be hard for the Iranian Kurds to mobilise. Trump's lack of clarity on how long the war could last had also led to hesitancy.
Iran has been attacking Kurdish armed groups inside Iraq, along with U.S. bases in the area, and on Friday warned Iraqi Kurdistan that it would retaliate against any deployment of hostile forces on the frontier.
The three sources said Kurds within Iran had been providing targeting intelligence on the border areas to the U.S. and Israel.
Israeli ‚Äãanalyst Jonathan Spyer said Israel was seeking to "destroy the ‚Äãregime by any means available".
But Danny Citrinowicz, ‚Å†an Iran expert and former Israeli intelligence officer, said an insurgency in Iran did not have broad support among Iraqi and Iranian Kurds:
"I think they're all waiting to see if the regime will hold on or not."
Turkish and Iraqi officials, neither keen to support separatism among an ethnic group spread across parts of Iraq, ‚ÄãTurkey, Syria and Iran, have also expressed reservations about any insurgency in Iran.
Citrinowicz said supporting an uprising might backfire on the U.S. and Israel by ‚Äãfanning nationalism.
Israel has maintained discreet ‚Å†military, intelligence and business ties with various Kurdish groups since the 1960s, viewing them as a buffer against shared adversaries.
The two Kurdish sources said the factions were in closer coordination with the U.S. than with Israel, but that any cross-border offensive would require air support from both.
One of those sources said they had not yet received weapons, but would request air defence systems, drones, small arms, and artillery support.
Kurdish groups have a long history of working ‚Å†with the U.S., ‚Äãbut recent incidents have strained ties.
One of the Iranian Kurdish sources said Kurdish leaders had concerns about being "betrayed" like the ‚ÄãKurdish groups in northern Syria, who had been forced to cede territory after long serving as the primary U.S. partner in the area.
The source said Iranian Kurdish leaders had requested guarantees from the U.S., without saying what they were.
Both Iranian Kurdish sources said ‚Äãthe factions' goal would be to establish a semi-autonomous region in a federal Iran, similar to the model in Iraq.

item.title: Exclusive: US pressing Sri Lanka not to repatriate Iranian crew and survivors from sunken ship, memo says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - The United States is pressing Sri Lanka's government not to repatriate the survivors ‚Äãfrom the Iranian warship it sank this week, as well as the crew of a second Iranian ship ‚Äåthat is in Sri Lankan custody, according to an internal State Department cable seen by Reuters on Friday.
A U.S. submarine sank the IRIS Dena warship in the Indian Ocean about 19 nautical miles off Sri Lanka's southern port city of Galle on Wednesday, killing dozens of sailors and dramatically widening ‚ÄãWashington's pursuit of the Iranian navy.
On Thursday, Sri Lanka began offloading 208 crew members from a second Iranian ship, the ‚Äãnaval auxiliary vessel IRIS Booshehr, which had found itself stranded in Sri Lanka's exclusive economic zone ‚Å†but outside its maritime boundary.
President Anura Kumara Dissanayake said his island nation had a "humanitarian responsibility" to take in the crew.
The torpedoing ‚Äãof the Dena - which U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth described as "quiet death" - was the first such action by the United States since ‚ÄãWorld War Two and a clear sign of the Iran conflict's widening geographic scope.
The internal State Department cable, which was dated March 6 and has not been previously reported, said Jayne Howell, the charge d'affaires at the U.S. embassy in Colombo, had emphasized to Sri Lanka's government that neither ‚Äãthe Booshehr crew nor the 32 Dena survivors should be repatriated to Iran.
It said "Sri Lankan authorities should minimize Iranian attempts to ‚Äãuse the detainees for propaganda."
The State Department did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Representatives for Dissanayake's office and Sri Lanka's foreign ministry ‚Äåwere not ‚Å†immediately available for comment.
The cable said Howell also told the Israeli ambassador to India and Sri Lanka there was no plan to repatriate the crew to Iran. The envoy asked Howell whether there was any engagement with the crew to encourage "defection", the cable said.
A representative for the Israeli embassy in New Delhi did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
On Wednesday, Sri Lanka's deputy ‚Äãminister for health and mass ‚Äãmedia, Hansaka Wijemuni, told Reuters ‚Å†that Tehran had asked Colombo for help repatriating the bodies of those killed aboard the Dena, but a timeframe to do so has not yet been determined.
The Dena had taken part in naval ‚Äãexercises organised by India in the Bay of Bengal last month and was returning to ‚ÄãIran when it ‚Å†was struck by a U.S. torpedo.
A U.S. official, speaking on condition of anonymity, told Reuters the Dena was armed when it was hit and the United States did not provide a warning before carrying out the strike.
The State Department cable said the second vessel, the Booshehr, ‚Å†will ‚Äãremain in Sri Lankan custody for the duration of the conflict.
Sri Lankan authorities ‚Äãsaid on Friday that they were escorting the Booshehr to a harbor on the eastern coast and moving most of its crew to a navy camp near Colombo.

item.title: Exclusive: Turkey asks Britain's MI6 to step up protection of Syria's Sharaa, sources say; Ankara denies report
item.description:
ISTANBUL/DAMASCUS/LONDON, March 6 (Reuters) - Turkey‚Äôs intelligence agency asked ‚Äãits British counterpart MI6 last month to take a larger role in protecting Syrian President Ahmed al-Sharaa after recent assassination plots, according to five people ‚Äåfamiliar with the matter.
After this story was published, Turkey denied that its intelligence agency MIT had made any such request to MI6.
The request highlights efforts by foreign allies to shore up a country still shaken by sporadic violence 15 months after the overthrow of President Bashar al-Assad, with the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran now rattling the wider region.
Those allies see Sharaa as crucial to preventing a relapse into sectarian fighting or civil war, after ‚Äã14 years of civil conflict drove millions of refugees abroad and allowed Islamic State to control swathes of Syria.
The militants last month stepped up attacks on military and ‚Äãsecurity personnel across Syria and declared Sharaa, a former rebel, their "number one foe".
It was unclear what specifically Turkey‚Äôs National Intelligence Organization, or MIT, had ‚Å†asked of MI6, or what new role, if any, MI6 had taken up.
The Turkish presidency said in a statement issued by its communications directorate that MIT maintains effective cooperation in the ‚Äãfight against terrorism with the international intelligence community and security units in Syria, but that this story did not reflect the truth.
"Contrary to what is claimed in the report in question, ‚Äãit is not true that MIT has made any request to MI6 regarding the protection of the Syrian President or sought to assume such a role," it said.
Turkey, Britain and the U.S. last year threw their backing behind Sharaa to try to reunite and rebuild his country of 26 million. London and Washington have scrapped most sanctions on Syria and on Hayat Tahrir al-Sham (HTS), the Islamist group he ‚Äãonce led.
The sources who spoke to Reuters requested anonymity owing to the sensitivity of the matter.
MIT, the Turkish foreign ministry, Britain's foreign office and Syria's defence and interior ministries did ‚Äãnot comment when contacted ahead of publication.
The sources, including Syrian and foreign officials, all cited rising anxiety over a series of reported Islamic State plots to kill Sharaa.
A Turkish source said that MIT, which has played ‚Äåa key ‚Å†role in helping the new government to establish itself, appealed to MI6 for more support after one such incident last month. A senior Syrian security source said the request came after a ‚Äúhigh-risk assassination plot‚Äù, adding that MIT, MI6 and Syrian authorities were constantly sharing intelligence.
Details of the plot were unclear.
A separate Western intelligence source briefed on the matter believed Turkey wanted to introduce a Western presence in Damascus to provide something of a buffer between the agencies of Turkey and Israel, currently at loggerheads.
Last year, Sharaa and two senior cabinet ministers ‚Äãwere targeted by Islamic State in five ‚Äãfoiled assassination attempts, according to the U.N. ‚Å†Office of Counter-Terrorism. In November, Reuters reported that Syrian authorities had foiled two of the attempts .
Describing Sharaa as a "watchdog" of the global anti-Islamic State coalition, the group mounted six attacks on Syrian authorities last month in what it called a ‚Äúnew phase".
On Thursday, Damascus openly acknowledged for the first ‚Äãtime that it coordinates with MIT, saying they had cooperated to foil an Islamic State attack in the capital.
Turkish security sources said ‚ÄãMIT had identified a team ‚Å†of three preparing remote bomb attacks, enabling Syrian counterparts to prevent an "imminent assault".
A U.S. diplomat briefed on the matter said MIT's request to MI6 had been prompted by the Islamic State resurgence.
The Western intelligence source said the two agencies could intensify joint planning and technical operations, but that no decision had been made on whether to send British personnel to Damascus.
A Syrian security source said a ‚Å†physical British ‚Äãpresence would be "highly risky". They said MI6 had been discussed at a meeting in Damascus on February 26 between ‚Äãa delegation headed by Britain's special envoy for Syria, Ann Snow, and Syria's deputy interior minister, Major General Abdulqader Tahan.
Sharaa was a commander of Al Qaeda's Nusra Front in Syria before cutting ties with the group in 2016, then led ‚Äãa coalition of Islamist rebel factions in late 2024 to topple Assad.

item.title: Hungary's $82 million bank transit swoop deepens spat with Ukraine
item.description:
BUDAPEST/KYIV, March 6 (Reuters) - Hungary ‚Äãsaid on Friday it had detained seven Ukrainians carrying around $82 million in cash and gold on suspicion of money laundering, ‚Äåas Kyiv accused Budapest of taking bank employees hostage amid a dispute over oil shipments.
The Hungarian Tax Authority's decision to have counter-terrorism forces swoop on two Ukrainian armoured vehicles transporting cash to Ukraine marks a dramatic escalation of tensions that have already resulted in Budapest blocking tens of billions of euros in European Union aid for Kyiv.
Ukrainian Foreign Minister ‚ÄãAndrii Sybiha said on X that the people detained were employees of Oschadbank and accused Hungary of "taking hostages and stealing money".
"This is ‚Äãstate terrorism and racketeering," he added, demanding their immediate release and asking the EU to "provide a clear qualification of Hungary‚Äôs ‚Å†unlawful actions".
Video posted on the Hungarian government's Facebook account showed armed counter-terrorism officers jumping out of a van as the Ukrainian vehicles pulled into ‚Äãa service station, pointing their guns at the windscreen before handcuffing the people inside and forcing them to lie face down on the ground.
"The National Tax ‚Äãand Customs Administration (NAV) is pursuing criminal proceedings on suspicion of money laundering," the authority said in a statement.
"On March 5, 2026, it detained seven Ukrainian citizens, including a former Ukrainian intelligence service general, and two armoured cash-in-transit vehicles, which were transporting a total of $40 million, 35 million euros and nine kilograms of gold from Austria to Ukraine."
The tax ‚Äãauthority said it was working together with counter-terrorism forces.
Government spokesperson Zoltan Kovacs said the seven detainees would be expelled from Hungary. It was not ‚Äãimmediately clear what would happen to the money they were transporting.
Ukraine's ambassador to Hungary, Sandor Fegyir, went to the Budapest headquarters of the Counter-Terrorism Centre to try and ‚Äåmeet with ‚Å†the detained Ukrainians, two Reuters witnesses said.
The detainees' lawyer, Lorant Horvath, said he had been unable to contact them. Kyiv's ambassador to Budapest, Sandor Fegyir, was also unable to meet the detainees.
"This is an outrageous procedure... (T)he embassy, and indeed the consular service, must in all cases be allowed access to the accused," Horvath told Reuters. "Defence lawyers must also be allowed (access)."
Ukraine advised its citizens against travel to Hungary, saying it could not guarantee their safety amid what it called ‚Äãthe "arbitrary actions" of Hungarian authorities.
Hungary and ‚ÄãSlovakia accuse Ukraine of deliberately delaying ‚Å†the resumption of oil flows via the damaged Druzhba pipeline for political reasons. Kyiv denies the charge, saying it needs time to repair the damage caused to energy infrastructure by a Russian drone strike on January 27.
Hungarian Prime ‚ÄãMinister Viktor Orban, who faces a serious challenge to his 16-year rule in an election on April 12, has made ‚Äãthe war in Ukraine ‚Å†a central plank of his campaign, saying the opposition would drag Hungary into the conflict.
He has vetoed new EU sanctions on Moscow as well as a huge loan for Ukraine over the oil dispute.
Speaking on state radio, Orban again accused Kyiv of blackmail and said Hungary would use all means at its disposal until oil flows resume. ‚Å†He did ‚Äãnot mention the detention of the Ukrainians.
Ukraine's Oschadbank said its employees had been engaged in a routine operation.
"Since the ‚Äãstart of the full-scale invasion, foreign currency and bank metals have been transported exclusively by land," it said in a statement. "Similar trips are carried out by Oschadbank's cash collection vehicles on ‚Äãa weekly basis."

item.title: Anglican conservative bloc calls for boycott of Canterbury leadership
item.description:
ABUJA, March 6 (Reuters) - A powerful ‚Äãgroup of conservative Anglicans on Friday urged its members to boycott meetings convened by the ‚ÄåArchbishop of Canterbury and halt any financial contributions to the current leadership, escalating a long‚Äërunning rift within the church.
The declaration by the Global Anglican Future Conference (GAFCON) - a grouping of conservative churches mainly from Africa and Asia that claims to represent a majority ‚Äãof the world's Anglicans - follows a decision to establish a new council rivalling the current leadership.
Three days ‚Äãof meetings of GAFCON members in Nigeria have underscored the deep rifts within the ‚Å†Anglican Communion over theological and social issues, including the ordination of women and the inclusion of LGBTQ+ members.
They ‚Äãcome just weeks before the Communion is due to enthrone Sarah Mullally as its Archbishop of Canterbury, ‚Äãuntil now spiritual leader of the world's 85 million Anglicans spread across 165 countries.
Laurent Mbanda, installed on Thursday as chairman of the new rival council, read a statement saying the Global Anglican Communion required "a principled disengagement" from structures associated with the Church ‚Äãof England.
"Leaders who hold office in the Global Anglican Communion must not attend future Primates' meetings called by ‚Äãthe Archbishop of Canterbury, nor attend the Lambeth Conference, nor attend ACC meetings or participate in Commissions of the ACC," the ‚Äåstatement ‚Å†said.
It added that leaders "should not personally approve financial contributions to the ACC. It is also expected that they will not receive financial assistance from compromised sources."
It was not immediately clear how much money was at stake.
The Church of England did not immediately comment.
Founded some 500 years ago when the Church of England broke ‚Äãfrom Rome, the Anglican Communion ‚Äãhas spread to many ‚Å†parts of the world, particularly former British colonies.
In recent decades, there have been some liberal shifts within part of the Church. GAFCON was set up in 2008, drawing ‚Äãon the resistance to these changes, especially in Africa and Asia, where the ‚Äãchurch is expanding ‚Å†fastest.
On Thursday, a spokesperson for the Anglican Communion Office in London said GAFCON was ignoring years of dialogue aimed at reforming the church.
On whether the church had now split, Diarmaid MacCulloch, emeritus professor of church history at the ‚Å†University of ‚ÄãOxford, told Reuters: "Of course it's a schism."
But MacCulloch said the ‚Äãrupture need not necessarily be permanent.
"Schisms do eventually get healed, when both sides see that the issues that caused the schism don't seem ‚Äãthat important any longer," he said.

item.title: Germany's Merz warns against spread of war in Middle East
item.description:
MUNICH, March 6 (Reuters) - German Chancellor Friedrich Merz said on Friday Berlin was working with ‚Äåpartners to find an approach to end the fighting with Iran as he warned against allowing the country to fall into chaos as the war widens across the Middle East.
Merz said Germany shared the aims of the United States and ‚ÄãIsrael to stop Tehran's nuclear and missile programme , its threats to Israel and its support for ‚Äãproxy groups and he said the Iranian people had the right to decide ‚Å†their own future.
"However, as the fighting continues and spreads, we also see growing risks," he said in ‚Äãa statement. "An endless war is not in our interest."
He said Germany was working with partners to formulate ‚Äãa common perspective on ending the conflict. "These considerations are ongoing," he said, without giving details.
Merz's comments reflect the unease felt in Europe at the war unleashed by the U.S. and Israeli operation that killed Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei a ‚Äãweek ago and which has since spread into Lebanon with no clear sign of an end in ‚Äãsight.
As American and Israeli jets have continued to pound targets across Iran and Iranian missiles and drones have hit ‚Äåcities ‚Å†in the Gulf and Israel, the economic risks have risen with global air transport in chaos and the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world's key oil shipping lanes, shut down.
Iran has not yet named a successor to Khamenei, who held power for 37 years, and Merz said the collapse of Iranian statehood ‚Äãor proxy conflicts fought ‚Äãon Iranian soil would ‚Å†not be in anyone's interest.
"Such scenarios could have far-reaching consequences for Europe," he said, including uncontrolled migration by Iranians fleeing across the border if the country ‚Äãfell into chaos.
"The Iranian state must remain functional, and public order and ‚Äãbasic services must ‚Å†be maintained. The Iranian economy must not collapse," Merz said.
Merz said a political process of dialogue and trust building in the region would have to begin once fighting ends. Iran's nuclear and ballistic missile programmes ‚Å†would have ‚Äãto end and be subject to verification. Iran would have ‚Äãto stop supporting proxy groups and all nuclear material would have to be secured.
If that happened sanctions might be lifted and aid ‚Äãcould be provided.

item.title: White House says US well on way toward controlling Iran airspace
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - The United States is well on its way toward controlling Iranian airspace, ‚ÄãWhite House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said on Friday, adding that ‚ÄåWashington expects the achievable U.S. objectives to be completed in four to six weeks.
Speaking to reporters at the White House, Leavitt also said ‚ÄãWashington was looking at potential candidates to lead Iran, ‚Äãa day after President Donald Trump told Reuters in ‚Å†an interview that the United States must be involved ‚Äãin choosing the next leader of Iran.
"I know there's a number ‚Äãof people that our intelligence agencies and the United States government are looking at, but I won't get any further on that," Leavitt said.
‚ÄãIn the interview on Thursday, Trump said that he thinks ‚Äãthe next leader of Iran is unlikely to be the late Supreme ‚ÄåLeader ‚Å†Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's son, who has emerged as a frontrunner to succeed his father, who was killed in a military strike at the start of the war.
Earlier on Friday, Trump ‚Äãsaid there would ‚Äãbe no ‚Å†deal struck with Iran except "unconditional surrender."
"What the President means is that when he, as Commander in Chief ‚Äãof the US Armed Forces, determines that Iran ‚Äãno ‚Å†longer poses a threat to the United States of America, and the goals of Operation Epic Fury has been fully realized, ‚Å†then Iran ‚Äãwill essentially be in a place ‚Äãof unconditional surrender, whether they say it themselves or not," Leavitt said.

item.title: Pentagon taps former DOGE official to lead its AI efforts
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - The Pentagon on Friday named as Chief Data Officer ‚Äãa computer scientist who aided billionaire Elon Musk's efforts to ‚Äåoverhaul the government last year and who has boosted white supremacists and misogynists online.
In a social media post, the Pentagon said Gavin Kliger's new role "places him at the center of the Department‚Äôs most ‚Äãambitious AI efforts," focusing on "day-to-day alignment and execution of the Department‚Äôs ‚ÄãAI projects, working directly with America's frontier AI labs to ‚Å†support the warfighter."
The Pentagon did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for ‚Äãmore comment. Kliger did not immediately return a message.
Kliger, in social media posts between ‚ÄãOctober 2024 and January 2025, has voiced controversial views and reposted content from white supremacist Nick Fuentes and self-described misogynist Andrew Tate.
The Pentagon's use of AI has taken center ‚Äãstage after a heated weeks-long dispute with Anthropic over guardrails on how the ‚Äãmilitary can use its AI tools led to last week's decision by the Trump administration to ‚Äåshun ‚Å†the company and replace it with OpenAI.
On Thursday, the Pentagon slapped a formal supply-chain risk designation on Anthropic - an extraordinary rebuke by the United States against an American tech company that was earlier than its rivals to work ‚Äãwith the Pentagon.
Anthropic was ‚Äãthe most aggressive ‚Å†of its rivals in courting U.S. national-security officials. But the company and the Pentagon have been at odds for ‚Äãmonths over how the military can use its technology on ‚Äãthe battlefield. ‚Å†This conflict erupted into public view earlier this year.
Anthropic has refused to back down on bans for its Claude AI to power autonomous weapons and mass ‚Å†U.S. surveillance. ‚ÄãThe Pentagon has pushed back, saying it ‚Äãshould be able to use this technology as needed, so long as it complies with U.S. law.

item.title: Iranian attacks on civilians won't go unanswered, CENTCOM commander says
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - The United States ‚Äåwill respond to Iranian attacks on civilians ‚Äãthroughout the Middle ‚ÄãEast, the leader of the ‚Å†U.S. Central Command, ‚ÄãBrad Cooper, said on ‚ÄãFriday.
Cooper said that Iran has attacked 12 different countries ‚Äãsince the U.S. ‚Äãand Israel launched strikes on ‚Äåthe ‚Å†Islamic Republic last week, including firing seven attack drones at ‚Äãcivilian, residential ‚Äãneighborhoods ‚Å†in Bahrain on Thursday night.
"This ‚Äãis unacceptable and ‚Äãwill ‚Å†not go unanswered," Cooper said in a ‚Å†statement.

item.title: Oil derivatives signal traders see Middle East shock as short-lived
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Oil options and futures are signalling that the latest Middle East conflict may be short‚Äëlived, as traders pile into structures that profit from a retreat in prices after the initial spike.
Options and futures markets often provide the earliest signal of whether traders see ‚Äãa supply shock as fleeting or structural, creating opportunities to profit from sharp swings in prices.
The Israel‚ÄëU.S. attack on Iran has ‚Äåsent shockwaves through energy markets as war‚Äërisk insurance costs surge, freight rates hit record levels and disruptions at the Strait of Hormuz snarl oil flows and strand hundreds of vessels. Oil prices , on Friday were at multi-year highs.
In a sign traders see the price shock as temporary, 30-day at-the-money Brent implied volatility jumped 17.5 points to 68% over the past ‚Äãweek through Tuesday, while 60- and 90-day tenors rose only 5.9 and 2.8 percentage points, LSEG data shows.
"What we're watching in real time is ‚Äãthe difference between a logistics crisis and a structural one," Brian E. Kinsella, former Goldman Sachs energy specialist, told ‚Å†the Reuters Global Markets Forum. , opens new tab
"The market is betting it's logistical and I think that is the right read," he added.
The Brent futures curve is sending a ‚Äãsimilar signal.
The spread between the front-month Brent contract and the six-month contract widened to about $10, the steepest backwardation since the Russia-Ukraine war in 2022, pointing to ‚Äãtight near-term supply while suggesting short-term disruption.
Meanwhile, the put-to-call ratio on West Texas Intermediate options roughly halved to 0.35 on Monday from Friday's close, CME data showed, pointing to heavy bullish call buying before rebounding to 0.56 on Tuesday as demand returned for downside protection.
A call option gives the holder the right, but not the obligation, to buy a ‚Äãcrude futures contract at a set price, while a put option gives the right to sell one.
The ratio, meanwhile, compares bearish put options, which profit ‚Äãfrom declines, with bullish call options that benefit from rising prices to gauge market sentiment.
"Dealers are already short a meaningful amount of these deep out-of-the-money calls, creating a more ‚Äånegative gamma ‚Å†profile in crude," said Rebecca Babin, senior energy trader at CIBC Private Wealth US. That contrasts with the more typical environment where dealers are long gamma and sell into rallies.
Gamma shows how much an option's sensitivity to the futures price (delta) will change if the market moves.
Much of the 2027 Brent strip still trades below $70 a barrel, a sign markets are not yet pricing a structural shift in long-term supply, Babin said.
Producers have also used the rally to hedge ‚Äãforward output, creating natural selling pressure on ‚Äãlonger-dated volatility, she added.
Risk premiums ‚Å†remain concentrated at the front of the futures curve, reinforcing the view that traders still see the disruption as temporary, said Darrell E. Fletcher, managing director of commodities at trading firm Bannockburn Capital Markets.
Brent options open interest plunged in late ‚ÄãFebruary before rebounding in early March, suggesting traders unwound positions before rebuilding hedges.
Front-month open interest fell from around ‚Äã388,000 contracts on Feb. ‚Å†18 to roughly 73,000 by Feb. 27, before surging to more than 700,000 contracts on March 2 as fresh positions were established.
"The open interest data seems like a pretty clear tell that it was a sharp unwind in positioning and not a structural repricing. The front end is clearly closing a trade while the ‚Å†back end is ‚Äãwhat to pay attention to,‚Äù Kinsella said.
Futures positioning shows a similar pattern, with more than ‚Äã40% of open interest concentrated in April through July expiries and thinner positioning further out the curve, according to CME data.
(Join GMF, a chat room hosted on LSEG Messenger for live interviews: https://lseg.group/3KFHrhe , opens new tab )

item.title: US drillers add oil, gas rigs for first time in four weeks, Baker Hughes says
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. energy firms ‚Äåthis week added oil and natural gas rigs for the first time in four weeks, energy services firm Baker Hughes (BKR.O) , opens new tab said in its closely followed report on Friday.
The oil and gas rig count, an early indicator of future output, rose by one ‚Äãto 551 in the week to March 6. , ,
Despite this week's rig increase, Baker Hughes said the ‚Äãtotal count was still down 41 rigs, or 7% below this time last year.
Baker ‚Å†Hughes said oil rigs rose by four to 411 this week, their highest since early February, while gas ‚Äãrigs fell by two to 132, their lowest since early February.
In the Haynesville Shale in Louisiana and Texas, ‚Äãthe rig count rose by one this week to 53, the highest since May 2023.
In the Marcellus Shale in Pennsylvania, Ohio and West Virginia, the rig count rose by one to 27, the highest since May 2024.
In the Williston Shale in North ‚ÄãDakota and Montana, the rig count fell by one to 27, the lowest since January 2022.
In North Dakota, ‚Äãthe rig count fell by one to 25, the lowest since November 2021.
The oil and gas rig count declined by about ‚Äå7% ‚Å†in 2025, 5% in 2024, and 20% in 2023 as lower U.S. oil prices prompted energy firms to focus more on boosting shareholder returns and paying down debt rather than increasing output.
Financial services firm TD Cowen said 18 of the 21 exploration and production (E&P) companies it tracks planned to spend about 1% less in capital ‚Äãexpenditures in 2026 than in ‚Äã2025.
That compares with a ‚Å†decline of around 4% in 2025, roughly flat year-on-year spending in 2024, and increases of 27% in 2023, 40% in 2022, and 4% in 2021.
With U.S. spot crude ‚Äãprices expected to fall for the fourth year in a row in 2026, ‚Äãthe U.S. Energy ‚Å†Information Administration (EIA) projected crude output would hold at 13.6 million barrels per day (bpd) in 2026, matching 2025's record high.
The EIA, however, made that oil price projection before the U.S.-Iran war. Since then, oil prices have spiked to their highest ‚Å†since 2023.
While ‚Äãon the gas side, EIA projected output would rise from a ‚Äãrecord 107.6 billion cubic feet per day (bcfd) in 2025 to 110.0 bcfd in 2026 with spot prices at the Henry Hub benchmark in ‚ÄãLouisiana forecast to jump by about 22% in 2026.

item.title: South Bow says US, Canada policy shift boosts prospects for Keystone XL revival
item.description:
CALGARY, March 6 (Reuters) - The company ‚Äãbehind a plan to revive the Keystone XL pipeline system to increase Canadian oil exports to ‚Äåthe United States is hopeful a North American policy shift in favour of energy development will shelter its project from the political and regulatory hurdles that led to its cancellation in 2021.
CEO Bevin Wirzba of Canadian pipeline operator South Bow said Friday the world ‚Äãhas changed since U.S. President Joe Biden canceled the permit for Keystone XL after years of Indigenous ‚Äãand environmental opposition.
"The policy environment in North America has been far more constructive," Wirzba said. ‚Å†He said the current war in Iran as well as the ongoing conflict in Ukraine emphasize the need ‚Äãfor energy security.
"Those realities are a great backdrop for us to provide maybe a solution," Wirzba said on a ‚Äãconference call.
South Bow, which was set up by former Keystone XL proponent TC Energy in 2024 to take over its oil pipeline business, is considering reviving some of the line that was already built in Alberta and already has all necessary Canadian permits.
The proposal is ‚Äãnot identical to Keystone XL, as the U.S. segment of the pipeline would be built not by South Bow, ‚Äãbut by U.S. partners. The full route and details of how the various segments would come together remain unknown, but the ‚Äågoal is ‚Å†to ultimately transport up to 55,000 bpd of Canadian oil to the U.S. Gulf Coast.
The company's plan comes as U.S. President Donald Trump has sought to speed energy project permitting times and boost domestic oil and gas production. A presidential permit would be required for the proposed new pipeline to ‚Äãcross the Canada-U.S. border.
In Canada, ‚ÄãPrime Minister Mark Carney ‚Å†is depending on the energy sector to help boost the country's economy, and has pledged to speed permitting times in addition to dropping some climate legislation that the oil industry said ‚Äãwas impeding its growth.
Carney's moves thus far have materially moved the needle in favour ‚Äãof considering a ‚Å†pipeline development, Wirzba said. He said South Bow has launched a formal open season process aimed at gauging commercial interest in new pipeline space, and more broadly, gauging the Canadian oil industry's confidence in its ability to grow production in the ‚Å†coming ‚Äãyears.
Wirzba declined to say what its proposed project ‚Äî which South Bow is ‚Äãcalling the "Prairie Connector" pipeline ‚Äî would cost or when construction might start.
But he said many of Canada's oil sands companies have indicated they plan ‚Äãto increase output materially over the next three to five years.

item.title: Brazil's Petrobras raises possibility of extra dividends as oil prices surge
item.description:
RIO DE JANEIRO, March 6 (Reuters) - Brazil's state-run oil company Petrobras (PETR3.SA) , opens new tab could distribute extra dividends this year if ‚Äãcash flow is high, as oil prices surge while its operations ‚Äåhave so far been unaffected by the Middle-East U.S.-Iran conflict, executives told analysts on Friday.
Petrobras will need to be ready for variations in oil prices, CEO Magda Chambriard said, adding ‚Äãthat the company will not pass the sudden swings onto Brazilian consumers after attacks ‚Äãby the U.S. and Israel in Iran disrupted global energy flows earlier this week.
"We're living in ‚Å†a time of high geopolitical instability, where our concern is to leave the ‚Äãcompany prepared for any scenario that may occur regarding the price of oil," ‚ÄãChambriard said.
In 2025, Petrobras did not distribute extraordinary dividends, as lower Brent oil prices made it difficult for the firm to generate surplus cash flow. But Petrobras could do so this ‚Äãyear, if the sharp upturn in prices remains, said CFO Fernando Melgarejo.
"I would ‚Äãlove to submit an extraordinary dividend offering, provided we are certain that it will not impact ‚Äåthe ‚Å†financing of our projects," said Melgarejo, adding that Petrobras will remain cautious in its decision-making due to the unstable scenario.
In the short-term Petrobras has benefited from the conflict, said Petrobras' head of logistics, commercialization and markets, Claudio Schlosser, as it ‚Äãships mainly to China, ‚ÄãIndia and Europe, ‚Å†regions far from the conflict zone.
"We've seen better margins," he said. "In a way, there is an increase in value and ‚Äãan interesting positioning for the company."
Oil prices have hit highs ‚Äãin the ‚Å†wake of the conflict, with Brent hitting $90 per barrel for the first time since April 2024 and on track for its strongest weekly gain since the volatility seen ‚Å†during ‚Äãthe COVID-19 pandemic in the spring of 2020.
On Thursday ‚ÄãPetrobras posted results for 2025 with record exports offsetting a drop in Brent oil prices for the year.

item.title: Hezbollah saw new war with Israel as inevitable and rearmed for months, sources say
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 5 (Reuters) - Lebanese armed group Hezbollah spent months restocking its arsenal of rockets and drones, using support from Iran and its own weapons factories to prepare for a new war with Israel, six sources familiar with the group's preparations said.
Down but not ‚Äãout after its devastating 2024 conflict with Israel, Hezbollah had concluded that another round of fighting was inevitable - and that this time, it could face an existential threat, according to the sources.
Reuters spoke ‚Äåto three Lebanese sources briefed on Hezbollah's activities, two foreign officials in Lebanon and an Israeli military official, who all spoke on condition of anonymity as they were not authorised to speak to the press.
The details of Hezbollah's recent efforts to rearm have not been previously reported.
The head of Hezbollah's media office, Youssef al-Zein, told Reuters that Hezbollah would not comment on its military operations, though he said the group had decided to "fight to the last breath."
Founded by Iran's Revolutionary Guards in 1982, Hezbollah launched rockets and drones at Israel on Monday to ‚Äãavenge the death of Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, pulling Lebanon into the war raging across the Middle East.
Although the decision caught some of its own officials off guard , Hezbollah had been readying its military stockpiles and its command-and-control ‚Äãstructure for an eventual rematch with Israel, the six sources said.
To do so, it had drawn on a monthly budget of $50 million, most of it from Iran and earmarked ‚Å†for fighters' salaries, according to one of the Lebanese sources, who has been briefed on the group's finances and military activities. One of the foreign officials confirmed the $50 million budget.
It was not immediately clear how long the group had been ‚Äãrelying on that monthly budget and how it compared to its previous financial resources.
The group has said funds from Iran helped finance rents for people displaced by the 2024 war. Around 60,000 Lebanese, most of them from the Shi'ite Muslim community from which ‚ÄãHezbollah draws its popular support, remained displaced over the last year, with their homes still in ruins.
Hezbollah had also worked to replenish its drone and rocket stashes through local manufacturing, the first Lebanese source, the foreign officials and the Israeli military official said. The Israeli military official said Hezbollah had used Iranian funding both to smuggle arms and make its own weapons, but added that its manufacturing capability had been diminished.
The second foreign official said the group had stationed new rockets and Iranian-made logistical materials in southern Lebanon before the latest war began.
Hezbollah's ‚Äãmedia office did not immediately respond to questions on its rearmament and Iranian support for it.
Israeli military spokesperson Lieutenant Colonel Nadav Shoshani told Reuters that Hezbollah "had a lot of arms left" and was also seeking to rearm. "They were trying to ‚Äãsmuggle and we were preventing that," Shoshani said.
In 2024, a punishing two-month war with Israel ended with a ceasefire brokered by the United States. Hezbollah halted its attacks on Israel, which continued strikes on what it said were Hezbollah's efforts ‚Äåto rebuild military ‚Å†capabilities.
Israel also kept troops in five hilltop positions in southern Lebanon.
Last year, Lebanon also began confiscating Hezbollah weapons in the country's south - but Israel said the group was rearming faster than it was being disarmed.
Speaking to Reuters weeks before Hezbollah entered the regional war, the first Lebanese source confirmed that the group had been rebuilding its military capabilities "in parallel" with Israel's campaign to destroy them.
The pace of Hezbollah's attacks this week provides clues about its weapons stocks.
The group launched 60 drones and rockets on March 2, the first day it attacked Israel, and a similar number the following day, said the second foreign official, who tracks Hezbollah's activities closely.
But on March 4, Hezbollah launched more than double that number of projectiles, a sign it had been ‚Äãable to draw from its larger caches, the official said.
ALMA, ‚Äãan Israeli think tank that monitors security on Israel's ‚Å†northern border, said it assessed that Hezbollah's arsenal on the eve of its attack included approximately 25,000 rockets and missiles, most of them short- and medium-range.
A video published by Hezbollah on March 4 showed a fighter setting up a drone in a wooded area. Riad Kahwaji, a Dubai-based defence analyst and founder of the Institute for Near East and Gulf Military ‚ÄãAnalysis, identified the drone in the video as a Shahed-101, which he told Reuters could be produced locally.
Hezbollah has also dispatched fighters from its ‚Äãelite Radwan force back to ‚Å†southern Lebanon, Reuters reported this week . They had been withdrawn from the area after the 2024 conflict.
Israeli strikes after the 2024 ceasefire included the targeting of what Israel said were Hezbollah training camps. In late February, the Israeli military said it struck eight military compounds used by the Radwan force to store weapons and prepare for a confrontation.
The Israeli official and the first foreign official said Hezbollah had been struggling to recruit new operatives as a result.
The group lost 5,000 fighters in the 2024 war, an unprecedented blow ‚Å†to its fighting ‚Äãforce, though the second Lebanese source said it still had some 95,000 fighters left.
In the lead-up to its entry into the current regional war, ‚ÄãHezbollah had become convinced Israel would carry out a major strike on the group that would seek to "disable its ability to retaliate," the first Lebanese source said.
A third foreign official familiar with Hezbollah's thinking said that assessment had driven the group's decision to launch the first salvo, fearing Israel would eventually ‚Äãturn its attention from Iran to Hezbollah.
"They knew they were next on the list," the official said.

item.title: US files complaints for $15.3 million in proceeds allegedly linked to Iranian oil network
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - The United States on Friday filed two civil ‚Äãforfeiture complaints for more ‚Äåthan $15.3 million used to allegedly fund an illicit Iranian oil ‚Äãdistribution network, the Justice ‚ÄãDepartment said in a statement.
The ‚Å†complaints by the Justice ‚ÄãDepartment allege that the funds ‚Äãwere being used by Mohammad Hossein Shamkhani to "operate multiple distribution companies" ‚Äãengaged in selling and ‚Äãshipping Iranian oil and other commodities in ‚Äåviolation ‚Å†of U.S. sanctions.
Hossein Shamkhani is the son of Ali Shamkhani, it said, a ‚Äãtop adviser ‚Äãto ‚Å†the former Supreme Leader of Iran Ayatollah ‚ÄãAli Khamenei. Both Ali ‚ÄãShamkhani ‚Å†and Ali Khamenei were killed in the U.S. and ‚Å†Israeli strikes ‚Äãagainst Iran ‚Äãlast week.

item.title: Swedish coast guard boards suspected stateless ship in the Baltic Sea
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - The Swedish coast guard said it boarded and took control of a suspected stateless vessel in Swedish ‚Äãwaters of the Baltic Sea on Friday, and it ‚Äåwas carrying out investigations on the ship.
"At 3:50 p.m. today, the Coast Guard took control of a vessel with unclear flag ‚Äãstatus and thus suspected of being a stateless vessel," ‚Äãit said in a statement, adding that the ‚Å†boarding had taken place off the coast of the ‚Äãsouthern Swedish town Trelleborg.
The ship Caffa was sailing under a ‚ÄãGuinean flag, but the coast guard said it regarded the vessel to be stateless "based on national and international legislation". Swedish police said the ‚Äãship was suspected of sailing under a false flag.
According ‚Äãto ship-tracking service Marine Traffic, Caffa is a 96-meter general cargo ‚Äåship.
Sweden's ‚Å†minister for civil defence, Carl-Oskar Bohlin, said in a post on X that the ship was a freighter on Ukraine's sanctions list.
"The ownership structure is unclear and there are ‚Äãsuspicions that the ‚Äãvessel is ‚Å†not insured. As recently as this summer, the ship is said to have changed from ‚Äãa Russian to a Guinean flag," Bohlin ‚Äãwrote.
The coast ‚Å†guard said it had launched a preliminary investigation into suspected violation of maritime law regarding lack of seaworthiness.
"The Coast Guard ‚Å†has personnel ‚Äãon board to gather information ‚Äãabout the condition of the vessel and crew, conduct searches, and conduct interviews," ‚Äãit added.

item.title: Ghana peacekeeping battalion hit by missile attacks in Lebanon, Ghana army says
item.description:
ACCRA, March 6 (Reuters) - The headquarters of Ghana's United ‚ÄãNations peacekeeping battalion in Lebanon ‚Äåwas hit by missile attacks on Friday, leaving two soldiers critically ‚Äãinjured, Ghana's armed forces ‚Äãsaid in a statement.
Lebanon was pulled ‚Å†into the war in the ‚ÄãMiddle East on Monday, when Hezbollah ‚Äãfired rockets at Israel that ignited a new Israeli offensive against the group.
Ghanaian ‚Äãsoldiers are deployed as part ‚Äãof the UNIFIL mission in Lebanon.
The statement ‚Äåfrom ‚Å†Ghana's armed forces did not say who fired the missiles, but it said Ghana's foreign ‚Äãministry ‚Äãhad protested ‚Å†the incident at U.N. headquarters in New York.
In ‚Äãaddition to the two ‚Äãcritically ‚Å†injured soldiers, the statement said that another soldier "has been traumatised" ‚Å†while ‚Äãthe officers' mess ‚Äãfacility was hit and burned down.

item.title: Germany pulls further troops out of Middle East
item.description:
BERLIN, March 6 (Reuters) - Germany has pulled additional Bundeswehr troops out of the Middle East, ‚Äãa military spokesman told Reuters on Friday, ‚Äåthe seventh day of a U.S.‚ÄìIsraeli war on Iran that has convulsed the region.
Soldiers deployed ‚Äãwith the UNIFIL mission in Lebanon ‚Äãare being withdrawn due to the security ‚Å†situation, the spokesperson said after the ‚ÄãBundeswehr already sharply reduced its presence in Erbil ‚Äãin northern Iraq.
The RND newspaper network, which first reported the news, said that German troops stationed ‚Äãin Bahrain had already returned home, and ‚Äãpreparations for withdrawals from Kuwait were underway. The spokesperson ‚Äådeclined ‚Å†to comment on this.
The RND report also said that soldiers and staff from the German embassy in Baghdad were being ‚Äãrelocated to Jordan. ‚ÄãThe ‚Å†foreign ministry did not respond to a request for comment from ‚ÄãReuters on the move.
Roughly 500 German ‚Äãsoldiers ‚Å†are deployed across the region, mainly in Iraq and Jordan. Officials have recently moved ‚Å†personnel ‚Äãout of camps and ‚Äãreduced some contingents amid heightened security risks.

item.title: Barclays says Brent could test $120/bbl if Middle East tensions persist
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Barclays said on Friday that Brent crude could potentially test $120 a barrel if the Middle ‚ÄãEast conflict persists for another couple of weeks.
"These numbers ‚Äåmight seem too high, especially given widespread pessimism about the oil market outlook heading into this year, but we reiterate that fundamentals are stronger ‚Äãand risks are bigger than the Russia-Ukraine conflict , when we ‚Äãsaw these levels materialize," Barclays added.
Oil prices have jumped ‚Å†sharply as the widening U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran has effectively closed ‚Äãthe Strait of Hormuz, constraining Middle East supplies.
Shipping through the Strait ‚Äãof Hormuz, which carries about a fifth of global oil and oil and liquefied natural gas, has been disrupted after Iran threatened to fire on ‚Äãpassing vessels.
Brent crude futures were trading around $93.60 per barrel and ‚ÄãWest Texas Intermediate were at $91.62 as of 1857 GMT.
Barclays said oil volumes stranded ‚Äåon ‚Å†tankers in the Middle East Gulf have risen by about 85 million barrels since the conflict began, adding that risks to oil prices remain skewed to the upside.
U.S. President Donald Trump demanded ‚ÄãIran's "unconditional surrender" on ‚ÄãFriday, a dramatic ‚Å†escalation of his demands a week into the war he launched alongside Israel, which could make ‚Äãit more difficult to negotiate a swift end ‚Äãto hostilities.
"Production shut-ins ‚Å†in Iraq and Kuwait are already happening and might spread to UAE and even Saudi Arabia over time," Barclays said.
Barclays said ‚Å†the ‚Äãfar‚Äëend 10% scenario now implies Brent could ‚Äãhit $150 a barrel before the end of the month.

item.title: Trump to forge ahead with immigration crackdown driven by top aide Stephen Miller
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump is expected to forge ahead with his aggressive immigration crackdown driven by top aide Stephen Miller and a new homeland secretary nominee who shares Trump's ‚Äãhardline view, current and former U.S. officials and lawmakers said.
The Republican president fired Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem on Thursday after months of controversy over heavy-handed immigration enforcement tactics ‚Äåand lawmaker questions about government contracts and turmoil within her department.
But Miller - the White House deputy chief of staff and the architect of Trump‚Äôs immigration agenda - retains control of the issue, three U.S. officials said. And Trump‚Äôs pick to become homeland secretary, U.S. Senator Markwayne Mullin of Oklahoma, is viewed as closely aligned with Trump‚Äôs approach.
‚ÄúStephen is a survivor,‚Äù one of the officials said, requesting anonymity to discuss internal dynamics. The official said Noem‚Äôs firing was not a referendum on Trump's restrictive ‚Äãagenda, but rather on the execution of it.
In response to a request for comment, a White House official said Miller helps coordinate a wide range of issues - from immigration to counter-cartel ‚Äãoperations - and is "working to ensure the President's policy agenda is implemented."
The Department of Homeland Security and Mullin's office did not respond to requests for comment.
Trump won ‚Å†back the White House in 2024 with a campaign that heavily focused on stopping illegal immigration and ramping up deportations. Immigration was one of Trump's best-polling issues after returning to office - and a key ‚Äãfocus for the Republican Party broadly. But amid backlash over his aggressive enforcement in U.S. cities, support for his immigration approach declined in recent months , Reuters/Ipsos polls show.
Republicans currently hold narrow majorities in both chambers of ‚Äãthe U.S. Congress but are heading into midterm elections in November that threaten their control on Washington.
White House spokesperson Karoline Leavitt said on Thursday in an X post related to Noem‚Äôs dismissal that Trump‚Äôs immigration goals remained unchanged.
‚ÄúPresident Trump‚Äôs immigration agenda is keeping our border secure and deporting illegal alien criminals from our country, and it will continue without interruption,‚Äù she said.
Under Noem‚Äôs leadership, DHS sent thousands of federal immigration agents to Los Angeles, Chicago, Minneapolis and other Democratic-led cities to seek ‚Äãout immigration offenders, sweeping through residential neighborhoods and chasing day laborers in Home Depot parking lots.
But after federal agents shot and killed two U.S. citizens in Minneapolis - Renee Good and Alex Pretti - the administration said it ‚Äãwould move to a less public-facing ‚Äútargeted‚Äù approach to immigration arrests.
Current and former federal immigration officials said there had been no immediate policy shift since Noem‚Äôs departure was announced, but several said they expected the administration to continue to avoid ‚Äåhigh-profile surges into ‚Å†U.S. cities following the fallout in Minneapolis.
Mullin, 48, became a senator in 2023 after a decade in the U.S. House of Representatives. Like Noem, he is a rancher and owns a cattle ranch in Oklahoma that also serves as a wedding venue, among other businesses.
Even as a freshman, Mullin has stood out from the crowd of 100 senators, at times presiding over the Senate wearing a large, light-colored cowboy hat.
He had a short-lived career as a mixed martial arts fighter and garnered widespread media attention in 2023 when he rose from his chair during a Senate hearing to challenge Teamsters President ‚ÄãSean O'Brien to "stand your butt up" and fight.
Mullin voted ‚Äãfor a Republican-backed funding package last year ‚Å†that devoted a historic $170 billion to immigration enforcement through September 2029 and backs core elements of Trump‚Äôs immigration platform.
Republican lawmakers praised Mullin after the news that Trump would nominate him for DHS secretary, a position that requires majority confirmation in the Senate.
‚ÄúHe's strong on the border and that's what we need,‚Äù Republican Senator Eric ‚ÄãSchmitt of Missouri told reporters on Thursday. ‚ÄúThe deportations will continue."
Noem was sharply criticized by both Democrats and Republicans for swiftly saying Good and Pretti were ‚Äãengaged in ‚Äúdomestic terrorism‚Äù after they ‚Å†were killed and before a full investigation.
Mullin similarly portrayed Pretti as a threat despite video evidence that undercut that claim. He said Pretti was "a deranged individual" who had a loaded pistol and intended "to cause max damage," during an interview with Fox News on January 24, the day Pretti was killed.
Democrats on Capitol Hill welcomed Noem‚Äôs removal but said the Trump administration‚Äôs approach to immigration enforcement still needed to change. Democrats have blocked funding for DHS ‚Å†since mid-February in ‚Äãa push to force the White House to moderate its tactics.
Senator Chris Murphy, a Democrat from Connecticut, said funding talks are ‚Äãbeing run out of the White House and that he does not expect Noem‚Äôs firing to break the stalemate.
‚ÄúI think we‚Äôre better off without her, but she wasn‚Äôt running the department,‚Äù Murphy said on Thursday. ‚ÄúStephen Miller runs that department and will continue to ‚Äãrun the department so I don‚Äôt really think much will change.‚Äù

item.title: Israeli military says unintentionally struck UN agency truck in Gaza
item.description:
TEL AVIV, March 6 (Reuters) - Israel's military said on Friday that a "firing component" launched by its navy unintentionally struck a fuel ‚Äãtruck belonging to a United Nations agency in Gaza ‚Äåthe previous day, an incident that prompted the agency to publicly call for a full investigation.
The United Nations Office for Project Services, ‚Äãwhich oversees fuel distribution in Gaza, said that the ‚Äãempty fuel truck was struck on Thursday around ‚Å†5 a.m. from the direction of the sea, causing damage ‚Äãto the vehicle. There were no injuries.
"Our teams are taking ‚Äãextraordinary risks every day to keep humanitarian operations and life-sustaining services running," UNOPS Executive Director Jorge Moreira da Silva said in a statement, ‚Äãcalling for an investigation into the incident.
"They should not ‚Äãhave to do that under fire," he said.
In response to Reuters questions, ‚Äåthe ‚Å†Israeli military said that the incident occurred during defensive naval activity, and that a firing component deviated from its intended trajectory.
The fuel truck sustained "minor damage", the military said in a ‚Äãstatement. The military ‚Äãdid not ‚Å†say what type of munitions had been fired, or what had been the navy's intended ‚Äãtarget.
"The incident was reviewed, and lessons were learned ‚Äãaccordingly," ‚Å†it said, without providing further details.
The fuel truck had been on its way to the Kerem Shalom crossing when it was ‚Å†struck, ‚Äãand the truck's movements had been ‚Äãcoordinated with Israeli authorities in advance, UNOPS said.

item.title: Private credit woes come for Larry Fink‚Äôs legacy
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Rising disquiet in private credit is uncomfortable for a lot of Wall Street titans, but especially for Larry Fink. The BlackRock (BLK.N) , opens new tab boss moved aggressively to transform his firm, the kingpin of public-markets investing for ‚Äãthe masses, into a private-markets rival to the likes of Blackstone (BX.N) , opens new tab or Apollo Global Management (APO.N) , opens new tab . The timing now looks awkward, as worries ‚Äåabout overheated lending mount and a key retail fund caps investor withdrawals. Fink‚Äôs legacy project , and his company‚Äôs strengths, will be tested.
As part of a $28 billion string of deals to move into private markets, BlackRock agreed to acquire non-bank lender HPS Investment Partners in 2024. Included in that $12 billion acquisition was HPS Corporate Lending Partners, or HLEND, a nontraded business development ‚Äãcompany (BDC). Such funds target affluent individual investors, allowing them to withdraw their cash up to a set cap, typically of 5% of shares per ‚Äãquarter. According to a shareholder letter , opens new tab , HLEND received requests totaling 9.3%, but will only offer redemptions up to its limit, ‚Å†as it was designed to do.
This is far from the first hiccup in private credit. A Blue Owl (OWL.N) , opens new tab fund barred regular withdrawals, instead selling assets to raise ‚Äãcash and return a huge chunk at once.
