To satisfy elevated redemptions at BCRED, the largest nontraded BDC around, Blackstone employees and the firm itself put in ‚Äãa combined $400 million to ensure all requests were covered.
HLEND, though, is the first BDC of its kind to actually prorate redemptions, though others are expected to end up following suit, according to Kevin Gannon of investment bank RA Stanger. Turning theoretical fine-print restrictions into reality may well annoy, and possibly frighten, customers.
When BlackRock bought HPS, private credit was running red-hot. ‚ÄãAssets under management in direct lending funds alone had climbed to $889 billion, according to Preqin data. Apollo and its ilk saw an addressable market in ‚Äãthe tens of trillions of dollars by expanding into IOUs secured by specific assets. These strategies are increasingly pitched as a fit for the kind of mom-and-pop savers that made ‚ÄåBlackRock a ‚Å†colossus, with $14 trillion of assets under management.
The company got there in part by inking perhaps the most successful deal in modern asset management history, snapping up Barclays‚Äô BGI in 2009 for $13.5 billion and riding then-explosive growth in exchange traded funds. If anyone has a track record of cracking new markets and reaching the retail masses through M&A, it‚Äôs Larry Fink.
His public-markets business is still immensely successful, and HLEND is only one small piece of a larger plan. Still, rivals ‚Äãlike Blackstone are increasingly trying to eat ‚ÄãBlackRock's lunch by selling retail products. ‚Å†Meanwhile, from Apollo boss Marc Rowan to JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon, warnings of a coming private credit , opens new tab shakeout are rising. As HLEND‚Äôs investor letter put it, disruptive periods breed new opportunities. BlackRock‚Äôs challenge is to prove it can capitalize on ‚Äãthem.
Follow Jonathan Guilford on X , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Iran's UN envoy says 1,332 Iranian civilians killed in war
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Iran's U.N. ambassador ‚ÄãAmir Saeid Iravani ‚Äåon Friday said at ‚Äãleast 1,332 ‚ÄãIranian civilians had ‚Å†lost their ‚Äãlives so ‚Äãfar in the conflict with Israel ‚Äãand the ‚ÄãUnited States and ‚Äåthousands ‚Å†more had been injured.
He made his comments ‚Äãwhile ‚Äãspeaking ‚Å†to reporters at ‚Äãthe United ‚ÄãNations ‚Å†in New York.

item.title: EU countries joining US, Israel attacks would become Iran's "legitimate target", Deputy Minister says
item.description:
PARIS, March 6 (Reuters) - European Union member states that join the U.S. and ‚ÄãIsrael attacks on Iran would ‚Äåbecome "legitimate targets" for Iran, Deputy Foreign Minister Majid Takht-Ravanchi said in an interview ‚Äãwith TV station France 24.
"Any ‚Äãcountry that joins in the ‚Å†aggression against Iran, joins America and ‚ÄãIsrael in the aggression against ‚ÄãIran, definitely, they will be also legitimate targets for Iran retaliation," Takht-Ravanchi said.

item.title: UK's Starmer discusses military, intelligence support with Saudi crown prince
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - British Prime Minister Keir Starmer spoke ‚Äãto Saudi Arabia Crown ‚ÄåPrince Mohammed bin Salman after Iran's strikes ‚Äãon the kingdom ‚Äãamid a sprawling conflict in the ‚Å†region, Downing Street ‚Äãsaid in a statement , opens new tab on ‚ÄãFriday.
"Additional British fighter jets, helicopters and a destroyer were ‚Äãbeing sent to ‚Äãthe region, and the UK ‚Äåstood ‚Å†ready to support the defence of the Kingdom of Saudi ‚ÄãArabia should ‚Äãit ‚Å†be needed," Starmer told bin ‚ÄãSalman.
The two leaders ‚Äãalso ‚Å†discussed greater intelligence cooperation in support of ‚Å†defensive ‚Äãoperations and civilians.

item.title: US, Ecuador bomb drug trafficker camp near Colombia border, militaries say
item.description:
WASHINGTON/QUITO, March 6 (Reuters) - The U.S. and Ecuador carried out a ‚Äãjoint operation targeting drug trafficking operations in the South American ‚Äåcountry, authorities in both countries said on Friday, with the U.S. calling the move "lethal kinetic operations."
Neither the U.S. Southern Command, a branch of its military that oversees forces ‚Äãin Latin America, nor Ecuador's defense ministry, said if anyone ‚Äãwas killed or captured in the strike, which Ecuador dubbed ‚Å†operation "Total Extermination."
The operations used helicopters, aircraft, river boats and drones to ‚Äãlocate and bomb a drug traffickers' training camp in north-east Ecuador near ‚Äãthe Colombian border, Ecuador's defense ministry said in a statement.
The camp belonged to the Comandos de la Frontera (CDF), a Colombian crime group made up of FARC dissidents, and ‚Äãhad a capacity for 50 people, it added.
Ecuadorean President Daniel Noboa ‚Äãhas made a military crackdown on organised crime a cornerstone of his administration, and ‚Äåhis ‚Å†government imposed tariffs on its larger neighbor Colombia, accusing it of not doing enough to fight drug trafficking.
He is set to travel to Miami this weekend to take part in the Trump administration's "Shield of the Americas" ‚Äãsummit, which brings ‚Äãtogether many right-wing ‚Å†leaders across the region with a focus on regional security and organized crime .
"The United States is a key ally ‚Äãin this fight," the defense ministry said.
"At the ‚Äãrequest of ‚Å†Ecuador, the Department of War executed targeted action to advance our shared objective of dismantling narco-terrorist networks," Pentagon spokesman Sean Parnell wrote on X.
The ‚Å†operation followed ‚Äãa similar U.S-Ecuadorean operation announced by the ‚ÄãU.S. Southern Command earlier this week .

item.title: US skips congressional review to approve munitions sale to Israel
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - The U.S. State Department ‚Äãsaid on Friday ‚Äåit has made a determination approving a ‚Äãpossible foreign military ‚Äãsale to Israel of ‚Å†munitions and munitions ‚Äãsupport for $151.8 million, without congressional ‚Äãreview.
Israel has requested to buy 12,000 BLU-110A/B general ‚Äãpurpose, 1,000-pound bomb ‚Äãbodies, the State Department said ‚Äåin ‚Å†a statement.
Secretary of State Marco Rubio determined that an ‚Äãemergency existed ‚Äãthat ‚Å†required the immediate sale to ‚ÄãIsrael. The announcement ‚Äãcomes ‚Å†during the U.S. and Israel's war ‚Å†against ‚ÄãIran.

item.title: US judge dismisses case by deported student who declined flight back
item.description:
BOSTON, March 6 (Reuters) - A U.S. judge who had ordered President Donald Trump 's administration ‚Äãto facilitate the return of a college student it deported to Honduras said on Friday the "sad truth" is ‚Äåthat her decision not to board a plane it arranged back to the United States meant her case must be dismissed.
Any Lucia Lopez Belloza, a 20-year-old freshman at Babson College in Massachusetts, declined on February 27 to board a flight arranged by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement to bring her back ‚Äãto the United States after the administration said it would try to deport her again if she returned.
Lopez Belloza, ‚Äãwho came to the United States from Honduras when she was 8, has said she was unaware ‚Å†she was subject to a final order of removal that was entered when she was 11.
Her lawyers had urged Boston-based U.S. ‚ÄãDistrict Judge Richard Stearns to let Lopez Belloza continue the lawsuit she filed after being detained by immigration authorities at Boston's ‚ÄãLogan International Airport in November while traveling to spend the Thanksgiving holiday with her family in Texas.
But Stearns reaffirmed his earlier conclusion that he did not have jurisdiction to hear the case concerning her detention because by the time it was filed on November 21, she had been flown by ‚Äãimmigration authorities to Texas.
His sole remaining basis for jurisdiction would have been to enforce an order issued by another judge just ‚Äãminutes after Lopez Belloza's case was filed barring her from being deported or transferred out of Massachusetts for 72 hours. Lopez Belloza was flown ‚Äåfrom Texas ‚Å†to Honduras the next day despite that judicial order.
A U.S. government lawyer later apologized to Stearns for a "mistake" made by a ICE officer who failed to properly alert others in the agency about the existence of the judicial order.
Stearns on February 13 ordered the administration to rectify the error by facilitating her return to the United States. The administration last week said it would do so ‚Äãby having Lopez Belloza board the ‚ÄãICE flight from Honduras to ‚Å†Texas.
But the administration also said ICE planned , opens new tab to move to deport her again upon arrival and had the authority to detain her. Lopez Belloza said the "nightmare" situation caused her to decline to board ‚Äãthe flight and remain in Honduras.
"The sad truth is that when Any declined the flight she ‚Äãalso waived this ‚Å†court's only remaining basis for jurisdiction," Stearns wrote.
Stearns said had she boarded the plane, the judicial order barring her swift deportation would have remained in effect, giving her "ample opportunity" to file a new case in Texas to challenge her detention.
Todd Pomerleau, Lopez Belloza's lawyer, said ‚Å†he will ‚Äãappeal.

item.title: Iran war enters second week as Trump demands 'unconditional surrender'
item.description:
BEIRUT/WASHINGTON/JERUSALEM, March 7 (Reuters) - The expanding war in Iran entered its second week on Saturday amid renewed uncertainty about how or when hostilities will end, as U.S. President Donald Trump declared he would only accept Tehran's "unconditional surrender" and Israel traded fresh attacks with Iran and Lebanon.
Trump's comments ‚Äãon social media on Friday came hours after Iran's president announced that unnamed countries had begun mediation efforts, briefly raising the possibility, however faint, of a diplomatic resolution a week after ‚Äåthe U.S. and Israel launched strikes on Iran.
"There will be no deal with Iran except UNCONDITIONAL SURRENDER!" Trump wrote. "After that, and the selection of a GREAT & ACCEPTABLE Leader(s), we, and many of our wonderful and very brave allies and partners, will work tirelessly to bring Iran back from the brink of destruction, making it economically bigger, better, and stronger than ever before."
Trump has offered shifting explanations of his war aims, raising the possibility of an extended regional conflict that has already spilled well beyond Iran's borders, shaken global financial markets ‚Äãand sent oil prices soaring.
In response to the attack, Iran has targeted Israel as well as multiple Gulf states that host U.S. military installations.
Inside Israel on Friday, explosions could be heard as Israeli defenses ‚Äãactivated to shoot down incoming Iranian fire. The UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain and Saudi Arabia all reported fresh drone and missile attacks.
Meanwhile, Israel pursued a major expansion of the ‚Å†war in Lebanon, pounding the capital Beirut on Friday after ordering an unprecedented evacuation of the city's entire southern suburbs.
Israel also launched a new wave of attacks on Iran, saying 50 of its warplanes had hit a bunker ‚Äãstill being used by Iran's leadership beneath slain Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's destroyed Tehran compound.
Early on Saturday, Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency reported that Mehrabad Airport in Tehran had been struck.
There was no immediate comment from Iran's Revolutionary ‚ÄãGuard or Hezbollah.
Israel has extended its bombing to Lebanon to root out Hezbollah , the Shi'ite militia allied to Iran that has been a dominant faction in Lebanese politics since the 1980s. Hezbollah fired on Israel this week to avenge the death of Khamenei.
"We're sleeping here in the streets - some in cars, some on the street, some on the beach," said Jamal Seifeddin, 43, who fled Beirut's southern suburbs and spent the night on the streets in the downtown district.
About 300,000 people have been displaced in Lebanon in the past four days, ‚Äãaccording to the Norwegian Refugee Council.
The Israeli military says it has destroyed 80% of Iran's air-defense systems in the first week of the campaign and disabled more than 60% of its missile launchers.
Trump's demand for Iran's surrender, ‚Äãand the likelihood that it would complicate any quick path to ending the conflict, sent European and U.S. stock indexes tumbling on Friday. Oil prices hit their highest prices in years with the critical shipping lane of the Strait of Hormuz effectively ‚Äåclosed down.
Trump ‚Å†told Reuters in a telephone interview on Thursday that he must have a say in selecting Iran's new supreme leader to replace Khamenei, killed on the war's first day , a demand he repeated on Friday in a remarkable assertion of power over the country of more than 90 million people.
Iran's U.N. ambassador, Amir Saeid Iravani, told reporters that new leadership would be chosen "in accordance with our constitutional procedures and solely by the will of the Iranian people - without any foreign interference."
Israel has said openly that it aims to overthrow Iran's ruling system. It has been bombing parts of western Iran to support Iranian Kurdish militias who hope to exploit the war to seize towns near the frontier, according to three sources ‚Äãfamiliar with Israel's talks with the factions.
Iran has cast the ‚Äãwar as an unprovoked attack and describes the ‚Å†killing of Khamenei as an assassination.
Earlier on Friday, Iran President Masoud Pezeshkian posted on social media: "Some countries have begun mediation efforts." He did not identify the countries or provide further details.
Russia is providing Iran with locations of U.S. warships and aircraft in the Middle East after Iran's ability to locate U.S. forces was degraded, the Washington ‚ÄãPost reported, citing three officials familiar with the intelligence.
Russian missions in the U.S. did not immediately respond to requests for comment on the report.
Trump met with executives from ‚Äãseven defense contractors on Friday, who ‚Å†he later said had agreed to accelerate weapons production. The administration has been pressuring contractors as Iran and other recent operations have drawn down supplies.
Karoline Leavitt, a White House spokeswoman, said the U.S. has enough weapons stockpiles to meet the needs of its Iran operations, which she said would take about four to six weeks to complete.
At least 1,332 people have been killed in Iran since the U.S. and Israel launched strikes on February 28, Iravani said, citing the Iranian Red Crescent ‚Å†Society.
The Lebanese health ‚Äãministry has reported 123 people killed and 683 wounded as a result of Israeli attacks. Iranian attacks have killed 11 people in ‚ÄãIsrael since the war started, and at least six U.S. service members have been killed.
Two U.S. officials told Reuters that military investigators believed it was likely that U.S. forces were responsible for an apparent strike on an Iranian girls' school that killed scores of children on the first day ‚Äãof the war. The investigators have not yet reached a final conclusion.

item.title: Pakistani convicted of plotting to kill Trump over death of Iran commander
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - A Pakistani man was convicted on Friday of planning to kill President Donald Trump and other prominent U.S. politicians two years ago at the behest ‚Äãof Iran , the Department of Justice said.
Asif Merchant was accused of trying to recruit ‚Äåpeople in the U.S. in a plan targeting Trump and others in retaliation for Washington's killing of Iranian military commander Qassem Soleimani in 2020, when Trump was in his first term.
Targets in the ‚Äã2024 plot also included then-President Joe Biden and Nikki Haley, who ran ‚Äãagainst Trump that year for the Republican presidential nomination, federal prosecutors said.
Merchant ‚Å†was convicted of "murder for hire and attempting to commit an act of terrorism transcending ‚Äãnational boundaries," directed by the Iranian authorities, the DOJ said , opens new tab in a statement.
The trial in ‚Äãthe New York City borough of Brooklyn started last week, days before Trump ordered an assault on Iran, carried out with Israel, that has expanded into the region's biggest war in years.
Merchant admitted to ‚Äãjoining the plot with Iran's elite Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps but testified he did ‚Äãso unwillingly, to protect his family in Tehran.
Merchant said he was never ordered to kill a ‚Äåspecific person ‚Å†but that his Iranian handler named three people in the course of conversations in the Iranian capital.
Law enforcement thwarted the plan before any attack occurred. A person Merchant contacted in April 2024 to help with the plot reported his activities and became a ‚Äãconfidential informant, the DOJ ‚Äãsaid. Merchant was arrested ‚Å†and pleaded not guilty that year.
The Revolutionary Guards have a central role in Iran, with a combination of military and economic power and ‚Äãan intelligence network. Tehran has denied accusations that it targeted Trump ‚Äãor other ‚Å†U.S. officials.
U.S. and Israeli attacks since Saturday have killed at least 1,332 Iranian civilians and wounded thousands, according to Iran's U.N. ambassador. Many top Iranian leaders, including Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali ‚Å†Khamenei, ‚Äãhave been killed.
The U.S. military has said six of its service ‚Äãmembers were killed in a strike on a facility in Kuwait, while Israeli tallies show at least 10 ‚Äãcivilians have been killed across Israel.

item.title: Tornadoes kill four, injure more than a dozen in rural¬†Michigan
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters) - Tornadoes and strong storms killed at least four people and injured more than a dozen in southern Michigan on ‚ÄãFriday, destroying multiple homes and leaving hundreds of residents without power, ‚Äålocal authorities said.
Soon after the National Weather Service issued tornado warnings for the area on Friday, videos on social media claiming to be from the towns of Three ‚ÄãRivers and Union City showed massive whirlwinds tearing roofs off buildings ‚Äãand lifting debris into the air.
Local media footage showed smashed buildings ‚Å†and cars, and poles, trees and road signs that had been blown ‚Äãover.
Three fatalities and a dozen injuries were reported in Branch County, where Union ‚ÄãCity is located, the county's sheriff's office said.
One person was killed and several injured in Cass County, according to local authorities there.
"Multiple large structures - including homes and pole barns - ‚Äãsustained damage ranging from major structural impacts to complete destruction," according to ‚Äãa statement on a website for Cass County.
Power outages had affected hundreds of people, local ‚Äåofficials ‚Å†said.
"We have multiple utility poles down, transformers damaged, and long stretches of power lines on the ground. The north side of Union Lake sustained some of the most severe damage, with roughly two miles of line brought ‚Äãdown in that area ‚Äãalone," according to ‚Å†a posting on Union City's official Facebook page that said restoration may take several days.
Michigan Governor Gretchen Whitmer said ‚Äãshe activated a State Emergency Operations Center for monitoring ‚Äãthe situation.
"The ‚Å†state is ready to fulfill any resource requests that may arise. We've yet to receive any just yet. It's sounding like the local response is and ‚Å†has ‚Äãbeen able to address what's been a very ‚Äãdevastating afternoon and evening in southwest Michigan," Clayton Cummins, a spokesperson for Michigan emergency services and ‚Äãpolice, told Reuters.

item.title: Iran conflict boosts U.S. Gulf oil prices to highest since 2020
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. Gulf Coast heavy grades continued to surge on Friday as the Iran conflict spurred several Middle Eastern producers of heavy crude to curtail ‚Äãproduction and drove buyers to scoop up U.S. barrels.
Prices of Mars ‚Äåsour crude, the flagship crude produced in the U.S. Gulf of Mexico and favored by refiners globally, traded at a $11 premium to U.S. benchmark West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude on Friday, ‚Äãbrokers said. That was the highest since April 2020, and up $4 from ‚ÄãThursday.
Just a week ago it traded at a premium of $1.50.
Other heavy grades ‚Å†such as the Heavy Louisiana Sweet and the West Texas Sour also ‚Äãrose.
Benchmark crude oil prices have surged since the initial attacks last week, with Brent ‚Äãcrude settling at $92.69 a barrel, its highest level since October 2023 on Friday.
The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz has forced several countries, including Iraq, to curb output. The strait is a ‚Äãkey route for medium and heavy sour crude from the Persian Gulf, and ‚Äãthose flows are now largely cut off. Additional production cuts announced in Kuwait on Friday ‚Äåalso ‚Å†helped lift Mars prices, a trader said.
‚ÄúRefiners that rely on these grades will need to find similar, or roughly similar, alternatives to replace the lost barrels, so Mars and other U.S. Gulf sour heavies and mediums are natural substitutes and are ‚Äãgetting bid up ‚Äãaggressively,‚Äù Kpler lead ‚Å†Americas oil analyst Matt Smith said, adding that buyers, especially in Asia, are scrambling for more of these medium and ‚Äãheavy crude barrels.
"This time of year also marks the shift ‚Äãfrom winter ‚Å†into driving season, when demand typically rises across all crude grades," said Tim Snyder, chief economist at Matador Economics, adding that ultimately the supply disruption caused by war ‚Å†was ‚Äãdriving prices.
"In the short term we will continue ‚Äãto see these grades rise until we see the Strait of Hormuz open up," Snyder said.

item.title: US pump prices surge as Iran war upends global energy supply
item.description:
MARIETTA/NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. retail gasoline and diesel prices are soaring as the U.S.-Israel war with Iran constrains oil and fuel exports, which could be a political test for President Donald Trump's Republican Party ahead ‚Äãof midterm elections in November.
Fuel prices jumped more than 10% this week as oil rose above $90 a barrel, its highest in years, adding pain at the ‚Äåpump for consumers already strained by inflation. Trump on Thursday shrugged off higher gasoline prices in an interview with Reuters , opens new tab , saying "if they rise, they rise."
The president had vowed to lower energy prices and unleash U.S. oil and gas drilling during his second term, but much of his tenure has been marked by volatility and uncertainty amid shifts in policies like tariffs and geopolitical turmoil. The U.S. is the world's largest oil producer. It is a major exporter ‚Äãbut also imports millions of barrels a day since it is the world's largest oil consumer.
As of Friday, the national average prices for regular gasoline stood at $3.32 a ‚Äãgallon, up 11% from a week ago and the highest since September 2024, according to data from the motorists association AAA. Diesel was at $4.33, ‚Å†up 15% from a week ago, surging to the highest since November 2023.
U.S. motorists in parts of the Midwest and the South, including states that supported Trump, have ‚Äãseen some of the steepest increases in fuel costs since the conflict in Iran started.
In Georgia, a swing state, average retail gasoline prices rose 40.1 cents a gallon over the past week, ‚Äãaccording to fuel tracking site GasBuddy.
Andrenna McDaniel, a healthcare insurance worker in South Fulton, Georgia, said she was surprised to see prices skyrocket overnight.
"They jumped up so quickly," she said on Friday, adding that she does not agree with the war at all.
McDaniel, a Democrat, said that for now she is only driving for the most important things, and feels lucky that she works from home so she does not have to drive as ‚Äãmuch as other people do.
Georgia voted for Donald Trump in the 2024 election.
Trump voter Richard Soule, 69, a U.S. Air Force veteran and a retired firefighter, said a little pain at the pump ‚Äãis worth Trump's efforts to protect America.
"When President Trump went in there and bombed out their nuclear, and they just thumbed their nose at it, I believe he did the right thing at the right ‚Äåtime," Soule said ‚Å†on Friday as he filled up his Ford F-150 truck in Marietta, Georgia.
Other states, including Indiana and West Virginia have seen prices rise by 44.3 cents and 43.9 cents, respectively.
More pain may be on the way, analysts said, as oil prices continue to trend upward. On Friday, U.S. oil futures settled at $90.90 a barrel, up nearly $10 and the biggest single-day rise since April 2020.
"Given current market conditions, the national average price of gasoline could climb toward $3.50 to $3.70 per gallon in the coming days if oil continues rising and supply disruptions persist," GasBuddy analyst Patrick De ‚ÄãHaan said.
The disruptions in the Middle East and ‚Äãthe Strait of Hormuz, a key trade ‚Å†conduit, have boosted demand for U.S. oil abroad, which in turn has driven up prices for domestic refiners too.
"The U.S. has weaned itself off of its dependence on Middle Eastern crude, but obviously Asian refineries, and to a lesser extent, European refineries have not," Denton Cinquegrana, chief oil ‚Äãanalyst with OPIS. "That's what you're seeing happen in the spot market, because the demand for U.S. exports rise, and so the price rise."
Seasonal ‚Äãfactors could add further ‚Å†pressure. Gasoline prices typically go up in the spring and peak in the summer due to higher gasoline demand and production of summer-blend gasoline, which is more costly to produce.
Diesel fuel saw an even more aggressive jump since Iran began retaliating against U.S. and Israeli strikes, significantly disrupting shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.
Global diesel inventories have remained in tight supply due to heavy demand for heating and power generation ‚Å†during a ‚Äãprolonged winter in the U.S. and other parts of the world and a structural tightness of refining capacity.
Sticker prices ‚Äãof everything from food to furniture go up when the cost of diesel goes up, as the fuel is mainly used in freight transportation, manufacturing, agriculture, and global shipping, analysts said.
"In a world where buzzword seems to be 'affordability', that is ‚Äãcertainly not going to help," Cinquegrana said.

item.title: World's largest urban coffee plantation welcomes new trees in Brazil
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 6 (Reuters) - The world's largest urban coffee ‚Äãplantation, located in Brazil's sprawling city of Sao Paulo, this week welcomed some ‚Äå1,500 new coffee plants as researchers prepare to study their capacity to resist climate change and pests.
Sao Paulo's Biological Institute was established in 1927 with the mission of tackling a crisis caused by pests ‚Äãlike the coffee berry-borer beetle which devour the beans hidden inside coffee cherries.
Brazil ‚Äãis the world's top producer of arabica coffee, as well as the ‚Å†second-biggest producer of canephora coffees, which include varieties like robusta and conilon.
The plantation in ‚ÄãSao Paulo's Vila Mariana neighborhood, which already boasted more than 2,000 coffee plants, welcomed arabica ‚Äãvarieties this week billed as resistant to pests and coffee rust, a type of fungus, as well as other plants that are more tolerant of drought-like conditions.
"The Biological Institute was created to control the ‚Äãcoffee berry-borer (which) was controlled using parasitoids, a biological control method," institute researcher and agricultural engineer ‚ÄãHarumi Hojo told Reuters on Thursday.
In her hand, Hojo held two coffee cherries, showing the difference between ‚Äåa ‚Å†healthy fruit with smooth white coffee beans and another rotten on the inside after being devoured by a beetle.
Over the years, the institute began to investigate other factors affecting coffee plants, like soil and climate, and now its range of varieties grown side by side ‚Äãunder the same conditions ‚Äãshows how different ‚Å†plants handle pests, disease and climate pressures, Hojo said.
Arabica coffee plants are also sensitive to hotter and drier weather caused by climate ‚Äãchange, and 300 of the new plants at the institute's plantation ‚Äãare tolerant to ‚Å†water deficits.
Now, research has produced coffee varieties that are resistant to droughts, Hojo said, adding that it would be valuable in the future to have coffee plants that can hold out ‚Å†for ‚Äãirrigation with captured rain, rather than groundwater sources that ‚Äãmay be scarce.
"We know that climate change and water availability are going to be problems for our future," Hojo ‚Äãsaid.

item.title: One week into Iran war, the dangers for the US and Trump multiply
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 7 (Reuters) - One week into the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran that has plunged the Middle East into turmoil, President Donald Trump faces a growing list of risks and challenges that raise questions about whether he will be able to translate military successes into a clear geopolitical win.
Even after the killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and devastating blows against Iranian forces on land, at sea and in the air, the crisis has ‚Äãquickly widened into a regional conflict that threatens a more prolonged U.S. military engagement with fallout beyond Trump‚Äôs control.
That is a scenario that Trump had avoided in his two terms in the White House, preferring swift, limited operations like the January 3 lightning raid in Venezuela ‚Äåand June‚Äôs one-off strike on Iran‚Äôs nuclear sites.
‚ÄúIran is a messy and potentially protracted military campaign,‚Äù said Laura Blumenfeld of the Johns Hopkins School for Advanced International Studies in Washington. ‚ÄúTrump is risking the global economy, regional stability and his own Republican Party's performance in the U.S. midterm elections.‚Äù
Trump, who came to office promising to keep the U.S. out of "stupid‚Äù military interventions, is now pursuing what many experts see as an open-ended war of choice unprompted by any imminent threat to the U.S. from Iran, despite claims to the contrary by the president and his aides.
In doing so, analysts say he has struggled to articulate a detailed set of objectives or a clear endgame for Operation Epic Fury, the biggest U.S. military operation since the 2003 Iraq invasion, offering shifting rationales ‚Äãfor the war and definitions of what would constitute victory.
White House spokesperson Anna Kelly rejected that assessment, saying Trump has clearly outlined his goals to "destroy Iran‚Äôs ballistic missiles and production capacity, demolish their navy, end their ability to arm proxies, and prevent them from ever obtaining a nuclear weapon.‚Äù
However, if the ‚Äãwar drags on, American casualties mount and the economic costs of interrupted Gulf oil flows multiply, Trump‚Äôs biggest foreign policy gamble could also hurt his Republican Party politically.
Despite criticism from some Trump supporters opposed to military ‚Å†interventions, members of his Make America Great Again movement have largely backed him on Iran so far.
But any softening of their support could imperil Republicans‚Äô control of Congress in the November midterm elections, given opinion polls showing opposition to the war among the broader electorate, including a crucial bloc of independent voters.
‚ÄúThe American people are ‚Äãnot interested in repeating the mistakes of Iraq and Afghanistan,‚Äù said Brian Darling, a Republican strategist. ‚ÄúThe MAGA base is split between those who relied on no-new-war promises and ones who are loyal to Trump‚Äôs judgment.‚Äù
High on the list of analysts‚Äô concerns is the mixed messaging from Trump and his aides on whether he is seeking ‚Äúregime change‚Äù in ‚ÄãTehran.
At the outset of the conflict, he suggested that overthrowing Iran‚Äôs rulers was a goal, at least by fomenting internal rebellion. Two days later, he stopped short of mentioning that as a priority.
But then on Thursday, Trump told Reuters he would play a role in picking Iran‚Äôs next leader and encouraged Iranian Kurdish rebels to launch attacks. That was followed by his demand in a social media post on Friday for Iran‚Äôs "unconditional surrender."
Across the region, the dangers have escalated with Iran's retaliatory strikes on Israel and other neighbors as it seeks to sow chaos and raise the costs for Israel, the U.S. and its allies.
Showing that Iran may still be able to activate proxy groups, Lebanon‚Äôs Hezbollah militia has renewed hostilities with Israel, expanding the war to another ‚Äãcountry.
American casualties have been low so far, with six service members killed, and Trump has largely shrugged off the prospects for more to come while declining to completely rule out deployment of U.S. ground troops.
Asked whether Americans should worry about Iran-inspired attacks at home, Trump said in a Time magazine interview published on Friday: ‚ÄúI guess ‚Ä¶ ‚ÄãLike I said, some people will die.‚Äù
But Jonathan Panikoff, a former deputy U.S. national intelligence officer for the Middle East, said: ‚ÄúNothing is likely to hasten an early end to the war more than American casualties ‚Ä¶ That‚Äôs what Iran is counting on.‚Äù
VENEZUELA MISCALCULATION?
Many analysts believe Trump, who has shown an increasing appetite for military action in his second term, miscalculated that the ‚ÄåIran campaign would unfold ‚Å†like the Venezuela operation earlier this year.
U.S. special forces captured Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro, opening the way for Trump to coerce more compliant former loyalists into giving him considerable sway over the country‚Äôs vast oil reserves ‚Äì without any extended U.S. military action needed.
By contrast, Iran has proved a much tougher, better-armed foe with an entrenched clerical and security establishment.
Even the joint U.S.-Israeli "decapitation" strike that killed Khamenei and some other senior leaders has failed so far to prevent Iran from mounting a military response and has raised questions whether they could be replaced by even more hardline figures.
Looming over the conflict, however, is whether Iran could slide into chaos and break apart if its current rulers fall, further destabilizing the Middle East.
Mark Dubowitz, CEO of the Foundation for the Defense of Democracies, a nonprofit research institute considered hawkish on Iran, praised Trump‚Äôs overall war strategy but said the president needs to make clear publicly that he does not want to see the country disintegrate.
For now, however, one of the most pressing ‚Äãconcerns is Iran‚Äôs threat to the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow chokepoint through ‚Äãwhich a fifth of the world's oil passes. Tanker traffic has halted, ‚Å†which could have grave economic consequences if it lasts.
Though Trump has publicly dismissed any concern about already-rising U.S. gas prices, he and his aides have scrambled for ways to mitigate the war‚Äôs impact on energy supplies as voters tell pollsters that the cost of living is their top concern.
‚ÄúIt's an economic pain point on the U.S. economy that it seems was not fully anticipated," said Josh Lipsky at the Atlantic Council think tank in Washington.
One former U.S. military official close to the U.S. administration said ‚Äãthe widening of the war's economic impact had caught Trump‚Äôs team by surprise in part because those with knowledge of oil markets were not consulted ahead of the attack on Iran.
The White House's Kelly said, ‚ÄúThe Iranian regime is being ‚Äãabsolutely crushed" but did not specifically address concerns ‚Å†about preparations for a war.
Trump made his decision to press ahead with the strikes
despite warnings from some senior aides that the escalation could be difficult to contain, according to two White House officials and a Republican close to the administration.
Some traditional U.S. allies were caught off guard. "It's a decision-making circle of one," said one Western diplomat.
The war‚Äôs duration is a major unknown likely to determine the extent of its repercussions. With the price tag of the Iran campaign mounting by the day, Trump has said that the operation could last four or five weeks or ‚Äúwhatever it takes‚Äù but has offered little explanation of what he envisions will follow.
Retired U.S. Army Lieutenant General Ben Hodges, who served in Iraq and Afghanistan ‚Å†and formerly commanded the ‚ÄãU.S. Army in Europe, commended the U.S. military for its tactics in Iran. But he told Reuters: "From a political, strategic and diplomatic standpoint, it seems not to have been thought all the way ‚Äãthrough.‚Äù
Trump also has a lot riding on helping oil-producing Gulf Arab states weather the Iran crisis given they have long hosted U.S. bases and have made pledges of massive new U.S. investments to him.
While Gulf allies appear to have fallen in line to support the campaign, especially after Tehran targeted them with missile and drone strikes, not everyone in the region is onboard with Trump‚Äôs war.
In an open ‚Äãletter to Trump published on Thursday, UAE billionaire Khalaf Al Habtor, a frequent visitor to Trump's Mar-a-Lago resort in Florida, asked: "Who gave you the right to turn our region into a battlefield?"

item.title: As war spreads, airline pilots contend with drones, missiles - and stress
item.description:
LONDON/COPENHAGEN/BEIRUT, March 7 (Reuters) - Airline pilots have faced escalating risks in recent years, from drone incursions to flight paths squeezed by conflict. Now U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran are making the skies even more perilous and ratcheting up the pressure on those flying through them.
The outbreak of war in the Middle East has put hundreds of ballistic ‚Äãmissiles and attack drones into the skies above some of the world's busiest airports. Tehran's retaliation against the U.S. and its allies has included hitting airports, grounding scores of flights from Dubai to Abu Dhabi. A trickle ‚Äåof rescue flights has made it through for thousands of stranded passengers.
Reuters spoke to eight pilots and more than a half-dozen aviation and security insiders who said the accumulation of conflicts - from Ukraine to Afghanistan and Israel - has increased the burden on pilots, forcing them to manage shrinking airspace and the wider use of military drones far from active war zones. That's increased the strain on the mental health of pilots desperate to keep themselves and their passengers safe.
"We are not military pilots. We are not trained to deal with these kinds of threats in the air," Tanja Harter, a pilot with Middle ‚ÄãEast experience and president of the European Cockpit Association, told Reuters.
The current crisis is the latest in a string of security threats the sector has faced over the years, she said, which could cause "fear and anxiety" for pilots. Airlines ‚Äãnow often have peer programs to help, she said, adding as a pilot she would not want to "share airspace with missiles."
Airspace safety has worsened in the last two-and-a-half years as conflicts have grown, ‚Å†industry experts said, through a combination of GPS spoofing - maliciously tricking planes about their position - and increased numbers of missiles and drones .
An Air France (AIRF.PA) , opens new tab flight to bring stranded French nationals home from the United Arab Emirates turned back due to missile fire on Thursday. A ‚ÄãLufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab pilot on Friday diverted from Riyadh to Cairo over regional security fears.
Middle East-trained pilots have become wearily used to emergencies, the head of Lebanon's civil aviation body said. The escalating conflict put those skills to the test quickly. Video footage ‚Äãon March 5 showed planes taking off from Beirut airport as smoke billowed over buildings in the Lebanese capital.
"Middle East pilots have always faced crises, so from the start we trained how to deal with contingencies, emergencies and everything else," said Captain Mohammed Aziz, director general of Lebanon's civil aviation authority.
"No one can give you a guarantee that they won't bomb the airport or will bomb the airport."
One Middle East Airlines' pilot with a decade of experience said routes to Beirut have become more complex. In the past, shoulder-fired anti-aircraft missiles in Lebanon usually had a range of 15,000 feet, so ‚Äãpilots would increase altitude to stay out of range, he said, while planes often carried extra fuel in case they were forced to divert.
Still, most missile strikes are far enough away not to be a risk and pilots are often too busy to ‚Äãworry about them.
"You're actually busy enough on the plane trying to make sure you have clearance to land, that everything is in order, so you don't have time to process your emotions over what's happening outside the plane," he said.
The risks are not ‚Äåconfined to the ‚Å†Middle East. Since Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, drones have become a key weapon on both sides. Airports in European cities from Stockholm to Munich have faced drone disruptions - suspected though not confirmed to be linked to the conflict.
Airline Captain Christian von D'Ahe, a commercial aircraft pilot for 15 years and head of the Danish Air Line Pilots Association, is alarmed by the emerging threat.
"Drones are not easily detected," Von D'Ahe said. "We can see them in the air, and they're very small. So sooner or later, something will happen."
Drones striking an aircraft's engines could cause total power loss, while damage to wings could compromise a jet's ability to manoeuvre.
Most registered planes emit a signal via a transponder, a device that identifies aircraft to radar, but drones do not, leaving pilots in the dark. Regular radars used ‚Äãby airports struggle to catch drones. Specialised radar exists, but is ‚Äãtypically operated by law enforcement or the military.
Dedrone, a ‚Å†company that produces counterdrone technology, said there were over 1.2 million drone violations in the U.S. in 2025, with more expected in coming years.
Airports can use radar, frequency sensors and jamming tools to counter drones, while some systems can "spoof" them off course. But safety concerns mean airports cannot shoot drones down.
Tim Friebe, an air traffic controller in Germany and a ‚Äãvice president at the Air Traffic Controllers European Unions Coordination (ATCEUC), said drones were a "threat that is growing," while airports often had limited options.
"For now we have reports, pilot reports, or ‚Äãsometimes controllers spot drones. The problem ‚Å†is there's not much you can do except shutting down the airport," he said.
Drones shut down , opens new tab some of the world's biggest airports from Munich to London's Gatwick last year, driving operators to beef up their foreign object and drone detection systems, according to half a dozen industry officials.
Moritz Burger, a commercial pilot based in Germany, recalled spotting an object that looked like a balloon with a structure underneath as he was about to land at a European airport.
"I was looking out of the window and suddenly there appeared an object that passed by just ‚Å†below our aircraft. ‚ÄãWe could see it for maybe one, maximum two seconds," he said, adding it startled him and left him no time to take any evasive ‚Äãaction.
"When you encounter such a near-miss or some passing object, there is not enough time to react. So it is unrealistic to expect that pilots could fly around such an object. There's pretty much nothing we can do."

item.title: Russia hits Ukraine with drones, missiles, kills at least seven in Kharkiv
item.description:
KYIV, March 7 (Reuters) - Russia launched a barrage of drones and missiles ‚Äãat Ukraine overnight on Saturday, killing at least ‚Äåseven people, including two children, in the northeast city of Kharkiv, Ukrainian officials said.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said that ‚ÄãRussia launched 480 drones and 29 missiles ‚Äãtargeting the energy sector and railway infrastructure across ‚Å†the country.
"There should be a response from partners ‚Äãto these savage strikes against life," Zelenskiy said on ‚Äãthe Telegram app.
"Russia has not abandoned its attempts to destroy Ukraine's residential and critical infrastructure, and therefore support should continue," ‚ÄãZelenskiy said, urging partners to continue air defence ‚Äãand weapons supplies.
Oleh Syniehubov, Kharkiv regional governor, said that seven ‚Äåpeople, ‚Å†including two children, were killed after a Russian ballistic missile slammed into a five-storey residential building.
Rescue workers continue to work on the site, clearing the rubble, ‚Äãhe said.
In ‚ÄãKharkiv, seven ‚Å†residential apartment houses, commercial and administrative buildings, electricity distribution lines, and cars were ‚Äãdamaged by the Russian attacks, Syniehubov said.
Ukrainian ‚Äãofficials ‚Å†said that Russia attacked four railway stations and other railway infrastructure in central Ukraine and port infrastructure in ‚Å†the ‚Äãsouthern Odesa region, setting on ‚Äãfire containers with vegetable oil and damaging a grain warehouse.

item.title: Refugees, migrants in Lebanon find rare sanctuary from Israeli strikes in Beirut church
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 7 (Reuters) - When Israeli strikes began pummelling Beirut‚Äôs southern suburbs early on Monday, Sudanese refugee ‚ÄãRidina Muhammad and her family had no choice but to flee home on foot, eventually reaching the only shelter ‚Äåthat would accept them: a church.
Eight months pregnant, Muhammad, 32, walked with her husband and three children for hours in the dark streets until they found a car to take them to the St. Joseph Tabaris Parish, which has opened its doors to refugees and migrants.
They are among 300,000 people displaced across Lebanon this week by heavy ‚ÄãIsraeli strikes, launched in response to a rocket and drone attack into Israel by the Lebanese armed group Hezbollah.
Just 100,000 of the ‚Äãdisplaced are in government shelters. Others are staying with relatives or sleeping in the streets. But migrants and ‚Å†refugees say government shelters were never an option for them, saying they were turned away during the last war between Hezbollah and Israel.
Muhammad‚Äôs oldest ‚Äãdaughter, now seven, stopped speaking after the 2024 war.
This time, they are even more vulnerable: their home was destroyed in this week‚Äôs strikes and ‚ÄãMuhammad is due to give birth at the end of the month.
‚ÄúI don't know if there's a doctor or not, but I'm really scared about it because I haven't prepared any clothes for the baby, nor arranged a hospital, and I don't know where to go,‚Äù she told Reuters as her younger daughter leaned ‚Äãagainst her pregnant belly.
Muhammad said she was registered with the United Nations‚Äô refugee agency (UNHCR) but had not received support.
‚ÄúUs, as refugees, ‚Äãwhy did we register with the U.N., if they are not helping us in the most difficult times?‚Äù she said.
Dalal Harb, a spokesperson for UNHCR Lebanon, ‚Äåsaid the ‚Å†agency had mobilized but reaching everyone immediately was extremely challenging given the scale and speed of displacement. The UNHCR operation in Lebanon is currently only around 14% funded, she said.
The Jesuit Refugee Service (JRS), which helped the church host displaced in 2024, is doing so again.
Michael Petro, JRS‚Äô Emergency Shelter Director, said the church was full within the first day of strikes, with 140 people from South Sudan, Ethiopia, Bangladesh, and other countries sheltering ‚Äãthere.
‚ÄúThere are many, many more people ‚Äãcoming than there were in 2024, ‚Å†and we have fewer and fewer places to put them,‚Äù he said.
Petro said he was told weeks ago that government shelters would be open to migrants if war erupted.
But when the strikes began and even ‚ÄãLebanese struggled to find shelter, the policy seemed to change, he said.
‚ÄúWe're hearing from hotlines up to ‚Äãgovernment officials and ‚Å†ministries that migrants are not welcome,‚Äù Petro said.
Lebanon‚Äôs Minister for Social Affairs Haneen Sayyed did not respond to a request for comment. On Thursday, Sayyed said Beirut shelters were full.
When Israeli strikes began, Othman Yahyeh Dawood, a 41-year-old Sudanese man, put his two young sons on his motorcycle.
They drove 75 ‚Å†kilometres (46 miles) ‚Äãfrom the southern Lebanese town of Nabatieh to St. Joseph‚Äôs, where they had sheltered ‚Äãin 2024.
‚ÄúI know the area is safe and there are people who will welcome us,‚Äù he said.
‚ÄúWe don't know where to go; there's war there (in the south), war here (in ‚ÄãBeirut), war in Sudan, and nowhere else to go,‚Äù he said.

item.title: In Perpignan, French far right tests tough-on-crime blueprint
item.description:
PERPIGNAN, France, March 7 (Reuters) - In the streets of Perpignan, a faded Mediterranean city near France's border with Spain, the incumbent far-right mayor Louis Aliot is pitching a simple message as he asks voters for another term: more police, more cameras and more order.
The National Rally (RN) mayor ‚Äãhas made security the centrepiece of his administration, and his party is holding up this city of 122,000, the biggest it controls, as a blueprint for governance that it hopes to ‚Äåreplicate elsewhere ahead of nationwide municipal elections this month.
Aliot, who leads in polls despite an embezzlement conviction that could bar him from office if his appeal fails, said Perpignan has been a laboratory for RN governance since he won city hall in 2020 and a showcase for its national ambitions when the French vote for a new president in 2027.
"When the National Rally is in office, well, we govern, we run cities and we run them well," he said in an interview.
Wins in other cities would give Marine ‚ÄãLe Pen's party a springboard going into next year's presidential election. Polls show the RN performing well in Toulon, Nice and Marseille, although a two-round system makes final outcomes hard to forecast.
The ‚ÄãRN's law-and-order message resonates with many voters in Perpignan, where just streets from the Catalan-influenced, historic centre lie run-down neighbourhoods that are among France's poorest.
Two weeks before the ‚Å†March 15 and 23 elections, RN leader Jordan Bardella and hard-left rival Jean-Luc M√©lenchon - both presidential hopefuls - held duelling weekend rallies, turning the city into a symbolic battleground.
"Faced with the violence engulfing our country year after year, we ‚Äãintend to make public order an absolute priority," Bardella told supporters.
Aliot promised 50 more police officers and 200 surveillance cameras, particularly in outer suburbs to which the middle class has fled from poverty-stricken inner neighbourhoods that are home ‚Äãto the city's sizeable North African and Roma populations.
The city has already expanded its municipal force to 199 officers from 161 in 2020, and now deploys 1.6 municipal police per 1,000 residents - proportionally the highest among French cities of more than 100,000 and nearly three times Paris's 0.6, according to Reuters' analysis of Interior Ministry data.
More aggressive policing has seen drug-trafficking cases climb sharply, with Perpignan now reporting the seventh most among over 50 large French cities, up from 18th in 2020. Overall trafficking cases ‚Äãopened by police have more than doubled, while fixed-penalty fines for minor possession have quadrupled.
The security focus comes at a cost. Municipal debt stands at 1,600 euros per inhabitant, well above the 1,200 average in similar-sized ‚Äãcities, according to Finance Ministry data.
Property tax rates are higher than in 71% of comparable cities, and 98% of peers have lower local business taxes.
Even so, business weekly Challenges ranked Perpignan the third best-run city in France last week, a ‚Äåtalking point for ‚Å†Aliot's allies as they try to convert security-minded voters beyond the RN's base.
Aliot leads with 44% in the first round according to an early December Ifop poll, benefiting from a fractured opposition split among five rivals.
A far-right political veteran, Aliot has been central to Le Pen's campaign to soften the image of the anti-immigration party and make it more palatable to the mainstream voter. Opinion polls project either Le Pen or Bardella, her prot√©g√©, winning the first round of next year's presidential vote and facing a tight run-off contest.
On the ground, reactions were mixed. At a city-centre market, retiree Marie Nivet said she welcomed cleaner streets and visible policing, though she wanted more action on ‚Äãdrugs.
She was undecided on whether to support Aliot because ‚Äãof the looming appeal court verdict over misuse ‚Å†of EU funds that also threatens Le Pen's political survival .
Aliot said he would appeal any unfavourable outcome to France's highest court, but that others in his team could take his place if needed.
The legal uncertainty didn't bother Laure Gu√©rin, a retired private-school administrator, who said she would back Aliot because of the RN's law-and-order stance.
Others were more ‚Äãwary. Christian Pyguillem, a retired property manager, credited Aliot with cleaner streets and better security, but said entrenched poverty in some neighbourhoods requires deeper urban renewal ‚Äãthan any mayor has attempted.
"We'll ‚Å†give him another six years, but it's a vote motivated by the local situation, I don't agree with their other ideas," he said.
Rivals said the security push has failed to deliver and that city finances have deteriorated. Centre-right challenger Bruno Nougayr√®de accused Aliot of neglecting local duties for national politics and putting publicity before results.
"These five years have been marked by complete inaction for the people of Perpignan," Nougayr√®de told Reuters. "A lot of resources went into PR, but the ‚Å†city itself ‚Äãhas not changed."
Hard-left candidate Micka√´l Idrac called Aliot's record "catastrophic," blaming "frenzied" investment in arming police and surveillance cameras. He also criticised what he ‚Äãdescribed as divisive rhetoric.
Aliot countered that voters would judge by how they feel in their streets. He casts the RN as the party willing to enforce order after decades of drift by rivals on left and right.
"If the left had defended the poor, we wouldn't be ‚Äãhere today. If the right had defended order, we wouldn't be here today," Aliot said.

item.title: India let Iran warship dock the day US sank another off Sri Lanka, officials say
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 6 (Reuters) - India has allowed an Iranian warship to dock as a humanitarian gesture, Foreign Minister Subrahmanyam Jaishankar said on Saturday, after the U.S. ‚Äãsank another Iranian navy vessel off neighbouring Sri Lanka.
The Lavan docked ‚Äåat India's southern port of Kochi on Wednesday, the same day the U.S. submarine struck Iranian navy frigate Dena, after an urgent request from Tehran, an Indian government source told ‚ÄãReuters.
U.S. President Donald Trump has said destroying the Iranian navy is one ‚Äãaim of the war he and Israel launched against the Islamic ‚Å†Republic a week ago.
The Lavan - an amphibious landing vessel, according to the U.S. ‚ÄãNaval Institute's online news site - and two other ships "were coming in for a ‚Äãfleet review and then they got, in a way, caught on the wrong side of the events," Jaishankar told the annual Raisina Dialogue event.
"I think we really approached it from ‚Äãthe point of view of humanity, of other than whatever the legal ‚Äãissues were," he said. "I think we did the right thing."
At least 87 people were killed in ‚Äåthe U.S. ‚Å†attack on the Dena in Sri Lanka's exclusive economic zone 19 nautical miles off the coast, outside its maritime boundaries.
India received the docking request for the Lavan on February 28, the day the Iran war started, the source ‚Äãsaid late on Friday, ‚Äãadding that the ‚Å†request "was urgent as the vessel had developed technical issues".
Its 183 crew members have been accommodated at naval facilities in ‚ÄãKochi, said the source, who asked not to be identified ‚Äãciting confidentiality.
The ‚Å†Dena was on its way back from a naval exercise organised by India, according to the drill's website and Sri Lankan officials.
Sri Lankan authorities said on Friday ‚Å†that ‚Äãthey were escorting the Iranian naval ship Booshehr ‚Äãto a harbour on the eastern coast and moving most of its crew to a navy camp ‚Äãnear Colombo.

item.title: Iran to suspend strikes on neighbours unless attacks come from them
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian ‚Äãsaid on ‚ÄåSaturday that its temporary leadership ‚Äãcouncil had ‚Äãapproved the suspension ‚Å†of attacks ‚Äãagainst neighboring countries ‚Äãunless an attack on Iran came ‚Äãfrom those ‚Äãcountries.
The announcement came as ‚ÄåIran ‚Å†continued to launch attacks in the ‚Äãregion ‚Äãin ‚Å†response to U.S.-Israeli strikes ‚Äãon the ‚ÄãIslamic ‚Å†Republic.

item.title: Azerbaijan says it foils Iranian plots including plan to attack major pipeline
item.description:
BAKU, March 7 (Reuters) - Azerbaijan said it had prevented several acts of "terrorist" sabotage planned by Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), including ‚Äãa plot to attack a major oil pipeline running ‚Äåthrough the South Caucasus to Turkey.
The targets included the Baku-Tbilisi-Ceyhan (BTC) oil pipeline, the Israeli embassy in Azerbaijan, an Ashkenazi synagogue, and a leader ‚Äãan ancient Jewish community in Azerbaijan called the Mountain ‚ÄãJews, according to a State Security Service statement cited ‚Å†by the Azertag state news agency late on Friday.
Iran ‚Äãhas not commented on the statement.
The BTC pipeline travels via Georgia ‚Äãand Turkey and sends oil to Europe, and also accounts for roughly a third of Israeli oil imports. Any damage to its infrastructure ‚Äãcould drive global energy prices even higher as the war in ‚Äãthe Middle East enters its second week.
The Azerbaijani statement came just a ‚Äåday ‚Å†after Baku vowed to retaliate for what it said was an incursion of four Iranian drones into its Nakhchivan exclave, which injured four people and damaged airport infrastructure. Iran flatly denied it ‚Äãsent the drones ‚Äãinto Azerbaijan.
Baku said ‚Å†an investigation found two Iranian citizens and an Azerbaijani national had colluded to bring over ‚Äãseven kilograms of the C-4 explosive into Azerbaijan ‚Äãon ‚Å†the instructions of the IRGC. Authorities have issued international arrest warrants for four people.
On Friday Azerbaijan ordered the evacuation of its ‚Å†diplomats ‚Äãfrom Iran, citing safety concerns, amid ‚Äãalready tense relations between the two countries over Baku's ties to Israel and ‚ÄãTurkey.

item.title: Putin calls for immediate halt to Iran conflict
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 7 (Reuters) - Russian President Vladimir Putin expressed his condolences to Iran's president over the ‚Äãnumerous civilian casualties resulting from "the armed Israeli-American ‚Äåaggression against Iran" and called for an immediate halt to hostilities, the Kremlin said.
In a phone ‚Äãcall late on Friday with Iranian President ‚ÄãMasoud Pezeshkian, Putin expressed his deep condolences ‚Å†over the killing of Iranian Supreme ‚ÄãLeader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, members of Khamenei's family, ‚ÄãIranian political and military leaders and "numerous civilians".
"Vladimir Putin reaffirmed Russia's principled stance in favour of an immediate ‚Äãcessation of hostilities, the rejection of force ‚Äãas a method to solve any issues surrounding Iran or ‚Äåarising ‚Å†in the Middle East, and a swift return to the path of diplomatic resolution," the Kremlin said.
Putin said he was in ‚Äãconstant contact with ‚Äãthe leaders ‚Å†of Gulf Cooperation Council member states.
"Masoud Pezeshkian expressed gratitude for Russia's ‚Äãsolidarity with the Iranian people as ‚Äãthey ‚Å†defend their sovereignty and the independence of their country. He also provided a detailed update ‚Å†on ‚Äãthe developments during the ‚Äãlatest active phase of the conflict," the Kremlin said.

item.title: Inside the Nepal campaign that could vault Balendra Shah to power
item.description:
KATHMANDU, March 7 (Reuters) - The campaign that swept aside Nepal's established political heavyweights and brought 35-year-old rapper-turned-politician Balendra Shah to the cusp of power emerged out of the top floors of a six-story building in west Kathmandu.
Shah is the candidate for prime minister from the ‚ÄãRastriya Swatantra Party, which is leading the vote count two days after Nepal's first election since youth-led protests in September forced the previous elected government out of power.
The final results ‚Äåfor 165 seats in direct, first-past-the-post tallies and 110 seats through proportional representation are expected by next week, according to the election commission.
And if current trends hold, Shah - or Balen, as he is better known - will have successfully harnessed a wave of support unleashed by the September uprising with one of the most sophisticated campaigns ever seen in the small Himalayan nation.
Reuters interviewed six RSP officials to gain insight into Shah's campaign, much of it coordinated out of the ‚Äãtop three floors of the party's headquarters in the capital city's Balaju neighbourhood and heavily bankrolled by the Nepali diaspora.
"We are overwhelmed by the support and love we received from ‚Äãpeople on the ground," said Bigyan Gautam, a member of RSP's national campaign team.
Shah's stunning performance stands in contrast with Bangladesh's election last month, where ‚Å†a youth-driven party fizzled at the polls after emerging out of the "Gen-Z" protests that unseated the country's long-serving premier in 2024.
The heart of RSP's political machine is the Research, Strategy and ‚ÄãDocumentation Department, run by an 11-member board overseeing 300 party workers divided into three national groups that supplement smaller teams led by individual candidates.
These national-level teams plan election tactics, organise rallies, create and manage online ‚Äãcontent, and track ground-level campaigning and feedback from across Nepal, three officials said.
In the run-up to Thursday's vote, the party also followed a carefully calibrated media strategy, with Shah delivering a major speech every eight days, allowing enough time for each rally to be amplified by a 660-strong social media team.
The RSP also held road shows in five to seven districts each day, backed by daily brief appearances in one of Nepal's seven provinces where Shah would ‚Äãmeet and connect with the voters.
"If you keep giving speeches, people get confused," a party official said, asking not to be named. "We let opposition parties raise some issues, and then respond once. This way, ‚Äãour message stays clear."
The centralised campaign system and large events were funded directly by the party, which has received large donations from Nepalis living abroad, particularly from those in the United States, according to two officials.
But individual ‚Äåcandidates were ‚Å†responsible for organising and financing their own campaign events, said RSP's Treasurer Lima Adhikari.
Before he joined RSP in December ahead of the polls, Shah served as the mayor of Kathmandu, a position he had won in 2022 by leveraging his popularity as one of Nepal's biggest rap stars, with millions of followers on social media.
On January 19, Shah stood with RSP founder and TV host-turned-politician Rabi Lamichhane at a rally in the capital city of Nepal's Madhesh province and told the thousands gathered: "A Madhesi boy is going to become prime minister."
Although Madhesh and the surrounding Terai plains are the most populous ‚Äãregions of Nepal, the country has long been ‚Äãdominated by political elites from Kathmandu and its ‚Å†mountainous belt.
Shah's January speech went viral on social media, underlining the early success of the RSP's bet that he could become the first leader from the plains to secure the premiership, three party officials said.
"It was very clear to us that the nation was fed up with the old corrupt leaders ‚Äãand they were seeing hope in young leaders like Balen Shah and Rabi Lamichhane," said Birendra Kumar Mehta, a member of RSP's central committee.
"The ‚Äãparty saw this as an ‚Å†opportunity."
Shah himself is contesting the election from Jhapa-5, a constituency in the plains that has long been part of the home turf of K.P. Sharma Oli, who quit as prime minister in the wake of the September protests.
In this rural pocket of Nepal, Shah has stuck with his unorthodox campaign playbook, shunning sit-down media interviews, making impromptu stops to talk with voters, and forming a group of youth volunteers across the constituency.
Part of ‚Å†his team, reinforced ‚Äãby the RSP campaign in Kathmandu, has also been collecting voter feedback and complaints, including on development projects and general ‚Äãgovernance.
"People from every part of Nepal will feel that the government is theirs and most importantly, that it is there for them," said D.P. Aryal, RSP's vice chairperson, adding that Shah's administration will look to bring in outside experts to bolster ‚Äãits capacity.
"Youth engagement and the maximum integration of technology will also be among our top priorities."

item.title: Israel carries out rare airborne raid in Lebanon; 16 killed in strikes
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 7 (Reuters) - Israel's military carried out an airborne operation that ‚Äãdropped troops into a town in eastern Lebanon overnight, residents and Lebanese state media said on ‚ÄåSaturday, as heavy Israeli strikes on the area left more than a dozen people dead.
Lebanon was plunged deeper into the conflict engulfing the Middle East on Monday when Iran-backed Hezbollah launched rockets and drones into Israel, which has responded with heavy bombardment across Lebanon's south, east and ‚Äãnear the capital Beirut.
Overnight, Israeli helicopters dropped troops near the town of Nabi Chit in Lebanon's eastern ‚ÄãBekaa Valley, Lebanese state media and residents said. Residents and Hezbollah fighters fired on the ‚Å†troops as they moved on foot, the reports said.
The Israeli troops withdrew and left by helicopter as Israeli strikes ‚Äãpounded Nabi Chit and nearby towns, state media and residents said. Israel's military did not immediately respond to Reuters questions ‚Äãon the incident.
Lebanon's health ministry said 16 people had been killed in Israeli attacks in the last 24 hours in Nabi Chit. A spokesperson told Reuters the toll included people killed in strikes throughout Friday as well as during intense overnight bombardment, and that it was ‚Äãexpected to rise further.
Two security sources told Reuters that three army troops were among the dead.
Israeli strikes have killed ‚Äãmore than 200 people across Lebanon, and orders to evacuate have displaced around 300,000 people, only a third of whom are now ‚Äåliving in ‚Å†government shelters.
Shawki al-Masri, who lives in a town adjacent to Nabi Chit, described the overnight bombing as "a night of hell".
"We heard the helicopters over our house all night - they were so low we thought they would land on us," he told Reuters.
"People in the town woke up and started shooting at them, then the warplanes started bombing. ‚ÄãIt was a very violent ‚Äãnight and only calmed ‚Å†down when the sun came up," he said.
In a statement overnight, Hezbollah said it had fired on Israeli troops who had been dropped near Nabi Chit by four Israeli helicopters. ‚ÄãThe group said the Israeli troops withdrew.
While Israeli forces are carrying out ground operations ‚Äãin southern Lebanon, ‚Å†pushing deeper into a strip of border villages, airborne operations are rare. In a 2024 war between Israel and Hezbollah, Israeli naval forces kidnapped a man from a Lebanese coastal town.
The United Nations on Saturday warned that the conflict was set to get "even ‚Å†worse," ‚Äãand that talks between Israel and Lebanon "must be pursued with urgency" to ‚Äãend hostilities.
Its Special Coordinator for Lebanon, Jeanine Hennis-Plasschaert, said in a statement that it was "clear that ongoing military actions will not deliver a lasting win ‚Äãto anyone".
"Rather, they will only deepen instability and inflict further suffering," she said.

item.title: India raises cooking gas prices as Iran war hits supply
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 7 (Reuters) - Indian companies have raised the prices of liquefied petroleum ‚Äãgas, mostly used as a cooking ‚Äåfuel, for the first time in about a year, as global prices surge with the U.S.-Israel war ‚Äãon Iran disrupting supplies from the ‚ÄãMiddle East.
Indian Oil Corp, the country's top ‚Å†refiner and LPG seller, has increased ‚Äãthe prices of a 14.2-kg LPG cylinder in ‚ÄãDelhi by 7% to 913 rupees ($9.93), according to its website.
State refiners IOC, Bharat Petroleum Corp (BPCL.NS) , opens new tab and Hindustan ‚ÄãPetroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab raised prices in tandem.
India, the ‚Äãworld's second-biggest importer of LPG, last year consumed 33.15 ‚Äåmillion ‚Å†metric tons of cooking gas, a mixture of propane and butane, with imports accounting for about two-thirds of LPG consumption. Middle ‚ÄãEastern LPG accounts ‚Äãfor ‚Å†85% to 90% of those imports.
India on Friday asked refiners to boost ‚ÄãLPG production to avoid any shortage ‚Äãof ‚Å†cooking gas in the country.
Indian companies have also raised the prices of 19-kg commercial LPG ‚Å†cylinders, ‚Äãmainly used by hotels and ‚Äãrestaurants, to 1,883 rupees from 1,768.50 rupees.
($1 = 91.9280 Indian ‚Äãrupees)

item.title: White House seeks bolder action on energy prices amid Iran conflict
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - The White House is asking federal agencies to step up efforts ‚Äãto address soaring energy prices from the Iran conflict , signaling a concern that steps taken so far may not be enough, ‚Äåaccording to two people familiar with the matter.
Senior officials have asked the departments of Energy, Transportation and Treasury and the Environmental Protection Agency to provide more policy options, with an emphasis on measures that President Donald Trump could implement without congressional approval, one of the sources told Reuters.
The requests indicate that the White ‚ÄãHouse is preparing for the possibility that bolder action may be needed if oil and gas prices continue to climb. ‚ÄãPolitical analysts say higher gasoline prices could hurt Trump and his Republican Party in November's midterm elections, ‚Å†when control of Congress is at stake.
‚ÄúObviously the White House is coordinating with the interagency on this important issue, if we were ‚Äãnot, it would be a problem. President Trump and his entire energy team have had a strong game plan to keep oil prices ‚Äãstable well before Operation Epic Fury began, and they will continue to review all credible options and execute on them when appropriate," White House spokeswoman Taylor Rogers said in a statement.
U.S. and global crude oil futures climbed above $90 a barrel on Friday, with U.S. prices rising more than 12% as Middle Eastern ‚Äãsupply remained constrained by the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz amid the expanding U.S.-Israeli war against Iran.
Gasoline prices in the U.S. have ‚Äãsurged in recent weeks, reaching levels not seen since late 2024. The national average for regular unleaded has climbed above $3.30 per gallon, while diesel has ‚Äårisen to $4.26 ‚Å†per gallon.
The White House has taken a cautious approach to intervening in energy markets, wary that an overly aggressive strategy could backfire. Officials say any broad measures must be carefully calibrated, noting that heavy-handed steps that fail to lower gasoline or crude prices could unsettle markets, erode confidence and trigger political backlash. Analysts, meanwhile, have expressed skepticism about how much the White House can do to rein in prices.
Officials ‚Äãhave discussed a wide array ‚Äãof options, including a federal ‚Å†gasoline tax holiday and looser environmental regulations around summer gasoline that will allow higher blends of ethanol, Reuters previously reported .
The Treasury Department is weighing a plan that involves using the oil futures market, Reuters reported , but ‚Äãthere is no immediate plan to announce the move.
Trump ordered on Tuesday that the U.S. International ‚ÄãDevelopment Finance Corporation provide ‚Å†insurance against losses stemming from political instability or conflict for maritime trade in the Gulf. The move came after oil and liquefied natural gas tanker transit ground to a halt in the Strait of Hormuz , a chokepoint for about 20% of the world's daily oil supply.
Markets met the ‚Å†move with ‚Äãsome skepticism. Analysts question whether financial guarantees alone can offset the operational and security ‚Äãrisks posed by the growing tensions in the region.
On Friday, the administration announced it would provide reinsurance for losses up to $20 billion in the Gulf region to bolster confidence ‚Äãfor oil and gas shippers during the war with Iran.

item.title: Shipper MSC to introduce emergency fuel surcharge
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Shipping company MSC said on Saturday it would implement an ‚Äãemergency fuel surcharge to all ‚Äåcargo from the Mediterranean (including West Mediterranean, Adriatic, East Mediterranean, Greece and Turkey) ‚Äãand Black Sea to the ‚ÄãIndian sub-continent, Red Sea and East ‚Å†Africa, effective March 16.
It said ‚Äãthe surcharge would be $30 per ‚Äãtwenty-foot equivalent unit (TEU) from the Mediterranean and Black Sea to the Red Sea for ‚Äãdry containers, and $50 per TEU ‚Äãfor refrigerated containers.
Dry containers from the Mediterranean ‚Äåand ‚Å†Black Sea to East Africa will be charged $60 per TEU, while refrigerated containers will be charged $90 ‚Äãper TEU, ‚Äãthe world's ‚Å†largest carrier of ocean container cargo said.
MSC will ‚Äãalso impose a surcharge of $40 ‚Äãper ‚Å†TEU from the Mediterranean and Black Sea to the Indian sub-continent ‚Å†for ‚Äãdry containers, and $60 ‚Äãper TEU for refrigerated containers.

item.title: South Africa central bank to redraft risk scenarios as Iran war boosts oil price
item.description:
LONDON, March 6 (Reuters) - South Africa's central bank will redraft its risk scenarios for its next rate-setting ‚Äåpolicy meeting as the widening Middle East conflict pushes up oil prices, the country's central bank governor told Reuters.
The central bank is scheduled to decide on March 26 on interest rates after keeping its main lending rate unchanged at 6.75% in a split decision in late January, citing at ‚Äãthe time that policy makers wanted to see inflation expectations fall further.
"We had our baseline (in the January meeting) and ‚Äãwe had an optimistic scenario and an adverse scenario," said South African Reserve Bank Governor Lesetja ‚Å†Kganyago. He said the adverse scenario had assumed the average oil price for the year would reach $75 per barrel and ‚Äãthe rand would weaken to 18.50 to the dollar.
Now the previous adverse scenario, he said, "is gone - it was in the past ... ‚Äãwe will come up with a completely new one."
The Middle East crisis set off by Israel and Washington's bombing of Iran lifted Brent crude futures to more than $94 per barrel this week, while the rand weakened to 16.82 to the dollar.
Kganyago said a 10% move in the exchange ‚Äãrate would have a much stronger impact on inflation in South Africa than a similar jump in oil prices.
"Yes, it's the ‚Äãadverse scenario, but it is not playing out as we had feared," Kganyago said, adding that policy makers would only get concerned once ‚Äåthey saw ‚Å†the impact of the exchange rate filter through to prices.
"The call that as a policy maker you must make is - is this transitory, or is it persistent? And you only respond to the persistent, not to the transitory - and that is not an easy call to make."
The knock-on effects from global tensions that have kept markets on edge in recent months - and the ‚Äãmost recent crisis - would be ‚Äãdominating discussions at the next ‚Å†meeting, Kganyago added, speaking on the sidelines of an investor conference in London.
"We will discuss what is the impact of this geopolitics on the markets, on oil, emerging markets, and on ‚Äãexchange rates."
Asked whether South Africa would continue to buy dollars - as it has since the ‚Äãstart of the ‚Å†year - after the rand weakened nearly 4% this week against a strong greenback, the governor said the policy had not changed.
"If we think that there are cheap dollars available in the market, we will pick them up," he said, adding that overall reserve levels ‚Å†had been ‚Äãbolstered not only by dollar purchases, but by the rising value of gold in ‚Äãthe central bank's war chest and the proceeds of borrowing by the Treasury.
Central bank data released earlier on Friday showed that South Africa's net foreign reserves rose ‚Äãto $75.84 billion at the end of February from $74.88 billion in January.

item.title: Flash floods in Nairobi kill 10, disrupt flights at major airport
item.description:
NAIROBI, March 7 (Reuters) - Aid workers ‚Äåpulled bodies from floodwaters across Nairobi on Saturday after overnight flash floods killed at least 10 people, swept away dozens of cars and ‚Äãdisrupted flights at East Africa's biggest airport, authorities said.
In ‚Äãthe industrial neighbourhood of Grogan, security guard John Lomayan, ‚Å†34, looked at the body of an elderly man ‚Äãhe recognised - a roadside egg seller - trapped beneath a car that ‚Äãhad been washed away when the Nairobi River burst its banks.
"I saw him being carried by the water from up there," he said, gesturing ‚Äãup the road. "We didn't know where he had gone. ‚ÄãIt is only now that we see him under the car".
A Reuters reporter ‚Äåsaw ‚Å†three bodies pulled from underneath cars. Police said 10 people had been confirmed dead so far.
Scientists say global warming is worsening floods and droughts across East Africa by concentrating rainfall ‚Äãinto shorter, more ‚Äãintense bursts. ‚Å†A 2024 World Weather Attribution study found climate change had made devastating rains in the region ‚Äãtwice as likely as before.
Kenya Airways said ‚Äãthe rains ‚Å†had disrupted flights to Nairobi and forced some to divert to the coastal city of Mombasa.
"So many cars, so much stuff, ‚Å†I ‚Äãdon't know. Everything was just (washed away). ‚ÄãAll of the water (came) ... from that river," shocked resident Cedric Mwanza said, referring ‚Äãto the Nairobi River.

item.title: Days after Iran strikes, Trump hosts Latin American leaders with China in focus
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump will host Latin American leaders in Florida on Saturday as his administration seeks to counter China's ‚Äãgrowing influence in the region, a diplomatic show just days after U.S. strikes on Iran opened a new front in the Middle ‚ÄåEast.
The summit comes as Trump prepares for talks with Chinese President Xi Jinping in Beijing at the end of March. The Trump administration hopes to pull Latin America closer to Washington after years of growing Chinese trade, lending and infrastructure investment in the region.
Saturday's gathering, dubbed the "Shield of the Americas," also gives Trump a chance to project strength closer to home even ‚Äãas the conflict in the Middle East leads to consequences he may not fully control, such as rising prices for oil and gas.
Kristi Noem ‚Äãwill be special envoy for the "Shield of the Americas", Trump posted Thursday. Noem was Homeland Security secretary until Trump removed her ‚Å†from that post this week after mounting criticism of her from Congress.
The summit brings together conservative leaders aligned with Trump on security, migration and economics, reflecting a ‚Äãbroader rightward shift in parts of Latin America.
Among those expected to attend are Argentine President Javier Milei, Chile's president-elect Jose Antonio Kast and Salvadoran President Nayib Bukele, ‚Äãwhose gang crackdown, criticized by human rights groups, has become a model for parts of Latin America's right.
