Politicians from across the region have toured Bukele's sprawling "mega-prison," where the United States last year deported more than 200 Venezuelans without trial.
Also expected are Honduran President Nasry Asfura, who narrowly won a disputed election with Trump's backing, and Ecuador's President Daniel Noboa, who has ‚Äãechoed parts of Trump's economic agenda and recently announced joint operations with the U.S. in a military crackdown on drug trafficking.
Many of the leaders share Trump's ‚Äãhardline view of crime and migration, favoring crackdowns over deeper social fixes and private business over the state. Their rise reflects a broader rightward turn in parts of Latin America ‚Äåat a ‚Å†time when the region is being pulled between Washington and Beijing.
Ryan Berg, who heads the Americas Program at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, wrote this week that the summit is the first time in Trump's second term that Washington has brought together Latin American leaders in this way.
He said the talks are likely to focus on security, drug trafficking, money laundering and China's role in ports, telecommunications and other infrastructure.
Berg said China's trade with the region hit ‚Äãa record $518 billion in 2024, with ‚ÄãBeijing loaning more than $120 billion to ‚Å†governments across the Western Hemisphere.
China's increased involvement in Latin America - from satellite tracking stations in Argentina and a port in Peru to economic support for Venezuela - has been an irritant for successive U.S. administrations.
China has expanded its reach through trade, loans ‚Äãand infrastructure, while the Trump administration has pushed governments in the region to curb Beijing's role in ports, energy ‚Äãprojects and other strategic ‚Å†assets.
That pressure was on display recently when Panamanian authorities moved against a Hong Kong-based firm tied to operations in the Panama Canal, a key global freight channel.
Washington has also taken more direct steps in the region.
The United States captured Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro on January 3 and moved to seize control of the country's oil exports, and ‚Å†has tightened enforcement ‚Äãof the decades-long U.S. embargo on Cuba.
Several Trump administration officials have told Reuters that Trump's ‚Äãmove against Maduro was intended in part to counter China's ambitions, and that Beijing's days of leveraging debt to get cheap oil from Venezuela were "over."

item.title: Nigerian army kills 45 in Katsina clash
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - At least 45 "bandits" were killed in a clash ‚Äãwith Nigerian troops in ‚Äåthe Danmusa area of Katsina state in northern Nigeria, according ‚Äãto a statement from ‚Äãthe Katsina State Government on ‚Å†Saturday.
The violence followed an attack ‚Äãby armed gangs of ‚Äãmen from neighboring Zamfara state who rode into Alhazawa village in Musawa ‚Äãon March 5 and ‚Äãattempted to steal cattle, the statement ‚Äåsaid.
The ‚Å†attackers returned the following day and encountered troops stationed at the army's Forward Operating ‚ÄãBase ‚Äãin ‚Å†Dan Ali near Maidabino ward, leading to a ‚Äãclash in which ‚Äãthe ‚Å†army killed 45 of the attackers, the statement said.
A captain ‚Å†and ‚Äãtwo soldiers ‚Äãdied in the encounter, the statement added.

item.title: Iranian hardline clerics seek swift naming of new supreme leader
item.description:
DUBAI, March 7 (Reuters) - Two influential and hardline Iranian clerics have called for the swift selection of a new supreme leader to help guide ‚Äãthe nation amid a new wave of U.S. and Israeli ‚Äåstrikes, Iranian media reported on Saturday.
The calls by the clerics suggest that at least some in the clerical establishment are uncomfortable with leaving a three-man council in ‚Äãcharge, even temporarily under constitutional rules, after the killing of ‚ÄãSupreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
U.S. President Donald Trump has said ‚Å†the U.S. should have a role in choosing the new leader, ‚Äãa demand Iran has rejected.
Naser Makarem Shirazi, a grand ayatollah, which means ‚Äãhe commands a broad following for his religious rulings, said an appointment was needed swiftly to "help better organise the country‚Äôs affairs", state media reported.
Last week, two senior Shi‚Äôite ‚Äãreligious authorities also issued fatwas, or religious decrees, calling on Muslims ‚Äãaround the world to avenge the killing of Khamenei. Makarem Shirazi said it was ‚Äåa ‚Å†religious duty for Muslims "until the evil of these criminals is eradicated from the world."
Grand Ayatollah Hossein Nouri Hamedani also urged members of the Assembly of Experts, a clerical body charged with choosing the new leader, ‚Äãto accelerate the ‚Äãprocess of picking ‚Å†Khamenei's successor, state media reported.
Following rules laid out in Iran's constitution, a three-man council comprising the president, a ‚Äãsenior cleric and the head of the judiciary, ‚Äãhas taken ‚Å†on the supreme leader's role until the Assembly of Experts decides.
The constitution states a supreme leader should be chosen within three months, although with ‚Å†war raging, ‚Äãit is not immediately clear how quickly ‚Äãthe 88-member Assembly of Experts can convene. Sources have said some clerics have held some ‚Äãconsultations online.

item.title: Ukraine's interceptor drone makers look at exports to the Gulf as Iran war flares
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Ukrainian manufacturers of cheap interceptor drones designed to knock out enemy unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) say they have the capacity to export in large volumes, amid enquiries from the United ‚ÄãStates and Middle East prompted by the Iran war .
Hundreds of drones based on Iran's Shahed model and now made in Russia fill Ukraine's skies during frequent attacks, ‚Äåand many are downed by air defences including Western missiles, fighter jets, truck-mounted guns and interceptor drones.
Now, as Iran launches drones at U.S. allies across the Gulf and beyond in response to intense U.S. and Israeli strikes, Ukraine is hoping the Middle East crisis will increase its leverage with allies by showing them the expertise it has built during four years of Russia's full-scale invasion could be invaluable to partners' own long-term security.
With missiles sometimes costing millions of dollars each, ‚Äãand in short supply as Western armies scramble to bolster their own defences, interceptors are seen as an efficient and cost-effective way to counter attacks by relatively cheap ‚Äãdrones.
The United States and its allies in the Middle East have swiftly sought Ukraine's help with supplying such interceptors.
President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Thursday ‚Å†Ukraine would provide assistance in response to a U.S. request for help and said earlier that Middle Eastern nations had approached Kyiv.
Zelenskiy did not specify what this would entail, but a source familiar with ‚Äãthe matter told Reuters that the United States and Qatar were in talks to purchase Ukrainian interceptor drones.
SkyFall, a major manufacturer of drones including interceptors, said its manufacturing capacity ‚Äãhad outgrown Ukraine's ability to purchase its systems and the company was ready to export.
"We have had interest and inquiries from our (allies) and countries in the Middle East," said Ares, a representative of SkyFall who wore a balaclava as he spoke to hide his identity.
Ares spoke on condition that only his call-sign be used.
SkyFall says its P1-SUN interceptor has downed more than 1,500 Shaheds and 1,000 other drones since it entered operations four months ago.
"The ‚Äãcompany is ready to provide any necessary assistance if we get the green light from our government," he said, adding that it would only do so if it did not affect Ukraine's ‚Äãability to defend itself.
Ihor Fedirko, CEO of UCDI, Ukraine's state-backed arms manufacturers' association, estimated that manufacturers of interceptor UAVs and other counter-drone systems were making about twice as many as the Ukrainian military needed to ‚Äådeal with Russia's frequent ‚Å†barrages .
SkyFall asked that the location of its factory not be disclosed. During a recent visit, Reuters reporters saw long rows of 3D printers churning out plastic drone components and halls of workers soldering and assembling drones.
The company estimated it could manufacture up to 50,000 interceptor drones a month and export 5,000 to 10,000 without impinging on Ukraine's needs.
Although Ukraine does not shoot down every drone fired by Russia, this is not always down to munition availability and can instead be a question of co-ordination.
Kyiv is striving to better layer its air defences in order to allow fewer targets ‚Äãthrough, and sees boosting interceptor drone use as ‚Äãa key piece of the puzzle.
In February, ‚Å†they were responsible for 70% of the drones downed in and around Kyiv, according to Ukraine's military.
Most Ukrainian interceptor drones cost a few thousand dollars or less. In comparison, PAC-3 missiles used in the coveted U.S. Patriot air defence system, can cost $4 million each and ‚Äãtend to be used to bring down enemy missiles.
Shahed-136 drones, designed by Iran and now mass-manufactured by Russia, are estimated to cost between $50,000 ‚Äãand $100,000 each.
SkyFall markets the ‚Å†P1-SUN as a highly cost-effective option. Depending on the model's specifications, it charges Ukraine's military about $1,000 per drone.
"If we are talking about export and ... assistance to partners, then the price will most likely be higher," Ares said, although he believed it would still represent the cheapest option.
Industry representatives said one of the biggest obstacles to using Ukrainian interceptor drones in the Gulf was pilots rather than drones. Ukraine is ‚Å†the only ‚Äãcountry with drone crews who know how to use these systems in combat.
SkyFall has its own academy that provides ‚Äãa three-week course to new pilots, and the company said it would be ready to send instructors abroad in the event that Ukraine's government allowed it to sell drones to other countries.
It also said it has developed capabilities to pilot ‚Äãits drones remotely, meaning drones could potentially be flown in the Gulf from a screen in Ukraine.

item.title: China's Xi says the military must be politically loyal, root out corruption
item.description:
SHANGHAI, March 7 (Reuters) - Chinese President Xi Jinping said on ‚ÄãSaturday the military must ‚Äåbe loyal to the Communist Party and root out ‚Äãcorruption, according to a ‚Äãreport by the official Xinhua ‚Å†news agency.
"There is ‚Äãno place for corruption to ‚Äãhide," said Xi.
"The fight against corruption must move forward."
The remarks ‚Äãcome amid the annual ‚Äãmeetings of the nation's top political ‚Äåbodies, ‚Å†where officials unveiled this year's economic targets.
Two senior Chinese officials were absent ‚Äãon ‚ÄãWednesday from ‚Å†the ranks of China's highest tier ‚Äãof power at ‚Äãthe ‚Å†opening of the annual meetings, with top Communist Party ‚Å†cadres ‚Äãensnared in ‚Äãa wave of purges .

item.title: Lebanon's Hezbollah warns residents in Israel's north to evacuate
item.description:
TEL AVIV, March 7 (Reuters) - Lebanese Shi'ite militant group Hezbollah on Saturday warned ‚Äãresidents of a northern Israeli city near ‚Äåthe border with Lebanon to evacuate and head south.
Hezbollah did not specify what action, if any, it ‚Äãplanned to take against the city, ‚Äãwhich lies only a few kilometres from ‚Å†the border.
"Warning. All residents of Kiryat Shmona ‚Äãare asked to evacuate immediately. Head south," it ‚Äãsaid in a statement.
Lebanon was pulled into the widening U.S.-Israel war with Iran on Monday after Hezbollah, an ‚ÄãIranian-aligned group, fired rockets and drones into ‚ÄãIsrael. Israel responded with heavy strikes across Lebanon's south, ‚Äåeast ‚Å†and near Beirut.
Tens of thousands of Israelis living in the north evacuated their homes after war broke out in Gaza between Israel and ‚ÄãPalestinian group ‚ÄãHamas in ‚Å†2023, as Hezbollah began firing rockets and other projectiles at northern ‚Äãcommunities.
Residents returned after a November 2024 ‚Äãceasefire ‚Å†between Israel and Hezbollah. Israeli Defence Minister Israel Katz has this week repeatedly urged Israelis ‚Å†in ‚Äãthe north to remain in ‚Äãtheir homes, saying the military will protect them.

item.title: Trump says Iran will be 'hit very hard' on Saturday
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - U.S. President ‚ÄãDonald Trump said Iran ‚Äåwould be "hit very hard" on Saturday and that he ‚Äãwas considering widening the ‚Äãareas and groups of people ‚Å†being, targeted, without providing ‚Äãdetails.
"Today Iran will be ‚Äãhit very hard! Under serious consideration for complete destruction and certain ‚Äãdeath, because of Iran's ‚Äãbad behavior, are areas and groups ‚Äåof ‚Å†people that were not considered for targeting up until this moment in time" ‚ÄãTrump ‚Äãsaid ‚Å†in a post on Truth Social.
He also ‚Äãnoted that Iran had ‚Äãapologised ‚Å†to its neighbours for its strikes against them, ‚Å†which ‚Äãhe cast as ‚Äãa surrender.

item.title: Pope Leo names former envoy to Duterte's Philippines as ambassador to US
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 7 (Reuters) - Pope Leo on Saturday named a ‚Äãsenior Church diplomat who represented the Vatican in the Philippines during former President Rodrigo ‚ÄåDuterte 's war on drugs as the Vatican's U.S. ambassador and envoy to the Trump administration.
Italian Archbishop Gabriele Caccia, 68, served for two years in Manila before becoming the Vatican's U.N. ambassador in 2020.
His appointment follows Vatican ‚Äãcriticism of U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran , with the Vatican's top diplomat, Cardinal Pietro Parolin, saying ‚Äãon Wednesday that the military campaign had undermined international law .
Leo, the first U.S. ‚Å†pope, has also openly criticised some of Trump's right-wing policies. He has described Trump's immigration crackdown, ‚Äãwhich has convulsed cities across the United States, as " inhuman ."
Caccia, widely seen as one of the more ‚Äãexperienced diplomats in the Vatican's foreign service, will arrive in Washington as some U.S. bishops have ramped up criticism of Trump.
He will replace Cardinal Christophe Pierre, 80, who is retiring and had been the Vatican's U.S. ‚Äãambassador since 2016.
Three top U.S. cardinals in January decried the direction of American foreign policy, saying ‚Äãthe country's "moral role in confronting evil around the world" was in question.
In ‚ÄåManila, ‚Å†Caccia was known for tamping down tensions between local Catholic bishops and Duterte, who frequently attacked Church officials in speeches.
Duterte, who left office in 2022, is being held in The Hague over murders committed during his war on drugs .
The Vatican, which maintains about 110 embassies worldwide, considers Washington one ‚Äãof its most important ‚Äãdiplomatic postings.
The ambassador ‚Å†must represent the Church's priorities at the White House, and envoys over the decades have contested policies of various U.S. administrations.
The Vatican's U.S. ambassador ‚Äãalso plays a sweeping role inside the national Church. He is ‚Äãresponsible for recommending ‚Å†to the pope U.S. priests who could serve as bishops, shaping the lives of the country's 72 million Catholics.
Caccia, originally from Milan, has previously served as the Vatican's ambassador to Lebanon and ‚Å†spent seven ‚Äãyears as the number-three official in the Vatican's powerful ‚ÄãSecretariat of State.
The Vatican and the U.S. formally established diplomatic relations in 1984 under President Ronald Reagan. None of the ‚Äãprevious six Vatican ambassadors in Washington had served first at the United Nations.

item.title: Trump cannot end protections for 350,000 Haitians, US appeals court rules
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - A divided U.S. appeals court has refused to let the Trump administration revoke legal protections that allow more than 350,000 Haitians to live and ‚Äãwork in the U.S. and avoid being returned to their gang-violence -stricken country.
A 2-1 ‚Äåpanel of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit late on Friday rejected , opens new tab the administration's bid to pause a February 2 ruling that blocked the U.S. Department of Homeland ‚ÄãSecurity from ending Haiti's Temporary Protected Status.
TPS is a humanitarian program that shields ‚Äãeligible migrants from deportation and allows them to work.
Under outgoing Homeland ‚Å†Security Secretary Kristi Noem, the department has moved to end TPS for a dozen ‚Äãcountries as part of President Donald Trump's immigration crackdown, arguing the program was never ‚Äãintended to serve as a "de facto amnesty."
The administration had asked the D.C. Circuit to stay U.S. District Judge Ana Reyes' February order while it appeals. Her decision came in a class-action lawsuit brought by ‚ÄãHaitians seeking to prevent DHS from exposing them to deportation.
Reyes found that Noem's November ‚Äãmove to end the Haitians' legal protections likely violated TPS termination procedures and the U.S. Constitution's ‚ÄåFifth ‚Å†Amendment guarantee of equal protection under the law.
The administration on appeal noted that the U.S. Supreme Court had twice allowed it to end TPS for Venezuelans.
But U.S. Circuit Judges Florence Pan and Brad Garcia, both appointed by Democratic President Joe Biden, distinguished the cases ‚Äãand said Haitians sent ‚Äãhome would "be vulnerable ‚Å†to violence amid a 'collapsing rule of law' and lack access to life-sustaining medical care."
U.S. Circuit Judge Justin Walker, a Trump appointee, ‚Äãdissented, saying the case and the earlier Supreme Court litigation involving ‚ÄãVenezuelans were "the ‚Å†legal equivalent of fraternal, if not identical, twins."
DHS did not respond to a request for comment.
Haitians were first granted TPS in 2010 after a devastating earthquake. The U.S. has ‚Å†repeatedly renewed ‚Äãthe designation, most recently under the Biden administration ‚Äãin July 2024.
At that time, DHS cited Haiti's "simultaneous economic, security, political, and health crises", driven by gangs and ‚Äãthe absence of a functioning government.

item.title: Turkey considering deployment of F-16s to Cyprus, ministry source says
item.description:
ANKARA, March 7 (Reuters) - Turkey is considering ‚Äãthe deployment of ‚ÄåF-16 aircraft to Cyprus, a Turkish ‚Äãdefence ministry ‚Äãsource said on ‚Å†Saturday.
The person said ‚Äãsuch a ‚Äãmove was one of the steps being ‚Äãconsidered amid ‚Äãthe phased planning underway ‚Äåto ‚Å†ensure the security of the breakaway Turkish Cypriot ‚Äãstate ‚Äãin ‚Å†the north of the ‚Äãisland, as ‚Äãconflict ‚Å†spreads in the region.

item.title: EU looks to soften energy bill pressures for industry, document shows
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 7 (Reuters) - The European Union is examining energy ‚Äåtaxes, network charges and carbon costs as possible areas for short-term measures to ease pressure on industries hit by high energy prices, a document seen by Reuters showed.
Brussels is looking ‚Äãfor quick fixes after companies warned they cannot compete with rivals in ‚ÄãChina and the U.S. - even before this week's surge in oil ‚Å†and gas prices sparked by the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
European Commission President ‚ÄãUrsula von der Leyen has pledged to present options for EU leaders to consider at ‚Äãa summit on 19 March.
A Commission paper prepared for a meeting of EU Commissioners on Friday showed the bloc is exploring short-term measures to help the hardest-hit regions and sectors, without ‚Äãundermining longer-term climate laws meant to shift Europe to a cheaper, low-carbon energy ‚Äãsystem.
"Any proposal for legislative change will not deliver immediately and a bridge solution may be ‚Äåneeded ‚Å†to reduce energy prices in the next 2-5 years until the clean transition eases pressure on power prices as already seen in some regions," said the document, seen by Reuters.
The paper said the Commission would look at network charges - which make ‚Äãup about 18% ‚Äãof industrial power ‚Å†bills - and national taxes and levies, as well as carbon costs, which account for around 11% of bills.
It noted that ‚Äãgovernments are underusing existing tools to cut companies' energy bills, ‚Äãincluding state ‚Å†aid to offset carbon costs and contracts for difference that guarantee industrial consumers a stable power price.
The document said that if energy supplies are disrupted further, Brussels must ‚Å†be ‚Äãready to introduce measures to encourage consumers to ‚Äãuse less energy, as it did in 2022 when Russia slashed gas deliveries.
A Commission spokesperson did not ‚Äãimmediately respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Kuwait cuts oil production as precaution amid Iran tensions, KPC says
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Kuwait said it had implemented a ‚Äãprecautionary reduction in crude ‚Äåoil production and refining throughput following the ‚Äãongoing attacks by ‚ÄãIran against Kuwait and "Iranian threats ‚Å†to safe passage ‚Äãof ships through the ‚ÄãStrait of Hormuz," Kuwait Petroleum Corporation (KPC) said in a ‚Äãstatement on Saturday.
The ‚Äãstate oil company said the ‚Äåmove ‚Å†was part of its "risk management and business continuity strategy."
It said ‚Äãthe adjustment ‚Äãwas ‚Å†strictly precautionary and would be ‚Äãreviewed as the ‚Äãsituation ‚Å†develops, and it remained ready to restore ‚Å†production ‚Äãlevels once conditions ‚Äãallow.

item.title: Trump announces new military coalition to 'eradicate cartels' in Western Hemisphere
item.description: March 7 (Reuters) - U.S. President ‚ÄãDonald ‚ÄåTrump on Saturday ‚Äãannounced ‚Äãa new ‚Å†military ‚Äãcoalition ‚Äãto "eradicate cartels" in the ‚ÄãWestern ‚ÄãHemisphere and ‚Äåsaid ‚Å†17 nations had formally ‚Äãentered ‚Äãthe ‚Å†alliance.

item.title: Trump says Cuba negotiating deal with him and Rubio
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - U.S. ‚ÄãPresident Donald Trump said ‚Äåon Saturday that Cuba wants to make a ‚Äãdeal and is ‚Äãnegotiating with him and ‚Å†Secretary of State Marco ‚ÄãRubio.
Speaking at the "Shield ‚Äãof the Americas" gathering of Latin American leaders in Miami, ‚ÄãFlorida, Trump said ‚ÄãCuba was "at the end of ‚Äåthe ‚Å†line."
"They want to negotiate, and they are negotiating with Marco (Rubio) and ‚Äãmyself ‚Äãand ‚Å†some others, and I would think ‚Äãa deal would ‚Äãbe ‚Å†made very easily with Cuba," he said.

item.title: Sweden says ship¬†seized in Baltic Sea had false flag, one crew member notified of crime
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 7 (Reuters) - A ship seized by Sweden in its Baltic Sea waters sailed ‚Äãunder a false flag and was suspected ‚Äåof violating maritime law and the national ship safety act because of its lack of ‚Äãseaworthiness, the Swedish police said on ‚ÄãSaturday.
One crew member was notified about the ‚Å†suspected breach of the national and ‚Äãinternational laws, Johan Andersson, deputy head of police's ‚ÄãNational Operations Department, told a press conference.
Police and coast guard on Friday took control of Guinea-flagged Caffa ‚Äãoff southern Sweden, saying it had unclear ‚Äãflag status and was thus suspected of being stateless, ‚Äåand ‚Å†that they had launched a probe into suspected violation of maritime law.
"Our investigation so far strengthens our suspicions and our ‚Äãopinion that this ‚Äãship ‚Å†has extensive sea safety deficiencies," Andersson said on Saturday.
"We also have ‚Äãinformation that the vessel would be ‚Äãon ‚Å†the Ukraine sanctions list," he said.
Most of the 11 crew members on Caffa, which ‚Å†according ‚Äãto ship-tracking service MarineTraffic ‚Äãis a 96-meter general cargo ship, were Russian, Andersson said.

item.title: Flavio Bolsonaro draws even with Lula in Brazil election matchup, Datafolha shows
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 7 (Reuters) - A second-round runoff between Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva and Senator Flavio Bolsonaro would ‚Äãresult in a technical tie, according to a Datafolha ‚Äåpoll released on Saturday.
The poll shows that if the runoff were held today, Lula would receive 46% of the vote, compared with ‚Äã43% for Flavio, the eldest son of former President ‚ÄãJair Bolsonaro.
If Lula were to face off against Tarcisio ‚Å†de Freitas, governor of the state of Sao Paulo, ‚Äã45% of respondents said they would vote for Lula in ‚Äãthe second round, while 42% would vote for de Freitas, also a technical tie.
Brazil's general election is scheduled for October 4. If no presidential ‚Äãcandidate receives a majority of the vote, the two ‚Äãcandidates with the most votes will face off in a runoff on ‚ÄåOctober ‚Å†25.
According to the survey, Lula, 80, who will be vying for his fourth presidential term, leads in all first‚Äëround scenarios. However, Flavio‚Äôs candidacy has gained strength since his name was put ‚Äãforward in December ‚Äãby Jair ‚Å†Bolsonaro, who is in jail.
Lula and Flavio, 44, also have the highest rejection rates in the ‚Äãpoll. Some 46% of respondents said they ‚Äãwould not ‚Å†vote for Lula under any circumstances, while 45% said the same about Flavio - another technical tie.
Datafolha interviewed 2,004 voters from ‚Å†Tuesday to ‚ÄãThursday in 137 municipalities. The survey ‚Äãhas a margin of error of two percentage points in either direction.

item.title: UAE president says country is well and is no easy prey in first public comments since Iran strikes
item.description:
DUBAI, March 7 (Reuters) - The president of the United Arab Emirates said ‚Äãhis nation was in a ‚Äåtime of war but was well and told his enemies it was ‚Äãno easy prey, in his ‚Äãfirst public comments since Iran ‚Å†launched missiles at its Gulf ‚Äãneighbour amid U.S.-Israeli strikes.
"The UAE has ‚Äãthick skin and bitter flesh - we are no easy prey," Mohammed bin ‚ÄãZayed Al Nahyan, who is ‚Äãalso ruler of Abu Dhabi, said in ‚Äåcomments, ‚Å†made on Friday when visiting those injured in strikes, aired on Abu Dhabi TV on Saturday.
"We ‚Äãwill carry ‚Äãout ‚Å†our duty towards our country, our people, and ‚Äãour residents who are also ‚Äãpart ‚Å†of our family," he said.
The UAE, which consists of seven ‚Å†emirates ‚Äãincluding Dubai, would protect ‚Äãeveryone in the country, he said.

item.title: Associate of liquidated Brazilian lender Banco Master's owner dies, lawyers say
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 7 (Reuters) - Luiz Phillipi Mourao, an associate of lender Banco Master's owner Daniel Vorcaro, died ‚Äãon Friday night, according to a statement ‚Äåreleased by his attorneys on Saturday.
Mourao was declared brain dead after a suicide attempt, police said on Wednesday, when he ‚Äãwas among those arrested in the latest ‚Äãphase of the investigation involving the liquidated ‚Å†Master.
Master, which held under 1% of Brazil's banking ‚Äãassets, was liquidated in November due to what the ‚Äãcentral bank called a severe liquidity crisis, sharp financial deterioration and serious rule violations.
The investigation into the lender's unraveling ‚Äãhas exposed a network of influence and conflicts ‚Äãof interest shaking trust in some of Brazil's most powerful ‚Äåinstitutions.
Vorcaro ‚Å†was arrested on Wednesday, accused of bribing two central bank officials and also plotting to attack and intimidate people he perceived as acting against his interests ‚Äãwith Mourao, ‚Äãwhom he ‚Å†called "Sicario" after the hit men employed by Mexican cartels.
Vorcaro's lawyers said they denied ‚Äãthe allegations, and they expected to ‚Äãdemonstrate "the ‚Å†regularity of his conduct."
Minas Gerais state civil police said on Saturday that Mourao‚Äôs body was sent to ‚Å†forensic ‚Äãscientists for examination and would ‚Äãbe released to his family after tests are completed.

item.title: Israeli settler fatally shoots Palestinian man in West Bank, health ministry says
item.description:
RAMALLAH, West Bank March 7 (Reuters) - An Israeli settler shot dead a 27-year-old ‚ÄãPalestinian man in the occupied ‚ÄåWest Bank on Saturday, the Palestinian Health Ministry said.
The man was killed in ‚Äãthe village area of Masafer ‚ÄãYatta, near the Palestinian city of ‚Å†Hebron, according to the Health ‚ÄãMinistry and the official Palestinian news ‚Äãagency WAFA.
The Israeli military did not immediately respond to a request for comment on ‚Äãthe incident.
Masafer Yatta is among ‚Äãthe sites where Palestinians have been increasingly displaced ‚Äåby ‚Å†settler violence that has risen sharply over the past two years, according to the United Nations.
Two Palestinian brothers ‚Äãwere shot ‚Äãdead ‚Å†by an Israeli on Monday near the city of ‚ÄãNablus in the northern West ‚ÄãBank, ‚Å†the Palestinian Health Ministry said.
Israeli newspaper Haaretz said the military police ‚Å†were ‚Äãinvestigating a reserve soldier ‚Äãfor Monday's shooting.

item.title: Indian Oil Corp books some oil cargoes from Red Sea, source says
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Top Indian refiner, Indian Oil Corp (IOC.NS) , opens new tab , has booked some oil cargoes for loading ‚Äãfrom the Red Sea port of Yanbu, ‚Äåa company source said on Saturday.
The U.S.-Israel war with Iran has disrupted oil and natural gas ‚Äãexports from the Middle East, while ‚Äãtop oil exporter Saudi Arabia is increasing shipments ‚Å†from the Red Sea as an alternative.

item.title: Earthquake of magnitude 5.5 strikes Indonesia's southern Sumatra region, GFZ says
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - An earthquake ‚Äãof magnitude ‚Äå5.5 struck ‚ÄãIndonesia's ‚Äãsouthern Sumatra region ‚Å†on ‚ÄãSunday, the ‚ÄãGerman Research Center ‚Äãfor ‚ÄãGeosciences (GFZ) said.
The quake ‚Äåwas ‚Å†at a depth of ‚Äã101 ‚Äãkm (62.76 ‚Å†miles), ‚ÄãGFZ said.

item.title: Over 150 Iranian nationals leave Lebanon including diplomats, Lebanese security source says
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - More than 150 Iranian nationals, including ‚Äãdiplomats and their families, ‚Äåleft Lebanon on Saturday, a senior Lebanese security ‚Äãsource told Reuters, ‚Äãafter an Israeli military ‚Å†spokesperson threatened "representatives" of Iran in ‚ÄãLebanon.
The security source ‚Äãsaid they were being flown to Russia on a Russian ‚Äãplane, and that ‚Äãanother 20 Iranians had left ‚Äåon ‚Å†Friday following the start of a new war between Lebanese armed ‚Äãgroup ‚ÄãHezbollah ‚Å†and Israel. An Israeli military spokesperson ‚Äãon Tuesday told ‚Äãrepresentatives ‚Å†of the Iranian government "still in Lebanon to ‚Å†leave ‚Äãimmediately before ‚Äãthey are targeted."

item.title: Venezuela's Rodriguez commits to dialogue in message to Trump
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Venezuela's acting president, Delcy Rodriguez, said on Saturday that "diplomatic dialogue" ‚Äãwith the U.S. was the way ‚Äåto resolve the two countries' differences, after both governments agreed on diplomatic and consular relations.
"We reiterate our willingness ‚Äãto build long-term relations based on ‚Äãmutual respect, equality, and international law, with ‚Å†a view to promoting a work agenda ‚Äãthat strengthens cooperation for the benefit of both ‚Äãcountries," Rodriguez said in a post on X addressed to U.S. President Donald Trump.
The U.S. State Department announced ‚Äãon Thursday that the two countries would formally ‚Äãre-establish diplomatic ties.
Caracas severed diplomatic relations with Washington in 2019 ‚Äåafter ‚Å†the first Trump administration refused to recognize Venezuela's then-President Nicolas Maduro as the country's legitimate leader, following a disputed election, and instead recognized ‚Äãan opposition ‚Äãlawmaker as ‚Å†the country's president.
U.S. forces captured Maduro in January after months of ‚Äãheightened tensions between the two countries, ‚Äãsetting ‚Å†off a chain of changes in Venezuela, including the swearing-in of interim President Delcy Rodriguez.
The two ‚Å†have ‚Äãsince gradually resumed bilateral relations, ‚Äãafter Rodriguez's interim government expressed interest in rebuilding ties with ‚ÄãWashington.

item.title: ADNOC says it is managing offshore output, onshore operations continue
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Abu Dhabi National Oil Company said on Saturday it is actively managing offshore output levels to ‚Äãaddress storage requirements amid the U.S.-Isaeli war on Iran, ‚Äåwhile its onshore operations are continuing.
"This approach preserves operational flexibility and will enable the company to resume normal operations without prolonged delay," ADNOC said in ‚Äãa statement.
The war, now in its eighth day, has ‚Äãblocked shipments through the Strait of Hormuz, the crucial waterway ‚Å†responsible for roughly 20% of global oil and LNG supply.
Analysts ‚Äãhave predicted the UAE and Saudi Arabia would soon have to cut ‚Äãoutput as their oil storage fills up.
ADNOC said its operations are continuing, and that it is using export capacity that bypasses the strait as well ‚Äãas international storage facilities to ensure supply continuity to global markets.
The ‚ÄãUAE can bypass the strait using the Abu Dhabi Crude Oil Pipeline, also ‚Äåknown ‚Å†as the Habshan-Fujairah Pipeline.
The pipeline, which has a capacity of 1.5 million to 1.8 million barrels per day, transports oil from Abu Dhabi's fields directly to the port of Fujairah on the ‚ÄãGulf of Oman, ‚Äãfeeding its storage ‚Å†terminals and refining facilities.
"Business units are assessing the situation on a product-by-product and transaction-by-transaction basis, considering ‚Äãthe ongoing disruption that is affecting shipping through the ‚ÄãStrait ‚Å†of Hormuz," it said.
ADNOC has activated well-established protocols and is working closely with authorities to protect its people, assets and operations, it added.
Kuwait ‚Å†Petroleum ‚ÄãCorporation began cutting oil output on Saturday ‚Äãand declared force majeure, adding to earlier oil and gas reductions from Iraq and ‚ÄãQatar.

item.title: Earthquake of magnitude 5.2 strikes Afghanistan's Hindu Kush region, GFZ says
item.description: March 7 (Reuters) - An earthquake ‚Äãof magnitude ‚Äå5.2 struck ‚ÄãAfghanistan's ‚ÄãHindu Kush region ‚Å†on ‚ÄãSaturday, the ‚ÄãGerman Research Center for ‚ÄãGeosciences (GFZ) ‚Äãsaid. The quake ‚Äåwas ‚Å†at a depth ‚Äãof ‚Äã206 ‚Å†km (128 miles), ‚ÄãGFZ ‚Äãsaid.

item.title: Cracks emerge in Iran's leadership
item.description:
DUBAI, March 7 (Reuters) - Iran's hierarchy is showing signs of fracturing over a war its leaders see as existential, with angry divisions between hardliners and more pragmatic factions laid bare by a row over President Masoud Pezeshkian's promise not to strike Gulf states.
Fissures within Iran's ruling elite were ‚Äãlong suppressed under the iron rule of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, but his killing a week ago has allowed them to spill out into the open as ‚ÄåU.S. and Israeli strikes pile pressure on Tehran.
The unrelenting bombardment mortally imperils the Islamic Republic and has prompted its fiercest acolytes, the Revolutionary Guards , to seize a bigger role in strategy despite a decapitation campaign that has killed many top commanders.
Sources close to Iran's leadership, speaking from inside the country, told Reuters the strains were starting to show among leading figures still alive after a series of killings in the U.S.-Israeli strikes. They spoke anonymously due to the ‚Äãsensitivity of the matter.
In a sign of the growing stresses to the system, clerics are accelerating the appointment of a new supreme leader with a decision possible on Sunday - though ‚Äãit is far from clear if Khamenei's successor will wield enough authority to stamp out factional disputes.
While his son, Mojtaba Khamenei , is seen as a frontrunner ‚Å†backed by the Guards and his father's powerful office, he is untested, junior to most of Iran's senior ayatollahs, and has alienated moderates within the system.
Other potential candidates could struggle to uphold ‚Äãthe unquestioning obedience of the Guards required to maintain discipline within the system.
"Wartime tends to clarify power structures, and in this case the decisive voice is not that of the civilian leadership but of ‚Äãthe IRGC," said Alex Vatanka, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, using an abbreviation of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps.
Pezeshkian's apology to Gulf states for a week-long blitz of their territory - and his pledge to rein in such attacks - quickly prompted pushback from hardliners in the Revolutionary Guards and clerical elite, forcing him into a partial climbdown.
In one of the most open criticisms of Pezeshkian - and a sign of ‚Äãinternal division, hardline cleric and lawmaker Hamid Rasai addressed the president on social media, saying: "your stance was unprofessional, weak and unacceptable."
When the president later repeated his earlier statement on social media, he ‚Äãleft out the apology that had so angered the Guards and other hardliners - an embarrassing retreat.
To be sure, all senior figures within the hierarchy are steadfast in their commitment to defending the Islamic Republic and its revolutionary ‚Äåtheocracy from ‚Å†U.S. and Israeli attacks, but there are clear splits over their strategic approach.
Iran's leadership has sometimes played up differences between hardliners and moderates as a tactic in negotiations with the West, but the dispute over Pezeshkian's statement on Saturday revealed genuine divisions, two senior sources said.
A hardliner close to Khamenei's office, which remains a central node in the hierarchy, told Reuters that Pezeshkian's comments had angered many senior commanders in the Guards.
Another senior Iranian source, a moderate former official, said nobody would be able to fill Khamenei's shoes, describing the late leader as a formidable strategist who had led Iran through ‚Äãmany difficult periods.
With anxiety increasing in Iran's top ‚Äãranks, senior ayatollahs began to publicly urge that ‚Å†the clerical body responsible for appointing a supreme leader accelerate its work.
"It should expedite the process so that it leads to the disappointment of the enemy and the preservation of the unity and solidarity of the nation," Ayatollah Nouri Hamedani said in a statement carried by the semi-official Fars ‚ÄãNews Agency.
In Iran's unusual system, an elected president, government and parliament are subservient to a clerically appointed ayatollah ‚Äãwho wields ultimate authority as ‚Å†supreme leader and personally oversees the Revolutionary Guards and other powerful bodies of state.
As leader for 36 years, Khamenei often played hardline and moderate factions within the ruling system against each other while retaining the ultimate say, allowing them to voice disagreements so long as they bowed to his writ.
When he died, leadership formally passed to a constitutionally mandated interim council that included Pezeshkian, the clerical head of the judiciary ‚Å†and another cleric ‚Äãfrom a hardline body called the Guardian Council.
In Khamenei's absence, strains are showing even inside that tight body, with ‚Äãthe judiciary chief, noted hardliner Ayatollah Gholamhossein Mohseni-Ejei, saying some regional states had allowed their territory to be used for attacks.
"Heavy strikes on those targets will continue," he said, contradicting Pezeshkian's more conciliatory statement.
Still, even though Khamenei did sometimes allow moderate ‚Äãor reformist voices to carry the day in disputes with hardliners, they were usually overruled when the system seemed to come under threat.

item.title: As Iran's retaliation began, US officials scrambled to arrange evacuations
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 7 (Reuters) - As the first explosions from Iran's retaliatory attack sounded across the United Arab Emirates last Saturday, the State Department was still scrambling ‚Äãto finalize a key bureaucratic task - securing approvals for at least three U.S. embassies in the region to evacuate non-essential personnel.
Memos asking State Department leadership to approve evacuations for U.S. missions in Bahrain, ‚ÄåKuwait and Qatar, all of which were already coming under fire from Tehran on Saturday, were not sent up for clearance and approved until hours after the United States and Israel launched their war on Iran , and in several cases not until the following day, according to two sources familiar with the matter and half a dozen internal State Department cables seen by Reuters.
The release of public announcements that the U.S. was starting to pull out non-essential staff from Gulf Arab countries began Monday, three days into the war. For the U.S. ‚Äãembassy in Riyadh, the approval for authorized departure came on Tuesday, four days into the war and on the same day that it was struck by Iranian drones that led to a fire that ‚Äãdamaged the mission's facilities.
The delay was unusual. Typically, the United States starts evacuations for a planned military action well before the event itself.
When the United States invaded Iraq in ‚Å†2003, staff and U.S. citizens in the region had weeks to prepare, and at least two evacuations began more than a week before the actual operation started. Ahead of last week's strikes, Israel and Lebanon were the only regional embassies with orders for ‚Äãnon-essential personnel to depart.
The attack on Iran - the biggest U.S. military operation in the Middle East since 2003 - has put enormous strain on officials from the U.S. and other countries with citizens in the region. But lawmakers, former diplomats ‚Äãand sources involved in the process said the United States was unusually slow in activating contingency plans both for its own personnel and for thousands of stranded Americans.
The State Department's principal deputy spokesperson, Tommy Pigott, said hundreds of people were involved in the effort to help Americans return.
"We are working 24/7 and have contingency plans ready to go and implement when needed, including the ability to immediately activate the task force, which was done here," Pigott said in a statement to Reuters.
One factor underlying the uneven ‚Äãapproach, said sources familiar with the matter, was that ahead of the war's start, Trump officials kept contingency planning to a small group of officials.
In one case, officials involved in helping Americans get home had found out from a social ‚Äãmedia post from a senior Trump official that Washington was now offering charter flights to U.S. citizens, according to two people familiar with the situation.
"No directive came from anywhere," one of the people said.
Pigott said announcements on the task force that the ‚ÄåDepartment created specifically ‚Å†to tackle the fallout from the crisis and charter flights were coordinated with relevant officials.
In another instance, the alert for Americans to leave the region came not through normal State Department channels but again via a social media post.
The top State Department official for consular affairs, Mora Namdar, posted on X on Monday urging Americans across 14 countries in the Middle East to leave and said the U.S. was working to facilitate charter flights for U.S. citizens.
But because that message was drafted outside normal channels, State Department staff were surprised and had to update the department's formal travel advisory system that American companies and others rely on for guidance for their overseas personnel, according to two people familiar with ‚Äãthe situation.
As of Saturday, the U.S. State Department said ‚Äãit has completed "over a dozen charter flights and has ‚Å†safely evacuated thousands of Americans" from the Middle East. It did not say from which locations exactly the charter flights arrived.
One flight that departed Dubai bound for Washington on Friday carrying 182 embassy personnel and their families, and 51 private U.S. citizens, was only the second charter to depart from that country, according to a March 6 cable reviewed by ‚ÄãReuters. Since then, additional flights were set to take place.
When asked to confirm if specific plans were finalized ahead of the war to help evacuate American citizens from ‚Äãthe 14 countries, a State Department ‚Å†official briefing reporters earlier this week gave a broad response.
"I wouldn't say that specifically. What I would say is, we always have contingency plans, and we're always ready to assist Americans. That's what I would say to that question," the official, speaking on the condition of anonymity, said.
The Department on Friday said it had assisted 13,000 Americans who reached out seeking help to depart.
As the Department rushed to implement plans to help Americans, novelist and filmmaker Mohana Rajakumar in Doha dialed the hotline that top ‚Å†U.S. officials said ‚Äãstranded U.S. citizens should call for help. The recorded audio told her not to rely on government help to depart even as the ‚ÄãU.S. government repeatedly says the safety and security of U.S. citizens around the world is its top priority.
"I can tell you every WhatsApp group that I'm in with Americans, nobody feels that way," Rajakumar said, speaking to Reuters from Doha. "Everyone is asking why didn't they tell us to leave ‚Äãgiven they knew they were going to do this? Why didn't we have the option to leave?"
Officials said that recorded message was later updated.

item.title: Over 30 hurt in Peru after nightclub explosion causes chaos
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - An early morning explosion at a nightclub in northwestern Peru injured at ‚Äãleast 31 people, authorities said on ‚ÄåSaturday.
The government of Peru's La Libertad region said hospitals were treating individuals after the blast ‚Äãat the nightclub in Trujillo, ‚Äãwhich authorities are investigating.
Four people are in ‚Å†grave condition, the newspaper La Republica ‚Äãsaid, citing the regional health authority, which ‚Äãsaid minors were among those injured.
It is unclear what caused the incident. Peru has experienced a ‚Äãsurge in violence related to organized ‚Äãcrime, and Saturday's incident follows smaller attacks on ‚Äånightclubs ‚Å†in Trujillo, according to local media reports.
The explosion occurred during a show by a Peruvian musical group and sent revelers ‚Äãrunning for ‚Äãsafety.
Members of ‚Å†the Amor Rebelde group were unhurt after what they said ‚Äãwere "unfortunate events" that occurred at ‚Äãthe ‚Å†nightclub where they performed, according to a statement from the group on Facebook.
Video shared ‚Å†on ‚Äãa Peruvian news program ‚Äãshowed scenes of chaos after a loud boom was ‚Äãheard.

item.title: Exclusive: Saudi has told Iran not to attack it, warns of possible retaliation, sources say
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Saudi Arabia has told Tehran that while it favours a diplomatic settlement to Iran's conflict with the United States, continued attacks on the kingdom and ‚Äãits energy sector could push Riyadh to respond in kind, four sources familiar with the matter told Reuters.
The message was conveyed before a speech on ‚ÄåSaturday in which Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian apologised to neighbouring Gulf states for Tehran‚Äôs actions -- an apparent attempt to defuse regional anger over Iranian strikes that hit civilian targets.
Two days earlier, Saudi Foreign Minister Prince Faisal bin Farhan spoke to Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi and set out Riyadh‚Äôs position with clarity, the sources said.
Saudi Arabia is open to any form of mediation aimed at de‚Äëescalation and a negotiated settlement, the sources quoted ‚Äãthe minister as saying, underlining that neither Riyadh nor other Gulf states had let the U.S. use their airspace or territory to launch airstrikes on Iran.
But Prince ‚ÄãFaisal was also quoted by the sources as saying that if Iranian attacks persisted against Saudi territory or energy infrastructure, Saudi Arabia would ‚Å†be forced to permit U.S. forces to use their bases there for military operations. Riyadh would retaliate if attacks on the kingdom's critical energy facilities continued, he said.
The sources said ‚Äãthe kingdom had remained in regular contact with Tehran through its ambassador since the U.S. and Israeli military campaign against Iran began on February 28 following the collapse of talks on ‚ÄãIran's nuclear programme.
The Saudi and Iranian foreign ministries did not respond to requests for comment.
The United Arab Emirates, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain and Saudi Arabia have all come under heavy drone and missile fire from Iran over the past week.
Iran's Supreme Leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei , was killed on the first day of the war. Tehran responded by hitting Israel and Gulf Arab states hosting U.S. military installations, ‚Äãand Israel has attacked Lebanon's Iran-backed Hezbollah armed group.
Araqchi said in an interview on Saturday that he remained in constant contact with his Saudi counterpart and other Saudi officials, adding ‚Äãthat Riyadh had assured Tehran it was fully committed to not allowing its territory, waters or airspace to be used for attacks against Iran.
Pezeshkian said Iran's temporary leadership council had approved suspending attacks on ‚Äånearby countries - unless ‚Å†an attack on Iran came from those nations.
"I personally apologise to neighbouring countries that were affected by Iran's actions," he said.
To what extent Pezeshkian's remarks signal a change is unclear. There were further reports of strikes directed at Gulf states on Saturday.
Also, in a sign of possible divisions within Iran‚Äôs leadership, Khatam al-Anbiya Central Headquarters - the unified combatant command of the Iranian armed forces - said in a statement afterwards that U.S. and Israeli bases and interests across the region would remain targets.
The command said Iran's armed forces respected the sovereignty and interests of ‚Äãneighbouring states and had not taken action against ‚Äãthem so far. But it said U.S. ‚Å†and Israeli military bases and assets on land, at sea and in the air across the region would be treated as primary targets and face "powerful and heavy" strikes by Iran‚Äôs forces.
U.S. President Donald Trump said in a social media post that Iran had "apologized and surrendered to its Middle ‚ÄãEast neighbours, and promised that it will not shoot at them anymore. This promise was only made because of the relentless U.S. ‚Äãand Israeli attack."
Two Iranian ‚Å†sources confirmed that a call had taken place in which Riyadh warned Tehran to halt attacks on Saudi Arabia and neighbouring Gulf states. Iran, they said, reiterated its position that the strikes were not aimed at Gulf countries themselves but at U.S. interests and military bases hosted on their territory.
One Iranian source said that Tehran had in response demanded that U.S. bases in the region be ‚Å†closed and ‚Äãsome Gulf states stop sharing intelligence with Washington that Iran believes is being used to carry out attacks against it.
Another ‚ÄãIranian source said some military commanders were pressing to continue the strikes, accusing the U.S. of using bases in Gulf states and these countries' airspace to conduct operations against Iran.
Iran had in recent years mended fences with its ‚ÄãGulf neighbours, including former regional arch-rival Saudi Arabia. The diplomatic campaign imploded in the blitz of drones and missiles launched by Iran's Revolutionary Guards in the past week.

item.title: Katyusha rockets target US embassy in Baghdad, security sources say
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Katyusha rockets targeted the U.S. embassy in Baghdad on Saturday ‚Äãand sirens sounded, security sources and witnesses said, though ‚Äåit was not immediately clear whether the rockets caused any damage or casualties.
The attack was the first time the embassy had been fired ‚Äãon in more than two years. The last ‚Äãrecorded strike was in late 2023, when around seven ‚Å†mortar rounds landed in the compound amid a wave ‚Äãof attacks by Iran‚Äëaligned militias on U.S. assets in Iraq ‚Äãand Syria over Washington‚Äôs support for Israel in its war against Hamas in Gaza.
Explosions were heard near the embassy in central Baghdad around ‚Äã9 p.m. (1800 GMT), and sirens warning people to take cover ‚Äãsounded, witnesses said.
A senior Iraqi security official told Reuters the C‚ÄëRAM defence ‚Äåsystem ‚Å†brought down one of the rockets and that none landed inside the embassy. The official said there were no American casualties.
The attack indicates that Iran‚Äëaligned Iraqi militias ‚Äî who have ‚Äãvowed to retaliate ‚Äãfor the ‚Å†killing of Iran‚Äôs supreme leader ‚Äî have widened their targets beyond U.S. military bases in Iraqi ‚ÄãKurdistan and U.S. energy interests to now include ‚Äãthe ‚Å†U.S. Embassy.
Iraqi Prime Minister Mohammed Shia al‚ÄëSudani ordered security forces to track down those who launched the projectiles, calling them ‚Äúrogue groups ‚Å†operating ‚Äãoutside the law that do not ‚Äãrepresent the will of the Iraqi people,‚Äù a statement from his office said.

item.title: Ukraine's Zelenskiy says he spoke to Saudi crown prince
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Ukrainian President Volodymyr ‚ÄãZelenskiy said on Saturday he ‚Äåhad spoken to Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman about the situation in ‚ÄãIran and the Middle East ‚Äãand restated Kyiv's offer to help ‚Å†deal with Iranian drones.
"Ukraine has ‚Äãbeen fighting against (Iranian-designed) 'Shaheds' for years, and ‚Äãeveryone acknowledges that no other country in the world has such experience," Zelenskiy said ‚Äãon Telegram of his conversation.
"We ‚Äãare ready to help and expect that ‚Äåour ‚Å†people will also receive the necessary support."
Zelenskiy said earlier this week he had spoken to the leaders ‚Äãof Bahrain, ‚ÄãJordan, ‚Å†Kuwait, Qatar and the United Arab Emirates.
Zelenskiy has also ‚Äãsaid Ukraine, which has faced ‚Äãregular ‚Å†attacks by Shaheds in the four-year-old war with Russia, would help ‚Å†the ‚ÄãUnited States by providing ‚Äãspecialists to counter the weapons.

item.title: Trump tells Britain he does not need its help to win Iran war
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump said on Saturday that Britain is giving "serious thought" to ‚Äãsending two aircraft carriers to the Middle East, but added ‚Äåthat the U.S. does not need them to win the war with Iran, in the latest clash between the military allies.
Trump has repeatedly criticised British Prime Minister Keir ‚ÄãStarmer suggesting this week that he helped "ruin" the countries' historically close ‚Äãrelationship after London blocked the U.S.'s initial use of British ‚Å†bases to attack Iran.
In a post on Truth Social, Trump said ‚Äãhe "will remember" the lack of British support during the conflict with Iran.
"The United ‚ÄãKingdom, our once Great Ally, maybe the Greatest of them all, is finally giving serious thought to sending two aircraft carriers to the Middle East," Trump said.
"That‚Äôs OK, Prime ‚ÄãMinister Starmer, we don‚Äôt need them any longer ‚Äî But we will ‚Äãremember. We don‚Äôt need people that join Wars after we‚Äôve already won!"
The social media post ‚Äåcomes ‚Å†after the British Ministry of Defence said on Saturday it was preparing the Prince of Wales aircraft carrier for possible deployment.
But no final decision has been taken about whether to deploy the aircraft carrier to the Middle ‚ÄãEast, a British ‚Äãofficial said.
Starmer has ‚Å†defended his decision not to allow U.S. forces to use British bases to support initial strikes on Iran, ‚Äãsaying he needed to be satisfied that any military action ‚Äãwas ‚Å†legal and well planned.
He later granted U.S. forces permission to use British bases for what he called defensive strikes against Iranian missiles in storage depots or ‚Å†launchers.
Starmer ‚Äãearlier this year criticised Trump's desire to buy ‚ÄãGreenland and said his comments that European troops avoided frontline combat in the war in ‚ÄãAfghanistan were "frankly appalling."

item.title: White House halts security bulletin warning of Iran-related threats
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 7 (Reuters) - The White House has halted, at least for now, a federal security bulletin warning of ‚Äãa heightened threat to the U.S. in light of the conflict ‚Äåin Iran, a Trump administration official told Reuters.
The bulletin from the FBI, Department of Homeland Security, and National Counterterrorism Center is meant for state and local law enforcement agencies. ‚ÄãThe Trump administration asked an agency to hold the immediate release ‚Äãof the bulletin for review to ensure its accuracy, said the ‚Å†official, who spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss internal government matters.
The ‚Äãwarning, which was drafted by DHS‚Äô Office of Intelligence and Analysis, did not ‚Äãoffer sufficient insight and was not well written, the official told Reuters.
The Daily Mail reported on Friday the White House blocked the release of the bulletin that contains specific details ‚Äãon how Iranian proxies may carry out attacks across the United States.
The ‚ÄãFBI and the National Counterterrorism Center did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
A ‚ÄåU.S. ‚Å†Department of Homeland Security spokesperson told Reuters it is normal procedure for the agency to share intelligence bulletins with the White House for review before they are sent out.
"The White House is coordinating closely with all government agencies to ‚Äãensure information being disseminated ‚Äãis accurate, up ‚Å†to date, and has been properly vetted ‚Äî even if that means taking additional time to review to ensure nothing ‚Äãis done in a vacuum,‚Äù the White House said in ‚Äãa ‚Å†statement.
The attack on Iran is the biggest U.S. military operation in the Middle East since the 2003 invasion of Iraq.
Since last week, the U.S. completed over a ‚Å†dozen charter ‚Äãflights and evacuated thousands of Americans from the ‚ÄãMiddle East, the State Department said on Saturday.

item.title: Four killed in Beirut hotel strike, Israel says it targeted Iranian commanders
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 8 (Reuters) - At least four people were ‚Äãkilled when an Israeli strike hit an apartment ‚Äåin the Ramada hotel building in central Beirut early on Sunday, with Israel saying it targeted Iranian commanders operating in the Lebanese capital.
The ‚Äãattack marked the first Israeli strike to hit ‚Äãthe heart of Beirut since Israel-Hezbollah hostilities resumed last ‚Å†week.
Ten people were also injured in the attack on ‚ÄãBeirut's Raouche area, the Lebanese health ministry said in a ‚Äãstatement.
Israel said it targeted key commanders of Iran's elite Quds Force of the Revolutionary Guards but did not name them.
"The commanders of the ‚ÄãQuds Force's Lebanon Corps operated to advance terror attacks ‚Äãagainst the state of Israel and its civilians, while operating simultaneously for ‚Äåthe ‚Å†IRGC in Iran," the Israeli military said in a statement.
The hotel was housing displaced people fleeing the war in southern Lebanon and Beirut's southern suburbs, and some were seen leaving ‚Äãthe building ‚Äãfor fear of ‚Å†further air strikes.
Last week, Israel said it had killed the commander of Iran's Quds Force ‚Äãin Lebanon, Daoud Ali Zadeh, in a ‚Äãstrike in ‚Å†Tehran.
Lebanon was pulled into the widening U.S.-Israel war with Iran on Monday after the Iran-backed militant group Hezbollah fired rockets ‚Å†and ‚Äãdrones into Israel. Israel responded with ‚Äãheavy strikes across southern and eastern Lebanon and near Beirut.

item.title: Explosion hits US embassy in Oslo, causing minor damage, police say
item.description:
OSLO, March 8 (Reuters) - The U.S. embassy in Oslo ‚Äãwas hit by an explosion early ‚Äåon Sunday, causing minor damage but no injuries, Norwegian police said.
A loud blast was heard near ‚Äãthe embassy compound in western Oslo ‚Äãat around 1 a.m. (0000 GMT), and smoke ‚Å†was seen rising from the compound, eyewitnesses ‚Äãtold local media.
"We've determined that an explosion ‚Äãhit the American embassy," police spokesperson Mikael Dellemyr told public broadcaster NRK, adding that the blast occurred ‚Äãat the entry to the consular section.
It ‚Äãwas not immediately clear what caused the blast or ‚Äåwho ‚Å†was involved, the Oslo police department said in a separate statement.
"The police are in a dialogue with the embassy and there ‚Äãare no ‚Äãreports of ‚Å†any injured persons," the statement said.
The embassy did not immediately respond ‚Äãto a request for comment outside ‚Äãof ‚Å†regular business hours.
The U.S. State Department did not immediately respond to a request for ‚Å†comment.

item.title: Trump rejects settling Iran war, raises prospect of killing all its potential leaders
item.description:
BEIRUT/MIAMI/TEL AVIV/DUBAI, March 8 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump said he is not interested in negotiating with Iran and ‚Äãraised the possibility that the Iran war would only end once Tehran no longer has a functioning military or any remaining leadership in power.
Speaking to reporters aboard Air Force One on Saturday, Trump said ‚Äåthe air campaign could make negotiations a moot point if all potential leaders of Iran are killed and the Iranian military is destroyed.
"At some point, I don't think there will be anybody left maybe to say 'We surrender,'" Trump said.
Israel and Iran traded numerous attacks on Saturday as the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran entered a second week. Iran's president apologized to neighboring states for its attacks on U.S. facilities in those countries, in an attempt to cool anger across the Gulf, but stirred criticism from hardliners at home.
"I personally apologize to ‚Äãneighboring countries that were affected by Iran's actions," Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian said, urging them not to join U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran.
He dismissed Trump's demand for the Islamic Republic's unconditional surrender as "a dream," but said its temporary ‚Äãleadership council had agreed to suspend attacks on nearby states unless strikes on Iran originated from their territory.
Pezeshkian's comments caused a political stir in Iran, prompting his office to reiterate ‚Å†Iran's military would respond firmly to attacks from U.S. bases. Ali Larijani, Iran's secretary of the Supreme National Security Council, said on state television there was no rift among Iranian officials over its handling of the war.
The U.S. State Department did ‚Äãnot immediately respond to a request for comment.
Saudi Arabia has told Tehran that continued Iranian attacks on the kingdom and its energy sector could push Riyadh to respond in kind, four people familiar with the matter told Reuters.
The governments of Saudi ‚ÄãArabia, Kuwait and the United Arab Emirates reported Iranian drone attacks in their countries on Saturday and early Sunday with varying degrees of damage but no reported deaths. Iran's Revolutionary Guards also targeted U.S. forces at a base in Bahrain, Iranian state media said.
In the United States, the White House halted for now a federal security bulletin that would have warned of a heightened threat to the U.S. in light of the Iran conflict, a Trump administration official told Reuters. But a recent U.S. intelligence assessment had warned that Iran and its proxies "probably" ‚Äãpose a threat of targeted attacks on the United States.
In Oslo, the U.S. embassy was hit by an explosion early on Sunday, causing minor damage but no injuries, Norwegian police said. Smoke was seen rising from the area around the embassy ‚Äãcompound, eyewitnesses told Norwegian daily Verdens Gang. It was not immediately clear what caused the blast or who was involved.
Huge explosions were heard in parts of Tehran, state media reported, while Israel said it had struck Iranian missile sites and command centers.
The U.S.-Israeli ‚Äåattacks have killed at ‚Å†least 1,332 Iranian civilians and wounded thousands, according to Iran's U.N. ambassador, Amir Saeid Iravani.
U.S. forces were likely responsible for an apparent strike on an Iranian girls' school that killed scores of children, U.S. officials have told Reuters. But Trump, without citing evidence, told reporters on Saturday that Iran was responsible.
"We think it was done by Iran because they are very inaccurate as you know with their munitions. They have no accuracy whatsoever. It was done by Iran," said Trump. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, standing behind Trump aboard Air Force One, said the matter was still under investigation.
Iranian attacks have killed 10 people in Israel. At least six U.S. service members have been killed. Their remains arrived on Saturday at an Air Force ‚Äãbase in Delaware.
In ‚ÄãIran, local news agencies, citing an Iranian Oil Ministry ‚Å†source, said its fuel depots were hit by strikes in three areas, including Karaj, west of Tehran.
Tehran has responded to the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran by hitting Israel and Gulf Arab states hosting U.S. military installations. Israel has launched fresh attacks in Lebanon after the Iran-aligned militia Hezbollah fired across the border.
With the conflict spreading, Israel warned Lebanon of a "very heavy price" ‚Äãif it did not rein in Iran-allied Hezbollah militants, as it pounded the group's strongholds with airstrikes and mounted a deadly airborne raid in the east.
On Saturday morning, ‚Äãmore buildings in Beirut's Hezbollah-controlled southern ‚Å†suburbs had been reduced to rubble, dust and tangled wires, Reuters video showed.
The death toll from Israel's attacks on Lebanon since Monday rose to around 300, after at least four people were killed when an Israeli strike hit an apartment in the Ramada hotel building in central Beirut, Lebanon's health ministry said. It was the first strike to hit the heart of the capital since Israel-Hezbollah hostilities resumed last week.
Iran's apparent strategy of maximum chaos has driven up the costs of the conflict by raising energy ‚Å†prices and hurting ‚Äãglobal business and logistics links.
Kuwait's national oil company began cutting output on Saturday, adding to earlier oil and gas cuts from Iraq and Qatar.
The ‚Äãwar has roiled global markets and oil prices have hit multi-year highs with the Strait of Hormuz effectively shut.
Hardline clerics have called for the swift selection of a new supreme leader, Iranian media reported on Saturday, with meetings occurring as soon as Sunday.

item.title: Australia weighing requests for assistance from countries attacked by Iran
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 8 (Reuters) - Australia's government said on Sunday it was considering requests to help protect countries attacked by Iran in ‚Äãthe widening Middle East conflict, but reiterated it would ‚Äånot take part in any military operations in Iran.
"We've had many countries, which are non participants, (that) have been attacked by Iran through this. ‚ÄãYou would anticipate as a consequence that we have ‚Äãbeen asked for assistance, and we will work ‚Å†through that carefully," Foreign Minister Penny Wong said in televised ‚Äãremarks.
Australia, a close U.S. ally, has said it will not deploy ‚Äãtroops to the Middle East if the conflict escalates.
Asked if Australia could help protect nations from Iranian drone and missile attacks, Wong said "correct".
"We will ‚Äãwork through that in accordance with the position that ‚ÄãI have outlined, which is we are not participating in offensive action ‚Äåagainst ‚Å†Iran and we have made clear we will not participate in any ground troop deployment," Wong added.
Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said on Friday that three Australian defence personnel were on board ‚Äãa U.S. ‚Äãsubmarine that sank ‚Å†an Iranian warship with a torpedo in the Indian Ocean, but stressed that they did not ‚Äãtake part in the attack.
The conflict in the ‚ÄãMiddle ‚Å†East has escalated this week, with U.S. and Israeli jets striking areas across Iran, and many Gulf cities - including in the ‚Å†UAE ‚Äãand Qatar - coming under Iranian bombardment.
Australia ‚Äãis currently working with airlines to help thousands of Australians stranded in the ‚ÄãMiddle East.

item.title: Families of flight MH370 passengers urge Malaysia to extend search
item.description:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 8 (Reuters) - Families of those aboard Malaysia Airlines flight MH370 on Sunday urged the Malaysian government to extend a contract it signed with deep-sea exploration firm ‚ÄãOcean Infinity to continue a search for the aircraft that disappeared 12 ‚Äåyears ago.
The Boeing 777 was carrying 227 passengers and 12 crew when it vanished en route from Kuala Lumpur to Beijing on March 8, 2014, becoming one of the world‚Äôs enduring ‚Äãaviation mysteries.
Multiple search operations for the plane have been conducted in the ‚Äãsouthern Indian Ocean since then but all have proved fruitless.
Malaysia in ‚Å†March last year agreed to allow Ocean Infinity to resume the hunt under a ‚Äúno ‚Äãfind, no fee‚Äù principle, with the firm to be paid $70 million only if the ‚Äãwreckage was successfully located.
Malaysia‚Äôs Air Accident Investigation Bureau said on Sunday, however, that operations had not yielded any findings so far, after two search phases covering 28 days and around 7,571 square ‚Äãkilometres (2,923 square miles) of seabed.
Operations were periodically disrupted by weather and sea conditions, ‚Äãwith the second phase ending on January 23, the AAIB said.
‚ÄúThe government remains committed to keeping the ‚Äåfamilies ‚Å†informed and will continue to provide updates as appropriate,‚Äù it said.
Voice370, a group representing families of those onboard, said it was unlikely for Ocean Infinity to resume the search before its contract ends in June, due to the coming winter months ‚Äãin the southern hemisphere ‚Äãand deteriorating sea ‚Å†conditions.
It urged the government to grant any request for Ocean Infinity to extend its agreement, as well as expand the ‚Äãsame terms to other interested exploration firms.
‚ÄúA simple addendum extending ‚Äãthe contract ‚Å†period without altering the core terms of the agreement would allow the search to continue without delay,‚Äù it said.
Ocean Infinity had conducted prior searches for the plane but failed ‚Å†to ‚Äãfind substantive wreckage.
Malaysian investigators in a 2018 report drew ‚Äãno conclusion about what happened aboard the flight, but did not rule out the possibility that the ‚Äãaircraft had been deliberately taken off course.

item.title: Majority consensus reached on Iran's next supreme leader, Mehr news reports
item.description:
DUBAI, March 8 (Reuters) - A majority consensus over a ‚Äãsuccessor to Iran's slain ‚ÄåSupreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei has more ‚Äãor less been ‚Äãreached, Assembly of Experts ‚Å†member Ayatollah Mohammadmehdi Mirbaqeri ‚Äãsaid on Sunday, ‚Äãaccording to Mehr news agency.
He said, though, that "some obstacles" ‚Äãneed to be ‚Äãresolved regarding the process, according ‚Äåto ‚Å†the report.
Iranian media said the body tasked with appointing Iran's supreme ‚Äãleader ‚Äãhad ‚Å†a minor disagreement over whether their ‚Äãfinal decision must ‚Äãfollow ‚Å†an in-person meeting or instead be issued ‚Å†without ‚Äãadhering to ‚Äãthis formality.

item.title: BHP Australia boss in running to lead Woodside, Bloomberg reports
item.description:
PERTH/MELBOURNE, March 8 (Reuters) - Global miner BHP‚Äôs (BHP.AX) , opens new tab Australia President Geraldine Slattery is among the ‚Äãcontenders for the top job at ‚ÄåWoodside Energy (WDS.AX) , opens new tab , Bloomberg reported on Friday, citing people familiar with the situation.
Woodside is expected ‚Äãto name a replacement for ‚ÄãMeg O‚ÄôNeill soon, after she unexpectedly left ‚Å†the company in December to lead ‚ÄãBP (BP.L) , opens new tab , a role she formally begins in April .
‚ÄúAs ‚Äãhas been disclosed, Woodside‚Äôs Board intends to announce a permanent CEO appointment in the first ‚Äãquarter of 2026. We do ‚Äãnot comment on market speculation,‚Äù a Woodside spokeswoman ‚Äåsaid, when ‚Å†asked whether Slattery had been formally interviewed for the role.
Acting CEO Liz Westcott and two other internal candidates are ‚Äãalso in ‚Äãthe ‚Å†running for the CEO job, said MST Marquee analyst Saul ‚ÄãKavonic.
BHP declined to comment and ‚ÄãSlattery ‚Å†did not respond to a request for comment at the time of writing.
Slattery ‚Å†headed ‚ÄãBHP's petroleum division before ‚Äãit was acquired by Woodside.

item.title: Two Kuwait fire officers killed 'while performing duties,' Fire Force says
item.description:
CAIRO, March 8 (Reuters) - Kuwait's Fire Force ‚Äãsaid on ‚ÄåSunday that two of its ‚Äãofficers were ‚Äãkilled "while performing duties," according ‚Å†to a ‚Äãpost on ‚ÄãX.
It did not provide details on ‚Äãthe circumstances ‚Äãof their deaths, but ‚ÄåKuwait ‚Å†has been intercepting drones and missiles launched ‚Äãfrom ‚ÄãIran ‚Å†following U.S. and Israeli ‚Äãattacks ‚Äãon ‚Å†the Islamic Republic.

item.title: Trump praises Italy PM Meloni's willingness to help in U.S.-Israel war with Iran
item.description:
ROME, March 8 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump praised Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni ‚Äãand said she was willing to ‚Äåhelp the United States and Israel in their war with Iran, Italy‚Äôs Corriere della ‚ÄãSera reported on Sunday.
Speaking to ‚Äãthe newspaper in a telephone call ‚Å†on Saturday, Trump described Meloni as ‚Äúa ‚Äãgreat leader‚Äù and said Italy was ‚Äãdoing what it could to assist.
* "I love Italy, I think she is a great leader," ‚ÄãTrump was quoted as saying of ‚ÄãMeloni.
* "She always tries to help, she is an ‚Äåexcellent ‚Å†leader and she is a friend of mine," Trump said, according to Corriere della Sera.
* Italy is planning to ‚Äãsend air-defence ‚Äãaid to ‚Å†Gulf countries in the face of Iranian air strikes, ‚ÄãMeloni said on Thursday.
* An Italian ‚Äãnavy ‚Å†vessel is preparing to sail to Cyprus, a navy spokesperson said on Friday, ‚Å†as ‚Äãpart of a joint ‚ÄãEuropean mission to protect the island after it ‚Äãcame under Iranian fire.

item.title: Israeli military says it will pursue every successor of Iran's Khamenei
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - The Israeli ‚Äãmilitary warned it would ‚Äåcontinue pursuing every successor of Iran's slain Supreme Leader Ayatollah ‚ÄãAli Khamenei, who was ‚Äãkilled in U.S. and ‚Å†Israeli strikes on Iran.
In ‚Äãa post on ‚ÄãX in Farsi, the Israeli military also warned it would pursue every ‚Äãperson who seeks ‚Äãto appoint a successor for ‚ÄåKhamenei, ‚Å†referring to the clerical body charged with choosing the Islamic Republic's ‚Äãsupreme ‚Äãleader.
The ‚Å†warning comes as the clerical body ‚Äãis set to ‚Äãmeet ‚Å†to choose Iran's next supreme leader.

item.title: Explainer: Who might succeed in Iran's theocratic system of power?
item.description:
March 8 - Iranian clerics involved in choosing a successor to Ayatollah Ali Khamenei after his killing a week ago in U.S.-Israeli strikes say they are close to naming the Islamic Republic's new supreme leader.
Iran's revolutionary theocracy has never been in greater jeopardy , and with the clerical body tasked with naming a new leader ready for an announcement as soon as Sunday, it is hard to predict what might happen next.
Israel and the United States ‚Äãhave vowed no let up in their war, promising to kill whoever replaces Khamenei and even those involved in selecting the new leader - a group that may include the clerics who formally make the choice ‚Äåand the Revolutionary Guardsmen and political insiders who influence them.
The following explains how power is meant to operate in the Islamic Republic, how a new supreme leader can be chosen, some of the main candidates, and how the U.S. and Israeli attacks have changed the equation:
Iran's theocratic system dates to the 1979 revolution that ousted the Shah. Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, the leader of the revolution, introduced a new system of rule: vilayat-e faqih, or guardianship of the Islamic jurist.
The theory holds that until the return of the Shi'ite Muslim 12th Imam, who disappeared in the ninth century, power on earth ‚Äãshould be wielded by a venerable cleric.
It means whoever takes over as supreme leader, empowered by the constitution as the ultimate authority guiding the elected president and parliament, will have to be a senior cleric.
Under Khomeini, who died ‚Äãin 1989, and Khamenei, who has ruled since then, the supreme leader has had the last say in all matters of state. But any new leader will have to assert his authority at ‚Å†a moment of enormous rupture - and may struggle to do so.
The constitution says a new leader must be chosen within three months. Until then President Masoud Pezeshkian, Guardians Council member Ayatollah Alireza Arafi and Judiciary chief Ayatollah Gholamhossein ‚ÄãMohseni-Ejei have taken charge as a temporary leadership council.
Choosing a new leader is meant to be the responsibility of the Assembly of Experts, a body of around 90 senior clerics who are elected every eight years, many of them very elderly.
With strikes continuing, they have been ‚Äãconducting their consultations online, Iranian officials have told Reuters, and appear close to naming a new leader.
Khamenei never publicly tapped a preferred successor and in practice the decision probably rests with the most senior figures in the Islamic Republic who have wielded power under Khamenei for many years. It is their preferred candidate who is most likely to be approved by the assembly.
Among the most important of those senior figures is Khamenei's veteran adviser Ali Larijani , often seen as Iran's foremost powerbroker. The Guards will also have a critical backroom voice in the process.
Khamenei's son, Mojtaba Khamenei , is widely seen as the most ‚Äãlikely candidate after surviving the first salvo of strikes, which killed his wife. Although Iran's ruling ideology frowns on the principle of hereditary succession, he has a powerful following within the Guards and his dead father's still-influential office.
The grandson of the revolutionary founder Ruhollah ‚ÄãKhomeini is another possible choice. Hassan Khomeini is closely associated with the reformist faction that has for decades tried to moderate the Islamic Republic's stance and may be seen as better able to assuage Western enmity and calm the fury of an embittered population.
Arafi and Mohseni-Ejei are less ‚Äåprominent possibilities who ‚Å†would likely continue Khamenei's hardline stance. Mohseni-Ejei was responsible for stamping out internal protests following a disputed election outcome in 2009 when he was intelligence minister.
Assembly of Experts members Ahmad Alamolhoda and Mohsen Araki are also hardline senior clerics with a close involvement in Iranian politics who might be considered. Former president Hassan Rouhani is a senior cleric but he was distrusted by some of the most powerful hardliners who would have great sway over the choice.
The assembly could theoretically pick an even lesser known ayatollah as leader. But the ruling system has been so fractured by the strikes that it would be much harder to buttress the position of a newcomer.
The Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps had long been expected to play a central role behind the ‚Äãscenes in determining Khamenei's successor. Unlike the ordinary military, which ‚Äãcomes under the elected president, the guards answer only to ‚Å†the supreme leader.
But its top echelons have been hollowed out by U.S. and Israeli strikes over recent years and it is far from clear how far it will still be able to influence the decision.
The most important Guards leader of recent times was Qassem Soleimani, head of the elite Quds Force unit that spearheaded Iran's regional strategy of exporting the revolution through affiliated Shi'ite militias ‚Äãin Arab countries. He was killed by a U.S. strike in 2020.
During the brief summer war last year Israeli strikes killed other top Guards commanders. And the latest strikes killed its ‚Äãlatest top commander Mohammed Pakpour, three ‚Å†sources familiar with the matter said.
The Basij militia, a part-time paramilitary force under Guards control, is often used to quell protests inside Iran, giving the corps a formidable role in internal control.
