Other countries' stock markets have ‚Äãbeen rocked. Benchmark European, Asian and emerging market indices, which fell 5-7% last week, are putting up more big losses. Japan's Nikkei is down another 5% today, with Korea's benchmark index falling even further.
Granted, many of these countries are net energy importers, meaning they are more exposed to the historic spikes in oil and natural gas prices than the Unite States. Japan, for example, imports 90% of its energy, with 95% of its oil imports coming from the Middle East.
But can Wall ‚ÄãStreet remain an outlier?
From a U.S. macroeconomic perspective, the Middle East conflict couldn't be more ill-timed. Inflation was already running at 3%, well above the Federal Reserve's 2% target and creeping higher, and payrolls ‚Äãdata on Friday showed that the U.S. economy lost 92,000 jobs in February.
The whiff of stagflation is growing stronger, and Fed officials can only hold their noses for so long. Chair Jerome Powell, and his likely successor Kevin Warsh, ‚Äåmay be ‚Å†damned by Wall Street ‚Äì or, more likely, bond investors ‚Äì if they risk letting inflation get out of control by taking a dovish stance moving forward. But they also risk angering markets if they take a hawkish, anti-growth position that prioritizes price stability.
Yet policy paralysis can't be good either.
Investors appear to be banking that history repeats itself. In recent decades, most bouts of global geopolitical turmoil have sparked a few weeks of mild volatility, then a quick recovery, leaving everyone wondering what all the fuss was about.
Research by Parag Thatte and Binky Chadha at Deutsche Bank shows that geopolitical shocks have had an average negative impact of around 6-8% on U.S. ‚Äãequities over the subsequent three-week period, with markets ‚Äãrecovering those losses within another three weeks.
Larry Adam, ‚Å†chief investment officer at Raymond James, notes that since the 1990s, the S&P 500 has been higher on average one, three, six, and 12 months after geopolitical shocks.
JPMorgan analysts say the typical scenario around a major geopolitical event is a 5-6% drawdown in stocks, which is then clawed back within a few weeks.
"There is ‚Äãa tendency among macro strategists to dismiss geopolitics and oversimplify the response: just buy the dip," they wrote on Friday, adding that this rule of ‚Äãthumb has been true approximately ‚Å†80% of the time over the past 60 years.
"We think the current episode with the Iran invasion is indeed a buy-the-dip scenario," they added.
Yet the drawdowns appear to be getting shallower, and as of Friday, there was barely a dip to buy.
Are markets getting smarter and better able to look through headline noise now that computers are doing the trading? Maybe the playbook of past crises still applies. Or, has complacency set in, and it really is ‚Å†different this ‚Äãtime?
The jury is still out, but Wall Street futures are down early on Monday. So a verdict may be imminent.
(The opinions expressed ‚Äãhere are those of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for Reuters)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the ‚Äãbiggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: South Korea to impose fuel price cap to shield economy from energy shock
item.description:
SEOUL, March 9 (Reuters) - South Korean President Lee Jae Myung said on Monday that authorities would cap ‚Äådomestic fuel prices for the first time in nearly 30 years to contain a spike in prices after the conflict in the Middle East sent global crude prices sharply higher .
Speaking at an emergency meeting on the impact of the Middle East crisis, Lee said the ‚Äãgovernment would "swiftly introduce and boldly implement" a maximum price system on petroleum products "that have recently seen excessive price ‚Äãincreases".
The current crisis "is a significant burden on our economy, which is highly dependent on global ‚Å†trade and energy imports from the Middle East," Lee said in opening remarks.
He added that South Korea will ‚Äãalso look for sources of energy beyond supplies shipped via the Strait of Hormuz.
Presidential policy adviser Kim Yong-beom said after ‚Äãthe meeting that the industry ministry would proceed quickly so that the price capping system could be implemented as soon as this week.
The maximum price could be changed every two weeks, he said, noting South Korea had sufficient oil reserves to meet 208 days ‚Äãof consumption.
The amount of crude oil shipped to South Korea affected by the Strait of Hormuz blockade ‚Äãis around 1.7 million barrels per day, Kim said.
South Korea could secure 20 million barrels of crude stock it jointly stores with ‚Äåoil-producing ‚Å†countries if it exercises the right to buy, Kim said, as well as divert overseas production by the state-run Korea National Oil Corp for domestic use.
Kim said 14% of natural gas due to be imported this year is from the Middle East, with about 5 million tons of Qatari gas imports expected to be disrupted. Domestic supplies are unlikely ‚Äãto be hit, however, since ‚ÄãKorea Gas Corp and ‚Å†other players could import from alternative sources, he said.
When asked if a supplementary budget might be drawn up in case the Middle East crisis is prolonged, Kim said: "If any ‚Äãadditional financial resources are needed... we have to seriously think about it."
Earlier on Monday, ‚ÄãPresident Lee said a ‚Å†100 trillion won ($66.94 billion) market stabilisation programme should be expanded if needed, and called on the government and the central bank to prepare additional measures to respond to the volatility of the financial and foreign exchange markets.
The benchmark KOSPI (.KS11) , opens new tab closed 6% ‚Å†lower on ‚ÄãMonday, after falling as much as 9% to trigger circuit breakers ‚Äãfor a second time this month. The won weakened nearly 1% to trade near a psychological barrier of 1,500 per dollar, while the benchmark ‚Äãbond yield hit more than two-year highs.
($1 = 1,493.9000 won)

item.title: NATO drills for war in Arctic with focus on civilians being ready
item.description:
OSLO, March 9 (Reuters) - NATO began its biennial drills in the Arctic on Monday, this ‚Äãtime placing more emphasis on the role of civilians in supporting the military, at ‚Äåa time of high tension over U.S. President Trump's push to take over Greenland from fellow NATO member Denmark.
The drills, named Cold Response, focus on defending the alliance in the European Arctic, where NATO-members Norway and Finland share a border with ‚ÄãRussia, and will run March 9-19 this time.
The exercise has now become part of Arctic Sentry , ‚Äãthe NATO mission to strengthen its presence in the polar region that was launched ‚Å†to defuse tensions with Trump over Greenland.
Trump insists the U.S. needs Greenland to fend off threats from ‚ÄãRussian and Chinese interests in the Arctic, and that Denmark cannot ensure its security. The governments of ‚Äãboth Denmark and Greenland say the island is not for sale.
This year, some 25,000 troops from some 14 nations - including the U.S. and Denmark - will take part in Cold Response, mostly across northern Norway and Finland.
The U.S. is expected to ‚Äãhave some 4,000 troops present.
Ahead of the exercise, the U.S. military withdrew one squadron of F-35 fighter ‚Äãjets from the exercise. It declined to say whether the war in the Middle East played a role in ‚Äåthe decision.
"The ‚Å†U.S. military is a globally deployed force and it is not abnormal for forces to be dynamically re-tasked or reallocated for a host of reasons," a spokesperson for the U.S. Marine Corps Forces Europe told Reuters.
Norway has declared 2026 the year of "total defence", which puts emphasis on boosting the ‚Äãreadiness of civilians, businesses ‚Äãand public institutions to ‚Å†cope with war and other catastrophes, the latest move by a Nordic nation to boost civilian preparedness.
"We want our military to do its job of defending ‚Äãthe country. To do that, we are completely reliant on most aspects of ‚Äãsociety functioning ‚Å†as normal," Major-General Lars Lervik, head of the Norwegian Army, told Reuters.
"This is also an opportunity to rehearse specifically where civilians can give direct support to the military effort, for instance with the health service treating a ‚Å†higher ‚Äãnumber of injured soldiers, Norwegian or from allied forces, than usual."
On ‚ÄãThursday, the military will run a scenario testing the ability of hospitals in northern Norway to treat a high number of ‚Äãcasualties being transported from an imaginary frontline in Finland.

item.title: Tehran threatens to confiscate property of Iranians abroad who back attacks on Iran
item.description:
DUBAI, March 9 (Reuters) - Iranians living abroad could have property confiscated and face other legal penalties if ‚Äãthey express support for the United States and ‚ÄåIsrael, the Iranian prosecutor general's office said on Monday.
Some members of the Iranian diaspora who want political change ‚Äãin Tehran took to the streets of ‚ÄãEuropean and American cities to celebrate the ‚Å†killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei ‚Äãin the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran. Iran on Monday named ‚ÄãMojtaba Khamenei to succeed his father.
"A warning has been issued to those Iranians living abroad who in different ways ‚Äãsympathise, support or cooperate with the American-Zionist (Israeli) ‚Äãenemy," the prosecutor general's office was quoted as saying by ‚Äåstate ‚Å†media.
"They will be met with the confiscation of all their properties and other legal penalties in accordance with the law."
Newly established channels on Telegram ‚Äãhave shared details ‚Äãof prominent ‚Å†Iranians living abroad who have posted comments critical of Iran's clerical authorities ‚Äãand supportive of the U.S.-Israeli military campaign ‚Äãthat ‚Å†began on February 28.
Up to 5 million Iranians live abroad, the majority of them in the United ‚Å†States ‚Äãand Western Europe, according to ‚ÄãIranian government data. Iranian media put their numbers closer to ‚Äã10 million.

item.title: Guinea dissolves 40 political parties, opposition leader cries foul
item.description:
DAKAR, March 9 (Reuters) - Guinea's main opposition leader said "direct resistance" to the country's coup leader-turned-president was now the only way to bring about change ‚Äãafter the government consolidated its position by dissolving 40 political ‚Äåparties.
President Mamady Doumbouya, a former special forces commander, seized power in 2021 and won a seven-year term as president in December in a result contested by his opponents. Legislative elections ‚Äãare expected in May in the West African nation, which is ‚Äãrich in bauxite and iron ore.
Guinea's Ministry of Territorial Administration ‚Å†and Decentralization said in a decree late on Friday that 40 parties' ‚Äãheadquarters and local offices would be closed and use of their logos, ‚Äãacronyms "and other distinctive signs" would be prohibited.
The decree said the parties had failed to meet legal obligations such as filing financial statements; several of the dissolved political parties have ‚Äãprotested, maintaining they had fulfilled all legal requirements.
In a video statement published ‚Äãon social media on Sunday, Guinea's main opposition leader Cellou Dalein Diallo said "war has been ‚Äåopenly ‚Å†declared" on Doumbouya's challengers.
He called on Guineans to engage in "direct resistance", without specifying what exactly that should look like. And he said political change would not happen through dialogue or democratic processes.
"The head of the junta and ‚Äãhis malevolent clique want ‚Äãto rewrite the ‚Å†country's history by erasing from the political landscape all forces likely to overshadow his nascent one-party state," he ‚Äãsaid.
A government spokesperson did not respond to a request for ‚Äãcomment.
Diallo is ‚Å†in exile outside the country, as is former President Alpha Conde, who Doumbouya toppled and whose party was also dissolved on Friday.
Guinea has a history ‚Å†of political ‚Äãviolence, including during the 2020 election which ‚ÄãDiallo said he had won. The December 2025 election was tightly controlled and unfolded without major ‚Äãsecurity incidents.

item.title: At least 14 dead after migrant boat crashes into coast guard vessel off southern Turkey
item.description:
ANKARA, March 9 (Reuters) - At least 14 people were killed when ‚Äãan inflatable boat carrying migrants ‚Äåcrashed into a Turkish coast guard vessel off Turkey's southern ‚Äãprovince of Antalya on ‚ÄãMonday, the coast guard said ‚Å†in a statement.
The coast guard ‚Äãsaid the high-speed boat was ‚Äãdetected early on Monday off the coast of the Finike district ‚Äãof Antalya and that ‚Äãit tried to flee from the coast ‚Äåguard ‚Å†despite repeated warnings to stop. It later collided with a coast guard vessel, the ‚Äãstatement ‚Äãadded.
It said ‚Å†six migrants and one Turkish national were ‚Äãrescued, but that 14 ‚Äãwere ‚Å†found dead and 15 others were caught after continuing ‚Å†in ‚Äãtheir boat until ‚Äãthey reached land.

item.title: EU can no longer rely on 'rules-based' system against threats, von der Leyen says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 9 (Reuters) - The EU must be prepared to project its power more assertively as ‚Äãit can no longer rely on a "rules-based" ‚Äåsystem against threats and must determine if its institutions and systems help or hinder its credibility, ‚ÄãEuropean Commission President Ursula von der ‚ÄãLeyen said on Monday.
"We will always defend ‚Å†and uphold the rules-based system that we ‚Äãhelped to build with our allies, but ‚Äãwe can no longer rely on it as the only way to defend our interests or assume its ‚Äãrules will shelter us from the ‚Äãcomplex threats that we face," von der Leyen said ‚Äåat ‚Å†a conference for EU ambassadors.
"We urgently need to reflect on whether our doctrine, our institutions and our decision making ‚Äì all designed in ‚Äãa postwar ‚Äãworld of ‚Å†stability and multilateralism ‚Äì have kept pace with the speed of change ‚Äãaround us. Whether the system that ‚Äãwe ‚Å†built ‚Äì with all of its well-intentioned attempts at consensus and compromise ‚Äì is more a help ‚Å†or ‚Äãa hindrance to our credibility ‚Äãas a geopolitical actor," she added.

item.title: Middle East conflict could spur palm oil demand from biodiesel sector
item.description:
MUMBAI/KUALA LUMPUR, March 9 (Reuters) - Rising crude oil prices and higher freight rates driven by the Middle East conflict could boost demand for palm oil ‚Äãfrom the biodiesel sector and for food use, as Asian buyers seek prompt ‚Äåshipments, industry officials told Reuters.
Indonesia and Malaysia's output rose to a record high in 2025, swelling stocks and weighing on prices. But the conflict has suddenly made palm oil attractive to the ‚Äãbiodiesel industry, pushing prices to their highest level in more than a year.
"Palm oil ‚Äãis now trading at a steep discount to gasoil, with the ‚Å†current spread lucrative enough to boost demand from the biodiesel industry," said Anilkumar Bagani, ‚Äãresearch head of Mumbai-based vegetable oil broker Sunvin Group.
Oil prices surged more than 25% on Monday ‚Äãto their highest levels since mid-2022 as some major producers cut supplies and fears of prolonged shipping disruptions gripped the market.
Indonesia, the world's largest user of palm oil-based biodiesel, said it may revive plans ‚Äãto roll out a B50 grade of palm oil biodiesel mid-year to counter surging ‚Äãcrude oil prices.
In January, Jakarta shelved the plan to produce B50, an equal blend of palm ‚Äåoil biodiesel ‚Å†and conventional diesel, citing technical and funding challenges and continued with the B40 mandate instead.
A long-term policy shift from countries such as Indonesia is likely only if palm oil trades at a consistent discount to gasoil over an extended period, said Bagani.
Used in ‚Äãeverything from cakes and ‚Äãfrying fats to ‚Å†cosmetics and cleaning products, palm oil makes up more than half of global vegetable oil shipments and is especially popular among ‚Äãconsumers in emerging markets, led by India.
Southeast Asia is well positioned ‚Äãto consistently ‚Å†supply palm oil to buyers in Asia, the Middle East, and Europe, said Carl Bek-Nielsen, vice chairman and chief executive director at United Plantations Berhad.
Palm oil supplies remain ample and ‚Å†can ‚Äãbe shipped quickly to Asian buyers, but it has ‚Äãnow become more expensive than rival soyoil, which could limit gains in demand, said a New Delhi-based dealer ‚Äãwith a global trade house.

item.title: Indonesia may revive B50 biodiesel mix plan as oil prices soar
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 9 (Reuters) - Indonesia may revive a plan to launch a mandatory B50 grade of palm oil-based biodiesel in the middle of this year because of ‚Äãsurging crude oil prices due to the conflict in the ‚ÄåMiddle East, deputy energy minister Yuliot Tanjung said.
No decision has been made yet by Indonesia, the world's largest palm oil producer, Yuliot added in comments sent to Reuters over the ‚Äãweekend.
In January, authorities scrapped a plan to launch B50 - a blend of ‚Äã50% palm oil-based biodiesel and 50% conventional diesel - this year ‚Å†due to technical and funding concerns, instead sticking with a B40 blend.
In light ‚Äãof the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, however, the government is now looking at ‚Äãtwo scenarios, Yuliot said.
"B50 might be implemented in the second semester or even earlier...But for now the steering committee's decision for B40 until the end of 2026 still stands," ‚Äãhe said, adding that authorities were monitoring price movements in real time.
The steering ‚Äãcommittee consists of several ministries which determine biodiesel policy, led by chief economic minister, Airlangga Hartarto.
Indonesia's ‚Äåbiodiesel ‚Å†mandate often affects global palm oil prices as increased domestic use reduces available exports.
Oil prices surged on Monday to above $100 per barrel . Palm oil prices also rallied on expectations that rising crude prices will boost demand for biodiesel feedstocks.
Yuliot ‚Äãdid not respond to ‚Äãa request for ‚Å†further comment on Monday. Energy minister Bahlil Lahadalia said the government may accelerate its biofuel programmes, which include B50 ‚Äãand a plan to require a certain blend of bioethanol ‚Äãwith gasoline, ‚Å†according to local media reports.
Asked about potential implementation of B50 this year, Airlangga told Reuters that authorities were monitoring the impact of the conflict on biodiesel ‚Å†policy.
Finance minister ‚ÄãPurbaya Yudhi Sadewa said Indonesia is prepared ‚Äãto raise the allocation for fuel subsidies to absorb the shock from rising global oil prices.

item.title: Norway police search for suspect after U.S. embassy blast
item.description:
OSLO, March 9 (Reuters) - Norwegian ‚Äãpolice are searching for ‚Äåa suspect seen on surveillance footage following an explosion ‚Äãduring the weekend ‚Äãat the U.S. embassy in ‚Å†Oslo, investigators said ‚Äãon Monday.
The early Sunday morning ‚Äãblast damaged one of the entrances to the embassy, sending ‚Äãthick smoke into ‚Äãthe street but causing no injuries, ‚Äåin ‚Å†what police said may have been linked to the crisis in the Middle East.
"The ‚Äãinvestigation ‚Äãso ‚Å†far has shown that this was an ‚Äãimprovised explosive device (IED)," ‚Äãthe ‚Å†Oslo police said in a statement.
Norway's government has ‚Å†promised ‚Äãa thorough investigation ‚Äãof the incident.

item.title: King Charles' trip to US should be cancelled over Trump comments, Lib Dem leader says
item.description:
LONDON, March 9 (Reuters) - A planned visit by King Charles to the United States should be cancelled after President Donald Trump repeatedly insulted Britain, the leader of the country's ‚Äãthird-biggest party in parliament said on Monday.
Trump has repeatedly criticised Prime Minister Keir ‚ÄåStarmer after London initially blocked the U.S. from using British bases to attack Iran , saying he was "not Winston Churchill" and that Starmer had helped to "ruin" the so-called Special Relationship between the two countries.
That followed ‚ÄãTrump's comments in January that European troops had stayed off the front lines ‚Äãin Afghanistan, drawing a rare rebuke from Starmer who described them ‚Å†as " frankly appalling " and insulting to the families of the 457 British service personnel who ‚Äãdied in that war.
Ed Davey, the leader of the Liberal Democrats, said Starmer should avoid ‚Äãputting the king, Britain's ceremonial head of state, in the centre of a "highly political situation" with an April visit.
"A state visit from our king would be seen as yet another huge diplomatic coup ‚Äãfor President Trump, so it should not be given to someone who repeatedly insults ‚Äãand damages our country," Davey told LBC Radio, citing Trump's recent comments and his "reckless" war in Iran.
In ‚ÄåBritain, ‚Å†the government advises the monarch on his relations with foreign leaders. The Starmer government has not been slow to harness the soft power of the royal family to help smooth relations with Trump over the last year.
Charles hosted Trump for an unprecedented second state visit to ‚ÄãBritain last September, treating ‚Äãhim to a ‚Å†carriage ride and state banquet at Windsor Castle.
Heir-to-the-throne Prince William is also due to visit the U.S. in the summer during the ‚Äãsoccer World Cup.
Davey said that while it was clear Trump ‚Äãliked the ‚Å†royal family, he did not appear to treat Britain any better as a result, referring to his tariff policy.
Asked about Davey's remarks, senior minister Steve Reed said the king did a ‚Å†remarkable ‚Äãjob of representing Britain abroad and it was not ‚Äãfor him or Davey to comment on the arrangements.
Buckingham Palace does not comment on official trips before they ‚Äãhave been confirmed.

item.title: Hezbollah reports Israeli raid in east Lebanon as war enters second week
item.description:
BEIRUT/JERUSALEM, March 9 (Reuters) - Hezbollah said on Monday its fighters clashed with Israeli troops in eastern Lebanon during an Israeli airborne raid overnight, ‚Äãin what would be the second such operation in the area in recent days, as the war between ‚ÄåIsrael and the Iran-backed group entered a second week.
A spokesperson for the Israeli military had no immediate comment when asked about the Hezbollah statement.
A Hezbollah statement said its fighters "confronted the helicopters and the infiltrating forces with appropriate weapons".
Lebanon was pulled into the regional war when Hezbollah, founded by Iran's ‚ÄãRevolutionary Guards in 1982, opened fire to avenge the killing of Iran's former supreme leader, igniting a new Israeli ‚Äãoffensive against the group.
The Israeli military kept up its airstrikes in the Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs of ‚Å†Beirut on Monday, sending plumes of smoke across the city. It reiterated warnings to its residents to leave, and announced ‚Äãit would act against a Hezbollah financial institution, Al-Qard Al-Hassan.
Nearly 400 people have been killed in Lebanon by Israeli strikes since ‚ÄãMarch 2, Lebanon's health ministry reported on Sunday, including at least 83 children and 42 women. The toll does not otherwise distinguish between combatants and civilians.
Israel's military announced on Sunday that two Israeli soldiers had been killed in southern Lebanon, the first casualties in the Israeli army since ‚Äãthe war began.
In its statement, Hezbollah said its fighters had observed 15 helicopters flying over eastern Lebanon just after midnight, ‚Äãdropping Israeli troops who were observed approaching Lebanese territory from an area on the Syrian side of the border.
Lebanon's eastern Bekaa Valley region ‚Äåis one ‚Å†of the Shi'ite Muslim group's political and security strongholds.
The Israeli military carried out an airborne raid in the same area, near the village of Nabi Chit, overnight Friday-Saturday. Lebanon's health ministry said 41 people were killed in Israeli attacks in the Nabi Chit area on Saturday.
The Israeli military said that raid was an operation to seek the remains of Ron Arad, an Israeli ‚Äãair force navigator missing in ‚ÄãLebanon since 1986 but no ‚Å†findings related to him were recovered.
Hundreds of thousands of people in Lebanon have fled their homes since the start of the war, pouring out of the predominantly Shi'ite Muslim southern Lebanon, ‚Äãand the southern suburbs known as Dahiyeh.
Israel has also struck outside those areas.
On Sunday, an ‚ÄãIsraeli drone strike hit ‚Å†a hotel in Beirut's seafront Rouche district. The Israeli military said the strike killed five senior commanders of what it described as the Lebanon Corps of Iran's Revolutionary Guards Quds Force.
The Israeli military has sent more troops into southern Lebanon since the start of ‚Å†the war, ‚Äãestablishing what it described as forward defensive positions to guard against Hezbollah attacks ‚Äãinto Israel.
Israel had kept soldiers at five positions in southern Lebanon since a war with Hezbollah in 2024.

item.title: Prosecutors expand Swiss ski bar fire probe to include mayor, document shows
item.description:
GENEVA, March 9 (Reuters) - Swiss prosecutors have extended their investigation into a ‚Äåfatal ski bar fire that killed 41 people, mostly teenagers, and added the mayor of the ski town of Crans-Montana to the list of suspects, a document showed.
Mayor Nicolas ‚ÄãFeraud is named as a defendant in one of the prosecutors' ‚Äãdocuments seen by Reuters summoning him for questioning on April ‚Å†13. He did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Feraud, in ‚Äãhis 50s, has previously admitted that the municipality had missed multiple annual safety checks. "We ‚Äãare deeply sorry. We had no indication that the checks had not been done as requested," Feraud told reporters in January.
The Swiss prosecutor's office in the Valais canton confirmed ‚Äãto Reuters that new individuals had been placed under investigation in the ‚Äãcase involving suspected crimes including negligent homicide, without naming them.
The fire that tore through "Le Constellation" ‚Äåbar ‚Å†on January 1 was one of the worst disasters in modern Swiss history and has tested relations with neighbouring Italy, which lost six of its nationals in the blaze.
Many young people remain hospitalised with burn injuries.
The tragedy has also sent ‚Äãchills through the lucrative ‚Äãtourism sector. ‚Å†The affluent town of Crans-Montana is popular among French, Italian and American tourists and well known both for its ‚Äãsunny, south-facing ski slopes and its golf courses.
Prosecutors' inquiries initially ‚Äãfocused on ‚Å†the French bar owners who remain under investigation. The probe was expanded in late January to also include a current and a former local official.
Bar owners ‚Å†Jacques ‚Äãand Jessica Moretti have both voiced grief over ‚Äãthe fire and said they would cooperate with the probe.
If convicted, defendants could face a maximum ‚Äãpenalty of 4-1/2 years in prison.

item.title: Amid wave of kids‚Äô online safety laws, age-checking tech comes of age
item.description:
NEW YORK/STOCKHOLM/SYDNEY, March 9 (Reuters) - For years, tech companies successfully resisted pressure from child safety advocates to do more to keep kids off their services, claiming technical limitations would make any attempt to restrict access for teens impractical, overly broad or a security risk.
Now, a growing list of governments is concluding those hurdles are not insurmountable, and pushing ahead with aggressive new age-checking requirements for social networks, AI chatbots ‚Äãand porn purveyors alike.
Three months after Australia launched a landmark ban on teen social media accounts, regulators across Europe , in Brazil and in a handful of U.S. states are moving to emulate it . California Governor Gavin Newsom - seen as a likely Democratic candidate for president in 2028 - joined the ‚Äåcall , opens new tab last month, while Republican President Donald Trump is also reportedly ‚Äútaking an interest‚Äù , opens new tab in age limits, according to his daughter-in-law.
Spurring them along are escalating concerns over online abuse and teen mental health , and a recent outcry over the spread of AI-generated child sexual images , as well as increased confidence in the capabilities of "age assurance" software that backers say can suss out a person's approximate age using facial analysis, parental approval, ID checks and other digital clues.
Recent advancements in artificial intelligence have boosted the effectiveness and slashed the cost of those age-gating tools, according to Reuters interviews with more than a dozen regulators, child safety advocates, independent researchers and vendors who perform the age checks for big tech companies, including TikTok, Facebook owner Meta (META.O) , opens new tab and OpenAI.
‚ÄúThe age-assurance market ‚Äãhas matured a lot in the last couple of years,‚Äù said Ariel Fox Johnson, a senior adviser to San Francisco-based Common Sense Media, a children‚Äôs online advocacy group. She pointed to improving technology, as well as the establishment of trade groups, technical protocols and certification schemes standardizing evaluation ‚Äãof the various tools‚Äô effectiveness.
Social media companies now can often confidently guess a person‚Äôs age group using digital breadcrumbs like the year an account was established or the type of content it views, they said, while ‚Å†a burgeoning industry of age assurance vendors like Yoti, k-ID and Persona offer additional layers of checks via automated tools like face scans and machine-based analysis of government IDs.
At the app-store level, too, Apple and Alphabet‚Äôs (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab Google have rolled out tools that allow parents to indicate their child‚Äôs age range to app developers.
‚ÄúThe tech ‚Äãdefinitely has gotten better, not just for age verification specifically but for overall identity verification,‚Äù said Merritt Maxim, a vice president at Massachusetts-based research firm Forrester. ‚ÄúThat, in turn, has driven down the average cost of verification, so that where you were using it five years ago only for higher-value types of transactions, now you ‚Äãcan use it for pretty much anything without a significant financial impact.‚Äù
Vendors generally charge well under $1 per check for basic machine-only age assurance tools, though for large volumes the price is often as low as single-digit cents, said industry executives. More costly traditional processes like human confirmation and triangulation of personal data that were standard a decade ago are still available at a premium, but are needed less frequently, the executives said.
Independent evaluations back up executives' descriptions of rapid progress. According to an ongoing study run by the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), face-scanning software from firms including Yoti ‚Äì which performs checks for TikTok and Meta‚Äôs (META.O) , opens new tab Facebook, Instagram and Threads ‚Äì were off in their age estimations by an average of ‚Äã4.1 years as of initial testing in 2014, while by 2024 that average had dropped to 3.1 years, and is currently 2.5 years.
UK-based Yoti said the performance of its latest face analysis model due out in April surpasses that of models it submitted for the NIST and Australian studies, with an ‚Äãaverage error of only 1.04 years for kids in regulators‚Äô target age range of 14 to 18. Persona, a San Francisco-based identity verification firm used by OpenAI and Reddit (RDDT.N) , opens new tab , touts a similar average error of 1.77 years for the 13-to-17-year-old age range.
A report commissioned by the Australian government likewise determined last year that photo-based age estimation products were ‚Äåbroadly accurate, although it ‚Å†acknowledged that users within three years of the law‚Äôs age cutoff of 16 were in a ‚Äúgrey zone where system uncertainty is higher‚Äù and recommended they be diverted to ‚Äúsupplementary assurance methods, such as ID-based verification or parental consent."
The systems also struggle more with certain skin types, with grainier imagery captured by older phones and when using privacy-protective ‚Äúon-device‚Äù data processing, which entails performing a check entirely on the person's phone without sending their data out to a cloud server, executives said.
For instance, systems using on-device processing are less likely to catch attempts by enterprising youngsters to appear older than they are, said Rick Song, CEO of San Francisco-based Persona. Common tricks used by teens include donning masks, applying heavy makeup or fake facial hair, or scanning the plastic faces of action figures instead of their own, he said.
Still, said executives, facial age estimation can provide a digital version of the kind of screening performed daily at bars and liquor stores in the offline world.
"If you look ‚Äãyoung, you can be challenged, and you may have to provide your ‚ÄãID," said Robin Tombs, CEO of London-based Yoti.
He added that social media ‚Å†services generally require fewer face scans and ID checks than porn or gambling sites because they already have reams of personal information on their users. This means they can lean more on an age assurance method called ‚Äúinference‚Äù ‚Äî involving analysis of online activities, connected financial information and other signals ‚Äî to satisfy regulators‚Äô requirements.
The 10 social media companies included in Australia‚Äôs teen ban all declined Reuters requests for data on the effectiveness of their age assurance tools.
Australia‚Äôs internet regulator, the ‚ÄãeSafety commissioner, has said it will collect population data for two years to assess the ban's impact and publish first results later this year. Already, companies have locked 4.7 million suspected underage accounts since the law came ‚Äãinto effect in December, it said, although industry ‚Å†participants have told Reuters that some of the accounts were likely underage Google accounts that were prevented from logging in to YouTube, regardless of whether they were active.
Meta said it took down about 550,000 Instagram, Facebook and Threads accounts suspected to be underage in the first weeks of the Australian ban. Snapchat (SNAP.N) , opens new tab said it took down about 415,000.
Regulators elsewhere are watching carefully. European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen is set to discuss age verification during an upcoming visit to Canberra, according to a European lawmaker briefed on her agenda. The United Kingdom, which requires age verification for porn websites and is considering tightening child safety rules for social media and AI chatbots as ‚Å†well, is likewise swapping ‚Äãnotes with Australian counterparts.
Early results from the Australian experiment should be taken with a grain of salt, as companies affected by the ban generally were doing the bare minimum to comply with ‚Äãlegal requirements, said Iain Corby, the executive director of the Age Verification Providers Association, a trade association that represents about three dozen vendors including Yoti and Persona.
In some cases, he added, the social media companies asked AVPA member firms to turn off controls that make the age checks more robust.
‚ÄúThey are extremely worried this is going to be contagious and be a policy that is ‚Äãadopted around the world, so they are not really motivated for it to be a glowing success,‚Äù he said.
‚ÄúThey are testing the regulator‚Äôs patience to see what they can get away with.‚Äù

item.title: Republicans confront cooling support from young men over Trump policies
item.description:
MANCHESTER, New Hampshire, March 9 (Reuters) - In a college library lined with volumes on America's greatest presidents, six young men who voted for Donald Trump gathered to size up his second term. Their verdicts ranged from guarded approval to disappointment, reflecting growing tension in a demographic that helped propel Trump to victory in 2024.
While the students form a very small sample ‚Äãsize, their mixed reviews - including criticism of what several called overly harsh immigration enforcement and frustration over rising prices - mirror a broader shift in national polling that shows the Republican president is losing ground with young men.
Public opinion polling suggests that this ‚Äåsoftening of support, part of a broader unhappiness among Americans over Trump's policies, threatens Republicans' hopes of retaining their slim majorities in the Senate and House of Representatives.
Analysts who track the youth vote told Reuters many young men report feeling little tangible improvement in their economic prospects since Trump returned to office in January 2025.
Sitting with five classmates in the political library at Saint Anselm College in New Hampshire, 20-year-old sophomore Tyler Witzgall captured a sentiment common among his peers: The president, for all his accomplishments, has fallen short on issues shaping their daily lives, like high prices.
"I'm still going to graduate and be in an enormous amount of debt. I won‚Äôt be able to buy a home for a ‚Äãwhile," Witzgall said, grading Trump a C or C-plus, describing his disappointment in the president's efforts to tackle domestic economic issues.
None of the students said they regretted their vote, viewing the 2024 Democratic presidential nominee, then Vice President Kamala Harris, as a continuation of a ‚Äãpresidency they associated with unchecked immigration and spiraling costs.
Yet even solid Trump backers like Ian Pomfret, a 20-year-old sophomore, joined others in the student panel criticizing what he describes as overly aggressive tactics used by Immigration and ‚Å†Customs Enforcement agents implementing Trump's crackdown.
"The ICE thing is a huge problem," Pomfret said, pointing to the two U.S. citizens killed by federal agents in Minneapolis. "I feel like there is a better way of going about it than raiding and killing and instant deportation."
Pomfret still broadly supports Trump, grading him a ‚ÄãB-plus.
Last month, some 33% of men aged 18-29 approved of Trump's performance in the White House, down from 43% in February 2025, according to Reuters/Ipsos polling. Trump garnered the support of 46% of young males in the 2024 presidential election, up 7 points from 2020, according to exit polling analyzed by ‚ÄãPew Research.
A party strategist who works on House races pointed to a CBS News survey in February showing that 43% of young voters view Republican positions as "mainstream", and said the party could still secure midterm wins if it captures at least 40% of the youth vote.
Turnout among young voters is historically low in midterm elections, with only a quarter casting a ballot in 2022, half the overall rate.
Still, young voters can tip close races, as in 2018 when a wave of mass shootings spurred youth turnout and helped flip more than a dozen congressional seats to the Democrats, according to John Della Volpe, a pollster who specializes in the Gen Z vote.
"All evidence in the last 15 ‚Äãor 16 months is that this cohort cannot be counted on at this stage to be reliable Republican voters," Della Volpe said. "Mostly because they haven't felt tangible improvements in their day-to-day lives."
Trump‚Äôs pledges in his 2024 campaign to curb inflation, boost economic growth, and toughen immigration ‚Äãenforcement helped attract young males to his MAGA movement. A string of attention-grabbing moves, like attending a sneaker event, sitting down with popular podcasters like Joe Rogan and Theo Von and posting content on TikTok, also enhanced his standing with younger men, political analysts said.
Last month, the Republican National Committee, the party's governing body, launched ‚Äåits own account on ‚Å†TikTok, which is used by six in 10 Americans under 30, according to Pew Research.
White House spokesman Davis Ingle said no other president has done more for young men than Trump "who is working tirelessly to create jobs, cool inflation, increase housing affordability, and more."
John Brabender, a media consultant who helped the Trump campaign target young men in 2024, said without Trump on the ballot in November, Republican candidates will need to do a better job of talking about tax cuts and other policies that directly benefit someone in their 20s, rather than broad indicators like how the stock market is performing.
"I will be the first to say that collectively we've done a poor job of ... communicating directly to the hearts and minds of people at a much younger age," Brabender said.
Brabender's comments echo concerns being expressed by Republican lawmakers, party strategists and White House aides who say the president needs to give more priority to ‚Äãhis economic messaging and less to foreign policy. The economy is the top ‚Äãissue for voters, polls show.
The battle ‚Å†for the attention of young voters can be seen in the race for an open Senate seat in New Hampshire, one of four competitive seats in the chamber that Democrats must defend in November.
U.S. Representative Chris Pappas, the Democratic frontrunner, launched a TikTok account in October and has been working to build a following, with several posts addressing the high cost of housing, a key concern for young voters.
"If you ask someone in their 20s about owning ‚Äãa home, that seems like a far-off notion," Pappas said in an interview. "We've got to make that dream within reach of more people."
The Pappas campaign plans to host a series of events with online influencers to ‚Äãhelp engage with younger voters.
Former Senator Scott ‚Å†Brown, who is seeking the Republican nomination, told Reuters that his own hobbies - including playing guitar in a rock band and coaching high school basketball - help him connect with younger voters.
"It‚Äôs not a stretch for me to walk in and start playing hoops with somebody or get up on stage and jam with some teenagers," said Brown, adding that two staffers, ages 17 and 26, were "social media whizzes" and helping build his presence online.
Brown will speak this month at the New Hampshire Institute of Politics at Saint Anselm, a hub for political discussion in the early presidential primary state.
Students pass through the institute each day for classes, walking hallways ‚Å†decorated with photographs of ‚Äãpresidents who have visited and past rooms filled with more than half a century of political memorabilia, much of it from an era of more convivial bipartisanship.
Several of the students ‚Äãinterviewed by Reuters said they longed for a return to more civil discourse. While backing many of Trump's policies, they disliked his caustic style, in which he casts Democrats or other critics as the enemy. All said they were still likely to vote Republican in November.
Tyler Delaney, a 19-year-old freshman, said the divisiveness of Trump's approach will come back to bite him ‚Äãif the Democrats, as expected, take control of the House.
"It gets to a point where you need to have some bipartisanship, and I think that's gonna hit Trump hard after the midterms."

item.title: EU to sign defence partnerships with Australia, Iceland and Ghana, Kallas says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 9 (Reuters) - The EU will ‚Äãsign defence partnerships ‚Äåwith Australia, Iceland and Ghana in ‚Äãthe coming ‚Äãdays, EU foreign ‚Å†policy chief Kaja ‚ÄãKallas said ‚Äãon Monday.
"There are many other interested countries ‚Äãknocking at ‚Äãour door," Kallas added ‚Äåin ‚Å†a speech in Brussels.
"A growing number of countries ‚Äãaround ‚Äãthe ‚Å†globe are seeking to ‚Äãdiversify their ‚Äãpartnerships ‚Å†to manage the heightened risk."

item.title: UK talking to partners about limiting economic impact of Iran crisis, says PM
item.description:
LONDON, March 9 (Reuters) - The British government is talking to international partners, and its central bank, to assess ‚Äãways to limit the economic damage from the escalating Iranian ‚Äåcrisis, warning that the longer it goes on, the worse it could get.
British borrowing costs have soared since the conflict erupted more than a week ago - by more than those of ‚Äãother European countries and the United States - as investors fear that ‚Äãsurging oil and gas prices will stoke already stubborn inflation ‚Å†higher.
That also risks forcing the government to intervene again to cushion the economic ‚Äãblow, a potential huge challenge to an already unpopular government which has limited ‚Äãroom to increase spending.
Prime Minister Keir Starmer told an event in London on Monday that he wanted people to know that the government was working to deal with the ‚Äãfallout from the Iranian war.
"What we're doing is monitoring the risk, working with ‚Äãothers to mitigate the risk," he said.
"The chancellor is talking with the Bank of England ‚Äåevery ‚Å†day to make sure that we're ahead of that, on energy prices for households."
He also said the government was talking to international partners about how to reduce the likely impact on people in Britain, and said Britain wanted ‚Äãto seek a way ‚Äãto de-escalate the ‚Å†conflict.
Starmer said that he was acutely aware that people would be worried about their bills given the rising oil ‚Äãand gas price, but he noted that for consumers, an ‚Äãexisting energy ‚Å†price cap was in place until June.
He also said the economy was more resilient than the last energy shock, when Russia launched its full scale invasion ‚Å†of Ukraine.
"People ‚Äãwill sense, you will sense, the longer ‚Äãthis goes on, the more likely the potential for an impact on our economy," he said.

item.title: Saudi Aramco reducing output at two oilfields, two sources say
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Saudi oil giant Aramco has begun cutting ‚Äãoutput at two of ‚Äåits oilfields, two sources said on Monday, after ‚Äãthe vital Strait of ‚ÄãHormuz was choked by ‚Å†the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran ‚Äãand subsequent attacks on ‚Äãthe waterway.
It was not immediately clear at which fields and ‚Äãby how much ‚Äãproduction was being curtailed. Aramco, which ‚Äåhas ‚Å†been rerouting some of its crude cargoes to the Red Sea port ‚Äãof ‚ÄãYanbu, ‚Å†did not immediately respond to an ‚Äãemailed request for ‚Äãcomment.
Several ‚Å†of Saudi Arabia's neighbours, which have also been ‚Å†subject ‚Äãto attacks, have ‚Äãalso cut production.

item.title: China makes biggest retail fuel price cap increase in four years amid Iran war
item.description:
BEIJING, March 9 (Reuters) - China raised regulated ceiling prices for retail gasoline and diesel in the sharpest ‚Äãincrease since March 2022 on Monday, following a surge in ‚Äåinternational oil prices due to the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
Gasoline and diesel retail price caps in the world's second-largest oil consumer will increase by 695 yuan ($100.46) and ‚Äã670 yuan ($96.84) per metric ton, respectively, from Tuesday, according to ‚Äãa notice from the National Development and Reform Commission. ‚Å†The previous adjustment was made on February 24.
International benchmark Brent crude ‚Äãfutures had climbed 27%, and West Texas Intermediate crude (WTI) futures were up ‚Äãby 35.6% last week.
China last week asked refiners to halt fuel exports and try to cancel shipments already committed as the Iran war curbed refinery output, several ‚Äãpeople with knowledge of the matter said on Thursday.
China's state planner ‚Äãreviews retail gasoline and diesel prices every 10 working days and applies uniform adjustments ‚Äånationwide, ‚Å†though benchmark prices vary by region.
The adjustment rate reflects changes in international crude oil prices while also taking into account average processing costs, taxes, distribution expenses and appropriate profit margins.
China allows retail gasoline and ‚Äãdiesel prices to ‚Äãfloat freely between ‚Å†floors and ceilings. However, when international crude prices reach $130 per barrel, retail fuel prices are generally not ‚Äãraised or are raised only minimally. When crude prices ‚Äãfall to $40 ‚Å†per barrel or below, retail fuel prices are calculated as if crude were priced at $40, with normal processing margins, according to the price ‚Å†adjustment mechanism.
($1 = ‚Äã6.9185 Chinese yuan renminbi)

item.title: Lebanon parliament extends its own mandate for two years as war intensifies
item.description:
DUBAI, March 9 (Reuters) - Lebanon‚Äôs parliament extended its own mandate for two years on ‚ÄãMonday, a statement from the speaker's ‚Äåoffice said, pushing back elections that were meant to take place in May of ‚Äãthis year.
The office of parliamentary speaker ‚ÄãNabih Berri said 76 lawmakers of ‚Å†the 128-seat body had voted in ‚Äãfavour of the extension. The vote ‚Äãcame a week after Lebanon was dragged into the regional war by militant group Hezbollah's ‚Äãlaunch of rockets and drones into ‚ÄãIsrael, which has responded with heavy bombardment across ‚Äåthe ‚Å†country.
Lebanon last held parliamentary elections in 2022 and had been considering an extension of the current body's mandate before ‚Äãthe latest ‚Äãround of ‚Å†fighting erupted between Hezbollah and Israel.
Lawmakers have taken similar measures ‚Äãin the past. The parliament ‚Äãelected ‚Å†in 2009 voted to extend its own mandate until 2017 , citing security concerns linked ‚Å†to ‚Äãthe war that was ‚Äãthen raging in neighbouring Syria.

item.title: Morning Bid: Oil's triple-digit trouble
item.description:
March 9 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets
‚ÄòOil shock‚Äô and ‚Äòstagflation‚Äô are overused phrases in financial markets, often pulled out as a scare tactic when mapping out worst-case scenarios. But both are now very ‚Äãmuch in play with energy markets in turmoil as the Middle East war enters its second week with no end in sight.
Crude oil ‚Äåprices soared above $100 per barrel on Monday to their highest since just after the Ukraine invasion four years ago. If triple-digit oil prices are more than a short-term phenomenon, the U.S. and global economy could be in for some real pain - and central bankers may find themselves facing their worst nightmare: stagflation.
I‚Äôll get into that and more below.
But first, for more on the market impact ‚Äãof the eye-popping oil surge, listen to today's episode of Morning Bid .
The energy crisis set off by the war in Iran is escalating. U.S. ‚Äãfuel prices have surged well above $3 per gallon, and they‚Äôre set to rise further. President Donald Trump wrote on social media overnight ‚Å†that rising oil prices were ‚Äúa very small price to pay‚Äù for winning the war, though many voters may question that.
The worrying thing for central banks and investors ‚Äãis that this latest inflationary spur comes after Friday‚Äôs surprisingly weak U.S. employment report. The data may have been skewed by bad weather in February, but there were few ‚Äãpositive points and many signs that the labor market has stalled.
This raises the dark prospect of ‚Äòstagflation‚Äô - slow or no growth coupled with mounting price rises. How the Fed and other central banks react to that is very much an open question. Most likely, they will just stand pat for longer, which is apt to please almost no one.
The renewed inflation concerns have rattled global bond markets , ‚Äãintensifying last week's selloff and sending yields higher - especially in Britain, where two-year gilt yields are on track for their biggest one-day rise since 2022.
Meantime, stock markets tumbled around ‚Äãthe world , with Japan‚Äôs Nikkei losing over 5% and South Korea‚Äôs high-flying KOSPI shedding nearly 6%. That comes on top of losses last week of 5.5% and more than 10% for ‚Äåthose indexes, ‚Å†respectively.
U.S. stock futures are down more than 1% before the bell and - much like last week - the greenback climbed as investors sought safety in the liquidity of dollar cash. Gold, usually the most reliable safe haven, once again failed to rise to the occasion , weighed by the stronger dollar and climbing Treasury yields.
Markets will now look to see if countries begin tapping their national oil reserves to stem the bleeding, with reports on Monday suggesting G7 finance ministers would discuss a joint release from emergency reserves.
Meantime, U.S. Senate ‚ÄãDemocratic Leader Chuck Schumer called on President Trump ‚Äãto release oil from the U.S. ‚Å†Strategic Petroleum Reserve, a move the president has so far not supported.
How far or for how long any of that can alleviate the situation remains to be seen as the crisis itself deepens, with Gulf states continuing to cut output amid persistent threats ‚Äãto shipping through the vital Strait of Hormuz.
Meantime, Tehran appears to be doubling down as it appoints hardliner Mojtaba Khamenei, ‚Äãson of Ali Khamenei, ‚Å†as Iran‚Äôs new supreme leader.
Chart of the day
The jump in fuel prices since the start of the U.S.-Israel war with Iran could prove a political headache for the Republican Party ahead of November's midterm elections.
Some of the steepest rises have been in parts of the Midwest and the South, including swing states that supported Donald Trump in the 2024 ‚Å†presidential election.
Today's events ‚Äãto watch
* U.S. 3-month and 6-month bill auctions
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox ‚Äãevery weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of ‚ÄãReuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Hungary asked EU to suspend sanctions on Russian energy, PM says
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 9 (Reuters) - Hungary's Prime Minister Viktor ‚ÄãOrban said on ‚ÄåMonday that he asked European Commission President ‚ÄãUrsula von ‚Äãder Leyen in a ‚Å†letter to suspend ‚Äãall sanctions on ‚ÄãRussian energy, as that was needed to stop ‚Äãa rise ‚Äãin crude oil prices in ‚ÄåHungary.
Orban ‚Å†also said in a video posted on his Facebook ‚Äãpage ‚Äãthat ‚Å†he called a government meeting ‚Äãfor later ‚Äãin ‚Å†the day to "prevent the price of ‚Å†diesel ‚Äãand gasoline ‚Äãfrom rising to unbearable ‚Äãlevels."

item.title: France will send two warships to Red Sea, Macron says
item.description:
PAPHOS, Cyprus, March 9 (Reuters) - France will send ‚Äãtwo frigates as ‚Äåpart of the European Union's naval mission ‚ÄãAspides in ‚Äãthe Red Sea, French ‚Å†President Emmanuel Macron ‚Äãsaid on Monday.
"We ‚Äãare in the process of setting up a ‚Äãpurely defensive, ‚Äãpurely escort mission, which must ‚Äåbe ‚Å†prepared together with both European and non-European states," Macron ‚Äãsaid ‚Äãafter ‚Å†meeting with Cypriot President Nikos ‚ÄãChristodoulides and ‚ÄãGreek ‚Å†Prime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis in Cyprus.

item.title: Kuwait mourns two officials killed as Iran war rages
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Kuwait held a military funeral on Monday for two interior ministry officials ‚Äãit said were killed "while performing their ‚Äåduty".
The state - which hosts U.S. military installations and has come under Iranian drone and missile fire ‚Äãduring Tehran's war with Israel and ‚Äãthe U.S. - said the two men died ‚Å†early on Sunday, but did not ‚Äãgo into further details.
Kuwait's army separately said ‚Äãareas near the international airport were hit on Sunday and its forces had intercepted missiles and ‚Äãdrones.
The Interior Ministry said on Sunday ‚Äãit mourned "the martyrs of duty, Lieutenant Colonel Staff Abdullah ‚ÄåEmad ‚Å†Al-Sharrah and Major Fahad Abdulaziz Al-Mejmed, from the General Administration of Land Border Security, who were martyred at dawn".
The funeral ‚Äãin Sulaibikhat ‚ÄãCemetery was ‚Å†led by First Deputy Prime Minister and Interior Minister Sheikh ‚ÄãFahad Al-Yousef Al-Sabah, along with other ‚Äãsenior ‚Å†figures.
Authorities there say five people have been killed since the start of the ‚Å†Iran ‚Äãcrisis, including four military personnel ‚Äãand a young girl, and dozens have been wounded.

item.title: Germany's Merz rules out cooperation with far-right AfD after state election
item.description:
BERLIN, March 9 (Reuters) - German Chancellor Friedrich ‚ÄãMerz repeated on ‚ÄåMonday his conservatives would not ‚Äãcooperate with ‚Äãthe far-right Alternative for ‚Å†Germany party ‚Äãfollowing the close ‚Äãresult in the state election in ‚Äãthe southern ‚Äãregion of Baden Wuerttemberg.
He ‚Äåsaid ‚Å†the election, in which the environmental Greens narrowly ‚Äãbeat ‚ÄãMerz's ‚Å†conservative Christian Democrats, would ‚Äãnot affect ‚Äãthe ‚Å†work of the coalition ‚Å†government ‚Äãin Berlin.

item.title: Ukraine received 11 requests on countering Iran's Shahed drones, Zelenskiy says
item.description:
KYIV, March 9 (Reuters) - President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on ‚ÄãMonday that Ukraine ‚Äåhad received 11 requests from countries neighboring Iran ‚Äãas well as ‚ÄãEuropean states and the ‚Å†United States for ‚Äãdrone interceptors, electronic warfare ‚Äãsystems and training.
"We also reviewed in detail requests ‚Äãfrom the states ‚Äãfor security support from our ‚Äåside ‚Å†in countering 'Shahed' drones and other similar challenges," Zelenskiy said on ‚Äãthe Telegram ‚Äãapp ‚Å†after meeting his top military ‚Äãand government officials.
"Some ‚Äãrequests ‚Å†have already been met with concrete decisions ‚Å†and ‚Äãspecific support."

item.title: Turkey says second Iranian ballistic missile shot down by NATO defences in airspace
item.description:
ANKARA, March 9 (Reuters) - Turkey said on Monday that NATO ‚Äåair defences shot down a second ballistic missile that was fired from Iran and had entered Turkish airspace, warning it will take any necessary steps against threats.
This marks the second Iranian ballistic missile in the last week that has targeted the south of Turkey, ‚Äãwhich is a NATO member and Iran's neighbour. Ankara had warned Iran against attacking again on Saturday, but has ‚Äãnot suggested it wants to formally call on alliance members for further protection.
"We once ‚Å†again emphasise that all necessary measures will be taken decisively and without hesitation against any threat directed at ‚Äãour country's territory and airspace," the Turkish Defence Ministry said, adding some ammunition parts had fallen in the ‚Äãsoutheastern province of Gaziantep but there had been no casualties.
"We also reiterate that it is in everyone's interest to heed Turkey's warnings in this regard," it said.
It was unclear where the missile was headed before it was intercepted by NATO defences stationed in ‚Äãthe eastern Mediterranean Sea.
U.S. air forces are stationed at the Incirlik base in southern Turkey, and there is ‚Äãa NATO radar base in Malatya province to the northeast that provides vital protection for the alliance. Ankara said fragments ‚Äåfrom the ‚Å†missile fell in empty fields in Gaziantep, which sits roughly between the two.
Burhanettin Duran, President Tayyip Erdogan's communications director, said Ankara was strongly reiterating its warning to all parties, namely Iran, to avoid steps that endanger regional stability and civilians.
Ankara says that Washington has not used Incirlik in its air assault, alongside Israel, on Iran, ‚Äãwhich triggered Tehran's missile and ‚Äãdrone attacks.
Iran did not ‚Å†immediately comment on the incident, but it has said repeatedly that it is not at war with regional countries and is not explicitly targeting Turkey.
Turkey, which had sought to ‚Äãmediate U.S.-Iran talks before the air war that began last week, has previously ‚Äãsaid it had ‚Å†no intention currently of invoking NATO's Article 4 that would call allies to consult if a member is threatened.
That could lead to invoking the alliance's Article 5, which would call NATO to defend its attacked ally.
Turkey has the trans-Atlantic alliances' second-largest ‚Å†army ‚Äãand has become a leading global actor in the defence industry ‚Äãin recent years. However, it still lacks fulsome air defence of its own despite efforts to develop them. It has relied on NATO's ‚Äãair defences in both incidents in the last week.

item.title: Serbia suspends energy exports to shield market as crude prices surge on Iran war
item.description:
BELGRADE, March 9 (Reuters) - Serbia has suspended crude oil and fuel ‚Äåproduct exports to safeguard its market from shortages and price spikes, the energy minister said on Monday, as global crude prices jump above $119 a barrel amid supply ‚Äãuncertainty due to the war in Iran .
The suspension, effective until March 19, applies ‚Äãto the export of diesel, gasoline and crude oil by ‚Å†all modes of transport, Energy Minister Dubravka Djedovic Handanovic said in ‚Äãa statement.
Crude oil price on Monday reached its highest since mid-2022 of above $119 ‚Äãa barrel, due to uncertainty over global supplies amid the war in Iran.
"The essence of this (temporary) ban is to protect the domestic market from shortages and price spikes ‚Äãdue to global disruptions in the world market,‚Äù she said.
The government ‚Äãin neighboring Croatia on Monday introduced fuel price caps until March 23 to counter effects ‚Äåof ‚Å†surging crude oil prices.
Croatian Prime Minister Andrej Plenkovic said that without caps, the price of diesel could rise by 16 percentage points to 1.72 euros per litre. The government cap limited it at 1.55 euros/litre.
Croatia last July ‚Äãabolished fuel price ‚Äãcaps introduced in ‚Å†2022 when the war in Ukraine threatened global energy supplies.
Serbia has kept a price cap on fuel since ‚ÄãFebruary 2022. The government controls the maximum retail prices ‚Äãfor Euro ‚Å†diesel and Euro Premium BMB 95 gasoline to mitigate the impact of global price surges on the local economy.
Serbia's fuel exports in 2024, mainly to ‚Å†neighboring countries, ‚Äãaccounted for around 3.6% of all exports, ‚Äãor $1.26 billion. In 2025, they sharply fell due to U.S. sanctions against the country's only ‚Äãoil refiner, Russian-owned NIS .

item.title: India not planning to release oil in coordination with IEA, source says
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - India is not ‚Äãplanning to ‚Äãrelease oil reserves ‚Å†in coordination ‚Äãwith the ‚ÄãInternational Energy Agency and has ‚Äãno immediate ‚Äãplans to raise ‚Äåretail ‚Å†prices for gasoline and diesel as ‚Äãof ‚Äãnow, ‚Å†a government source ‚Äãtold ‚ÄãReuters ‚Å†on Monday.
(This story has been refiled to correct the author's name in the byline)

item.title: Helsinki energy group plans large investment in small nuclear reactors
item.description:
HELSINKI, March 9 (Reuters) - The city of Helsinki's energy utility Helen plans to invest between 1 billion and 5 billion euros ($1.16 billion-$5.78 billion) in Small Modular Reactor ‚Äãcapacity in the coming years, the head of its newly-established nuclear subsidiary ‚Äåtold Reuters.
Helsinki is not alone in eyeing new nuclear generation, as countries ranging from the United States to South Korea , or companies such as Meta (META.O) , opens new tab turn to SMRs to cater for the rapid rise in ‚Äãpower demand from artificial intelligence and electrification.
SMRs are advanced reactors that can ‚Äãbe factory-assembled and transported for installation elsewhere. They have a power ‚Å†generating capacity of up to 300 megawatts, about a third that of traditional reactors.
Finland, ‚Äãtoo, expects its power demand to increase. But it also needs more steady base ‚Äãload following a rapid rise in wind power production, which has rewarded Finns with some of Europe's lowest power prices on average, but also resulted in high price volatility during consumption peaks.
For ‚ÄãHelen, which shut down Finland's last coal-fired power plant last year , nuclear is a way ‚Äãto add capacity while further reducing CO2 emissions, Helen Nuclear Ltd's CEO Pekka Tolonen said.
"Nuclear power ‚Äåis ‚Å†now the most promising and, among current options, the best way for us to achieve a future without combustion," he told Reuters. Helen is reviewing half a dozen potential Western SMR suppliers, he added.
"The value of the planned investment would be in ‚Äãthe low single billions, ‚Äãmeaning 1 to ‚Å†5 billion euros," he said. The amount would be in the lower end if Helen went for nuclear heat production only, ‚Äãand in the upper end of the range if it opted ‚Äãfor combined ‚Å†heat and power production.
Helen, which has to meet heat consumption peaks during cold winter days, believes power consumption in the capital region will double in a few years' time, ‚Å†due ‚Äãto electrification, data centres and expansion of clean tech, ‚Äãincluding green hydrogen.
Last month, the company initiated a land use planning process in Helsinki and expects to pick ‚Äãa site by late 2027, Tolonen said.
($1 = 0.8649 euros)

item.title: Exclusive: Trump's Gaza plan on hold as Iran war pauses disarmament talks
item.description:
JERUSALEM/CAIRO, March 9 (Reuters) - Talks to advance President Donald Trump's plan to end the Gaza war have been on hold since last week when the U.S. and ‚ÄãIsrael jointly attacked Iran, sparking a broader Middle East war , three sources with direct knowledge of the negotiations said.
The pause threatens to stall implementation of Trump's flagship Middle ‚ÄåEast peace initiative, which he has cast as a major foreign policy objective. It comes less than a month after he secured billions of dollars in pledges for Gaza from Gulf Arab states - countries that are now facing Iranian attacks as the conflict widens.
Trump's Gaza plan has hinged in part on whether Hamas militants would lay down their arms in exchange for amnesty, a step intended to pave the way for reconstruction and further Israeli military withdrawals. ‚ÄãWhite House mediators have been backchanneling between Israel and Hamas on the disarmament question.
Negotiations on this and other issues were paused when the Iran war began on February 28, ‚Äãthe three sources said, speaking on condition of anonymity to discuss sensitive talks. The pause in disarmament talks has not been previously reported.
Zaha Hassan, ‚Å†of the Washington-based Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, said countries including the United Arab Emirates and Qatar which pledged funds for Trump's Board of Peace mission may be questioning if this is "really ‚Äãmoney well spent now that they are dodging rocket fire."
One of the sources, who has direct knowledge of work by Trump's Board of Peace mission, described ‚Äãthe pause as a brief, minor delay caused by flight disruptions preventing mediators and representatives from travelling around the region. Talks have frequently been held in Cairo.
Over the longer term, the Board of Peace believes the war could accelerate a resolution of the disarmament issue by removing Iranian influence, which has long supported Hamas financially, the source said.
Another source ‚Äî a Palestinian official close to the mediation effort ‚Äî said Hamas had been expected to hold talks ‚Äãwith Egyptian, Qatari and Turkish mediators on the day the war erupted, but the meeting was scrapped and no new date has been set.
A Hamas official confirmed that talks on Trump's Gaza ‚Äãplan had been frozen for now, but declined to elaborate. Israel's government did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The White House did not immediately provide comment.
Israel's military has eased off strikes in Gaza ‚Äåsince the ‚Å†start of the war but, citing Hamas threats, has not ceased attacks, as Israeli jets carry out bombing campaigns in Iran and Lebanon. At least 16 Palestinians have been killed in Gaza since February 28, health officials say.
"The moment the war on Iran is over, (Israel) will come back at us with the same frequency, with the same violence," said Talal Hamouda, 46, who lives in Jabalia in the northern Gaza Strip, with his wife and five children.
Hamas, for its part, has continued to reassert its grip in areas under its control in Gaza since the war began. Sources close to ‚Äãthe group say Hamas fighters in recent days ‚Äãambushed several Israeli-backed militia members in Gaza's north ‚Å†and south, killing at least two people.
Trump's plan for Gaza began with an October ceasefire that left Israel controlling more than half the territory, with Hamas controlling the rest. The initiative appeared to gain momentum in the month leading up to ‚Äãthe war, including the reopening of Gaza's border crossing with Egypt and new pledges for reconstruction.
Much of Washington's multinational coordination on Gaza policy ‚Äãhas been run out ‚Å†of a U.S.-led military compound in southern Israel. Foreign diplomats posted there said momentum behind the plan appeared to stall as the Iran war escalated.
Three diplomats said the Civil Military Coordination Centre scaled back to minimal operations when the war started, and that there were concerns it could be targeted by Iranian missiles.
The diplomats said senior U.S. officials now appear focused on the Iran war, leaving Gaza with limited top-level attention. ‚Å†Still, working-level ‚Äãdiscussions among countries have continued in the hope that the plan could move forward once the war ends.
Natan Sachs, a ‚Äãsenior fellow at the Washington-based Middle East Institute, said that "only sustained attention from the Trump administration can keep the plan on track ‚Äî and the war with Iran has the potential to undermine exactly that."
"Without it, the divergent aims of the ‚Äãtwo warring parties could easily lead to very different outcomes, and potentially to a resumption of fighting."

item.title: Turkey's Halkbank, U.S. Justice Department in deferred prosecution agreement, judge says
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - Turkish state-run lender Halkbank (HALKB.IS) , opens new tab and the U.S. Justice Department have reached a deferred prosecution agreement, the judge overseeing the long-running U.S. criminal case against the bank said in a ‚Äãcourt filing on Monday, without providing further details of the deal.
In the case, prosecutors had accused ‚Äåof fraud, money laundering and conspiracy for allegedly helping Iran evade American economic sanctions.
Halkbank's case has long been a thorn in U.S.-Turkey relations, with Turkish President Tayyip Erdogan calling it an "unlawful, ugly" step.
The agreement was announced after the U.S. Supreme Court in October 2025 let ‚Äãstand a federal appeals court decision that let the prosecution proceed.
U.S. prosecutors originally charged Halkbank in ‚Äã2019, during President Donald Trump's first White House term.
Erdogan met with Trump in September, and expressed ‚Å†hope the following month that the meeting would resolve various issues between the countries.
The Turkish president said Trump ‚Äãtold him at the White House and in a subsequent phone call that "the Halkbank problem is finished for us."
Halkbank was accused of using money servicers and front companies in Iran, Turkey and the United Arab Emirates in its sanctions-evasion scheme .
U.S. prosecutors said the bank secretly transferred $20 billion of restricted funds, converted oil revenue into gold and cash to benefit Iranian ‚Äãinterests and documented fake food shipments to justify transfers of oil proceeds.
Halkbank pleaded not guilty to bank fraud, money ‚Äãlaundering and conspiracy charges.
The case was an outgrowth of several related criminal cases, including against Turkish-Iranian gold trader Reza Zarrab and ‚Äåformer ‚Å†Halkbank executive Mehmet Hakan Atilla.
Zarrab pleaded guilty to bank fraud, money laundering and conspiracy charges in 2017, but has not been sentenced.
He testified against Atilla, who was convicted in 2018 of bank fraud and conspiracy charges. Atilla returned to Turkey in 2019 after serving most of a 32-month prison term. He has denied wrongdoing.
Halkbank's case ‚Äãhas taken a circuitous path ‚Äãthrough the U.S. courts.
Berman originally ‚Å†ruled in 2020 that Halkbank did not deserve immunity from prosecution.
The following year, the 2nd U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals in Manhattan upheld that ruling but on different grounds. ‚ÄãIt said Halkbank could be prosecuted under the federal Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act of ‚Äã1976, because its ‚Å†alleged misconduct involved commercial activity not covered by that law.
In 2023, the U.S. Supreme Court temporarily voided the prosecution , despite agreeing that Congress' desire to shield foreign countries and their instrumentalities from civil liability did not cover criminal cases. Instead, ‚Å†it ordered ‚Äãthe 2nd Circuit to more thoroughly review whether immunity under centuries-old ‚Äãcommon law shielded Halkbank.
The appeals court found no such shield in October 2024, prompting Halkbank's second Supreme Court appeal. The Trump administration argued that the common ‚Äãlaw does not shield foreign state-owned companies from criminal prosecution.

item.title: Israel military says it is unaware of clash with Hezbollah on Syria border
item.description:
TEL AVIV, March 9 (Reuters) - The Israeli military on Monday said that it ‚Äãwas unaware of any clash with ‚ÄåHezbollah fighters in eastern Lebanon, after the Iran-backed Lebanese group said it had fought Israeli soldiers ‚Äãapproaching from Syria.
Hezbollah said in a ‚Äãstatement that its fighters had observed 15 ‚Å†helicopters flying over eastern Lebanon just ‚Äãafter midnight, dropping Israeli troops who were observed ‚Äãapproaching Lebanese territory from an area on the Syrian side of the border.
It said that its fighters "confronted ‚Äãthe helicopters and the infiltrating forces ‚Äãwith appropriate weapons". The Israeli military, however, said that ‚Äåit ‚Å†was unaware of any such incident.
Reuters was unable to immediately verify Hezbollah's account.
The Israeli military carried out an airborne raid in ‚Äãthe same area, ‚Äãnear ‚Å†the village of Nabi Chit, overnight Friday-Saturday in what it said ‚Äãwas a mission to seek the ‚Äãremains ‚Å†of a soldier missing since 1986. Lebanon's Health Ministry said 41 people were killed ‚Å†in the ‚ÄãIsraeli raid.

item.title: US to designate Sudanese Muslim Brotherhood a foreign terrorist organization, State Department says
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - The ‚ÄãTrump administration ‚Äåis designating ‚Äãthe ‚ÄãSudanese Muslim Brotherhood ‚Å†as ‚Äãa ‚Äãspecially designated global terrorist ‚Äãand intends ‚Äãto designate it ‚Äåas ‚Å†a foreign terrorist ‚Äãorganization ‚Äãeffective ‚Å†March 16, ‚Äãthe ‚ÄãU.S. ‚Å†State Department ‚Å†said ‚Äãon ‚ÄãMonday.

item.title: IEA called for joint release of emergency oil stocks at G7 online meeting, Japan says
item.description:
TOKYO, March 9 (Reuters) - The International Energy Agency called ‚Äãfor a coordinated ‚Äårelease of emergency oil reserves during online meeting ‚Äãwith the ‚Äãfinance ministers from the Group ‚Å†of Seven nations ‚Äãon Monday, Japanese ‚ÄãFinance Minister Satsuki Katayama told a briefing.
"IEA called for ‚Äãeach country to ‚Äãdo coordinated release of oil ‚Äåreserves," ‚Å†Katayama said, as the ministers gathered online to discuss the ‚Äãimpact ‚Äãof ‚Å†war in Iran on the ‚Äãmarkets which saw ‚Äãoil ‚Å†prices surging to more than $119 a barrel ‚Å†on ‚ÄãMonday.

item.title: Standard Lithium-Equinor JV inks lithium offtake deal with Trafigura
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Standard Lithium (SLI.V) , opens new tab said on Monday its joint venture with Norway's Equinor (EQNR.OL) , opens new tab ‚Äãhad signed its first binding offtake ‚Äåagreement to supply lithium carbonate from its s outhwest Arkansas project to commodities trader ‚ÄãTrafigura.
The joint venture, Smackover Lithium, will ‚Äãsupply Trafigura with 8,000 metric tons ‚Å†per year of battery-grade lithium ‚Äãcarbonate over a 10-year period starting when ‚Äãcommercial production begins.
The agreement marks a key milestone for the southwest Arkansas project as the ‚Äãpartners seek to secure long-term ‚Äãcustomers and financing ahead of a final investment ‚Äådecision ‚Å†and construction.
Smackover Lithium is targeting about 22,500 metric tons of annual lithium carbonate production capacity in the project's first ‚Äãphase and ‚Äãplans ‚Å†to secure offtake agreements for around 80% of that output.
The ‚ÄãTrafigura agreement covers more than ‚Äã40% ‚Å†of that targeted volume.
Smackover Lithium expects to reach a final investment decision ‚Å†on ‚Äãthe project in 2026, ‚Äãwith first production anticipated in 2028.

item.title: Oil shock to worsen should US-Israel seize Iran's Kharg Island, JP Morgan says
item.description:
LONDON, March 9 - Iran's oil exports would stall and output halve if the U.S. and Israel were to seize its port on Kharg Island, triggering further attacks from ‚ÄãTehran on regional oil infrastructure, JP Morgan said in a note.
Axios reported ‚Äåon March 7 that the U.S. administration had discussed seizing the island, which sits some 30 km off Iran's coast in the Gulf and processes 90% of its crude exports.
"A direct strike ‚Äãwould immediately halt the bulk of Iran‚Äôs crude exports, likely triggering severe ‚Äãretaliation in the Strait of Hormuz or against regional energy infrastructure," ‚Å†JP Morgan said.
Iran , the third largest producer in the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting ‚ÄãCountries, pumps about 4.5% of global oil supplies, with output of about 3.3 million ‚Äãbarrels per day of crude, plus 1.3 million bpd of condensate and other liquids.
During the 1979 Iran hostage crisis, U.S. President Jimmy Carter imposed sanctions on Iran but refrained from ordering strikes ‚Äãon the island. His successor, Ronald Reagan, during the 1980s Iran-Iraq Tanker War, prioritised ‚Äãprotecting shipping and targeting Iranian vessels and missile batteries, leaving Kharg untouched.
"Although Iraqi forces struck some ‚Äåterminals ‚Å†and tankers during the eight-year war, Kharg remained largely operational and damage was typically repaired quickly, demonstrating that disabling it would require sustained, large-scale attacks," JP Morgan said.
The island collects oil coming by pipeline from Iran‚Äôs largest producing fields, including Ahvaz, Marun ‚Äãand Gachsaran.
In the days ‚Äãleading up to ‚Å†the U.S.-Israeli attack, Iran ramped up exports from Kharg to near record levels, loading over 3 million bpd over February 15-20, ‚Äãnearly triple its normal export pace of around 1.3 million to ‚Äã1.6 million ‚Å†bpd, according to JP Morgan.
Storage capacity on Kharg is estimated at roughly 30 million barrels and, according to Kpler, approximately 18 million barrels of crude are currently stored on ‚Å†the ‚Äãisland, equivalent to roughly 10-12 days of exports under ‚Äãnormal conditions.
Oil prices jumped to $119 per barrel on Monday as production cuts in the Middle East spread with Iraq , Kuwait , Saudi Arabia and ‚Äãthe United Arab Emirates all affected.

item.title: Shipper MSC introduces emergency fuel surcharges
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Shipping company MSC on Monday announced emergency fuel ‚Äãsurcharges on all cargo ‚Äåfrom the Mediterranean and Black Sea to the Indian sub-continent, ‚ÄãRed Sea and ‚ÄãEast Africa.
The move includes the ‚Å†West Mediterranean, Adriatic, the ‚ÄãEast Mediterranean, Greece and Turkey ‚Äãand applies from March 16 until March 31, it said.
Details (per twenty-foot ‚Äãequivalent unit, or TEU):
Western ‚ÄãMediterranean and Adriatic
Eastern Mediterranean and Black Sea

item.title: Netherlands sends frigate to Mediterranean on protective mission
item.description:
