The students eventually walked up the steps and past the rope, however, prompting a rush of security guards who were eventually called off by Higher Education Vice Minister Modesto Ricardo Gomez.
The vice minister told reporters the students had raised legitimate concerns, and blamed the ‚ÄãTrump administration's tightening sanctions for ‚Äãthe problems. He said there ‚Å†would be no repercussions for those who protested.
"There are no consequences whatsoever. They have a concern, and we are addressing it. It will always be like this," Gomez said.
While the minister spoke to reporters, ‚Äãthe students left the steps and entered the university with administration officials and teachers, declining requests for ‚Äãinterviews.
The minister said ‚Å†the dialogue would continue inside the university.
One student, who did not give his name to a reporter but spoke before cameras, said there would be more protests if the issues were not resolved.
"We are willing to engage in dialogue if it is to represent our demands, but if ‚Å†dialogue, as ‚Äãhas already happened, does not work, we will sit down here again.‚Äù
Protests of any ‚Äãkind in Communist-run Cuba are exceedingly rare. Cuba's 2019 constitution grants citizens the right to protest, but a law more specifically defining that right is stalled in the ‚Äãlegislature, leaving those who take to the street in legal limbo.

item.title: Trading Day: Wall Street roars back on war resolution hopes
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - Stocks in Asia and Europe tumbled on Monday as the Iran war entered its second week and oil prices surged as much as 30% above $100 a barrel. But Wall Street rallied and oil later sank after President Donald Trump indicated the war may soon be over.
In my column today I put ‚ÄãWall Street's resilience under the microscope. As selling snowballs across other equity markets, why has the global avalanche not yet engulfed U.S. ‚Äåstocks? Are there reasonable explanations, or is complacency setting in?
If you have more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* Central bank paralysis
There can be no doubt now - the eye-watering surge in oil prices since the U.S. and Israel launched their joint attack on Iran on February 28 has put central banks in an unenviable bind. Price pressures are clearly intensifying, but the impact of $100 a barrel oil on economic activity will be ‚Äãharsh.
Do they hike rates to nip inflation in the bud, or adopt a more dovish stance in the face of rising unemployment and potential recession? In ‚Äãthe U.S., the labor market was already creaking, household savings are run down, and now gas prices are soaring. Higher borrowing costs will hurt the average consumer. But so will ‚Äåhigher inflation.
* ‚Å†Pre-war price pressures
Even before the Middle East crisis erupted, price pressures around the world were bubbling up. Figures on Monday showed that consumer inflation in China jumped in February to the highest in three years, Mexico 's inflation rose above the central bank's target, and real wages in Japan rose for the first time in 13 months.
With oil smashing through $100 a barrel - and now up considerably on the same period last year - inflation signals are only pointing one way. U.S. PCE inflation figures for February due ‚Äãout later this week are expected to ‚Äãfollow a similar path, rising further ‚Å†above 3%.
* To tap or not to tap?
Countries around the world are suddenly scrambling for ways to cushion the economic blow of $100 oil. Releasing emergency oil reserves is an obvious option, but it seems like it's not going to be exercised ‚Äãjust yet. G7 countries discussed it on Monday but say there's no immediate supply shortfall. Not yet, anyway.
Elsewhere, China has capped ‚Äãfuel prices, South Korea is ‚Å†considering a similar move for the first time in 30 years, and Japan is preparing for a possible release of crude and could draw down cash reserves set aside for emergency spending. Authorities will be reluctant to raise interest rates, so they may need to get inventive.
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want to receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, ‚Äãunder the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: US considering selling oil from strategic reserve, US energy chief says
item.description:
PLATTEVILLE, Colorado, March 9 (Reuters) - The U.S. is considering coordinating ‚Äãsales of oil from the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve with ‚Äåreleases from other countries as prices soar during the war on Iran , Energy Secretary Chris Wright said on Monday.
Wright also said the U.S. has "some other options" on allowing ‚Äãmore sales of Russian oil held in tankers on the ‚Äãwater in Asia. Late last week, for instance, Washington issued ‚Å†a 30-day waiver allowing the sale of Russian crude currently ‚Äãstranded at sea to continue to India.
"We are talking about coordinated releases ‚Äãfrom the SPR," Wright told reporters gathered at a natural gas plant in Colorado. The U.S. SPR , located on the coasts of Texas and Louisiana, holds 415 million ‚Äãbarrels of oil, more than what the entire world uses in ‚Äãfour days.
Global and domestic oil futures settled on Monday at the highest level ‚Äåsince ‚Å†August 2022 as the war led by the U.S. and Israel stranded tankers and shut in oil production in the Middle East.
The International Energy Agency, the West's energy watchdog, on Monday called for a coordinated ‚Äãrelease of oil, ‚Äãand G7 countries ‚Å†agreed to closely monitor developments in the energy markets. No release has yet been announced.
Fatih Birol, the ‚ÄãIEA head, told G7 finance ministers on Monday that ‚Äãhis ‚Å†group's members hold more than 1.2 billion barrels of public emergency oil stocks and a further 600 million barrels of industry stocks are under ‚Å†government ‚Äãobligation.
The U.S. was not considering imposing restrictions ‚Äãon exports of U.S. energy as a way to control prices, Wright said.

item.title: Canadian PM, Qatari emir discuss diplomacy to prevent wider war in Middle East
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney spoke with Qatar's Emir Sheikh ‚ÄãTamim bin Hamad al-Thani on Monday about avoiding a wider ‚Äåconflict in the Middle East , Carney's office said.
Here are some details:
‚Ä¢ They "underscored the importance of intensifying diplomatic engagement to avoid a wider conflict with ‚Äãglobal consequences," Carney's office said in a statement.
‚Ä¢ The statement ‚Äãsaid the leaders condemned Iranian missile and drone attacks ‚Å†on Qatar and Carney expressed Canada's solidarity with Qatar.
‚Ä¢ The U.S. ‚Äãand Israel began attacking Iran on February 28. Iran responded with its ‚Äãown strikes against Israel and Gulf states with U.S. bases. U.S. President Donald Trump has thus far offered shifting objectives and timelines for the war that ‚Äãhas killed scores in Iran , including Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei .
‚Ä¢ "In accordance with ‚Äãinternational law, the leaders called for an immediate end to attacks on civilians ‚Äåand ‚Å†civilian infrastructure that only serve to destabilise the region and worsen the global economic and energy situation," Carney's office said, adding he and the Qatari emir discussed efforts to de-escalate the Iran war .
‚Ä¢ ‚ÄãAt least 1,230 ‚Äãpeople have been ‚Å†killed in Iran, including 175 schoolgirls and staff killed in a missile strike on a primary ‚Äãschool in southern Iran, according to non-profit humanitarian ‚Äãgroup Iranian ‚Å†Red Crescent Society.
‚Ä¢ The Iranian army additionally says over 100 were killed after a U.S. submarine sank an Iranian warship off Sri ‚Å†Lanka's ‚Äãcoast last week.
‚Ä¢ Eleven civilians have been ‚Äãkilled in Israel, according to Israeli tallies. The U.S. military has said seven of ‚Äãits service members have been killed.

item.title: Alexander brothers, top NY real estate brokers, convicted at sex trafficking trial
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - Three ‚Äãwealthy brothers, including two of New York's top real estate brokers, were found ‚Äåguilty on Monday at a closely watched sex trafficking trial over allegations they lured women and girls to exclusive parties where they drugged and raped them.
Oren Alexander, Tal Alexander and Alon Alexander had each pleaded not ‚Äãguilty to all charges they respectively faced in an indictment alleging trafficking and other criminal ‚Äãconduct related to seven victims.
Oren and Tal Alexander co-founded the luxury real ‚Å†estate brokerage Official, while Alon Alexander was an executive at a private security company.
Defense lawyers ‚Äãhad contended that the women were willing participants who were not paid to have sex.
Tal Alexander ‚Äãfaced seven charges in total, and his twin brothers Oren and Alon faced six.
Over the course of a six-week trial in Manhattan federal court, eleven women testified that one or more of the brothers sexually ‚Äãabused them.
In his closing argument on March 3, prosecutor Andrew Jones said the brothers masqueraded ‚Äãas party boys but really were predators.
"They used a consistent playbook to lure, isolate and rape their victims," ‚ÄåJones ‚Å†said. "They did it with callousness and a perverse sense of pride."
Defense lawyers acknowledged that the brothers had discussed sex amongst themselves in crass terms and behaved in ways that angered women with whom they had relationships. But they argued the sexual encounters at issue were consensual.
"He should ‚Äãbe and is embarrassed ‚Äãby how he behaved ‚Å†over these years," Howard Srebnick, a lawyer for Alon Alexander, said in his closing argument on March 3. "That doesn't make the conduct a ‚Äãcrime."
The three brothers were arrested in December 2024 and have been ‚Äãjailed since ‚Å†then at Brooklyn's Metropolitan Detention Center.
Each brother had his own legal defense team. Oren Alexander's lawyers include Marc Agnifilo and Teny Geragos, who last year defended rapper and music mogul Sean "Diddy" Combs at his ‚Å†sex ‚Äãtrafficking trial.
Combs is serving a four-year prison sentence after being ‚Äãconvicted on prostitution charges, but acquitted of the more serious sex trafficking charges he faced that could have landed him ‚Äãin prison for life.

item.title: FBI examines Arizona election records in widening probe of Trump vote fraud claims
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - The FBI has obtained records related ‚Äãto the 2020 election in Arizona , a top state lawmaker said on Monday, as federal ‚Äålaw enforcement continues to pursue President Donald Trump's false claims that his defeat in that campaign was the result of voting fraud.
The FBI subpoenaed the Arizona Senate last week for material connected to a Republican-led audit of the 2020 election ‚Äãresults in Maricopa County, the most populous county in the state, Arizona Senate President ‚ÄãWarren Petersen said in a social media post.
"The FBI has the records," Petersen, ‚Å†a Republican, wrote in the post.
The demand for information from Arizona is the latest instance of ‚Äãthe Trump administration deploying U.S. law enforcement and intelligence agencies to gather evidence of alleged irregularities in the 2020 ‚Äãelection, despite courts and audits repeatedly rejecting claims of widespread voter fraud.
It was not immediately clear what crimes the FBI may be investigating. An FBI spokesperson did not immediately respond to a request for comment. A spokesperson for ‚ÄãPetersen declined to elaborate on his social media post.
The FBI in January seized 2020 ballots from a Georgia ‚Äãcounty elections center as part of a court-approved search of the facility. Fulton County has sued for the ‚Äåreturn of ‚Å†the material, alleging the search violated the U.S. Constitution and relied on discredited allegations.
The 2021 Maricopa County audit, which was run by Trump's Republican allies in Arizona, largely affirmed the initial 2020 election results, finding slightly more votes for Trump's Democratic opponent Joe Biden . Trump's loss in Maricopa County was key ‚Äãto his narrow defeat to ‚ÄãBiden statewide.
The audit ‚Å†made several claims of election irregularities, which county officials vigorously disputed .
"What the Trump administration appears to be pursuing now is not a legitimate law enforcement inquiry," ‚ÄãArizona State Attorney General Kris Mayes, a Democrat, said in a statement. "It ‚Äãis the weaponization ‚Å†of federal law enforcement in service of crackpots and lies."
The investigations into Georgia and Arizona, two states that have been hotly contested in recent presidential elections, come as Trump has spoken openly about the ‚Å†federal government taking ‚Äãcontrol of elections in certain unspecified areas of the ‚Äãcountry.
Trump has put a lawyer, Kurt Olsen, who backed the "Stop the Steal" movement to overturn the 2020 election results, i n charge ‚Äãof an effort to reexamine the 2020 vote.

item.title: Colombia's ruling party to be top force in Senate, but without majority
item.description:
BOGOTA, March 9 (Reuters) - Colombia's ruling party, the left-leaning Historic Pact, emerged from Sunday's elections as the strongest political force in the incoming Senate, ‚Äãanalysts said on Monday, although its failure to secure an absolute ‚Äåmajority means it will need to form coalitions and may struggle if Colombia's divided right wing wins the presidency.
The party, which brought President Gustavo Petro to power in 2022, ‚Äãwon 25 out of 102 Senate seats in Sunday's legislative elections, according ‚Äãto official results. Finishing second was the right-wing Democratic Center, led by ‚Å†former President Alvaro Uribe, with 17 seats.
The two forces will likely jostle ‚Äãto make deals with other parties in the fragmented Senate, which include Colombia's ‚ÄãLiberal Party with 13 seats, the Green Alliance with 10, the Conservative Party with 10, La U with nine, and Radical Change with seven. Eleven other seats were distributed among ‚Äãvarious smaller parties.
In the House of Representatives, results from the electoral authority ‚Äãshow the opposition Democratic Center leading with 32 out of 182 seats, followed by the ‚ÄåLiberal ‚Å†Party with 31, and the Historic Pact with 29. The Conservative Party obtained 18 seats, Radical Change 13, and the Green Alliance 7.
Ivan Cepeda, Historic Pact's presidential candidate in May's presidential election, described the legislative election results as a "categorical ‚Äãvictory," saying his ‚Äãparty had become ‚Å†the primary political force in Congress.
However, analysts suggest that if Cepeda loses the presidential election to right-wing candidates Paloma ‚ÄãValencia or Abelardo De La Espriella, his party will become ‚Äãthe main ‚Å†opposition force.
Ariel Avila, a re-elected senator from the Green Alliance, believes that Colombia's next president, whether from the right or the left, will have a tough ‚Å†time getting ‚Äãthings done, predicting a "vetocracy" where lawmakers block parties ‚Äãsimply because they come from the opposing side.
"It is a very fragmented Congress and it is ‚Äãdifficult to mend," Avila said.

item.title: Alexander Butterfield, White House aide who exposed Nixon's taping system, dead at 99
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - Alexander Butterfield, the White House aide who disclosed Richard Nixon's secret audio taping system, providing the "smoking gun" of the Watergate scandal that brought down the president, has died at age 99.
The death of Butterfield, whose revelation about the listening devices and recording system set off a ‚Äãroaring legal battle over the president's right of executive privilege, was confirmed by his wife Kim to The Washington Post and the New York Times.
Both newspapers said he died a ‚Äåmonth shy of his 100th birthday at his home in the seaside La Jolla area of San Diego, but no cause of death was cited.
Butterfield once told journalist Alicia Shepard he disliked being known as the man who exposed the existence of the tapes because it made it seem as if he had "eagerly and breathlessly" told the Watergate congressional committee about them.
A native of Pensacola, Florida, who grew up in California, Butterfield attended UCLA before joining the U.S. Air Force in 1948 and went on ‚Äãto serve as a combat pilot during the Vietnam War, commanding a squadron of tactical reconnaissance aircraft. He later served as a military assistant to a high-ranking Pentagon aide, gaining White House exposure ‚Äãin that job.
Butterfield finally left the Air Force to join the White House staff as a deputy to Nixon's chief of staff, H.R. Haldeman, an old friend ‚Å†from UCLA. Among Butterfield's White House duties was keeping a historical record of the presidency, which included overseeing installation of the voice-activated taping system.
Butterfield had left the White House for the top job at the Federal Aviation ‚ÄãAdministration by the time the investigation into the June 17, 1972, break-in at Democratic headquarters in the Watergate building was heating up.
He was one of the few in the White House who knew about the recording system, ‚Äãand upon learning that he would be questioned by the Senate Watergate Committee, known officially as the Select Committee on Presidential Campaign Activities, Butterfield resolved neither to lie nor volunteer information.
A Republican staff attorney for the committee was questioning him in a private preliminary session when he asked if the White House had a recording system. Butterfield reluctantly acknowledged there was.
On July 16, 1973, three days after his initial closed-door disclosure, Butterfield appeared before the Senate committee at a televised meeting, and Fred ‚ÄãThompson, then counsel for the Republicans on the committee and a future actor and senator, asked the same question.
After a long pause, Butterfield said, "I was aware of listening devices, yes, sir."
It was stunning news for the nation ‚Äãbecause it meant there was an actual record of what Nixon said, when he said it and to whom he said it.
Butterfield said the recording system had been secretly taping conversations and meetings in the Oval Office, Nixon's office in ‚Äåthe Executive Office ‚Å†Building and the Cabinet room, as well as four White House telephones. The purpose of the recordings, he said, was historical.
In a 1975 interview with People magazine, Butterfield said Nixon often forgot about the recorders and had disregarded advice to destroy the tapes because he never thought the Watergate affair would reach a point where he would have to surrender them.
"I'm sure that he hates me as much as anyone can," Butterfield said of his former boss, who died in 1994.
He said he thought Nixon should have resigned earlier.
"I don't feel awful about the president's resignation," he said. "Not at all."
A recording made six days after the Watergate break-in proved to ‚Äãbe Nixon's undoing - the "smoking gun" that showed he knew ‚Äãof the cover-up. He was heard agreeing to ‚Å†a plan to have the break-in investigation halted for national security reasons.
The U.S. Supreme Court ultimately rejected White House claims of executive privilege and ordered Nixon to turn over the subpoenaed tapes as his public and political support withered. Rather than face impeachment and a Senate trial, he resigned on August 9, 1974.
Since he was not ‚Äãinvolved with the break-in or cover-up, Butterfield was never indicted, but his old friend Haldeman would be among several Nixon insiders who went to prison in ‚Äãthe scandal.
Butterfield was the focus ‚Å†of the 2015 book "The Last of the President's Men" by Bob Woodward, the Washington Post reporter who helped break the Watergate story, and he provided Woodward with thousands of documents he had secretly removed from Nixon administration offices.
The documents and interviews with Butterfield portrayed what the former White House aide described as a "cesspool" within the administration and characterized Nixon as odd, isolated and resentful.
Butterfield said he frequently was the target of hostility from Nixon loyalists and told Time ‚Å†magazine that long-time ‚ÄãNixon secretary Rose Mary Woods, who said she accidentally erased 18 1/2 minutes of those White House tapes, had disparaged him as ‚Äãa "son of a bitch" who had "destroyed the greatest leader this country ever had."
Butterfield worked as an adviser to Oliver Stone on his 1995 film "Nixon" and had a cameo role in it as a White House staffer.
Butterfield's first marriage, to Charlotte Maguire, ended in divorce in 1985. ‚ÄãHe also had previously dated Audrey Geisel, widow of the children's author-illustrator Theodor Seuss Geisel, better known as Dr. Seuss.

item.title: Trump on Cuba: May or may not be a "friendly takeover"
item.description:
DORAL, Fla., March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump said on Monday Cuba is in "deep ‚Äãtrouble" on a humanitarian basis and ‚Äåthat U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio was dealing with the issue which may or may ‚Äãnot be a "friendly takeover."
"He's dealing (with it) ‚Äãand it may be a friendly takover, ‚Å†it may not be a friendly takeover. ‚ÄãWouldn't really matter because they're really down ‚Äãto...as they say, fumes. They have no energy, they have no money," Trump, speaking at a news ‚Äãconference in Doral, Florida told reporters.
The Cuban ‚Äãgovernment has said it is not in any high-level ‚Äåtalks ‚Å†with the United States but has not outright denied press reports that U.S. officials may be in informal talks with Raul ‚ÄãGuillermo Rodriguez ‚ÄãCastro, the ‚Å†grandson of former Cuban President Raul Castro.
Cuban exiles who are largely ‚Äãconcentrated in Miami have long dreamed ‚Äãof ‚Å†overthrowing the Cuban government or seeing it fall and have in the past plotted ‚Å†against ‚Äãthe government that was established ‚Äãby the late revolutionary leader Fidel Castro.

item.title: Trump says he discussed Ukraine and Iran conflicts with Putin
item.description:
DORAL, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump on Monday told reporters he had a "very good call" with ‚ÄãRussian President Vladimir Putin about Ukraine ‚Äãand the conflict in the Middle East.
"There's ‚Å†tremendous hatred between President Putin and (Ukraine's) ‚ÄãPresident Zelenskiy. They can't seem to ‚Äãget it together, but I think it was a positive call on that subject," Trump said.
Trump ‚Äãadded that Putin "wants to be helpful" ‚Äãwith the Iran conflict.
"I said, 'You could be more helpful ‚Äåby ‚Å†getting the Ukraine-Russia war over with. That will be more helpful,'" Trump told reporters in a press conference at ‚Äãhis Florida ‚Äãgolf club.
A ‚Å†foreign policy aide for Putin said on Monday that Putin proposed ‚Äãan idea for a "quick political ‚Äãand ‚Å†diplomatic end to the Iranian conflict."
Trump recently has expressed dismay about the ongoing conflict ‚Å†between ‚ÄãRussia and Ukraine, which ‚Äãstarted more than four years ago.

item.title: Russian drone injures six by apartment block in Ukraine's Kharkiv
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - A Russian drone ‚Äãstruck an area near a ‚Äåhigh-rise apartment building on Monday in Ukraine's second-largest city, Kharkiv, ‚Äãinjuring six people, smashing ‚Äãwindows and setting cars ablaze, ‚Å†the city's mayor said.
Ihor ‚ÄãTerekhov said a small child ‚Äãwas among the injured.
Kharkiv, located 30 km (18 miles) from the Russian ‚Äãborder, withstood early advances ‚Äãby Russian forces after their full-scale invasion ‚Äåin ‚Å†February 2022 and has since been a frequent target of Russian air attacks.
Separately, ‚ÄãRussian shelling ‚Äãof ‚Å†the southeastern city of Dnipro injured seven ‚Äãpeople, regional Governor Oleksandr ‚ÄãGanzha ‚Å†said on Telegram. Ganzha posted photos online showing rubble ‚Å†in ‚Äãstreets and damaged ‚Äãbuilding facades.

item.title: Americans expect gasoline prices to keep rising after Iran strikes, Reuters/Ipsos poll finds
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - Most Americans think gasoline prices are ‚Äãgoing to rise in coming months following President Donald Trump 's decision to launch military strikes on Iran , and ‚Äåmany expect a protracted conflict, according to a Reuters/Ipsos poll that closed on Monday.
Some 67% of respondents in the four-day poll - including 44% of Republicans and 85% of Democrats - said they expect gas prices in the U.S. to get worse over the next year. ‚ÄãSixty percent of Americans expect U.S. military involvement in Iran will "go on for an extended period of ‚Äãtime," according to the poll.
U.S. and Israeli forces launched coordinated strikes on Iran on February ‚Å†28, killing the nation's leader in an initial surprise attack.
The latest Reuters/Ipsos poll found just 29% of Americans ‚Äãapprove of the strikes, little changed from a 27% approval rate in a Reuters/Ipsos poll conducted in the hours immediately ‚Äãfollowing the start of the military campaign. Both polls had margins of error of about 3 percentage points.
The latest poll underscores the political risks Trump brings to his Republican Party ahead of the November midterm elections when Democrats hope to seize control from Trump's party ‚Äãof at least one chamber of Congress.
Some 64% of poll respondents - including one in four Republicans and nine ‚Äãin 10 Democrats - said Trump has not clearly explained the goals of U.S. military involvement.
Trump returned to the White House ‚Äålast ‚Å†year after promising to tame inflation and prevent the military from getting stuck in a foreign conflict. Since he launched strikes on Iran, U.S. gasoline prices have surged by roughly 50 cents a gallon and at least seven U.S. soldiers have been killed.
Gasoline prices are highly sensitive in the U.S., as one of the most immediate and visceral ‚Äãindications Americans have of increases ‚Äãand decreases in their ‚Å†cost of living.
Energy prices were rising daily last week across the country and internationally, including during the weekend as the Reuters/Ipsos poll was conducted. Analysts expect weeks or months of ‚Äãhigher fuel prices worldwide even if the week-old conflict ends quickly.
Trump is considering ways to ‚Äãcombat surging ‚Å†prices, but U.S. policy options could have limited sway over global oil markets. On Monday, he told reporters the military operation in Iran is ‚Äúahead of our initial timeline by a lot.‚Äù
Forty-nine percent of Americans - including a third of Republicans and two-thirds ‚Å†of Democrats - ‚Äãthink the war in Iran will have a mostly negative impact on ‚Äãtheir personal finances. About one in three Republicans said they were not sure how the war would affect their finances.
The Reuters/Ipsos poll, which ‚Äãwas conducted online, surveyed 1,021 U.S. adults nationwide.

item.title: Trump says he is 'disappointed' that Mojtaba Khamenei became Iran's supreme leader
item.description:
DORAL, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. ‚ÄåPresident Donald Trump said on Monday he was "disappointed" that ‚ÄãIran named Mojtaba ‚ÄãKhamenei to succeed his ‚Å†slain father Ayatollah ‚ÄãAli Khamenei as the ‚Äãsupreme leader of the country.
"We think it's going to ‚Äãlead to just ‚Äãmore of the same problem ‚Äåfor ‚Å†the country," Trump told reporters at a press conference in ‚ÄãFlorida.
When ‚Äãasked ‚Å†whether the new leader had a ‚Äãtarget on his ‚Äãback, ‚Å†Trump said it would be "inappropriate" to say ‚Å†whether ‚Äãor not ‚Äãhe does.

item.title: National Bureau of Economic Research cuts ties with Larry Summers, WSJ reports
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Former U.S. Treasury Secretary Larry Summers has lost his affiliation with The ‚ÄãNational Bureau of Economic Research, the ‚ÄåWall Street Journal reported on Monday, citing an email from the research organization's president.
Losing the ‚ÄãNBER affiliation would be the latest ‚Äãfallout for Summers from his ties with ‚Å†late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. ‚ÄãSummers said last month he would resign from ‚Äãteaching at Harvard University at the end of the academic year, months after receiving a lifetime ban ‚Äãfrom The American Economic Association.
NBER said ‚Äãan ad hoc committee reviewed Summers' conduct and recommended ‚Äåthe ‚Å†termination of his appointment as a research associate with the agency, the report said. "He is no longer an NBER affiliate," ‚Äãwrote NBER ‚Äãpresident James ‚Å†Poterba in an email to the Journal.
Documents released by the ‚ÄãU.S. House Oversight Committee showed a ‚Äãclose ‚Å†relationship between Summers and Epstein that continued well after the disgraced financier's 2008 ‚Å†conviction. ‚ÄãThere is no evidence Summers ‚Äãwas involved in Epstein's sex trafficking activities.

item.title: Australia to send missiles to UAE, deploy military surveillance aircraft
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 10 (Reuters) - Australia will deploy a military surveillance ‚Äåaircraft to the Middle East and send missiles to the United Arab Emirates but will not put troops on the ground in ‚ÄãIran, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said on Tuesday.
Australia's ‚Äãmilitary support would help the Gulf countries defend ‚Å†themselves against unprovoked attacks from Iran, Albanese said, stressing Australia ‚Äãwas "not a protagonist".
"Our involvement is purely defensive," Albanese told ‚Äãreporters. "And it's in defence of Australians who are in the region as well as in defence of our friends in the United ‚ÄãArab Emirates."
Albanese said Australia would deploy one of its ‚ÄãBoeing-manufactured E-7A Wedgetail airborne early warning and control system aircraft for ‚Äåan ‚Å†initial four weeks to protect the airspace above the Gulf countries.
Advanced medium-range air-to-air missiles would be provided to the United Arab Emirates, following a phone call with President ‚ÄãMohammed bin ‚ÄãZayed Al Nahyan, ‚Å†he added.
The war has effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz, a chokepoint for a ‚Äãfifth of global oil and liquefied natural ‚Äãgas, ‚Å†as the conflict escalated.
Around 115,000 Australians were in the Middle East when the conflict began 11 days ago and ‚Å†2,600 Australians ‚Äãhave now returned home by ‚Äãcommercial flights as several Gulf cities came under Iranian bombardment.

item.title: Trump says US is waiving certain oil-related sanctions to ensure supply
item.description:
DORAL, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump ‚Äãsaid on Monday that the ‚ÄåUnited States is waiving certain oil-related sanctions as a way to ensure ‚Äãadequate oil supply and lower ‚Äãprices.
At a news conference at his ‚Å†Doral golf club, Trump ‚Äãsaid oil prices have not spiked ‚Äãas much as he had feared.
Trump said the United States is waiving certain ‚Äãoil-related sanctions to cut ‚Äãprices.
"We have sanctions on some countries. We're ‚Äågoing ‚Å†to take those sanctions off till the Strait (of Hormuz) is up," Trump said.
He was not specific, ‚Äãbut the ‚ÄãUnited ‚Å†States last week issued a temporary, 30-day waiver ‚Äãto allow for the ‚Äãsale ‚Å†of Russian oil currently stranded at sea to India to alleviate ‚Å†pressure ‚Äãon the global ‚Äãoil market.

item.title: Petrobras rejects extra diesel orders as prices in Brazil lag global market
item.description:
RIO DE JANEIRO, March 9 (Reuters) - Brazilian state-run oil company Petrobras (PETR3.SA) , opens new tab is refusing requests from fuel distributors for additional diesel volumes as domestic prices fall to a record discount versus ‚Äãglobal levels, four sources familiar with the matter told Reuters.
Industry data shows that diesel ‚Äåsold by Petrobras, the main supplier of the fuel in Brazil , is currently 85% cheaper than imports, as global prices rise due to the U.S.-Iran conflict in the Middle East .
The imbalance between the two prices is stalling domestic diesel sales, impacting the country's ‚Äãbooming agricultural sector during harvest season, said sources.
Petrobras is selling only the contractually obligated diesel amount ‚Äãto its distributors, said a company source, adding that the firm won't sell extra ‚Å†so distributors can store it and make a profit when the state-run oil firm raises prices.
"We ‚Äãcannot hand out additional diesel now so distributors can stock up cheaply and then profit later," said the ‚Äãsource.
Petrobras CEO Magda Chambriard said last week the company does not pass short-term global volatility through to consumers and is still assessing the new oil price level before considering adjustments.
A spike in diesel prices would be the first and most immediate threat to Brazil's ‚Äãfarm sector from the Middle East conflict, raising costs for producers harvesting a record soybean crop and ‚Äãplanting corn they cannot afford to delay.
In Brazil, Petrobras is responsible for around 55% of diesel production, giving it outsized ‚Äåinfluence ‚Å†over the price of fuel, said Sergio Araujo, head of Brazilian fuel importers association Abicom.
The steep discount is drawing buyers away from imported cargoes and private refiners, reshaping fuel flows and creating logistical strains that are leading to supply imbalances, Araujo said.
The uncertainty is already rippling into the ‚Äãagricultural hub of Rio Grande ‚Äãdo Sul, a ‚Å†state with two refineries and normally ample diesel supply, where farmers have reported difficulty sourcing fuel.
Oil regulator ANP said on Sunday it would investigate complaints from rural ‚Äãproducers about supply constraints during the peak harvest period.
ANP said it contacted major ‚Äãsuppliers over ‚Å†the weekend and concluded that Rio Grande do Sul "has sufficient stocks to ensure regular diesel supply."
Araujo said the issue is not with supply, but with buyers in the region looking to purchase at current Petrobras prices, while sellers ask ‚Å†for higher ‚Äãprices to hedge against potential costs if the state-run firm ‚Äãadjusts them in the coming weeks.
"Sellers think they'll have to pay more to restock," said Araujo.
Petrobras did not immediately reply to a ‚Äãrequest for comment.

item.title: Democratic US senators demand immediate hearings on Iran war
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - Six Democrats in the U.S. Senate on Monday said they are embarking on an intensified campaign for public hearings on the Iran war, with top Trump administration officials testifying under ‚Äãoath and to take steps to interrupt regular Senate business if Republicans resist.
Senator Cory Booker ‚Äåof New Jersey told reporters that the senior Democrats on the Foreign Relations and Armed Services committees have made the requests to Republican chairmen James Risch and Roger Wicker, respectively.
The senators said their goal is to bring a quick end ‚Äãto the Iran war and protect American troops, who already have suffered some casualties.
Aides to the ‚Äãcommittees did not immediately comment.
Senator Chris Murphy of Connecticut said such hearings should be ‚Å†held next week, with Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Secretary of State and Acting National Security Adviser ‚ÄãMarco Rubio testifying.
Administration officials have held closed-door, classified briefings for Congress. Democrats argue that the American public needs ‚Äãto hear from them on the possible duration of the war, Trump's specific goals and related matters.
Republicans currently hold a narrow, 53-47 Senate majority, which gives them the power to control what legislation comes to the floor for debate.
But Booker noted ‚Äãthat individual senators have "a tremendous amount of power to disrupt the normal functioning of the Senate, as ‚Äãwell as certain privileges that we can exercise."
He said he would not detail the steps this group of senators is ‚Äåwilling to ‚Å†take.
Calling the Iran war "the biggest military engagement since the war in Afghanistan," Booker said, "We are not going to let the Senate continue as business as usual."
Both the House of Representatives, which also is run by Republicans, and the Senate have rejected Democrats' calls for debate and votes on the War Powers Act to authorize ‚ÄãU.S. military attacks on Iran.
Trump ‚Äãhas provided a series ‚Å†of rationales, often changing, for unilaterally launching the war.
The results of a Reuters/Ipsos poll released on Monday showed deep public discontent over the war.
Sixty percent of Americans expect ‚Äãthe U.S. military engagement to continue "for an extended period of time," causing gasoline ‚Äãprices to ‚Å†rise and only 29% of adults approve of the attacks, the poll found.
While Republicans mainly have stood by as President Donald Trump has taken actions that have bypassed Congress' powers to appropriate funding and to levy tariffs, ‚Å†Democrats have ‚Äãhad some successes in getting the Senate to stand up to ‚ÄãTrump on those issues.
Joining Booker and Murphy in this effort are Senators Tammy Duckworth of Illinois, an Iraq war veteran, Adam Schiff ‚Äãof California, Tammy Baldwin of Wisconsin and Tim Kaine of Virginia.

item.title: US says UN aid to Afghanistan needs evaluation
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Despite what it called a humanitarian "disaster" in Afghanistan, the U.S. said on Monday international assistance to the country should be evaluated, given Taliban "intransigence" and ‚Äãits exclusion of the female population from basic rights.
Speaking to a ‚ÄåU.N. Security Council meeting on Afghanistan, the U.S. ambassador to the United Nations , Mike Waltz, noted that the budget for the United Nations Assistance Mission in Afghanistan (UNAMA), the mandate of which is ‚Äãup for renewal next week, is the largest of any special U.N. ‚Äãmission in the world.
"In light of the Taliban's intransigence, we must ‚Å†carefully evaluate the utility of international assistance and engagement in Afghanistan," Waltz said, ‚Äãeven as he highlighted an ongoing "humanitarian disaster" there.
"This council must consider carefully the ‚Äãfunds we collectively provide for this mission's budget, when the mission's female national staff are not even able to go into the office to work," he added.
Afghanistan under the Taliban faces one ‚Äãof the world's most pressing humanitarian crises.
According to the U.N. World Food Programme, ‚Äãmore than 17 million Afghans - or one-third of the population - are facing acute food shortages, ‚Äåincluding 4.7 ‚Å†million facing emergency levels of hunger.
The temporary head of UNAMA, Georgette Gagnon, told the meeting Afghanistan had "urgent" humanitarian needs and the humanitarian crisis there had worsened due to funding cuts. She said humanitarian agencies aimed to assist 17.5 million Afghans ‚Äãin 2026 through an ‚Äãappeal for $1.71 billion, ‚Å†but this was currently only 10% funded.
Gagnon said Afghanistan's nearly two-week conflict with Pakistan had had "punishing human and economic costs" ‚Äãand the Iran war on its other border was causing ‚Äãprices of ‚Å†basic commodities to rise.
She said some positive developments showed the value of international engagement, including the Taliban ban on opium poppy cultivation. She warned that if rights and ‚Å†humanitarian issues ‚Äãwere not dealt with, Afghanistan could "once again become ‚Äãa driver of regional and global instability in the form of outmigration, terrorism, narcotics and more."

item.title: Indonesia says 7 killed in landfill collapse as rescue operation ends
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 10 (Reuters) - Indonesian authorities have ended rescue operations at a landfill collapse that killed ‚Äãseven people as all those who ‚Äåwere at the site have been accounted for, an official said on Tuesday.
A mountain of garbage collapsed ‚Äãon Sunday at the Bantargebang landfill, ‚Äãthe largest in the country, triggered by ‚Å†heavy rains.
Rescuers wrapped up the search ‚Äãoperation at the site on the outskirts of ‚ÄãJakarta, after finding the last of seven victims buried in the landfill, said Desiana Kartika Bahari, head ‚Äãof the local rescue agency. Six people ‚Äãsurvived the incident.
On Monday, the agency had said that four ‚Äåpeople ‚Å†were killed, four had survived and five were still missing.
"We received information from police that two among those missing were safe ‚Äãand had returned ‚Äãto ‚Å†their homes," Bahari said.
The casualties were truck drivers and owners of ‚Äãfood stalls at the landfill site, ‚Äãshe ‚Å†said.
More than 200 rescuers used excavators and thermal drones to find the victims during the ‚Å†search ‚Äãoperation.
Bantargebang spans around 110 hectares ‚Äãand receives 6,500 to 7,000 tons of waste every ‚Äãday.

item.title: Taiwan says US has not approached it about weapons transfers to Middle East
item.description:
TAIPEI, March 10 (Reuters) - The United States has not approached Taiwan about transfers of weapons to the Middle East, ‚ÄãDefence Minister Wellington Koo said on Tuesday, after ‚ÄåSouth Korea said it was in talks about the possible redeployment of some U.S. Patriot missile systems.
South Korean Foreign Minister ‚ÄãCho Hyun said on Friday the U.S. and ‚ÄãSouth Korean militaries are discussing the possible redeployment of ‚Å†some U.S. Patriot missile defence systems based in ‚ÄãSouth Korea to be used in the war against ‚ÄãIran.

item.title: South Korea president says can't stop US forces from redeploying weapons
item.description:
SEOUL, March 10 (Reuters) - South Korea President Lee Jae Myung said ‚Äãon Tuesday that his country ‚Äåcould not stop U.S. forces in Korea from shipping some weapons ‚Äãout of the Asian ‚Äãcountry, but that it would ‚Å†not impact deterrence against ‚ÄãNorth Korea.
"It appears that there ‚Äãis a controversy recently over U.S. Forces in Korea shipping some weapons ‚Äãout of the country," ‚ÄãLee said, noting while Seoul had expressed ‚Äåopposition ‚Å†it was not in a position to make demands.
South Korean Foreign Minister Cho Hyun ‚Äãsaid on ‚ÄãFriday ‚Å†the U.S. and South Korean militaries were ‚Äãdiscussing the possible redeployment ‚Äãof ‚Å†some U.S. Patriot missile defence systems based in South Korea ‚Å†to ‚Äãbe used in ‚Äãthe conflict in the Middle East.

item.title: Lawsuit challenges Trump administration's ending of protections for Somalis
item.description:
BOSTON, March 9 (Reuters) - Immigrant rights advocates filed a lawsuit on Monday seeking to stop U.S. President Donald ‚ÄãTrump's administration from next week ending legal protections that allow nearly 1,100 Somalis to live and ‚Äåwork in the United States.
The lawsuit , opens new tab , brought by four Somalis and two advocacy groups, challenges the U.S. Department of Homeland Security's decision to end Temporary Protected Status for Somali immigrants, whom Trump has derided in public remarks.
Outgoing Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem in January announced that TPS ‚Äãfor Somalis would end on March 17, arguing that Somalia's conditions had improved, despite fighting continuing between Somali forces ‚Äãand al-Shabaab militants.
The plaintiffs, who include the groups African Communities Together and Partnership for the ‚Å†Advancement of New Americans, in the lawsuit filed in Boston federal court argue the move was procedurally flawed and ‚Äãdriven by a discriminatory, predetermined agenda.
The lawsuit cites a series of statements Trump has made describing Somalis as "garbage" and "low ‚ÄãIQ people" who "contribute nothing."
The plaintiffs said the administration is ending TPS for Somalia and other countries due to unconstitutional bias against non-white immigrants, not based on objective assessments of country conditions.
"The termination of TPS for Somalia is racism masking as immigration policy," Omar Farah, executive ‚Äãdirector at the legal group Muslim Advocates, said in a statement.
DHS did not respond to a request for comment. It has ‚Äãpreviously said TPS was "never intended to be a de facto amnesty program."
TPS is a form of humanitarian immigration protection that shields eligible ‚Äåmigrants ‚Å†from deportation and allows them to work. Under Noem, DHS has moved to end TPS for a dozen countries, sparking legal challenges.
The administration on Saturday announced plans to pursue an appeal at the U.S. Supreme Court in order to end TPS for over 350,000 Haitians. It also wants the high court to allow it to end TPS for about 6,000 Syrians.
Somalia was first designated for ‚ÄãTPS in 1991, with ‚Å†its latest extension in 2024. About 1,082 Somalis currently hold TPS, and 1,383 more have pending applications, according to DHS.
Somalis in Minnesota in recent months had become a target of ‚ÄãTrump's immigration crackdown, with officials pointing to a fraud scandal in which many people ‚Äãcharged come from ‚Å†the state's large Somali community.
The Trump administration cited those fraud allegations as a basis for a months-long immigration enforcement surge in Democratic-led Minnesota, during which about 3,000 immigration agents were deployed, spurring protests and leading to the killing of two U.S. citizens by ‚Å†federal agents.
In ‚ÄãNovember, Trump announced he would end TPS for Somalis in Minnesota, ‚Äãand a month later said he wanted them sent "back to where they came from."
The U.S. Department of State advises against traveling to Somalia, citing crime ‚Äãand civil unrest among numerous factors.

item.title: North Korea's Kim Yo Jong says joint US-South Korea drills to harm regional stability
item.description:
SEOUL, March 10 (Reuters) - North Korea's Kim Yo Jong, ‚Äãthe sister of leader Kim Jong Un, ‚Äåsaid U.S.-South Korea military drills that began this week were a "provocative and aggressive war rehearsal" that would harm regional stability, state media ‚ÄãKCNA reported on Tuesday.
The annual Freedom Shield military ‚Äãexercises revealed the allies' "habitual hostile policy" toward North ‚Å†Korea and "will further destroy regional stability," Kim said in a ‚Äãstatement.
Kim described the drills in South Korea as involving ‚Äãmore than 18,000 South Korean and U.S. forces and being staged "day and night across the territorial ground, sea, air, ‚Äãouter space and cyberspace" of North Korea.
South Korea ‚Äãand the United States have said the drills, which run from ‚ÄåMarch ‚Å†9 to 19, were "defensive in nature" and would incorporate deterrence scenarios related to North Korea's nuclear weapons.
The exercise will also serve as an opportunity to support ongoing ‚Äãpreparations for ‚Äãthe transfer ‚Å†of U.S. wartime operational control to South Korea, officials from both countries have ‚Äãsaid.
South Korea aimed to complete the ‚Äãhandover ‚Å†of military command from the U.S. before President Lee Jae Myung's term ends in 2030.
The drills come ‚Å†a ‚Äãmonth after Kim Jong Un said ‚Äãat North Korea's ruling Party Congress that he would focus on expanding his ‚Äãcountry's nuclear arsenal.

item.title: Iran bets on endurance, energy disruption to outlast US, Israel
item.description:
DUBAI, March 10 (Reuters)- - Iran is wagering it can outlast the United States and Israel--not militarily, but by grinding the war into a brutal contest of endurance. Its strategy is stark: Unleash drones and missiles, cut vital energy routes and jolt global markets hard enough ‚Äãto force Washington to blink first.
Despite the shock of the U.S.‚ÄìIsraeli strikes and the loss of key figures, the Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps (IRGC)--long the ultimate guardian of the Islamic Republic -- is firmly ‚Äåin control, directing the battlefield, executing pre-planned contingencies and dictating strategy and targets in the war.
The IRGC also played the decisive role in elevating Mojtaba Khamenei as supreme leader after Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was killed in the opening U.S.‚ÄìIsraeli strikes.
‚ÄúFor them, they are waging an existential fight. This is an all-out war,‚Äù said Fawaz Gerges of the London School of Economics. ‚ÄúThey believe their very survival is at stake. They're willing to bring the temple down on everyone‚Äôs heads.‚ÄùAlex Vatanka, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute and expert on Iranian politics, added: ‚ÄúThey're ‚Äãlike a bleeding animal-- wounded, but therefore more dangerous than ever."
That all-out war mindset is behind Iran‚Äôs escalating strikes across the Gulf, targeting energy hubs from Qatar to Saudi Arabia to maximise economic disruption in ‚Äãa calculated attempt to drive up costs for its neighbours, Europe and the United States and test Washington‚Äôs political will.
U.S. President Donald Trump told Republican lawmakers on Monday the ‚Å†war would continue until Iran is "totally and decisively defeated" but predicted it would be over soon.
He added that once the United States is done with the military operation against Iran, Tehran will not have any weapons against the ‚ÄãUnited States, Israel and U.S. allies for a long time.
Iranian insiders say this escalation was anticipated long before the war began 11 days ago. Iranian planners assumed confrontation with Washington and Israel was inevitable, and prepared a layered strategy coordinated ‚Äãacross the Guards‚Äô sprawling military networks and proxy forces.
Now, with little left to lose, Iran is executing that plan and turning the conflict into a grinding war of attrition aimed at exhausting its adversaries politically and economically.
The consequences are already visible at home.
Mojtaba's selection as supreme leader, insiders say, proves the Guards‚Äô dominance as kingmakers. They say the balance of power has shifted. The supreme leader holds the title, but the future of the Islamic Republic, and the authority of the clerical establishment itself, now depends on whether the Guards can weather ‚Äãthe storm unleashed by the U.S.-Israeli campaign.
But a critical unknown in the war, says Mohannad Hage Ali, a senior fellow at the Carnegie Middle East Center, is how long the Guards can sustain its missile campaign, the backbone ‚Äãof its strategy against its adversaries.
U.S. officials say a large share of Iran‚Äôs arsenal has already been destroyed, but regional sources say Tehran may still retain more than half its pre-war stockpile. If that estimate holds, Iran could keep launching missiles for several ‚Äåmore weeks, ‚Å†a timeframe that could prove significant for Washington as economic pressure mounts at home and abroad.
The Guards‚Äô reach also extends far beyond the battlefield as it reshapes daily life. An Iranian observer said goods that once sat for weeks at ports are now cleared immediately. Paperwork comes later.
Officials described that as preparation for a war economy, ensuring supply lines keep moving under pressure, while also consolidating the IRGC‚Äôs control over the state and asserting continuity of governance.
Equally critical is internal stability. So far, there are no signs of protests, elite defections or fractures within the establishment, according to observers and contacts inside Iran.
An insider in Tehran described a city under bombardment but still functioning. ‚ÄúThe windows shake day and night,‚Äù the person said. ‚ÄúBut ‚Äãlife goes on.‚Äù Shops and banks remain open, supplies are ‚Äãavailable and most residents have not fled the ‚Å†capital.
The attacks, however, may be producing an effect opposite to what Washington and Israel intended, he noted. Despite long-standing grievances with the government, a surge of national solidarity is taking hold as strikes hit infrastructure and the possibility of internal insurgencies is openly discussed.
‚ÄúPeople are not prepared for Iran to disintegrate,‚Äù the source said.
For now, that sentiment may be buying ‚Äãthe leadership time. ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know if the regime will survive in the long term,‚Äù he added. ‚ÄúBut for the next couple of weeks, it will not collapse.‚Äù
For strategists on both sides, ‚Å†the war is increasingly defined by two parallel tests of endurance: whether Iran can keep firing missiles and whether the United States and Israel can sustain the economic, military and political costs of stopping them.
‚ÄúThe big question is who blinks first in this all‚Äëout war‚ÄîDonald Trump or Iran‚Äôs leaders?‚Äù Gerges said.
By driving up energy prices and spreading financial pain across Western economies, Tehran hopes the pressure will force a U.S. retreat.
Early signs are that the effects are already biting. Oil prices are spiking, gas ‚Å†costs are rising ‚Äãand political unease is growing in Washington as the economic fallout collides with November midterm elections.
Under that pressure, Trump, Gerges said, could eventually ‚Äãseek an exit by declaring victory, citing the killing of Iran‚Äôs supreme leader, the destruction of Iran's nuclear and missile capabilities and key military infrastructure.
For Tehran, however, survival alone would be enough.
Even if much of its strategic infrastructure is destroyed, Iran's leadership can claim triumph and survival against one ‚Äãof the greatest military armadas in history.
What emerges may be a wounded Iran, but a bleeding Iran could prove just as dangerous -- and perhaps more unpredictable -- than the establishment that entered this conflict.

item.title: Trump presses Republicans for voting restrictions ahead of midterm elections
item.description:
DORAL, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump, addressing Republican lawmakers directly on Monday, ‚Äãpressed them to enact sweeping new voting restrictions as a way to "guarantee" victory in November's midterm elections, saying he would refuse ‚Äåto sign new bills into law until they do.
The SAVE America Act would impose new voter restrictions including a requirement for proof of citizenship for people registering to vote. It first emerged two years ago in response to Trump's false claims that people in the country illegally were voting in U.S. elections.
"It'll guarantee the midterms. If you don't ‚Äãget it, big trouble, my opinion," Trump told House Republicans gathered at his Doral golf club in Miami to discuss the president's ‚Äãlegislative agenda.
Trump's Republican Party is heading into the campaign season facing political headwinds, and analysts say it could struggle ‚Å†to keep its slim majority in the House of Representatives as Democrats seize on voter unhappiness over the economy.
"I'm not going to sign anything ‚Äãuntil this is approved," Trump said. "If it takes you six months, I'm for not approving anything."
The legislation would require Americans to prove their citizenship while ‚Äãregistering to vote and show a photo ID before casting ballots. It would also restrict the use of mail-in ballots.
It is already illegal for non-citizens to vote in federal elections. Independent groups on the left and the right, as well as state election officials, have found such voting to be extremely rare .
Democrats say the legislation is intended to ‚Äãsuppress the vote and undermine their chances of taking control of the House in November. Democracy advocates say the legislation could perpetuate doubts about ‚ÄãU.S. election integrity and the outcome of the midterms.
The House passed the SAVE America Act last month but Trump wants Speaker Mike Johnson ‚Äåto revise ‚Å†it to add bans on transgender women in women's sports and transgender surgery for children, arguing that it will make it easier to pass Congress.
The latest version is still in the Senate. Under the current Senate filibuster rule, it would need 60 votes to pass. But Republicans have only a 53-47 seat majority.
Trump's speech ignored soaring energy costs from the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran , which has unsettled Republican lawmakers at a time when voters are already ‚Äãfrustrated with Trump's handling of the cost ‚Äãof living.
Instead, he urged Republicans ‚Å†to address affordability concerns by moving legislation that would codify his policies to lower drug prices and limit the participation of institutional investors in the housing industry.
Republicans are keenly aware that affordability, especially on healthcare and housing, ‚Äãis the overriding issue for voters in November. But Democrats hold a slight edge over Republicans on the ‚Äãcost-of-living issue, according to Reuters/Ipsos ‚Å†polling.
With the two parties locked in a stalemate over immigration reforms and funding for the Department of Homeland Security, House Republicans hope to find consensus in Florida on legislation that would use a parliamentary tool called budget reconciliation to circumvent Democratic opposition in the Senate.
Republicans used the same technique last year to pass ‚Å†Trump's " One Big ‚ÄãBeautiful Bill ," legislation that proved unpopular with the public and is expected to add $4.7 trillion ‚Äãto the U.S. deficit over 10 years. U.S. debt currently stands at $38.5 trillion.
House Republicans, who are promoting the law's tax cuts on tip and overtime income as affordability measures for American ‚Äãfamilies, believe another reconciliation bill would help win over voters.

item.title: China's property reset comes with a heavy price
item.description:
HONG KONG, March 10 (Reuters Breakingviews) - There is no doubt that China‚Äôs real-estate bubble needed popping. Beijing has spent nearly a decade letting air out of the country's historically speculative property market, which at times powered a quarter of the world‚Äôs second-largest economy. However, the structural distortions that fed the bubble still exist, while the cleanup is exerting a lasting drag on growth.
For years, real estate soaked up Chinese ‚Äãsavings, drove urbanisation and bankrolled local governments, who partly relied on land sales for income. Easy credit, perceived implicit state backing and a lack of attractive investment alternatives all pushed households and developers to ‚Äåbet on ever-rising prices. So entrenched was the mania that few people took Xi Jinping seriously in 2016 when the Chinese president declared that houses are for living in, not for speculation.
The real estate market only started buckling in 2020 when Beijing unleashed its ‚Äúthree red lines‚Äù policy, which limited developers‚Äô debt-fuelled growth by testing their borrowings against assets, equity and cash. By that point, the problem was acute. Floor space under construction amounted to over five times annual sales, implying a giant backlog of developments that would take years to shift, if they could even be sold at all.
Xi's ensuing crackdown ‚Äãupended the decades-old property order by deflating prices. In economic terms, the seismic shift faintly echoes the Communist Party‚Äôs early efforts, beginning in the 1940s, to reshape the distribution of wealth by uprooting the landlord class.
China Evergrande, ‚Äãa colossus of the boom years, went into liquidation in 2024 under the weight of $300 billion in liabilities. Its founding chairman Xu Jiayin, once listed as China‚Äôs wealthiest , opens new tab man, is under police control. ‚Å†Virtually all other major developers in the private sector have defaulted , opens new tab .
After much upheaval, China‚Äôs property debt reduction drive now seems to be over. Local media reported earlier this year that the "three red lines" policy has effectively been shelved.
The five‚Äëyear deleveraging campaign has proved ‚Äãtransformative. Property investment has declined from about 12% of GDP in 2021 to roughly half that share today. The financial system‚Äôs exposure to a sudden, uncontrolled real‚Äëestate blowup has therefore reduced.
The combined debt of the 46 largest developers listed in Hong Kong ‚Äãand mainland China stood at 5.19 trillion yuan ($753 billion) in 2025, down 17% since 2020, according to data from investment platform FSMOne. Much of the leverage reduction, FSMOne data shows, reflects up to $50‚ÄØbillion in offshore restructurings, referring to debts that were issued outside of mainland China. Creditors were forced to accept steep haircuts or swap their claims into equity.
Yet the real-estate bubble, despite being largely deflated, is having lasting consequences. Homebuyers' confidence has crashed, while a collapse in revenue has left developers in a worse position to service their debts. Contracted sales among the top 100 ‚Äãdevelopers last year plunged to 3.36 trillion yuan, down from 13‚ÄØtrillion yuan in 2020, based on figures from UBS. The combined net profit margin of 26 major listed developers fell from 10.7% in 2020 to -1.1% in 2024, according to UBS.
Worse, five years ‚Äãafter the start of the crackdown, the weakening property market continues to drag on growth. According to Andrew Batson of Gavekal Dragonomics, the sharp fall in construction and property investment shaved about 0.1 percentage points off China‚Äôs 5% GDP expansion last year.
After slipping into contraction ‚Äåin early 2022, ‚Å†property investment has now posted four straight years of declines, according to the National Bureau of Statistics. It culminated in a record 17.2% year‚Äëon‚Äëyear drop in December 2025. That compares with a more sedate 2.2% decline in infrastructure investment last year.
Property prices continue to fall, even though Beijing is trying to push in the other direction by nudging local governments to absorb excess inventory. Official data does not disclose the full scale of the slump, but new‚Äëhome prices in 70 major cities have fallen year‚Äëon‚Äëyear for 31 consecutive months as of January 2026, property research firm China Index Academy data shows. UBS analysts reckon the downturn could run to 2027 - even after a 40% national property price fall between 2021 and 2025.
The slump is eroding household wealth, prompting more ‚Äãprecautionary saving and less consumer spending. With no strong social ‚Äãsafety net and few attractive investment alternatives, many homeowners ‚Å†rushed to repay mortgages early, which counteracted the central bank's efforts to spur spending by injecting liquidity into the economy. Regulators responded with a raft of stimulus measures in September 2024, aimed at reinflating stock and property prices.
True, Xi‚Äôs administration has succeeded in channeling more funds into equities, partially dislodging households' historic obsession with property investment. The benchmark Shanghai SE Composite Index (.SSEC) , opens new tab has climbed ‚Äãabout 50% since September 2024.
But less than 10% of household wealth sits in equities, according to 2023 research by the China Academy of Social Sciences, versus nearly 70% in property ‚Äãand 20% in bank deposits and ‚Å†insurance policies. Savers remain jittery as the housing downturn eats into the value of their biggest asset. Even falling interest rates have not coaxed them out of cash. Household bank deposits soared to 166 trillion yuan last year, up 120% from 2020.
That has intensified calls for more forceful steps to halt the property slump. It's especially relevant now that policymakers have made higher consumption a strategic priority for the next five years. Households are unlikely to go out and spend more on goods and services if they feel their ‚Å†net worth is ‚Äãfading due to falling property prices.
A Communist Party journal in January even described real estate as having ‚Äúpronounced characteristics of a financial asset‚Äù. It's a tacit ‚Äãacknowledgment of the property market‚Äôs centrality to economic confidence, and reflects the fact that the forces that fed the bubble are still present.
During his annual policy readout to lawmakers, Premier Li Qiang last Thursday pledged that his administration would stabilise the real estate sector, by controlling new supply and reducing inventory. ‚ÄãThe lesson is that bursting bubbles is the easy part. Dealing with the fallout, and rebuilding confidence, is harder.

item.title: Egypt raises domestic fuel prices by up to 17% amid global energy turmoil
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Egypt raised prices on a wide range of fuel products on Tuesday, the petroleum ministry said, as the region continues to reel from rising global ‚Äãoil and gas prices and the disruption of Middle East output from ‚Äåthe U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
"This comes in light of the exceptional situation resulting from the geopolitical developments in the Middle East region and their direct effects on global energy markets", the ministry said ‚Äãin a statement.
The decision comes days after Egyptian Prime Minister Mostafa Madbouly ‚Äãsaid in a press conference on March 3 that the state might ‚Å†resort to "exceptional measures" if global fuel prices rise significantly because of the war.
Global oil ‚Äãand gas prices have been rising as the war has halted Middle East energy ‚Äãexports, with Tehran attacking ships and energy facilities, which has closed navigation in the Gulf and forced production stoppages from Qatar to Iraq.
The increases of about 14% to 17% on a wide range of ‚Äãpetroleum products are the first this year and follow a hike of nearly 10.5% ‚Äãto 12.9% in October. Egypt said at the time it was going to freeze domestic fuel prices ‚Äåfor at ‚Å†least a year, citing local, regional and global developments.
Prices for diesel, one of the most commonly used fuels in the country, were raised by 3 Egyptian pounds to 20.50 Egyptian pounds ($0.3887) from 17.50 pounds.
Gasoline prices increased by as much as 16.9%, depending on ‚Äãthe grade, with 80 ‚Äãoctane gasoline rising ‚Å†to 20.75 pounds per litre, while 92 octane rose to 22.25 pounds and 95 octane increased to 24 pounds.
Egypt has taken on ‚Äãback-to-back financing facilities with the International Monetary Fund since 2016, when ‚Äãit agreed ‚Å†a $12 billion loan programme to resuscitate its economy after years of political turmoil since the Arab Spring protests began.
Since then, the Washington-based lender has pushed the government to cut fuel, ‚Å†electricity ‚Äãand food subsidies while expanding social safety nets. Egypt ‚Äãagreed an expanded $8 billion loan programme with the IMF in March 2024.
($1 = 52.7400 Egyptian pounds)

item.title: Taiwan will see US natural gas imports increase from June
item.description:
TAIPEI, March 10 (Reuters) - Taiwan's government said on Tuesday that its natural gas imports from the United ‚ÄãStates will increase from June as it ‚Äåhas signed new supply contracts, adding that it was working to address the disruption caused by the war ‚Äãin the Middle East.
Taiwan's cabinet, in ‚Äãa statement following a meeting about how ‚Å†to deal with the impact of the war ‚Äãon energy and prices more broadly, said the ‚Äãeconomy ministry was tracking international crude oil conditions and inventory levels on a daily basis.
The ministry is also ‚Äãmonitoring shipping schedules for incoming cargoes, in ‚Äãorder to maintain a "full grasp of international crude oil ‚Äåand ‚Å†natural gas supply conditions".
Taiwan's domestic petroleum inventories and liquefied natural gas (LNG) inventories are both above the levels required by law, it added.
"In addition, ‚Äãnew international ‚Äãcontracts have ‚Å†been signed, and starting in June, imports of natural gas from the ‚ÄãUnited States will increase," the statement ‚Äãsaid, ‚Å†without giving details.
The ministry's plan also includes advanced cargo rescheduling, Asian regional cargo reallocation, and spot ‚Å†market ‚Äãpurchases, it said, adding there ‚Äãis sufficient domestic supply for March and April.

item.title: Punch the Japanese snow monkey captures hearts while kin face culls
item.description:
AZUMINO, Japan, March 10 (Reuters) - Punch the baby snow monkey and his stuffed orangutan have captured ‚Äãhearts , opens new tab around the world, spawned memes and even won over the White ‚ÄåHouse with their charm.
But in the wild, his fellow Japanese macaques often have a starkly different image: pests to be shooed away or even eliminated to prevent economic damage to farmers.
The ‚Äãagriculture ministry estimated that monkeys caused 770 million yen ($4.86 million) worth of ‚Äãdamage in 2024 - enough for Japan to sanction the capture and ‚Å†killing of thousands of primates every year.
The treatment of the monkeys is an ‚Äãissue that divides those who suffer the consequence of stolen crops and others who ‚Äãadvocate for a more humane solution.
"It's important to put countermeasures in place to prevent damage," said Takayo Soma, a primatologist at Kyoto University. "But it isn't very scientific to cull a certain number ‚Äãof monkeys without proper justification."
Killing a troop of monkeys only invites another to ‚Äãtake its place, rendering the practice ineffective and "never-ending", said Shigeyuki Izumiyama, a professor at Shinshu University.
Instead, some advocate ‚Äåfor ‚Å†non-lethal measures such as electric fences and "monkey dogs", or pet dogs that can be trained to chase the intruders away across an entire mountain.
Apple farmer Takumi Matsuda, one of few agriculturalists enamoured with snow monkeys, said humans need to recognise the ‚Äãrole they play in ‚Äãcausing the problem ‚Å†in the first place, such as by encroaching on the critters' natural habitat.
Matsuda has amassed a following on Instagram by ‚Äãsharing photos and videos he takes of the primates in ‚Äãthe mountains ‚Å†of Nagano prefecture, in central Japan, but said he also understands farmers' concerns.
"It's not that farmers hate the monkeys; they are worried about the impact on their livelihood," he ‚Å†said. "I ‚Äãreally hope Punch will be a starting point for ‚Äãa lot more people to go and see real Japanese macaques living in the wild."
($1 = 158.4800 yen)
(This story has been corrected to change the professor's first name to Shigeyuki, not Shigeki, in paragraph 6)

item.title: Earthquake of magnitude 6.1 strikes Tyrrhenian Sea near Italian coast, GFZ says
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - An earthquake ‚Äãof magnitude ‚Äå6.1 struck the ‚ÄãTyrrhenian ‚ÄãSea off the ‚Å†west coast ‚Äãof ‚ÄãItaly on Tuesday, the German ‚ÄãResearch ‚ÄãCentre for Geosciences (GFZ) ‚Äåsaid.
The ‚Å†quake was at a ‚Äãdepth ‚Äãof ‚Å†377 km (234 ‚Äãmiles), ‚ÄãGFZ ‚Å†said.

item.title: Iran says oil blockade will continue until attacks end, Trump threatens to hit harder
item.description:
DUBAI/CAIRO/WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Iran's Revolutionary Guards said on Tuesday they would not allow "one litre of oil" to be shipped ‚Äãfrom the Middle East if U.S. and Israeli attacks continue, prompting a warning from President Donald Trump that the U.S. would hit Iran much harder if it ‚Äåblocked exports from the vital energy-producing region.
Trump's comments came after global financial markets seesawed on Monday on concerns that Iran's security establishment was rallying behind new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei and was not prepared to back down any time soon.
Trump said the United States had inflicted serious damage on Iran's military and predicted the conflict would end well before the initial four-week time frame he had laid out, though he has not defined what victory would look ‚Äãlike.
Israel says its war aim is to overthrow Iran's system of clerical rule. U.S. officials mainly say Washington's aim is to destroy Iran's missile capabilities and nuclear programme, but Trump ‚Äãhas said the war can end only with a compliant Iranian government.
At least 1,332 Iranian civilians have been killed and thousands wounded since the ‚Å†U.S. and Israel launched a barrage of air and missile strikes across Iran at the end of February, according to Iran's U.N. ambassador.
Trump warned that U.S. attacks could rise sharply if Iran ‚Äãsought to block tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, which handles one-fifth of the world's oil supply.
‚ÄúWe will hit them so hard that it will not be possible for them or anybody else ‚Äãhelping them to ever recover that section of the world," Trump said at a news conference on Monday.
Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps said it would not allow any oil to leave the region if attacks from the United States and Israel continue.
"We are the ones who will determine the end of the war," a spokesperson said, according to state media.
In a later Truth Social post, Trump repeated his ‚Äãwarning.
"If Iran does anything that stops the flow of Oil within the Strait of Hormuz, they will be hit by the United States of America TWENTY TIMES HARDER than they have been ‚Äãhit thus far," he said.
The war has already effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz, leaving tankers unable to sail for more than a week and forcing producers to halt pumping as storage facilities fill.
Mojtaba Khamenei's appointment ‚Äåon Monday appeared ‚Å†to dash hopes of a swift end to the war, sending oil markets surging and share markets nosediving, before swinging in the other direction when Trump predicted a quick end to the war and reports of a possible ease in sanctions on Russian energy.
After speaking with Russian President Vladimir Putin, Trump said the United States will waive oil-related sanctions on "some countries" to ease the shortage. According to multiple sources, that could mean a further easing of sanctions on Russian oil, which could complicate efforts to punish Moscow for its war in Ukraine. Other options include a possible release of oil from strategic ‚Äãreserves or restricting U.S. exports, sources said.
Brent crude ‚Äãfutures fell more than 10% on Tuesday ‚Å†after soaring by as much as 29% on Monday to their highest since 2022. Global stock markets also bounced.
The price of gasoline has particular political resonance in the United States, where voters cite rising costs as a top concern ahead of the November midterm elections, when Trump's Republicans will try to ‚Äãkeep control of Congress.
A Reuters/Ipsos poll released Monday found 67% of Americans expect gas prices to rise over the coming months, and only 29% ‚Äãapprove of the war.
Tehran was choked in black smoke after an oil refinery was hit, an escalation in strikes on Iran's domestic energy supplies. World Health Organization chief Tedros Ghebreyesus warned the fire risks contaminating food, water and air.
Turkey said NATO air defences had shot down a ballistic missile that was fired from Iran and entered Turkish airspace , the second such incident of the war. Iran did not immediately comment on the report.
Israel's military ‚Å†said it ‚Äãhad launched new attacks in central Iran and struck the Lebanese capital Beirut, where Israel has extended its campaign after ‚Äãthe Iran-backed militia Hezbollah fired across the border.
In Australia, five Iranian women's soccer team players were granted humanitarian visas after they sought asylum fearing persecution in their home nation. Canberra has also promised to send military surveillance aircraft to the Middle East and ‚Äãmissiles to the United Arab Emirates to help them defend themselves against attacks from Iran.

item.title: Insight: White supremacist content grips teens plotting attacks in Southeast Asia
item.description:
SINGAPORE/JAKARTA, March 10 (Reuters) - When police detained an Indonesian teenager accused of bombing his high-school campus in Jakarta in November, he had a life-size toy rifle inscribed with "welcome to hell" and the names of white supremacist mass killers.
The November 7 attack, which injured 96 people, may have been the first in the country inspired by white supremacists but police fear it won't be the last.
At least 97 youths - the youngest just 11 - are being monitored after coming ‚Äãunder the influence of content glorifying mass violence and white supremacists spread largely on messaging app Telegram, Indonesian police told Reuters in March.
At least two were planning acts of violence following the Jakarta bombing, according to the police.
And it's not just Indonesia. Across Southeast Asia - home to hundreds of millions ‚Äåof people of different ethnicities and faiths - police are grappling with a surge in teenagers plotting violence inspired by white supremacists such as Christchurch mosque attacker Brenton Tarrant, according to interviews with security officials in Indonesia, Singapore, Malaysia, Thailand and the Philippines.
Singapore's domestic intelligence agency has detained four youths since December 2020 on grounds that they subscribed to "violent far-right extremism ideologies" and were planning attacks. Far-right extremism has since been named by the city-state's Internal Security Department (ISD) as a top threat.
None of the teenagers Singapore and Indonesia are monitoring are white. Some were plotting attacks they believed would protect the existing racial and religious composition of their countries, according to ISD statements on the detentions. Others, three Indonesian security officials say, were inspired by the violence of far-right attackers, even if they didn't have similar grievances.
In every instance in Singapore and Indonesia reviewed by Reuters, the ‚Äãteenagers were alleged by authorities to have been radicalized through social media posts and communities.
Many of the young people who have been detained or placed under monitoring appear to be disillusioned and lonely individuals "turning towards a nihilistic worldview after being radicalized by far-right messaging", said Pravin Prakash, who researches Southeast Asia at the Center for ‚Äãthe Study of Organised Hate, a Washington think-tank.
The Jakarta suspect, according to Indonesian authorities, had posted online video footage of his campus alongside Nazi symbols and text that appeared to be inspired by "Highway to Hell" from the rock band AC/DC: "Don't need no reason, ain't nothing ‚Å†I'd rather do. I am on the highway to hell and all my friends are going to be there."
Telegram groups, in particular, had provided the young people with a sense of belonging, according to Indonesian police.
That platform often doesn't take action on content that authorities have reported as extremist, said police commissioner Mayndra Eka Wardhana, a spokesperson for the counter-terrorism squad.
Telegram ‚Äãspokesperson Remi Vaughn said in response to questions that the platform "has an open channel of communication with Indonesian authorities" and "removes any content that breaches Telegram's terms of service whenever reported."
Telegram "supports the right to peaceful free speech, but calls to violence are explicitly forbidden," Vaughn added.
Southeast Asian security and police agencies are coordinating efforts, marking the first regional cooperation on this type of ‚Äãradicalization, according to officials from Singapore and Indonesia.
All the Indonesian teens authorities identified as being radicalized were affiliated with the "true crime community", a popular internet subculture.
In channels linked to the community, users share memes and other content that glorifies killers like Tarrant, whose name was found on the Jakarta suspect's toy rifle, according to screenshots shared with Reuters by police and a separate review of four such groups.
Some online posters also traded bomb-making tutorials and egged each other on toward violence, screenshots of their conversations show.
White supremacist content has also spread across other platforms, though often with a localized twist. Posts, for instance, may feature Southeast Asian iconography alongside Nazi symbols.
Reuters viewed hundreds of such videos from Southeast Asian users on TikTok showcasing racist caricatures of Chinese people and other minorities such as Rohingya Muslims alongside phrases like "TCD," or "Totally Cheerful ‚ÄãDay" and "TRD," or "Total Refreshing Day."
The phrases appear to be code calling for "Total Chinese Death" or "Total Rohingya Death", said Saddiq Basha of Singapore's S. Rajaratnam School of International Studies (RSIS), who has tracked such content since 2024.
One popular video by an Indonesian user featuring the hashtag #TCD has been viewed over 542,000 times. The creator did not respond to a request for comment.
Western white supremacist ‚Äãgroups have used phrases like "TND/Totally Nice Day" and "TJD/Totally Joyful Day" to advocate the extermination of black and Jewish people, according to anti-discrimination groups like the Anti-Defamation League.
