TikTok removed the Indonesian user's post, as well as similar content identified by Reuters, after the news agency sent the platform questions about its moderation policies.
"There is no place on our platform for those dedicated to spreading beliefs or propaganda ‚Äåthat encourage violence or ‚Å†hate," a company spokesperson said.
Two people working on online-safety teams at TikTok told Reuters they were not familiar with the existence of policies on moderating posts that featured localized takes on white supremacist slogans and had been unaware of such content. They were interviewed on condition of anonymity because they were not authorized to speak to media.
The TikTok spokesperson said the platform blocks "certain keywords from appearing as search suggestions to reduce their visibility if we find that they are being used as coded language" and consults with Southeast Asian advisors on online safety.
Tech companies have focused on moderating Islamist content in Southeast Asia, sometimes to the point where they fail to account for other extremist posts, said Chasseur Group director Munira Mustaffa, who has advised Southeast Asian governments and social media platforms on combating extremism.
"While the concept of neo-Nazism lies in the assertion that the white race reigns supreme, these ideas are easily adaptable into local context," she said, adding that teens who successfully carry out attacks believe they will gain status in their online communities.
Among the youths authorities say were radicalized by algorithms is Nick Lee Xing Qiu, who ‚Äãwas detained last year by the ISD as an 18-year-old on suspicion of plotting ‚Äãattacks against Singapore's Malay Muslim minority.
The agency said algorithms on unspecified platforms had recommended ‚Å†far-right extremist content to him.
Reuters couldn't reach Lee, who is being held under a law that permits his detention without trial. The news agency also couldn't identify a legal representative to direct questions to.
Lee and another teenager, who was separately detained and has not been named, self-identified as "East Asian supremacists", ISD said in statements about their cases.
The youths had in their online posts referenced the neo-Nazi "great replacement theory" - which posits that white populations are being forcibly replaced by minorities - and claimed to be inspired to fight back, according to ISD.
Mayndra, the Indonesian ‚Äãcounter-terrorism official, said authorities were worried that teenagers radicalized by the violence of extremist content could be targeted by "terror groups" for recruitment.
Many of the teenagers in detention or under monitoring in Indonesia and Singapore are under the age of majority or have not ‚Äãsuccessfully perpetrated acts of violence.
The Jakarta bombing suspect, for ‚Å†instance, is being held by child protective services while authorities construct their case, according to police spokesperson Budi Hermanto.
The suspect has not been charged or entered a plea, the official said.
"My hope, if it's possible, is do not punish him, just give him counselling so he can be a better person," Rudianti, a family member of the Jakarta suspect who goes by one name, told Reuters.
Indonesia this month announced plans to restrict social-media access for children under the age of 16, in a move that Mayndra said would also help combat youth radicalization though it was not a complete solution.
In Singapore, authorities have turned to the Religious Rehabilitation Group (RRG) to work with some teenagers detained for plotting far-right attacks. The non-profit was set up by Muslim scholars in 2003 to rehabilitate suspected Islamist ‚Å†militants and is staffed ‚Äãby volunteer educators.
The group counsels young detainees and prepares them for national exams, said Ahmad Helmi Bin Mohamad Hasbi, an RRG counsellor and expert on radicalism at RSIS.
RRG worked with Singapore's first far-right extremist detainee, who was ‚Äãheld in 2020 at the age of 16 for allegedly planning machete attacks on two mosques. He was released from rehabilitation in 2024.
Groups like RRG will have to contend, however, with the speed at which some Southeast Asian extremists are gaining influence globally.
Just a month after the Jakarta bombing, a 15-year-old Russian was accused of stabbing a Tajik migrant child to death in the Moscow area.
The Russian had written a manifesto, which was published on ‚ÄãTelegram and authenticated by researchers with the Global Project on Hate and Extremism, a U.S.-based non-profit.
In it, the Russian suspect labelled the Indonesian teen a hero. He also argued that if non-white youths could execute such attacks, white supremacists should be capable of more.

item.title: Vietnam urges people to work from home to save fuel as Iran war disrupts supplies
item.description:
HANOI, March 10 (Reuters) - Vietnam's trade ministry has called on local businesses to encourage their employees to work ‚Äãfrom home as part of efforts to save ‚Äåon fuel amid supply disruptions and price surges triggered by the Iran war.
Vietnam has been among the countries hardest ‚Äãhit by fuel disruptions since the U.S.-Israeli ‚Äãwar on Iran began, being heavily reliant on energy imports ‚Å†from the Middle East, the government said in ‚Äãa statement on Tuesday, citing a report from ‚Äãthe Ministry of Industry and Trade.

item.title: Hundreds killed in Haiti drone strikes, including 60 civilians, Human Rights Watch says
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Explosive drone strikes by Haitian security ‚Äãforces targeting gangs have killed over 1,200 people, including 43 adult civilians and 17 children , Human Rights ‚ÄåWatch said in a report on Tuesday, adding that operations have ramped up in recent months.
Since last March, Haitian security forces, with support from Vectus Global, have carried out anti-gang operations using quadcopter drones strapped with explosives, often in densely populated parts of the capital ‚ÄãPort-au-Prince. Vectus Global is a private U.S.-based military company led by Blackwater founder Erik Prince .
Haiti's prime minister's office, defense ‚Äãminister, national police, Vectus, and the U.S. State Department did not immediately respond to requests ‚Å†for comment. The U.S. charge d'affaires in Haiti told a Senate committee last month that the State Department had ‚Äãlicensed Vectus to export its services to Haiti.
A spokesperson for the U.N.-backed Gang Suppression Force in Haiti declined to comment.
HRW's Americas ‚Äãdirector, Juanita Goebertus, told Reuters that the rights group had documented unlawful use of lethal force, saying Haiti's partners should stop collaborating with its security forces until they implement safeguards to protect civilians.
"Haitian authorities should urgently rein in the security forces and private contractors working ‚Äãfor them before more children die," she said.
Despite Kenyan, U.S. and U.N. support , armed gangs have expanded well beyond the capital ‚Äãand security forces have yet to capture a major gang leader . The gangs have killed thousands , displaced over a million and crippled the economy.
Drawing ‚Äåfrom ‚Å†interviews with doctors, victims' relatives, community leaders and videos of the strikes, HRW found drone strikes killed 1,243 people between last March and January 21 of this year.
The strikes also injured 738 people, including 49 alleged civilians, it added, noting that from November to January there were nearly double the number of drone operations compared with the prior three months.
Some ‚Äãlocal residents told HRW they ‚Äãwere afraid to leave ‚Å†their homes due to the drones, which can maneuver between buildings and moving vehicles while their controllers track suspects using live video feeds.
Of the child victims, more than half were ‚Äã3-to-12-year-olds killed last September in an attack on a sports center where a local gang ‚Äãwas distributing gifts, ‚Å†HRW said.
The U.N. office in Haiti (BINUH) has also recorded dozens of civilian deaths and injuries from drones, including a mother of three killed while selling goods on the street, and another case of a woman who was killed in her own home ‚Å†where two ‚Äãgang members took refuge from a drone.
Last month, BINUH said it ‚Äãhad no indications the deaths and injuries were being investigated.
HRW said there was no evidence drones were being used widely by gangs.
The U.N.'s human rights ‚Äãchief said last October the drone strikes were disproportionate and likely unlawful , opens new tab .

item.title: Trump crackdown on protests and immigration led to Islamophobia, Muslim group says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Record ‚Äãhigh Islamophobia in the U.S. in 2025 was driven in part by President Donald ‚ÄåTrump's crackdown against pro-Palestinian protests and immigration , a Muslim advocacy group said on Tuesday.
The Council on American-Islamic Relations said it recorded 8,683 anti-Muslim and anti-Arab complaints in 2025, the highest since it began publishing data in 1996, compared ‚Äãto 8,658 in 2024.
Most complaints were about employment discrimination (12.7%), immigration and asylum (6.5%), hate incidents (6.4%), ‚Äãand travel discrimination like government watchlists and screenings (5.6%), CAIR's report noted.
Over the ‚Å†years, advocates have attributed Islamophobia to the September 11, 2001 attacks ; and more recently to anti-immigration sentiment , white ‚Äãsupremacy and the fallout of Israel's war in Gaza .
Trump denies being discriminatory and says his administration ‚Äãaims to curb illegal immigration and improve domestic security. Advocates say the immigration crackdown is unsafe and violates due process.
CAIR noted Trump's targeting of Somali Americans in Minnesota, a majority Muslim community, who he accused of fraud ‚Äãand called " garbage ."
CAIR said the government used isolated cases for collective targeting and dismissed Trump's ability ‚Äãto tackle fraud, citing pardons to those with past fraud convictions .
CAIR's Minnesota chapter reported 693 complaints, up from 353 in 2024.
CAIR ‚Äåalso ‚Å†noted targeting of Afghan immigrants after two National Guard soldiers were shot in Washington in November for which an Afghan was indicted.
Trump alleges pro-Palestinian protesters are antisemitic and sympathize with extremists.
His administration has attempted to deport foreign protesters, threatened freezing funds for universities where protests were held, and ordered ‚Äãaggressive screening of immigrants' online ‚Äãcomments .
Protesters, including some Jewish ‚Å†groups, say he wrongly conflates criticizing Israel's assault on Gaza and occupation of Palestinian territories with antisemitism, and advocating for Palestinian rights with supporting extremism.
"The ‚ÄãTrump administration framed anyone who holds pro-Palestinian views as inherently threatening," ‚ÄãCAIR said.
Notable ‚Å†deportation attempts were against Mahmoud Khalil , who was detained for months while his wife was pregnant, and Leqaa Kordia , who lost over 170 family members in Gaza and was briefly hospitalized following a seizure in ‚Å†detention.
CAIR raised ‚Äãfree speech, due process and academic freedom concerns. Trump's ‚Äãcrackdown has faced judicial roadblocks , opens new tab .
Republican governors in Florida and Texas have signed orders designating CAIR as a "terrorist" group. CAIR sued over the ‚Äãdesignations. A judge has blocked Florida's order.

item.title: Iranian women soccer players' hotel escape aided by Australian police
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 10 (Reuters) - Australian police extracted five Iranian women soccer players from the ‚Äãteam's hotel before they were granted asylum , the interior minister said on Tuesday, as details of their ‚Äåescape from Iranian government minders emerged.
The five players, including team captain Zahra Ghanbari, sought protection after the team were branded "wartime traitors" for refusing to sing their national anthem before an Asian Cup match.
The team's Asian Cup campaign began as the U.S. and Israel ‚Äãlaunched air strikes on Iran and Australian media reported the team had been accompanied by Iranian government ‚Äãofficials who were controlling their movements.
Conversations with the players about seeking asylum had been ongoing ‚Å†for several days, Interior Minister Tony Burke told a press conference as he confirmed the women had been granted asylum in Australia.
The ‚Äãplayers granted asylum were Ghanbari, Zahra Sarbali Alishah, Mona Hamoudi and Atefeh Ramezanizadeh - all in their early 30s - as well ‚Äãas 21-year-old Fatemeh Pasandideh.
The five players were moved to a safe location by the Australian Federal Police on Monday evening, where they remain under their protection, Burke said.
Even before their defection, Australia had deployed its own officers to protect the women.
"There's been a ‚Äãgood police presence at different points and we just made sure that opportunity was there," he said.
Once immigration ‚Äãofficials completed the processing of the women's humanitarian visas around 1:30 a.m. on Tuesday (1530 GMT Monday), celebrations broke out among those present.
"Once everything had been ‚Äåsigned ‚Å†off last night, there were lots of photos, lots of celebrating, and then a spontaneous outcry of ‚ÄòAussie, Aussie, Aussie, oi, oi, oi‚Äô," Burke said.
"These women are great athletes, great people, and they're going to feel very much at home in Australia."
Four of the players are teammates at the Bam Khatoon club, which has won the Iranian women's championship ‚Äãa record 11 times and ‚Äãis where Ghanbari also ‚Å†played until she moved to Persepolis for this season.
Captain Ghanbari was suspended for several days in 2024 after her hijab, the head covering that all Iranian women players must wear, slipped off during a goal ‚Äãcelebration in an Asian Champions League fixture.
The 33-year-old striker, Iran's record international goalscorer in ‚Äãthe women's ‚Å†game, was allowed to return to play only after she and Bam Khatoon issued apologies.
Ghanbari's head scarf also slipped off her head several times during Iran's final Asian Cup match against the Philippines on Sunday, when defeat ended their participation ‚Å†in the ‚Äãtournament.
Burke said the offer of asylum remained open for the other 21 ‚Äãmembers of the squad who were still at the Gold Coast hotel, though he said it was likely some would return home to Iran.
"These ‚Äãwomen have been weighing up an incredibly difficult decision," he said.

item.title: Eleven people died in US immigration custody this year, ICE says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - At least 11 immigrants have died in U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement custody from January 2026 through early March, according to ICE. That follows 31 deaths last year, a two-decade high.
Emanuel Cleeford Damas
Died: March 2 at a hospital in Scottsdale, Arizona
Nationality: Haitian
ICE took Damas into custody after he was arrested on assault and battery charges in September 2025 in Boston, ICE said. Damas was transferred to the Florence Detention Center in Florence, Arizona. ICE said he reported shortness ‚Äãof breath on February 19 and was sent to a local hospital and then transferred to a Phoenix hospital and placed in an intensive care unit.
He was intubated on a ventilator the following day and ‚Äådied on March 2, with the cause unknown, ICE said. In an interview with the Associated Press, his brother said he had died due to an untreated toothache and related infection.
In response to a request for comment, the U.S. Department of Homeland Security referred to ICE's public statement, which did not mention any dental issue.
Pejman Karshenas Najafabadi
Died: March 1 at a hospital in Natchez, Mississippi
Nationality: Iranian
Age: 59
ICE took Karshenas into custody in April 2025 after he was convicted of fentanyl possession, ICE said. He had several chronic health conditions but remained detained and was transferred in October 2025 to ‚ÄãLouisiana ICE Processing Center in Angola, a facility opened under the Trump administration, according to ICE.
On February 20, 2026, ICE health staff evaluated him again and he was then transferred to a hospital for long-term care, where he ‚Äãwent into cardiac arrest on March 1. He was transferred to the Merit Health Hospital in Natchez, Mississippi, where he was pronounced deceased, ICE said.
Alberto Gutierrez Reyes
Died: February 27 at a ‚Å†medical center in Victorville, California
Nationality: Mexican
Age: 48
Gutierrez was arrested by U.S. Border Patrol agents in Los Angeles in January, ICE said. He reported feeling faint on February 25 and was admitted to Victor Valley Global Medical Center for chest pain and shortness ‚Äãof breath, ICE said. He became unresponsive and died on February 27, ICE said.
Los Angeles City Council member Eunisses Hernandez said in a statement that her office had been in touch with his family and that he had been denied medical care while in ‚ÄãICE custody.
In response to a request for comment, the Department of Homeland Security said comprehensive medical care is provided to everyone in ICE detention.
Jairo Garcia Hernandez
Died: February 16 at a hospital in Miami
Nationality: Guatemalan
Age: 27
ICE took Garcia into custody after he was encountered by local police near Rochester, New York, on January 21, 2025, the agency said. ICE said he was immunocompromised and was "already in ill health" when he was detained. On February 16, 2026, more than a year after he was taken into custody, he collapsed unexpectedly and died, according to ICE.
Lorth Sim
Died: February 16 at ‚Äãthe Miami Correctional Facility in Miami County, Indiana
Nationality: Cambodian
Age: 59
Sim entered the U.S. as a refugee in 1983 but had been ordered deported following several convictions, ICE said. He was detained after arriving at an ICE office in Boston on December 30, 2025, ‚Äãand then transferred to the Indiana detention center, the agency said.
ICE staff found Sim unresponsive in his cell on February 16 and he was pronounced dead by staff and emergency personnel, ICE said, adding that the incident was under investigation.
Victor Manuel Diaz
Died: January 14 at Camp ‚ÄåEast Montana in ‚Å†El Paso, Texas
Nationality: Nicaraguan
Age: 36
Staff at the detention center, which is located on the grounds of Fort Bliss, found Diaz unconscious and unresponsive in his room on January 14 and he was pronounced dead shortly after, ICE said. ICE said the incident was under investigation but that he died of "presumed suicide."
Heber Sanchaz Dom√≠nguez
Died: January 14 at the Robert A. Deyton Detention Center in Lovejoy, Georgia
Nationality: Mexican
Age: 34
Sanchaz was detained by ICE after being arrested in Georgia for driving without a license, ICE said. He was found "hanging by the neck and unresponsive in his sleeping quarters" and later pronounced dead at an area hospital, ICE said. The agency said the incident is under investigation.
Parady La
Died: January 9 at a hospital in Philadelphia
Nationality: Cambodian
Age: 46
ICE said La was being detained at the Federal ‚ÄãDetention Center in Philadelphia after a January 6 arrest and ‚Äãwas being treated for "severe drug withdrawal" when he was ‚Å†found unresponsive in his cell. He was transferred to Thomas Jefferson University Hospital where he was diagnosed with brain and organ failure and declared deceased on January 9, ICE said.
Luis Beltran Yanez-Cruz
Died: January 6 at a hospital in Indio, California
Nationality: Honduran
Age: 68
Beltran was arrested by federal immigration officers in Newark, New Jersey, in November, according to ICE. At some point, he was moved to ‚Äãthe Imperial Regional Detention Facility in Calexico, California. On January 4, he complained of chest pain and was transferred to a hospital where he died two days later, ICE said.
Luis ‚ÄãGustavo Nunez Caceres
Died: January 5 at a ‚Å†Houston-area hospital
Nationality: Honduran
Age: 42
Nunez was arrested during an immigration operation in Houston on November 17, 2025, ICE said. He was eventually taken to Joe Corley Processing Center in Conroe, Texas.
He was transferred to HCA Houston Healthcare in Conroe "due to an exacerbation of congestive heart failure" on December 23, 2025, ICE said. After "multiple life-threatening medical emergencies," he was pronounced dead on January 5, ICE said.
Geraldo Lunas Campos
Died: January 3 at Camp East Montana detention center in El Paso, Texas
Nationality: Cuban
Age: 55
Lunas died on January 3 in Camp East Montana, a detention site ‚Å†opened by the ‚ÄãTrump administration on the grounds of Fort Bliss in Texas, according to ICE. The agency initially said he experienced "medical distress" and that they were investigating ‚Äãthe incident.
The Washington Post reported later that month that the local coroner would likely rule the death a homicide. The Post also interviewed a detainee held at the Camp East Montana site who said he saw guards choking Lunas when he refused to enter solitary confinement.
After the report, the Department of Homeland Security issued ‚Äãa new statement , saying Lunas attempted to take his own life while detained at the detention site.
DHS said the security staff "immediately intervened to save his life" but that Lunas "violently resisted" the security staff.

item.title: US solar installations down in 2025 after Trump policies jolt market, report says
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - The U.S. solar market added fewer new installations in 2025 compared to the year before, a study showed on Tuesday, pointing to cooling momentum ‚Äãacross the sector after President Donald Trump scrapped subsidies and tax ‚Äåbreaks for renewable energy developers.
The solar market installed 43 gigawatts in new capacity last year, compared to nearly 50 GW in 2024, according to a study by the Solar Energy ‚ÄãIndustries Association (SEIA) and Wood Mackenzie.
The administration's One Big Beautiful Bill Act has ‚Äãled to an industry-wide disruption, with utility-scale solar installations declining 16% ‚Å†and community solar declining 25% in 2025, the report said.
The solar industry is ‚Äãcontending with tariff pressures and a freeze on approvals for major projects under ‚Äãthe current administration, reflecting an energy agenda focused on oil, gas, coal and nuclear and marking a shift from Joe Biden's green policies.
Still, solar and energy storage accounted for 79% of ‚Äãnew capacity additions in the first year of the Trump administration, with ‚Äãmore than two-thirds of installations occurring in states won by him, the report said.
Texas led with ‚Äå11 ‚Å†GW of new solar capacity, followed by Indiana, Florida, Arizona, Ohio, Utah and Arkansas.
Solar power remains economically competitive, especially as electricity demand from data centers dedicated to AI surges to record highs, the report said.
"Washington must deliver policy certainty for ‚Äãthe market to ‚Äãwork and to ‚Å†keep pace with growing energy demands," said SEIA interim President and CEO Darren Van't Hof.
"Without this certainty, less solar will ‚Äãget built and Americans will pay the price with higher ‚Äãenergy bills."
The ‚Å†report estimates the U.S. would add 490 GW of new solar capacity by 2036, taking the cumulative installed capacity to nearly 770 GW.
"It's clear that solar will ‚Å†continue to ‚Äãbe the dominant source of new power capacity ‚Äãin the U.S., even as gas generation continues to grow," Michelle Davis, head of solar at ‚ÄãWood Mackenzie, said in the report.

item.title: US Supreme Court to hear Guam hazardous waste explosions case
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - The U.S. Supreme Court agreed on Monday to hear a bid by President Donald Trump 's administration to dismiss a challenge by environmentalists to the U.S. Air ‚ÄãForce's practice of detonating hazardous waste explosives on a beach in Guam.
The justices agreed to hear the Justice Department's appeal ‚Äåof a lower court's ruling allowing a lawsuit pursued by the environmental groups Prutehi Guahan and Earthjustice accusing the Air Force of ignoring a requirement under federal law to assess the environmental impact of a practice like this one.
Since 1982, the Air Force has disposed of hazardous munitions such as tear gas and propellants on ‚ÄãTarague Beach, a restricted-access location in Guam, a U.S. territory roughly 3,800 miles (6,100 km) from Hawaii that acts as an anchor for ‚Äãmilitary operations in the Western Pacific.
Tarague Beach serves as a nesting habitat for the endangered turtles and sits ‚Å†above an aquifer that provides more than 80% of the island's population with drinking water.
The U.S. government, under both Trump and his predecessor ‚ÄãJoe Biden, has sought to have the lawsuit thrown out. After a federal trial judge dismissed the lawsuit, the San Francisco-based 9th U.S. Circuit Court ‚Äãof Appeals reinstated it last year.
The government has said that the arguments made by the plaintiffs would burden the U.S. military and federal agencies with unnecessary environmental permitting requirements that Congress never contemplated when it passed the mandates at issue.
The Air Force did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
"Now that the Supreme Court ‚Äãhas decided to hear the case, we will continue to defend Guam residents' ability to protect their health, their land and their resources," Earthjustice ‚Äãlawyer David Henkin said.
The dispute concerns the National Environmental Policy Act, a law from 1970 requiring federal agencies to produce "environmental impact statements" - documents that assess the ‚Äåenvironmental consequences ‚Å†of proposed major federal actions.
In 2021, the Air Force asked Guam regulators to renew a permit to conduct open burning and open detonation of hazardous waste at Tarague Beach, located near Andersen Air Force Base. Prutehi Guahan, an environmental protection nonprofit in Guam, challenged the legality of the move in court. It is represented in the case by lawyers at Earthjustice.
Prutehi Guahan argued that, before seeking the permit renewal, the Air Force was ‚Äãlegally required to assess the environmental ‚Äãimpact of its waste disposal practices ‚Å†and propose alternatives. The suit seeks a court order requiring the Air Force to produce an environmental impact statement.
A federal judge in Guam dismissed the case, saying Prutehi Guahan lacked standing to sue. But a 9th ‚ÄãCircuit three-judge panel ruled 2-1 to revive the case, agreeing with Prutehi Guahan that the Air Force ‚Äãneeded to conduct ‚Å†an environmental impact assessment before submitting a permit-renewal application.
In appealing to the Supreme Court, the Justice Department argued that the Air Force did not need to comply with the National Environmental Policy Act because it had followed steps mandated under a different law, the Resource Conservation and Recovery Act of 1976, ‚Å†which regulates ‚Äãthe disposal of hazardous waste.
The 9th Circuit's decision threatens to "inflict significant burdens on both federal ‚Äãcourts and federal agencies in the form of premature and duplicative judicial and administrative proceedings, and to impede state and federal agencies' performance of their permitting responsibilities," the Justice ‚ÄãDepartment wrote.
The Supreme Court is due to hear the case during its next term, which begins in October.

item.title: New Mexico investigators search Epstein's former ranch
item.description:
STANLEY, New Mexico, March 9 (Reuters) - Investigators in New Mexico began searching on Monday the former high-plains ranch of Jeffrey Epstein where the late financier and his acquaintances ‚Äãare accused of sexually abusing women and girls, state authorities said.
Democratic-run New ‚ÄåMexico is acting on new information in documents released in January by the U.S. Department of Justice, including an accusation that Epstein ordered the bodies of two foreign girls buried in hills near the ‚Äãsecluded property.
The search follows New Mexico Attorney General Raul Torrez's decision last month ‚Äãto reopen the investigation into the late sex offender's alleged criminal activities at ‚Å†the ranch 30 miles (48 km) south of the state's capital of Santa Fe.
"The New ‚ÄãMexico Department of Justice will continue to keep the public appropriately informed, support the survivors, ‚Äãand follow the facts wherever they lead," it said in a statement.
On Monday, a Reuters witness heard dogs barking and saw a government vehicle bearing a paw-print symbol, suggesting it was carrying animals, ‚Äãleave the ranch. State police and a county fire and rescue vehicle were also ‚Äãspotted.
The Epstein files have become a persistent political problem for President Donald Trump.
New Mexico closed its previous Epstein ‚Äåinvestigation ‚Å†in 2019 at the request of federal authorities. There has never been a full investigation of the alleged assaults by Epstein, his partner Ghislaine Maxwell and ranch visitors.
Last month, New Mexico became the first U.S. state to launch a legislative "truth commission" to uncover possible ‚Äãpublic corruption that allowed ‚ÄãEpstein to operate ‚Å†in secrecy at the ranch for 26 years before his death in 2019.
Epstein's estate sold the property in 2023 to Texas businessman ‚ÄãDon Huffines who renamed it San Rafael Ranch.
The new owners are ‚Äãcooperating with ‚Å†the investigation and granted access for the search, the department said.
The January 30 release of millions more files exposed Epstein's social connections with politicians, business people and scientists who he invited ‚Å†to the ‚Äãranch.
Reuters/Ipsos polling shows most Americans view the Epstein case ‚Äãas an example of wealthy and powerful people rarely being held accountable.

item.title: Taiwan's Formosa Petchem issues force majeure as Iran war disrupts feedstock delivery
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 10 (Reuters) - Taiwan's Formosa Petrochemical Corp (FPCC) has issued a force majeure notice on some of ‚Äãits petrochemical supplies due to feedstock delivery delays stemming ‚Äåfrom shipping disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz, a spokesperson said on Tuesday.
Products affected include ethylene and propylene, said FPCC spokesperson ‚ÄãKY Lin.
"Our No.2 and No.3 crackers are still operating ‚Äãat around 70% for now," Lin said, adding ‚Å†that the refiner is considering shutting one cracker if "naphtha ‚Äãfeedstock does not come at all".
Steam crackers break down feedstocks ‚Äãsuch as naphtha into smaller molecules like ethylene and propylene, used to make plastics.
FPCC's No.2 and No.3 crackers at its Mailiao site ‚Äãcan produce up to 1.335 million metric tons per ‚Äãyear of ethylene, while its No.1 cracker is currently not in operation .
For refined ‚Äåfuel ‚Å†products such as diesel, jet fuel and gasoline, the company is still proceeding with its export deliveries for March and April, although there is likely to be no spot ‚Äãavailability for ‚ÄãApril, Lin said.
As ‚Å†of today, the refinery is processing around 490,000 barrels per day (bpd) of crude, although ‚Äãthe situation could change as some crude ‚Äãdeliveries "will be ‚Å†affected" after March 20, he added.
FPCC is slated to shut its No.2 crude unit for a scheduled maintenance in the ‚Å†next ‚Äãfew days, Reuters had earlier reported.
The ‚Äãprivately owned refiner has a total crude processing capacity of 540,000 bpd.

item.title: Quality is key as some coal rallies amid LNG's spike on Iran war
item.description:
LAUNCESTON, Australia, March 10 (Reuters) - The surge in the spot price of liquefied natural gas (LNG) has dragged seaborne thermal coal prices higher, but only for the higher quality grades that can substitute for natural gas in power generation.
The spot price of LNG in Asia more than doubled last week as ‚Äãthe market digested the loss of nearly 20% of the global supply of the super-chilled fuel after the U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran effectively closed the Strait of ‚ÄåHormuz , shutting off Qatar's LNG.
LNG for delivery to North Asia jumped 116% to a two-year high of $22.50 per million British thermal units (mmBtu) in the week ended March 6.
The surge in LNG prices has opened the window for gas-to-coal switching in Japan and South Korea, the two Asian countries with the biggest ability to arbitrage between the fuels used to generate electricity.
Japan and South Korea predominantly buy Australian thermal coal with an energy content of 6,000 ‚Äãkilocalories per kilogram (kcal/kg), and the index of this fuel at Newcastle Port , as assessed by commodity price reporting agency Argus, rose to $129.62 a metric ton in the week to ‚ÄãMarch 6.
This was a 14-month high and up 11.6% from the week to February 27, which was the day before the U.S. and Israel ‚Å†launched an aerial campaign against Iran, prompting Tehran to fire missiles and drones at targets across the Persian Gulf region.
EUROPE GAINS
The price of thermal coal heading to Europe, the other region where ‚Äãgas-to-coal switching can happen, also increased, with McCloskey assessing 6,000 kcal/kg coal from South Africa's Richards Bay port at $113.00 a ton on Monday, up 14.3% from $98.90 on February 27.
It's worth noting that the ‚Äãgains in seaborne thermal coal are considerably smaller in percentage terms than the rise in spot LNG prices.
This is likely because there is no threat to the supply of thermal coal from major exporters such as Australia, South Africa, Indonesia and Colombia.
But it's also the case that while the economics of gas-to-coal switching are currently attractive, the ability to do so is constrained by a lack of available coal-fired capacity, especially in Europe.
For example, ‚ÄãSpain retired 13.18 gigawatts (GW) of coal-fired capacity from 2000 to 2025 without adding any new plants, while Germany shut down 33.57 GW of capacity while adding just 13.69 GW, according to data ‚Äãfrom the Global Energy Monitor.
Japan retired almost 1,200 megawatts (MW) of coal-fired capacity in the past three years, without adding any new units.
While South Korea has boosted its coal-fired capacity in recent years the government has formally ‚Äåcommitted to ‚Å†a long-term phase-out of the fuel, planning to close 40 of 61 units by 2040.
The longer-term policy doesn't preclude a switch to more coal in the short term if LNG prices remain elevated.
It's still too early to ascertain if countries that can burn more coal for electricity are lifting imports, although analysts DBX Commodities are estimating that the European Union's seaborne thermal coal imports will rise to 2.17 million tons in March from 2.01 million in February.
Japan's March imports of seaborne thermal coal are estimated by DBX at 9.52 million tons, down from 10.18 million in February but up ‚Äãfrom 9.22 million in March last year, while ‚ÄãSouth Korea's arrivals are forecast at 6.12 ‚Å†million in March, up from 5.05 million for the same month in 2025.
CHINA, INDIA
It's also worth noting that the world's two largest coal importers, China and India, don't usually engage in gas-to-coal switching for power generation.
China's gas-fired generation is a tiny 3% of total electricity production, while coal is ‚Äãjust under 60%. For India, natural gas accounts for about 2.8% of electricity generation and coal is around 70%.
While high LNG prices will ‚Äãlikely curb imports in both ‚Å†China and India, it's unlikely to result in higher coal imports as rising domestic production will be able to compensate.
This dynamic can be seen in the prices of seaborne coal grades favoured by China and India.
Australian coal with an energy content of 5,500 kcal/kg, which is popular with Chinese utilities and Indian industrial users such as sponge iron producers, rose a modest 1.4% in the week to March 6 ‚Å†to $87.87 a ton, ‚Äãaccording to Argus.
Indonesian coal with an energy content of 4,200 kcal/kg performed better, rising 5.1% to $58.21 a ton in ‚Äãthe week to March 6, but this was still well below the gain for higher-quality grades.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI) , your essential new source for global financial commentary. ROI delivers thought-provoking, data-driven analysis of everything from swap rates ‚Äãto soybeans. Markets are moving faster than ever. ROI can help you keep up. item.title: Aramco posts drop in annual profit, announces its first buyback
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Saudi Arabia's Aramco (2222.SE) , opens new tab , the world's top oil exporter, reported a 12% drop in annual ‚Äãprofit mainly due to lower crude prices, but announced it ‚Äåwould repurchase up to $3 billion worth of shares in its first-ever buyback.
The buyback program will be conducted over the next 18 months. Until now, the company has relied ‚Äãon its massive dividend payouts to reward shareholders.
The results come ‚Äãat a highly volatile time for global oil markets as ‚Å†the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran has led to a near-closure of the ‚ÄãStrait of Hormuz and has forced several regional producers to curb output.
Brent ‚Äãcrude, which surged to near $120 on Monday, is trading around $93 on Tuesday.
Aramco reported $93.4 billion in net income for 2025, below an LSEG consensus estimate of $95.6 billion.
For the fourth ‚Äãquarter, net profit tumbled 20.5% to nearly $17.8 billion on higher operating ‚Äãcosts, marking its 12th consecutive quarter of year-on-year profit decline.
Aramco confirmed paying a base ‚Äådividend of $21.1 ‚Å†billion for the fourth quarter and $219 million in performance-linked dividends, a mechanism calculated based on free cash flow introduced following bumper profits in 2022 following the Ukraine war.
Total dividends paid for the year were $85.5 billion, down ‚Äãfrom $124 billion in ‚Äã2024.
Aramco has long ‚Å†been a cash cow for the Saudi state, which relies on fossil fuels for more than half of government ‚Äãrevenues. The kingdom directly holds nearly 81.5% of the ‚Äãcompany and ‚Å†its sovereign investor, the Public Investment Fund, holds another 16%.
Total revenue for the year fell 7.2% to $415.8 billion, on weaker prices for crude oil, as ‚Å†well ‚Äãas refined and chemical products.
The company's gearing ‚Äãratio - a measure of indebtedness ‚Äî dropped to 3.8% at the end of 2025, down from 4.5% ‚Äãat the end of 2024.

item.title: Thailand orders bureaucrats to use stairs and work from home in energy saving drive
item.description:
BANGKOK, March 10 (Reuters) - Thai Prime Minister Anutin Charnvirakul has ordered civil servants to conserve energy amid an energy squeeze brought on by the conflict ‚Äãin the Middle East, a government spokesperson said on Tuesday, ‚Äåwith measures including suspending overseas trips and using stairs instead of elevators.
"The prime minister ordered that starting from today civil servants will work from home," spokesperson Lalida ‚ÄãPeriswiwatana told reporters, adding that exceptions would be made for officials ‚Äãwho must serve the public.
Southeast Asia's second-largest economy has around 95 ‚Å†days of energy reserves left, and it has been seeking additional sources ‚Äãof liquefied natural gas from the United States, Australia and South Africa, ‚Äãaccording to Energy Minister Auttapol Rerkpiboon.
But it is urgently trying to reduce demand. Lalida said other government measures to conserve energy include setting air conditioning temperatures at 26 ‚Äãto 27 degrees Celsius (78.8 to 80.6 Fahrenheit) and wearing short-sleeved shirts instead ‚Äãof formal attire like suits and ties.
Nearly 68% of Thailand's energy needs are met ‚Äåfrom ‚Å†natural gas. Over half of its liquefied natural gas is sourced from domestic production in the Gulf of Thailand, while 35% comes from imports, including 13% from neighbouring Myanmar, according to the country's Energy Regulatory Commission.
Earlier ‚Äãthis month, Thailand ‚Äãalso halted energy exports ‚Å†to all countries other than Laos and Myanmar.
Government officials have been ordered to reduce electricity use at office ‚Äãbuildings by switching off lights and electrical equipment when ‚Äãnot needed, ‚Å†Lalida said. The government will also ask the public to cooperate and encourage energy saving measures such as carpooling.
"If the situation escalates, the government may ‚Å†consider ‚Äãmandatory measures including dimming advertising signs at ‚Äãshops, cinemas and businesses and closing gas stations at 10 p.m.," the government also said in ‚Äãa statement.

item.title: Disturbance hits submarine power link between Finland and Sweden
item.description:
COPENHAGEN, March 10 (Reuters) - A ‚Äådisturbance has occurred on the Fenno-Skan 2 ‚Äãpower connection between ‚ÄãFinland and Sweden, Finnish grid ‚Å†operator Fingrid said ‚Äãon Tuesday.
"The cause of ‚Äãthe failure is being investigated," Fingrid said in a ‚Äãstatement.
Fenno-Skan 2 is ‚Äãa 800 megawatt (MW) high-voltage direct ‚Äåcurrent ‚Å†electricity transmission link between Finland and Sweden.
The outage was expected ‚Äãto last ‚Äãfor ‚Å†around 18 hours, according to ‚Äãa separate regulatory ‚Äãstatement ‚Å†published by Swedish grid operator Svenska kraftnaet ‚Å†on ‚Äãthe Nord Pool ‚Äãmessaging platform.

item.title: How oil shock and financial stress can feed each other
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - Central banks are clearly watching the Iran war oil shock like hawks. But even if inflation is their main concern, it's not the only one - and a worst case for some top policymakers is that the crude price surge proves a breaking point for multiple financial stress fractures.
The surge in oil prices, driven by supply disruption from more than a week of war in the Middle East, is already challenging central banks' stretched mandates .
An age-old question is ‚Äãwhether oil spikes that lift inflation and inflation expectations ultimately crimp household and business finances so much that they depress demand and prices. And then there's the toxic scenario in which they do both, leaving policymakers with ‚Äåa conundrum of whether to prioritize taming inflation or supporting consumers and jobs.
Hawks say act quickly on prices and the hit to demand will lessen, particularly at central banks where price stability is the main or only goal. Others suggest "looking through" volatile inflation, much like central banks did post-pandemic - mistakenly, in hindsight.
A blizzard of "ifs" and "buts" informs all that - from how policy was positioned before the shock, to the scope for government subsidies or energy price caps, to the duration of the conflict and supply outage.
The weight of those uncertainties will likely argue for sitting tight ‚Äãand watching events and markets unfold for a bit before jumping to conclusions.
But there's another consideration under a third hat most major central banks now wear - financial stability.
BOND MARKET TERMITES
Senior officials fear that some ‚Äãof the excesses and behavioural trends they have been monitoring in financial markets for several years could be exposed by a mega-macro disturbance in energy, inflation, interest rates, currencies and generalised ‚Å†volatility.
The risk of a perfect storm is keeping some awake at night.
Of the many issues watchdogs have put on their radars in recent years, four stand out - and they typically involve "shadow banks" outside the traditional banking system and their growing role in lending to ‚Äãfirms and governments.
This includes the sharp rise in private credit funds - which have topped $3 trillion globally - where asset managers effectively lend directly to businesses. Without the public glare of bond market pricing or traditional bank lending norms, what happens ‚Äãunder the bonnet of these vehicles during a shock still unnerves many.
Regulators fear that this lack of transparency could trigger sudden investor rushes to exit these funds, with ripple effects for borrowers and, ultimately, for the banks that still help finance or manage many of these vehicles.
Arguably a bigger source of anxiety is the rising share of government debt now financed by highly leveraged hedge funds . Unease has been building for years about the scale of their activity in vital securities repurchase, or repo, markets and in today's gigantic U.S. government bond arbitrage trades, which exploit small ‚Äãgaps between cash and futures pricing with huge leveraged bets.
While these players may help smooth government financing, they also create significant vulnerabilities to shocks - and, once again, the stress ultimately feeds back to the real economy via the sizeable exposure of traditional banks that lend ‚Äãto these funds.
In January, for example, the G20's Financial Stability Board , opens new tab homed in on repos and flagged the potential hit to sovereign bonds from a sudden deleveraging by cash borrowers. It also warned of inadequately priced counterparty risk, often with zero haircuts on ‚Äåsovereign bonds for ‚Å†repos, and highlighted cross-border spillover risks. More than $16 trillion in repo backed by government bonds was outstanding last year, with some 60% of that in America.
Two other areas of concern have been the build-up of so-called stablecoins - crypto tokens pegged to the dollar or other currencies using assets in reserve - and their emergence as major holders of sovereign debt .
With the $300 billion market set to rise further, any disturbance to that ecosystem - or run on these tokens - could force an unwind of the bonds and assets backing them. In the meantime, there's a risk they rob banks of deposits .
And that's not to mention a long-standing concern among savers, investors and lenders about the overvalued and hugely concentrated artificial intelligence universe.
How might all those fragilities be affected by the war in the Middle East? One ‚Äãobvious area is the behaviour of giant regional oil-wealth and ‚Äãsovereign funds; another is a more traditional dash for the liquidity of ‚Å†dollar cash rather than paper or physical assets.
But the two main routes would be a surge in volatility in equity and debt and a dramatic change to the interest rate outlook from an inflationary energy price surge.
We have already seen rumblings of the latter over the past week with rising government borrowing rates and jolts to central ‚Äãbanks' interest rate horizons. But the disturbance doesn't yet appear to be at destabilising levels.
Yet since this sort of financial stability is now part of all central banks' remit, ‚Äãit raises a question over how well they ‚Å†could cope with a shocking, even viral, disturbance from a geopolitical event like this.
A further question is whether consideration of these financial risks might work against a firm and decisive central bank response to any inflation threat.
While the Ukraine invasion and the related inflation and interest rate jolts back then did pass without too much incident - at least beyond a dire year or two for asset price returns - there was also the tremor of the U.S. regional bank shakeout in 2023.
No two shocks are identical, ‚Å†however, and triple-digit ‚Äãoil and the pressure for a central bank response are unlikely to pass without impact.
(The opinions expressed here are those of the author, ‚Äãa columnist for Reuters.)

item.title: Aramco sees 'catastrophic consequences' for oil if shipping doesn't resume in Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
DUBAI, March 10 (Reuters) - Saudi Arabia's Aramco (2222.SE) , opens new tab , the world's ‚Äåtop oil exporter, said on Tuesday that there would be "catastrophic consequences" for the world's oil markets if the Iran war continues to disrupt shipping in the Strait ‚Äãof Hormuz.
The disruption has not only upended the shipping and ‚Äãinsurance sectors but also promises to have drastic domino effects ‚Å†on aviation, agriculture, automotive and other industries, Aramco CEO Amin Nasser told ‚Äãreporters on an earnings call.
Nasser noted global inventories of oil were at ‚Äãa five-year low and said the crisis will lead to drawdowns at a faster rate, adding that it was critical that shipping in the strait resumed.
"There would ‚Äãbe catastrophic consequences for the world‚Äôs oil markets and the longer the ‚Äãdisruption goes on, and the more drastic the consequences for the global economy," he ‚Äåsaid.
Nasser ‚Å†also said a small fire from an attack last week on Aramco's Ras Tanura refinery, its largest domestically, was quickly extinguished and brought under control, adding that the refinery was in the process of being restarted.
Iran's ‚ÄãRevolutionary Guards said on ‚ÄãTuesday they would ‚Å†not allow "one litre of oil" to be shipped from the Middle East if U.S. and Israeli attacks continue, ‚Äãprompting a warning from President Donald Trump that the ‚ÄãU.S. ‚Å†would hit Iran much harder if it blocked exports from the vital energy-producing region.
His comments come after Aramco reported a 12% drop in annual profit mainly ‚Å†due ‚Äãto lower crude prices. It also announced ‚Äãit would repurchase up to $3 billion worth of shares in its first-ever buyback.

item.title: Spain's Repsol hikes payout targets, reduces investments
item.description:
MADRID, March 10 (Reuters) - Spanish energy ‚Äãgroup Repsol (REP.MC) , opens new tab pledged on Tuesday ‚Äåto give shareholders between 30% and 40% of the cash ‚Äãflow it generates from ‚Äãits operations through 2028 via dividends ‚Å†and buybacks while slowing down ‚Äãinvestments.
The payout move its in ‚Äãline with the company's strategic plans in the past years, which had ‚Äãshareholder returns as a ‚Äãkey priority.
In its previous plan, the ‚Äåcompany ‚Å†had set a 25%-35% target for remuneration.
In coming years, Repsol will slow down investments after ‚Äãyears of ‚Äãsignificant ‚Å†capital expenditures.
The company sees net investments through 2028 ‚Äãat between 7.5 and ‚Äã9 ‚Å†billion euros. This is down from a 16 to ‚Å†19 ‚Äãbillion euros range ‚Äãin its previous multi-year plan.

item.title: 'Maybe we die together': Voices at the Iran-Turkey mountain crossing
item.description:
KAPIKOY, Turkey, March 10 (Reuters) - At a remote mountain pass in eastern Turkey, travellers from Iran step across the threshold with a mix of fear, exhaustion and relief - arriving after a week marked by war, long journeys by train or car, communications blackouts and borrowed phones.
Snow-covered hills surround the Iranian side of the frontier at the Kapikoy border gate in Turkey's Van province, where families and lone travellers emerge, many after days on the road. Hundreds have ‚Äãcrossed in recent days and there is now a steady flow in both directions as the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran expands across the region.
Some say they fled because bombs were falling in their cities. Others decided ‚Äåto leave after losing contact with loved ones, travelling by land when flights were cancelled. Most carry only small suitcases, a phone without a local SIM card, and a determination to finish an already punishing journey.
New arrivals ask how to reach Van, the nearest city two hours away, and how to reconnect to the outside world. Some journalists hand over their phones so travellers can reassure family that they are safe. Each person has a different reason for leaving ‚Äî work drying up, a sick relative to visit, or a life interrupted by conflict.
Ebrahim Eidi, 61, had recently been ‚Äãin Tehran and said many Iranians were waiting to see whether the government was weakened enough for protests to erupt. He said some believed opposition figure Reza Pahlavi ‚Äî the son of Iran's last shah ‚Äî could become a rallying point ‚Äãif large demonstrations begin.
"People are waiting for something to happen, for some change. They want the government to change completely, and many people say they are waiting for Reza Pahlavi."
Eidi left ‚Å†Iran 34 years ago for the Netherlands, where he works with asylum seekers and refugees in camps. He returns to Iran roughly once a year. This time, he said, the situation felt different.
He said many people chose to stay in Iran because ‚Äãthey still had hope for the country. But they feared their own power was not enough to change the government, and that the situation could spiral.
"People are afraid to go to the streets. They are not afraid of America. Unfortunately, they are afraid of ‚Äãtheir own government."
Leila, 45, was travelling in the opposite direction, heading back into Iran. After losing contact with her family in Shiraz, she decided to return from Istanbul, where she occasionally assists academics working with a German historical research institution.
"How can I be safe when I feel my family maybe they are in danger?"
One of her brothers is seriously ill and in a coma, increasing her worry. For her, being physically with her family - even in danger - felt more bearable than waiting abroad.
She plans to remain in Iran until the war is over.
"I cannot guard them against bombs. ‚ÄãBut when I feel I can be with them together, maybe we die together, or I can help them as long as we are alive."
Hamid Shirmohammadzadeh, 35, had travelled back to Iran shortly before the war and fled with his ‚Äãwife and children when Tehran came under attack. He had been building a life in Tokyo, working for an import-export firm, but returned to Iran after his visa lapsed. His wife and two children had remained there.
"The day before the war started I was in Tehran‚Ä¶ ‚ÄåThen the bombs ‚Å†started falling. We saw the war had begun, so we came to Turkey."
His wife, son and daughter ‚Äî aged 9 and 10 ‚Äî are waiting near the border as he seeks help from Japanese authorities to secure visas for them.
‚ÄúI worked in Japan, paid my taxes and followed the rules. I don‚Äôt understand why they are not helping me now.‚Äù
‚ÄúIn times like this we should help each other. I need help now because my country is at war.‚Äù
Mohammad Soltanzadeh, who lives in Hamburg and is originally from Afghanistan, had been visiting relatives in Iran's Mashhad when flights were cancelled, forcing him onto the road.
"The journey was very exhausting. We were on the train for about 24 hours, then we travelled four hours by car and finally came by taxi."
He described Mashhad as calm despite ‚Äãthe wider turmoil.
"People were a bit saddened and mourning but ‚Äãthey were not stressed. The shops were open, the ‚Å†markets and shopping centres were open and people were continuing their activities. Life was going on."
Egyptian factory worker Mohammad Fauzi, 46, crossed from Iran with no Turkish SIM card, no local currency and no knowledge of the language. He only had the phone numbers of two Egyptian friends in Ankara and Izmir - and a plan to reach Cairo.
He had watched work grind to a halt ‚Äãin Iran during his three months there working in the marble and granite sector, with many factories closing.
"The situation is very difficult and working has stopped. I can't work, I ‚Äãcan't stay because the situation is ‚Å†dangerous now, so I want to go to my home, my country."
Jalileh Jabari, 63, said she fled Tehran because "bombs are falling" and the situation had become unbearable. The highways to the border were calm, but uncertainty in the capital pushed her to leave. She was travelling to Istanbul, where her daughter studies.
"If things become good there, if Iran becomes good, I will come back. If there is peace, I will return."
Two sisters, Shaylin, 9, and Celine Azizour, 11, crossed into Turkey with their mother, travelling from ‚Å†Tehran toward Istanbul ‚Äãwith hopes of eventually reaching London.
"We from Tehran," Shaylin said. Asked about conditions there, she replied: "It‚Äôs not so good." Despite the arduous journey, she smiled: "I‚Äôm so ‚Äãhappy."
Yasna, 63, crossed over from Iran with her husband and one of her daughters, travelling to Antalya on Turkey‚Äôs southern coast to visit another daughter who lives there with her family.
"I came to see my daughter ‚Äî I haven't seen her for six years. She has children there and I have two grandchildren."
She ‚Äãsaid she preferred not to comment on Iran's political situation.
"I don‚Äôt know what to say about the situation in Iran because we will be back to Iran."

item.title: Congo Republic's Sassou set to extend long rule, focus on succession
item.description:
DAKAR, March 10 (Reuters) - Congo Republic President Denis Sassou Nguesso looks set to extend his decades-long rule in elections on Sunday, even as his advanced age and a term limit fuel speculation about who ‚Äãwill eventually succeed him.
The 82-year-old former paratrooper first took power in the oil-rich Central African nation in a coup in 1979. He lost ‚ÄåCongo Republic's first multi-party elections in 1992 but seized power again in 1997 after a civil war.
He has now ruled for a combined total of almost 42 years, making him Africa's third longest-serving leader, after Equatorial Guinea's Teodoro Obiang and Cameroon's Paul Biya.
Sassou will face six candidates in an election with an organising commission dominated by figures appointed by the ruling Congolese Labour Party. Two of ‚Äãthe main opposition parties are boycotting the vote, saying the process lacks transparency, and several potential challengers are in prison or in exile.
"This election is a ‚Äãmere formality. The real stakes lie in what comes next," Remadji Hoinathy of the Pretoria-based Institute for Security Studies think tank ‚Å†said.
A 2015 constitutional reform, adopted despite opposition protests, reset the presidential term limit and allowed Sassou to stay in power. But it also capped presidents at three five-year ‚Äãmandates, meaning that, barring another reform, this is his last election.
The president himself has begun speaking more openly about eventual succession, telling young supporters at his ‚Äãcampaign opening rally on February 28 that his generation was "laying the groundwork" for them to take over.
One potential successor is his son, Denis-Christel Sassou Nguesso, who entered government as Minister of International Cooperation and Public-Private Partnerships in 2021 and has since taken on a higher public profile.
Some analysts say he may not be ready.
"Denis-Christel does not command the same authority within the ruling party as ‚Äãhis father and is widely unpopular. His potential accession to power threatens to unleash a violent succession struggle," Maja Bovcon, an independent consultant focused on West and Central ‚ÄãAfrica, said.
Other figures within the ruling system who could take over are Jean-Dominique Okemba, head of Congo‚Äôs National Security Council and a nephew of the president, and Jean-Jacques Bouya, the Minister of ‚ÄåSpatial Planning ‚Å†and Major Works and a cousin of Sassou's.
"Sassou won‚Äôt leave power unless he can hand it to someone trusted in his close circle, his son or a trusted ally who guarantees stability," Hoinathy said.
A government spokesperson did not respond to a request for comment on the president's succession plans.
Sassou's campaign has focused on continuity. Five years ago, his slogan was "Let us continue the march towards development". This year, it is "Let us accelerate the march".
For all the turbulence of Sassou's early years in power, his ‚Äãcountry is currently one of Central Africa‚Äôs ‚Äãmore stable states after a series ‚Å†of upheavals, including the 2023 coup in Gabon and last year's post-electoral violence in Cameroon.
The economy returned to modest per-capita growth in 2024 after a decade-long recession triggered by a slump in global oil prices. Oil still accounts for about half of Congo's ‚ÄãGDP and 80% of its exports.
The debt-to-GDP ratio, which peaked at 103.6% in 2020, fell to about 93.6% in 2024, and ‚Äãa three-year IMF programme ‚Å†completed last year helped stabilise public finances. Liquidity pressures remain, with debt servicing on the regional market absorbing half of the country's tax revenues.
Sassou has promised new infrastructure upgrades, increased investment in agriculture and economic diversification, though most ordinary Congolese have seen little improvement to date.
More than half - 52% - of Congo's 6.1 million people live in poverty, a rate unchanged ‚Å†since 2021, according ‚Äãto the World Bank. Youth unemployment is around 42% in a country where nearly half the population ‚Äãis under 18.
Meanwhile, French and U.S. prosecutors have launched investigations into assets held abroad by Sassou's relatives. The family has regularly denied any wrongdoing.
"We need better health care and education," Fr√©d√©ric Nkou, a jobless voter ‚Äãin Brazzaville, told Reuters. "But with this new term, we will experience more of the same."
(This story has been refiled to fix the formatting)

item.title: US warns citizens in Nigeria of possible 'terrorist threat'
item.description: LAGOS, March 10 - The U.S. embassy in Nigeria warned American citizens of ‚Äãa possible "terrorist threat" against U.S. ‚Äåfacilities and affiliated schools in the West African country in a security ‚Äãalert late on Monday.

item.title: UAE condemns drone attack on its consulate in Iraq's Erbil
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - UAE's foreign ministry condemned ‚Äãa drone attack ‚Äåon its consulate in Iraqi ‚ÄãKurdistan's Erbil, ‚Äãit said in a ‚Å†statement early ‚Äãon Tuesday.
Iraqi ‚ÄãKurdistan's counter-terrorism service said late on Monday ‚Äãthat it ‚Äãhad downed three drones ‚Äåin ‚Å†Erbil, with debris from one of the ‚Äãdrones ‚Äãfalling ‚Å†near the UAE consulate. ‚ÄãIt said ‚Äãno ‚Å†casualties were reported.

item.title: Turkey says U.S. Patriot system deployed to boost air defence amid Iran war
item.description:
ANKARA, March 10 (Reuters) - Turkey said on Tuesday that a U.S. Patriot air defence system was deployed ‚Äãto its southeast, near a NATO radar base, as part ‚Äåof steps by the alliance to boost air defences in the face of missile threats from the Iran war.
It is being deployed to Malatya province, the ‚Äãlocation of the Kurecik NATO radar base, which provides vital ‚Äãdata for the alliance and helped identify two Iranian ballistic ‚Å†missiles heading toward Turkey over the last week.
Iran has said it ‚Äãis not at war with regional countries and denies explicitly targeting its neighbour ‚ÄãTurkey. Ankara has warned Tehran against firing any more missiles towards it and the two countries' presidents discussed the issue on Monday.
"In addition to the measures we ‚Äãtake on a national level, air and missile defence measures by ‚ÄãNATO have been increased. In that framework, one Patriot System is being deployed to ‚ÄåMalatya ‚Å†to contribute to defending our air space," the defence ministry said.
It added that Turkey would continue to evaluate regional developments and cooperate with NATO allies.
The deployment comes amid reports that Washington is looking into redeploying its military assets, ‚Äãincluding Patriot systems, ‚Äãcurrently stationed in ‚Å†South Korea.
It was not immediately clear where the Patriot system or its batteries were being redeployed from.
Turkey, ‚Äãan emerging leader in the global defence industry that ‚Äãhas the ‚Å†alliance's second-largest army, lacks its own fully fledged air defences despite development efforts, and has relied on NATO air defences stationed in the ‚Å†eastern Mediterranean ‚ÄãSea in both missile incidents in ‚Äãthe last week.
There is currently one Patriot system, from Spain, deployed in Turkey as part ‚Äãof NATO defences.

item.title: Indian restaurants warn of shutdowns as Iran war makes cooking gas scarce
item.description:
BENGALURU/CHENNAI, March 10 (Reuters) - Restaurants and hotels across India warned of disruptions ‚Äãand possible shutdowns on Tuesday, as the Iran war constricts supplies of cooking gas, prompting authorities to set up ‚Äåa panel to review industry requests.
The fuel shortage comes as the U.S.-Israel war on Iran has halted ship traffic in the Gulf and the Strait of Hormuz, driving up energy prices and transport costs, hitting exports and output from Gulf producers, such as Qatar and Saudi Arabia.
India, the ‚Äãworld's second-biggest importer of liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), invoked emergency powers last week to order refiners to crank up production ‚Äãfor domestic use, leaving the hospitality industry struggling to get sufficient supply.
"We have LPG stock for ‚Å†two days. We are working on contingencies," said Bert Mueller, founder of Mexican food chain California Burrito, with more than ‚Äã100 stores spanning Bengaluru and Chennai in India's south to Delhi and Noida in the north.
"We are conserving gas and installing ‚Äãinduction stoves at certain stores."
India's oil ministry said it had set up , opens new tab a panel to review requests for LPG supply to restaurants and other industries, following appeals from two industry bodies.
"The restaurant industry is predominantly dependent on commercial LPG for its operations," the National Restaurant Association of India, which ‚Äãrepresents more than half a million restaurants, told the food processing ministry on Monday.
"Any disruption therein will lead to a ‚Äãcatastrophic closure," the NRAI said in a letter, while another body, the Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Associations of India, also sought government help.
Indian ‚Äåcompanies ‚Å†have raised LPG prices for the first time in about a year, as the war boosts prices of the imports that fill two-thirds of annual LPG consumption.
India's largest supplier of LNG, Qatar, halted production last week after Iran's strikes on Gulf countries in retaliation for Israeli and U.S. strikes against it.
In the southern tech hub of Bengaluru dubbed India's Silicon Valley, several ‚Äãrestaurants said deliveries had dropped ‚Äãsharply, fanning that kitchens will ‚Å†come to a halt if the situation is not resolved.
"One of our restaurants did not receive gas cylinders today," Manish V Shetty, who runs the Udupi Food Hub chain of restaurants ‚Äãin Bengaluru told Reuters.
"Thankfully, one of our older vendors helped us," he added, because the ‚Äãchain offers immediate ‚Å†payment, rather than credit terms of a week or month. "We're also seeing a spike in the price of the sunflower oil that we use for cooking."
Few restaurants stockpile LPG cylinders, because of safety concerns, relying instead on frequent replacement, said Veerendra Kamat, secretary ‚Å†of the ‚ÄãBengaluru Hotels Association.
"It's a very grave situation. Most of the (gas) companies have ‚Äãstopped supplying," said Ananth Narayan of the NRAI's Bengaluru branch, adding that even those restaurants that do store gas could run out within a week or ‚Äãtwo.

item.title: Middle East countries cut daily oil output, Bloomberg News reports
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Saudi Arabia has lowered oil ‚Äãoutput by between ‚Äå2 million and 2.5 million ‚Äãbarrels a day, ‚Äãand the United ‚Å†Arab Emirates has ‚Äãcut its output ‚Äãby 500,00-800,000 barrels a day, Bloomberg News ‚Äãreported on ‚ÄãTuesday.
Kuwait has also cut output ‚Äåby ‚Å†half a million barrels a day, and Iraq ‚Äãby ‚Äãabout ‚Å†2.9 million, the report ‚Äãadded, citing ‚Äãpeople ‚Å†with knowledge of the matter.
Reuters could ‚Å†not ‚Äãimmediately ‚Äãverify the report.

item.title: India invokes force majeure, gives priority to gas sales to key sectors
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 10 - India has invoked emergency measures to divert gas supplies from non-priority ‚Äãsectors to key users after the ‚Äådisruption of liquefied natural gas shipments through the Strait of Hormuz, a government notification said.
India meets ‚Äãhalf of its 195 million standard ‚Äãcubic metres per day (mmscmd) of gas consumption ‚Å†through imports. The country was getting ‚Äãabout 60 mmscmd gas from the Middle East ‚Äãprior to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and force majeure by Qatar .
Depending on the availability of ‚Äãgas, India will be supplying the ‚Äãfuel in proportion to the average use over the ‚Äålast ‚Å†six months by priority sectors, the notification, issued late on Monday, said.

item.title: German start-up plans 30-megawatt AI data centre in boost to sovereign control
item.description:
FRANKFURT, March 10 (Reuters) - German start-up Polarise plans to build a new 30-megawatt artificial intelligence ‚Äãdata centre that would double Germany's domestically-run computing capacity as European nations push ‚Äåto gain more control over critical tech infrastructure.
The facility, set to come online in the Bavarian town of Amberg in mid-2027, could eventually expand to 120 MW, the company told Reuters.
AI data centres in ‚ÄãGermany boasted total capacity of 530 MW at the end of last year, according ‚Äãto figures from German lobby group Bitkom. Much of that, however, was ‚Å†operated by non-German providers.
European countries are pushing for more sovereign control over data and ‚Äãthe infrastructure required for AI due to global tensions - from tariffs to armed conflict - as well ‚Äãas sharply diverging rules around online content.
The plans for what would be one of Germany's largest data centres have not been previously reported. Large technology companies like Google (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab and Amazon's (AMZN.O) , opens new tab ‚ÄãAWS typically run data centres of around 100 MW or more.
Polarise, which operates 13 ‚Äãdata centres in Germany and abroad, declined to give details on the size of the required investment.
"The final ‚Äåinvestment ‚Å†volume depends heavily on how many customers install their own servers or rent computing power," said Marc Gazivoda, Polarise's marketing director, adding that it did not receive state subsidies and investments could vary.
A source close to the company said the first stage of the project would ‚Äãcost in the "triple-digit million ‚Äãeuro range", which ‚Å†includes the main infrastructure but not the chips themselves. The final cost would depend on the number of chips required and their specific ‚Äãtypes, the source said.
A 12-MW data centre opened by Polarise in ‚ÄãMunich last ‚Å†month cost 1 billion euros ($1.16 billion), according to an estimate by Deutsche Telekom. That facility already doubled Germany's existing locally-operated capacity.
With oil prices shooting above $100 per barrel, the cost of energy for ‚Å†power-hungry ‚Äãdata centres is increasingly in focus.
Polarise said its partner ‚ÄãWV Energie will install a wind and solar power plant for the centre as well as batteries for temporary ‚Äãstorage of electricity.
($1 = 0.8640 euros)

item.title: Horse racing-Leading British horses line up for stellar first day as Cheltenham Festival begins
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - British trainers Dan Skelton and Nicky Henderson will take on the might of the Irish at jump racing's showcase event with the likes of The New Lion ‚Äãand Lulamba vying for a share of the five-million-pound ($6.70 million) prize pot as the Cheltenham Festival opens on Tuesday.
Attracting some 200,000 racegoers across four ‚Äådays, the festival is worth an estimated 274 million pounds to the local economy boosting local restaurants, hotels and pubs.
It is also horse racing's biggest betting week of the year with bookmaker William Hill predicting 450 million pounds will be wagered across Britain. Guinness, reduced in price by 30 pence, will be drunk by the gallon.
But for those at the heart of the sport, it is all about the horses ‚Äãand whether this year's crop of jumps stars can rival the Irish.
It is almost two decades since the golden years of British jump racing when Somerset-based Paul ‚ÄãNicholls and his crop of horses like dual Gold Cup-winner Kauto Star and 18-times consecutive hurdle winner Big Buck's outclassed the rest, before ‚Å†the pendulum swung in favour of the Irish.
Since then, Willie Mullins' sizeable team has continued to dominate the festival, racking up 113 winners to snatch the leading trainer title ‚Äã12 times. Backed by big-spending owners like former Barclays executive Rich Ricci and businessman J.P. McManus, this year's squad looks no less dangerous.
Mullins' pick for jump racing's most prestigious chase is ‚ÄãGaelic Warrior who looks set to go off favourite in Friday's feature, the Gold Cup, a race that has so far evaded owner Susannah Ricci. Before that, Majborough is favourite for the Queen Mother Champion Chase on Wednesday.
In with a strong chance of ending Irish dominance is a fresh crop of talented trainers that includes Skelton and Ben Pauling who will take on Mullins in the 625,000-pound Gold Cup ‚Äãwith the fancied Grey Dawning and the Harry Redknapp-owned King George VI Chase winner The Jukebox Man.
Battle lines will first be drawn in Tuesday's opening race, the Grade 1 ‚ÄãSupreme Novices' Hurdle, when Mullins' Mighty Park takes on British hope and three-time winning 2/1 favourite Old Park Star, trained by Henderson.
Henderson has made no secret of the fact a win on Tuesday would ‚Äåbe a ‚Å†big boost for Seven Barrows, setting a strong tone for the week, after Constitution Hill and Sir Gino were sidelined. He also sends out highly fancied Lulamba in the Arkle Novices' Chase and Jango Baie in the Gold Cup.
Were Britain to also clinch Tuesday's feature race, the 450,000-pound Champion Hurdle in which Mullins' prolific mare Lossiemouth will battle with Skelton's The New Lion, nerves may start jangling in the Irish camp.
Skelton sends his strongest ever team to Cheltenham, lining up some 30-35 horses. But while he's bullish about some big British winners, ‚Äãhe described his chances of snatching the ‚Äãtop trainer prize at the festival as "a ‚Å†possibility... not a probability."
"I've thought about it because I'm a competitive bugger but the reality is Willie will be leading trainer," he told the Racing Post on Sunday. "With a bit of luck, we might be pitching behind him along with a few others."
Ireland has won ‚ÄãCheltenham's Prestbury Cup title, awarded to the country fielding the most winners, outright in nine of the last 10 years.
Cheltenham's opening ‚Äãday also marks an important ‚Å†one for British racing, which is in the spotlight for governance issues at its regulator and recent moves by gaming companies to reduce sponsorship in light of higher taxes .
Cheltenham is also looking to recover from lower ticket sales in recent years, something CEO Guy Lavender is trying to address by lowering the cost of Guinness to 2022 rates, reducing restrictions on drinking areas and reintroducing ‚Å†Ladies Day ‚Äãon Wednesday.
"There is no doubt that everyone operating in the leisure sector is having to work harder to ‚Äãmake their offerings stand out to the customer," said Lavender.
"However, British racing continues to provide an exceptional day out and we saw total attendance across all 60 racecourses top 5 million last year for the first time ‚Äãsince 2019."
Lavender added he was "very positive" about ticket sales with growth from last year. "We are definitely moving in the right direction," he said.
($1 = 0.7460 pounds)

item.title: Russia is the only winner of Middle East war, EU's Costa says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 10 (Reuters) - Russia has so far been the only winner from the war in the ‚ÄãMiddle East as energy prices soar and ‚Äåattention for its war against Ukraine has faded, EU Council President Antonio Costa said on Tuesday.
"So ‚Äãfar, there is only one winner ‚Äãin this war ‚Äì Russia," Costa said in ‚Å†a speech to EU ambassadors in Brussels.
"It ‚Äãgains new resources to finance its war ‚Äãagainst Ukraine as energy prices rise. It profits from the diversion of military capabilities that could otherwise have ‚Äãbeen sent to support Ukraine. And ‚Äãit benefits from reduced attention to the Ukrainian front ‚Äåas ‚Å†the conflict in the Middle East takes centre stage."
Costa stressed the need for the EU to protect the international rules-based order, which ‚Äãhe said ‚Äãwas now ‚Å†being challenged by the United States, and for all parties in ‚Äãthe Middle East to return to ‚Äãthe ‚Å†negotiating table.
"Freedom and human rights cannot be achieved through bombs. Only international law upholds them," ‚Å†he ‚Äãsaid.
"We must avoid further escalation. ‚ÄãSuch a path threatens the Middle East, Europe, and ‚Äãbeyond."

item.title: How many people have been killed in the US-Israel war on Iran?
item.description:
March 10 - Scores of people have been killed across the Middle East since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran on February 28, with Gulf states that host U.S. military bases and personnel ‚Äãas well as Lebanon quickly drawn into the conflict.
Here are the death tolls from the war ‚Äåas reported by countries as of March 9, more than a week after it began. Reuters has not independently verified these deaths.

item.title: Israel ahead of schedule on war goals in Iran, ambassador to France says
item.description:
PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - Israel is ahead of schedule in achieving its war goals in Iran, its ambassador to France ‚Äãsaid on Tuesday, adding that the operation aims ‚Äåto weaken Iran‚Äôs authorities to curb attacks beyond its borders and allow its people to shape their own future.
"When we were asked at ‚Äãthe start of this war about its duration, ‚Äãwe said it would last a few weeks. That ‚Å†hasn‚Äôt changed," Joshua Zarka told BFM TV. "We are ahead ‚Äãof schedule to achieve our war objectives."
Zarka, formerly Israel‚Äôs lead ‚Äãdiplomat on Iran, said the goals extend beyond ending Iran‚Äôs nuclear programme. They include weakening the government to the point that its population ‚Äãcan "take its fate into its own hands," and ensuring ‚ÄãTehran can no longer mount attacks beyond its borders.
Asked about the appointment ‚Äåof ‚Å†Mojtaba Khamenei as the new Supreme Leader, Zarka said that if he follows his father Ali Khamenei's positions, he "would also be on a list of those who should be eliminated."
Israel ‚Äãhas simultaneously ‚Äãlaunched major operations ‚Å†against Iran‚Äëbacked Hezbollah after it struck Israeli territory. The Lebanese government has said it wants ‚Äãdirect talks with Israel to halt the ‚Äãfighting, but ‚Å†Zarka argued that Beirut is not disarming Hezbollah.
"At this stage, I‚Äôm not aware of any decision to enter negotiations to ‚Å†end ‚Äãthis war," he said. "What would end ‚Äãit is the disarmament of Hezbollah ‚Äî and that is a choice for the ‚ÄãLebanese government."

item.title: Over 10,000 Chinese citizens returned from Middle East amid war
item.description: BEIJING, March 10 (Reuters) - Chinese authorities and airlines have ‚Äãassisted over 10,000 Chinese citizens in returning from ‚Äåthe Middle East, the foreign ministry said on Tuesday, after the war in the region forced airspace closures and grounded ‚Äãcommercial flights.

item.title: Bangladesh secures diesel supplies amid major energy disruptions, sources say
item.description:
DHAKA, March 10 (Reuters) - Bangladesh has begun receiving diesel from suppliers including China and India, with officials ‚Äãsaying the country now has enough fuel to cover about one month of demand, with arrangements under ‚Äåway for another month, after the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran disrupted shipments, including to the massive garments industry.
The South Asian nation of 175 million people, which relies on imports for roughly 95% of its energy needs, has imposed fuel rationing for vehicles, restrictions on diesel sales and closed universities as the war ‚Äãon Iran causes severe disruption to Middle East oil exports.
Some garment makers in Bangladesh, the world‚Äôs second‚Äëlargest clothing exporter ‚Äãafter China, have already complained they do not have enough diesel to run their back-up generators when ‚Å†public utilities cut power supplies.
Mahmud Hasan Khan, president of the Bangladesh Garment Manufacturers and Exporters Association, said power cuts had ‚Äãdoubled to up to five hours a day since the war began on February 28.
"When power goes out, we have to ‚Äãrun our standby generators on diesel, but we can‚Äôt buy enough diesel anymore, and it‚Äôs causing problems for us," he said.
