State-run Bangladesh Petroleum Corporation (BPC) is receiving around 60,000 metric tons of diesel from three traders, with a further 90,000 metric tons scheduled to arrive later this month, two energy ‚Äãofficials said, requesting anonymity because they were not authorised to speak to the media.
A 27,000-metric-ton diesel cargo from PetroChina arrived ‚Äãat Chittagong port on Monday, while a cargo of 28,000 metric tons from Vitol is waiting at the port‚Äôs outer anchorage, a BPC official ‚Äåsaid.
In addition, ‚Å†around 5,000 metric tons of diesel is being supplied through a cross-border pipeline from India‚Äôs Numaligarh Refinery. Officials said talks are ongoing to secure some 30,000 metric tons from Indian Oil (IOC.NS) , opens new tab .
"We have supplies for one month, and another month is being arranged,‚Äù a BPC official said.
The BPC did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Bangladesh normally needs about 380,000 metric tons of diesel ‚Äãeach month, but officials said ‚Äãthat since rationing began, one ‚Å†estimate put current monthly requirements at roughly 270,000 metric tons.
Officials said securing refined fuel cargoes had been relatively easy because most of the traders did not rely on the disrupted Gulf shipping ‚Äãroutes used for crude oil shipments.
However, prices have increased as a result of the intense market ‚Äãvolatility triggered by ‚Å†the Middle East crisis, they added.
Bangladesh also imports around 1.4 million metric tons of crude oil a year for its refineries under long-term contracts with Saudi Aramco and Abu Dhabi National Oil Company (ADNOC).
However, supplies from Saudi Aramco and ADNOC could be affected because their ‚Å†crude shipments ‚Äãto Bangladesh must pass through the Strait of Hormuz. One Aramco cargo ‚Äãof about 100,000 tons has already been delayed in the Gulf due to the ongoing crisis, officials said.
Severe gas shortages have already forced Bangladesh to halt operations ‚Äãat four of its five state‚Äërun fertiliser factories, redirecting available gas to power plants.

item.title: Greenpeace activists storm stage at France's nuclear summit, confront Macron
item.description:
PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - Two Greenpeace activists broke onto the stage at the start of a global nuclear summit in ‚ÄãFrance on Tuesday, interrupting President Emmanuel Macron and U.N. ‚Äånuclear watchdog chief Rafael Grossi as they were greeting heads of state.
The protesters, dressed sharply in black suits and ties, held banners bearing the Greenpeace logo ‚Äãand reading "Nuclear Power = Energy Insecurity" and "Nuclear power fuels Russia‚Äôs war".
One ‚Äãof them shouted at Macron, "Why are we still buying ‚Å†uranium from Russia?" to which the president replied, "We produce nuclear power ‚Äãourselves."
France has its own uranium enrichment capacity, but also imports enriched ‚Äãuranium for its power plants, including from Russia, according to the latest customs data published by the French government.
Russia‚Äôs state nuclear company Rosatom accounted for about ‚Äã44% of the global uranium enrichment capacity in 2025, according to ‚Äãthe World Nuclear Association, and European nuclear power producers have struggled to wean ‚Äåthemselves ‚Å†off these supplies four years after Russia invaded Ukraine.
Around 15 Greenpeace activists blocked arriving convoys outside the venue in Boulogne-Billancourt on the outskirts of Paris on Tuesday, the environmental campaigning group said in ‚Äãa statement.
France is ‚Äãhosting the ‚Å†second world nuclear energy summit on Tuesday, where world leaders will meet to discuss and promote nuclear ‚Äãpower.
"For Greenpeace France, the holding of such a ‚Äãsummit is ‚Å†an anachronism, an event completely out of touch with reality and with the lessons to be learned from the tragic situations of the ‚Å†Russian ‚Äãaggression in Ukraine, the strikes on ‚ÄãIran, and the impacts of the worsening climate disruption," the group said.

item.title: 'My friends are still in jail': Cubans take to the streets, but fear crossing line
item.description:
SAN ANTONIO DE LOS BA√ëOS, Cuba, March 10 (Reuters) - Without warning, the sleepy Cuban farm town of San Antonio de los Ba√±os burst into protest one morning nearly five years ago, triggering a series of copycat demonstrations across the country that shook the Communist government unlike any event in decades.
Frustrated by chronic shortages of food and medicine and ‚Äãlengthy power outages as the coronavirus pandemic lingered, protesters marched in the streets chanting ‚ÄúPatria y Vida,‚Äù or ‚ÄúHomeland and Life,‚Äù the title of a popular song that inverts the Communist slogan "Patria o Muerte," or "Homeland or Death."
The phrase became a rallying cry for a movement that ‚Äåwas quickly crushed by state security forces.
Now, with Cuba under a U.S.-imposed oil blockade and sanctions that have thrown the country into a deeper crisis, Reuters visited the same neighborhoods where police and protesters clashed most fiercely on July 11, 2021: San Antonio de los Ba√±os and the marginalized Havana neighborhood of La G√ºinera.
In interviews with 12 people on the record, and several others who declined to be identified, Reuters found that people remember the brutality of the last crackdown, a testament to the enduring power of Cuba‚Äôs security apparatus. Some, however, were eager to place blame on Washington and the punishing U.S. economic sanctions against Cuba.
While Cubans have expressed frustration over lengthy power blackouts, banging on kitchen pots in a plea to turn the ‚Äãlights back on, for now, at least, a repeat of July 11 seems unlikely. Dissident leaders have largely been jailed or exiled since then, and the government has shut off the internet at signs of protests, as it did that day. Fear, though, is also a factor.
‚ÄúI assure ‚Äãyou, people won't protest in the streets because they're afraid,‚Äù said Brian Jimenez, 26, a baker from San Antonio de los Ba√±os, who said he was beaten by police that day and detained for several days afterward.
Police showed restraint ‚Å†at first, he said, but then came the feared "black berets," a brigade of the Interior Ministry, and "they roughed up the people," setting an example that was still reverberating.
"A lot of my friends are still in jail," Jimenez said.
Reuters could not verify his account of police beatings of detainees, though other demonstrators and human ‚Äãrights groups reported similar accounts of abuse.
The Cuban government did not reply to a request for comment.
Cubans expressed a range of opinions, from sympathy toward the Cuban government in the face of U.S. antagonism to openly criticizing the Communist government for failing to produce prosperity. Others called for ‚Äãdialogue and understanding.
But all agreed there was little prospect of another massive popular uprising any time soon, citing the government's violent repression of 2021, resulting in long prison terms for many protesters, while also observing that demonstrations failed to produce any meaningful change.
Though only 40 km (25 miles) outside Havana, San Antonio de los Ba√±os, population 50,000, is a world apart, typical of many towns across the eyebrow-shaped island. Tractors and carts drawn by scraggly horses, their ribs showing through weather-beaten hides, are almost as common as cars on the streets, where vendors sell locally grown potatoes, onions and cucumbers.
At the town square where many Cubans gathered in front of the church that day, university student Robert Perez said the government has retaliated against ‚ÄãJuly 11 protesters and their families, effectively causing self-censorship.
"When you protest, you can't find work. They take action against you or your family," said Perez, 27, a sociology student at Artemisa University. "In Cuba, everyone knows that one way to make a living is to open a small business and sell goods. Those who ‚Äãrose up on July 11th can't do that."
Rights groups, the European Union and the United States estimate Cuba has jailed between 1,000 and 1,500 people since those widespread protests, many of whom continue to serve extended sentences.
The Cuban government did not respond to a query on how many prisoners it currently holds on convictions related to July 11 ‚Äåprotests.
Cuban authorities say those ‚Å†jailed were found guilty of crimes including public disorder, resisting arrest, robbery and vandalism. Cuba blames the United States for funding the unrest and encouraging its spread, part of a broader plot to overthrow the island's government.
Frustration over blackouts sometimes spills into the streets. Reuters witnessed several demonstrations in poor neighborhoods of Havana on Saturday night, as people banged on pots. In one instance, the lights came back on mid-protest. The protest promptly ended.
"We're tired of so many days of blackouts. Three days in a row of the same," said a man who identified himself as Edward Rafael, 18.
On Monday, about 20 students sat on the steps of the University of Havana in protest, airing grievances over blackouts and internet disruptions that have made remote learning almost impossible, as classes have been suspended during the crisis.
Long before July 11, the United States imposed the most comprehensive and longest‚Äërunning unilateral sanctions regime in the world on Cuba, largely with the intent to pressure and destabilize ‚Äãthe Communist government in the name of promoting democracy and human rights.
The ‚ÄãUnited States has further tightened the screws this year, capturing Venezuelan President ‚Å†Nicolas Maduro, Cuba's most important patron. It then imposed a near-total oil blockade on Cuba and joined Israel in attacking another longtime Washington adversary, Iran. Trump has said his government is negotiating with Cuban leaders to reach some kind of deal, though Cuba has yet to acknowledge official talks.
While tempers may flare should the shortages grow more acute, for now some Cubans are turning their ire toward Washington, not Havana.
Nieves Fuentes Osoria, an 82-year-old woman from San Antonio de los Ba√±os, ‚Äãcalled the U.S. embargo of Cuba a "genocide." She said Cuba would show the same type of fierce resistance as it did in the Bay of Pigs invasion of 1961, when the armed forces defeated an invasion of ‚Äãexiles financed and trained by the United States.
"When ‚Å†we're in danger, this is what we do," she said, holding up two fists. "Because I saw it on different occasions. When they tried to come through the Bay of Pigs, we stuck together."
Others took a neutral political stand but still concluded the protests went for naught.
"What did they accomplish? Nothing," said Deisy Garcia, 73. "They made a fuss, stirred up all this, and life goes on as usual."
Back in Havana, the streets of La G√ºinera bustled under the midday heat, despite the fuel blockade and lengthy blackouts. Pedestrians and electric motorbikes navigated the potholed streets, while barbers clipped hair with rechargeable cordless razors and food kiosks sold whatever produce was available. ‚Å†On this day, tomatoes ‚Äãwere abundant at several points of sale.
Yuniel Romero, 37, doubted there would be another uprising, as he cut hair from the front porch of a high-street business.
"The people are not going to ‚Äãget involved because in real life nobody wants to be imprisoned again. The people have no way to defend themselves," Romero said.
Ariel Anillo, 52, sold sacks of charcoal, suddenly in demand due to a lack of cooking gas, beneath a poster of Fidel Castro.
He said most Cubans were persevering - "the lights go out for three days and nobody dies" - but the repression was also a factor.
"You know ‚Äãthat if you go to a protest they can put you in prison for 15 to 20 years," Anillo said. "Then the situation is worse, and your family suffers."

item.title: Suspected Hamas member detained in Cyprus over weapons procurement
item.description:
BERLIN, March 10 (Reuters) - Cypriot authorities have detained a suspected member of Palestinian militant group Hamas ‚Äãwanted in Germany for procuring weapons ‚Äåand ammunition for attacks on Israeli or Jewish facilities, German federal prosecutors said on Tuesday.
The Lebanese-born ‚Äãsuspect, identified only as Kamel M. ‚Äãin line with German privacy rules, ‚Å†was detained at Cyprus' Larnaca airport on ‚ÄãMarch 6, arriving from Lebanon, they added ‚Äãin a statement.
The suspect is wanted in relation to the transport of 300 rounds of live ammunition, ‚Äãaccording to prosecutors. It wasn't clear ‚Äãfrom the statement where the rounds had come from, ‚Äåor ‚Å†where they were thought to be heading.
"The operation served as preparation for deadly Hamas attacks on Israeli or Jewish institutions in ‚ÄãGermany and ‚ÄãEurope," they ‚Å†said.
Police also searched the suspect's apartment in Berlin.
Once Kamel M. is ‚Äãextradited to Germany, a judge ‚Äãwill ‚Å†decide on pre-trial detention, the statement said.
Attacks against Jews and Jewish targets have risen worldwide ‚Å†since ‚ÄãIsrael's war against Hamas in ‚ÄãGaza, triggered by the militant group's 2023 attacks on ‚ÄãIsrael.

item.title: Sri Lanka says second Iranian ship nine nautical miles off its coast
item.description: NEW DELHI, March 10 (Reuters) - A second ‚ÄåIranian ship is ‚Äãcurrently ‚Äãnine nautical miles ‚Å†from ‚ÄãSri Lanka's ‚Äãcoast, a spokesperson for ‚ÄãSri ‚ÄãLanka's cabinet said ‚Äåon ‚Å†Tuesday, adding that it ‚Äãwould ‚Äãeventually ‚Å†be moved.

item.title: Kremlin says Moscow mobile internet outages are done for sake of security
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 10 (Reuters) - The Kremlin on Tuesday said that recent instances of ‚Äãmobile internet being turned off in ‚ÄåMoscow and other major cities were aimed at ensuring security and were in accordance ‚Äãwith the law.
In a call with ‚Äãreporters, Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said: "All ‚Å†disconnections and communication restrictions are carried ‚Äãout in strict compliance with current legislation."
"This ‚Äãis all related to the need to ensure security," he added.
Peskov said that the impact ‚Äãof the move on businesses needed ‚Äãadditional analysis, and that solutions to their problems would ‚Äåbe ‚Å†proposed.
On a call with reporters on Monday after Russian President Vladimir Putin spoke to U.S. President Donald Trump, Kremlin ‚Äãforeign policy ‚Äãaide Yury ‚Å†Ushakov's connection cut out at least three times, Reuters reporters ‚Äãon the call said.
In recent ‚Äãweeks, ‚Å†the Kremlin has moved to block messenger apps Telegram and WhatsApp, promoting the ‚Å†state-backed ‚Äãservice MAX, which critics ‚Äãsay is not secure.

item.title: Exxon looks to ditch¬†New Jersey incorporation for Texas homecoming
item.description:
HOUSTON/BOSTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Exxon Mobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab is seeking to abandon its corporate registration in New Jersey and reincorporate ‚Äåin Texas where its headquarters is located, the U.S. oil major said on Tuesday, in a move that could help bolster its defenses against activist shareholders and climate advocates.
Exxon announced the proposal in a proxy filing. If the move is approved by ‚Äãshareholders, Exxon would become the latest of several high-profile companies including SpaceX, Tesla (TSLA.O) , opens new tab and Coinbase (COIN.O) , opens new tab to register in ‚Äãthe business-friendly Lone Star State.
Last year, a new Texas law enhanced legal protections for ‚Å†businesses through several mechanisms, including reducing the threat of shareholder litigation by allowing companies to set stock ownership ‚Äãthresholds for lawsuits.
"The Board believes Texas legislators, judges, and juries who might make decisions that impact Exxon Mobil are ‚Äãgenerally more familiar with our business and operations," the company said in the filing.
The top U.S. oil producer, physically headquartered in Spring, Texas, has long been the target of environment-related lawsuits.
New Jersey officials sued Exxon, Chevron (CVX.N) , opens new tab and other fossil-fuel companies in 2022, ‚Äãalleging that they contributed to climate change, forcing the state to spend billions of dollars to clean ‚Äãup after major natural disasters such as Superstorm Sandy and Hurricane Ida. The suit was dismissed last year.
Incorporating in the same ‚Äåstate ‚Å†as a company's headquarters can help executives get the attention of state politicians whose help they might need on taxes or other policy questions, said Jill Fisch, a law professor at the University of Pennsylvania.
‚ÄúOne way you might show your loyalty and get the legislators to pay attention to you is to incorporate in your ‚Äãhome state,‚Äù she said.
Exxon's ‚Äãroots in New Jersey ‚Å†trace back to its formation after the breakup of Standard Oil in the early 1900s, though its headquarters has been in Texas since 1989.
In September, Exxon took a novel ‚Äãstep to help fend off activist shareholder resolutions during annual meetings, introducing a program ‚Äãto allow ‚Å†retail investors to automatically cast their ballots in step with the board's recommendations.
Nearly 40% of the company's shares are held by individuals but just a quarter of them vote during proxy season, though they mostly support the board, ‚Å†the company ‚Äãsaid at the time.
Texas has sought to reinforce its reputation as ‚Äãa safe haven for business and has been a major beneficiary as some companies have departed states such as Delaware, a top destination ‚Äãfor incorporation.

item.title: India gains favour with global investors on returns, ESG momentum
item.description:
MUMBAI, March 10 (Reuters) - India is maturing as a market in which foreign investors who take into account ‚Äãenvironmental, social and governance (ESG) factors are confident in dedicating more ‚Äåof their portfolio to, according to fund managers at the Indian Venture and Alternate Capital Association conclave in Mumbai, who cite the economy's scale, improving return outlook and advancing ‚ÄãESG standards as key differentiators.
India's market regulator SEBI has been ‚Äãexpanding the scope of ESG-related regulations for over a decade, ‚Å†making detailed Business and Sustainability Report disclosures mandatory for the ‚Äãcountry's top 1000 listed companies since 2023.
"India is clearly earning its space ‚Äãin terms of allocations from global investors," said Ralph Keitel, head of Fund Investments at Zurich-based responsAbility Investment, speaking to Reuters at the conclave.
"Fifteen to 20 years ‚Äãago, India was a great space, but returns took longer," Keitel ‚Äãsaid. Now he sees a stronger near-term case as well.
Investors also said the ‚Äåcountry's ‚Å†appeal rests on its relative resilience at a time when global allocations are being reassessed across China, the United States and other emerging markets.
India's economy is projected to grow 6.5% in both fiscal years 2024-25 and 2025-26, ‚Äãaccording to the ‚ÄãInternational Monetary Fund.
"Besides ‚Å†risk mitigation, ESG impact ‚Äî having (a) good impact on communities adds value," Neha Grover, South Asia Lead for ‚ÄãPrivate Equity at IFC told Reuters. Grover added that India has become ‚Äãone ‚Å†of the most consistent emerging markets for profitable exits, supported by a strengthening initial public offering (IPO) pipeline.
ResponsAbility Investment's Keitel also pointed to climate‚Äërelated opportunities as ‚Å†a ‚Äãcore area of interest, citing sectors such ‚Äãas mobility and solar panels. As incomes rise, environmental pressures tend to intensify, he added.

item.title: Russian strike on Ukraine's Sloviansk kills four, local official says
item.description:
KYIV, March 10 (Reuters) - A Russian ‚Äãstrike on the ‚Äåeastern Ukrainian frontline city ‚Äãof ‚ÄãSloviansk killed four people ‚Å†and ‚Äãinjured 16 others, ‚Äãlocal governor Vadym Filashkin said ‚Äãon Tuesday.
Filashkin ‚Äãsaid Russia had ‚Äådropped ‚Å†three guided bombs on the city, ‚Äãand ‚Äãthat ‚Å†a 14-year-old girl ‚Äãwas among ‚Äãthose ‚Å†wounded.

item.title: China ready to work with countries on inclusive nuclear governance, Vice Premier says
item.description:
PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - China stands ready ‚Äãto work ‚Äåwith countries on a more ‚Äãinclusive nuclear ‚Äãgovernance and development, ‚Å†Chinese Vice ‚ÄãPremier Zhang Guoqing ‚Äãsaid on Tuesday.
"We should open an ‚Äãinternational ‚Äãdivision of labour for ‚Äåan ‚Å†inclusive workforce across nuclear projects," Zhang Guoqing ‚Äãsaid ‚Äãat ‚Å†the world nuclear ‚Äãenergy summit ‚Äãin ‚Å†Paris.

item.title: Court bars Kosovo president from announcing snap election date
item.description:
PRISTINA, March 10 (Reuters) - Kosovo's highest court told the president late on Monday not to announce the date for a snap election before March 31, ‚Äãa move that further prolongs a political crisis that has ‚Äåengulfed the tiny Balkan nation.
Last Friday, President Vjosa Osmani dissolved the parliament and called a snap election - without setting a date - after lawmakers failed to pick a ‚Äãnew head of state within the constitutional deadline that expired at ‚Äãmidnight on Thursday.
Her move extended a political deadlock in ‚Å†Kosovo, which is Europe's youngest nation and has aspirations to join the ‚ÄãEuropean Union. Kosovo had no functioning government for most of last year ‚Äãas the fractured parliament failed to elect a speaker for months.
In a verdict published late on Monday, the Constitutional Court temporarily barred the president from setting an ‚Äãelection date and also froze the parliament's efforts to elect a ‚Äãnew head of state. The court decision stays in effect until March 31.
Prime Minister ‚ÄåAlbin ‚Å†Kurti had asked the Constitutional Court to rule on whether Osmani had acted in line with the constitution. Kurti is a former ally of Osmani but decided not to back her for a second term in ‚Äãthe ceremonial role ‚Äãof president.
Kurti's government ‚Å†welcomed the court verdict but added: "For three weeks we will be in a state of waiting, while ‚Äãa number of agreements and decisions of importance for ‚Äãcitizens continue ‚Å†to remain without approval by the parliament."
The parliamentary election, if it goes ahead, would be the third in just over a year.
Choosing a president - ‚Å†though the ‚Äãrole brings no significant political powers - has ‚Äãalways proven politically challenging because two-thirds of lawmakers must attend the voting session in parliament, ‚Äãmaking it easy to disrupt.

item.title: US designation of Afghanistan 'regrettable,' Afghan Taliban says
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - The Afghan Taliban on Tuesday ‚Äãcalled the U.S decision ‚Äåto designate Afghanistan a "state sponsor of ‚Äãwrongful detention" regrettable, ‚Äãand said it wanted ‚Å†to resolve the ‚Äãmatter through dialogue ‚Äãafter the U.S. demanded the release of U.S. ‚Äãcitizens held.
U.S. Secretary ‚Äãof State Marco Rubio said on ‚ÄåMonday ‚Å†he was designating the Taliban government a "state sponsor of ‚Äãwrongful ‚Äãdetention," ‚Å†and urged Kabul to free ‚Äãall detained ‚ÄãU.S. ‚Å†citizens, including Mahmood Habibi and Dennis Coyle.

item.title: Morning Bid: Oil's combustible calm
item.description:
March 10 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets
The fog of war remains thick. Crude oil prices have become wildly volatile this week, with intraday swings exceeding $30 per barrel - some of the biggest one-day moves ‚Äãon record.
After soaring as high as $120 early Monday - the highest in four years - oil prices then slumped back below $100 later in the day after ‚ÄåPresident Trump once again teased the prospect of a short-lived conflict with Iran, saying the war was ‚Äúvery complete‚Äù.
I‚Äôll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why inflation isn't the only risk keeping central bankers up at night as the conflict in Iran continues.
And listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast where I discuss how headline‚Äëdriven oil swings are ‚Äãrewiring stocks, bonds and rate expectations.
Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.
Trump‚Äôs apparent ‚Äãabout-face on Monday not only calmed oil prices but also the complex of world stocks and bonds that had shivered after oil's ‚Å†triple-digit price spike. Wall Street stocks closed up on Monday, while on Tuesday South Korea‚Äôs KOSPI index regained nearly 6% and Japan‚Äôs Nikkei climbed nearly 3%.
Meantime, U.S. ‚ÄãTreasury yields tumbled and the dollar took a breather on Tuesday, steadying against major currencies, helping gold edge up in turn. U.S. stock futures were up ahead of the bell, having ‚Äãremained remarkably calm amid yesterday's tumult.
Many will say this is the TACO (‚ÄúTrump always chickens out‚Äù) trade par excellence, but there are few signs that Trump‚Äôs optimistic turn is playing out on the ground, with Iran‚Äôs Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps still asserting that no oil will be exported as long as U.S.-Israeli attacks continued.
Responding to that over social media, Trump threatened further retaliation against Iran if ‚Äãit continued to disrupt oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz. So, for now, the tit-for-tat looks set to continue.
Through all of this, oil prices held above $90 per ‚Äãbarrel - a level that would have seemed frightening only last month. And pass-through to fuel costs is already being felt in the U.S., where a comfortable majority of Americans now believe prices ‚Äåwill worsen ‚Å†over the next year.
In the background, G7 finance ministers on Monday mulled a possible joint release of their oil reserves to calm the horses, though they stopped short of doing so now , with a G7 official telling Reuters that the decision was ‚Äújust about timing‚Äù.
Elsewhere, China reported a spectacular surge in its trade surplus for the first two months of the year, with exports up more than 20% year-on-year. While that certainly supports its new growth target of just under 5% and comes despite falling bilateral trade with the U.S., ‚Äãit also predates this month‚Äôs spike in ‚Äãoil prices.
Later in the day, after ‚Å†the bell, Oracle is set to report earnings, with traders likely to be on the lookout for signs of a payoff from spiralling capex as it ploughs billions into AI data center expansion.
Chart of the day
China roared into 2026 with exports far outstripping ‚Äãforecasts, fuelled by red-hot electronics demand, putting the economy on track to top last year's record $1.2 trillion trade surplus, despite ‚Äãthe persistent drop in ‚Å†bilateral trade with the United States.
Although the data preceded this month's energy and shipping shock from the war in Iran, outbound shipments from the world's second-largest economy grew 21.8% in U.S. dollar terms in the January-February period - up sharply from the 6.6% increase recorded in December and blowing past the median forecast of 7.1% in a Reuters poll.
Today's events to ‚Å†watch
* U.S. ‚ÄãFebruary existing home sales (10:00 AM EST)
* U.S. 3-year note auction
* U.S. corporate earnings: Oracle, Domino's Pizza
Want to ‚Äãreceive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the ‚Äãauthor. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: US ready to work with other countries to develop civil nuclear power, official says
item.description: PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - The United States ‚Äãis ready to ‚Äåwork with other countries ‚Äãto develop ‚Äãcivil nuclear power to ‚Å†meet the ‚Äãglobe's growing energy ‚Äãdemand needs, including from data centres, ‚ÄãThomas DiNanno, ‚Äãthe U.S. under secretary ‚Äåfor ‚Å†arms control and international security, said on ‚ÄãTuesday ‚Äãat ‚Å†a nuclear energy ‚Äãconference in ‚ÄãParis.

item.title: Al Qaeda-linked group killed at least 12 truck drivers in Mali, HRW says
item.description: DAKAR, March 10 (Reuters) - Islamist militants from an al Qaeda-linked group killed 10 long-haul truck drivers and two ‚Äãteenage apprentices who were travelling through Mali's western ‚ÄåKayes region in late January, Human Rights Watch (HRW) said on Tuesday.

item.title: Qatar wants to bolster security partnership with US after Iran's strikes
item.description:
DOHA, March 10 (Reuters) - Qatar wants to strengthen its defence partnership with the United States in ‚Äãthe wake of Iranian air strikes on Qatari territory, ‚Äåthe foreign ministry spokesperson said on Tuesday, even as it sees the existing deal as an important deterrent.
Iran launched missile and drone attacks ‚Äãon its oil-producing Gulf neighbours after U.S.-Israeli strikes that ‚Äãkilled its top leaders. The conflict disrupted output in the ‚Å†region and sent oil prices surging.
Qatar hosts the biggest U.S. ‚Äãbase in the Middle East, Al Udeid Air Base, which ‚Äãwas attacked by Iran during the war that erupted on February 28.
The spokesperson, Majed Al Ansari, told a media briefing on Tuesday the security partnership ‚Äãwith the United States was not in question, but added ‚Äãthat its deals with the U.S. and European countries need to be strengthened.
"The ‚Äåpartnerships ‚Å†are the main stop-gap and deterrent against any attack on our country," he added.
"When things go out of control and spiral, the result is deterrents do not work... But that doesn't ‚Äãmean you shouldn't ‚Äãhave deterrents. ‚Å†You should strengthen these deterrents and that is exactly what are we trying to do right," ‚ÄãAl Ansari said.
U.S. President Donald Trump pledged in ‚ÄãOctober ‚Å†to treat any armed attack on Qatar as a threat to the United States' own security.
Iran has vowed to retaliate ‚Å†against U.S. ‚Äãtargets in the region in case ‚Äãof any attack.

item.title: Tuesday will be most intense day of strikes on Iran, Hegseth says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - U.S. Defense ‚ÄãSecretary Pete ‚ÄåHegseth said Tuesday would ‚Äãbe ‚Äãthe most intense ‚Å†day of ‚Äãstrikes against ‚ÄãIran in the campaign so ‚Äãfar.
Speaking ‚Äãwith reporters, Hegseth said ‚ÄåTuesday ‚Å†would bring the most fighter ‚Äãjets ‚Äãand ‚Å†bombers against ‚ÄãIran.

item.title: Human Rights Watch says that Israel has been illegally using white phosphorus in Lebanon
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 10 (Reuters) - The Israeli military illegally used white phosphorus munitions over homes in the southern Lebanese town Yohmor on ‚ÄãMarch 3, posing a threat to civilians, Human Rights Watch said in a ‚Äåreport on Monday.
"The incendiary effects of white phosphorus can cause death or cruel injuries that result in lifelong suffering," Ramzi Kaiss, Lebanon researcher at Human Rights Watch, said in the statement.
The rights group said it ‚Äãhad verified eight images showing white phosphorus used over a residential part of Yohmor, ‚Äãand civil defense workers responding to fires in the area.
Reuters could not ‚Å†independently verify the group's findings.
The Israeli military told Reuters it was unaware and could not ‚Äãconfirm the use of shells that contain white phosphorus in Lebanon. It added that it ‚Äãhad not reviewed the same videos as HRW and could not comment on the claims.
Lebanese authorities have not commented.
The Israeli military told residents of Yohmor and 50 other villages and towns to evacuate in a ‚Äãstatement early on March 3.
Reuters previously reported on Lebanese farmers testing their soil to determine whether ‚Äãthey could resume planting in the aftermath of Israel's reported use of white phosphorus in 2023.
As of July 2024, the Lebanese ‚ÄåNational ‚Å†Council for Scientific Research said there had been 175 Israeli attacks on south Lebanon using white phosphorus from October 2023, many of them sparking fires that have affected over 600 hectares (1,480 acres) of farmland.
White phosphorus munitions can legally be used on battlefields to make smoke screens, ‚Äãgenerate illumination, mark targets ‚Äãor burn bunkers and ‚Å†buildings.
HRW said under international humanitarian law the use of airburst white phosphorus over populated areas is unlawful.
White phosphorus is considered an incendiary weapon ‚Äãunder Protocol III of the Convention on the Prohibition of Use ‚Äãof Certain ‚Å†Conventional Weapons. The protocol prohibits using incendiary weapons against military targets located among civilians, although Israel has not signed it and is not bound by it.
Nearly 400 people have been killed and hundreds ‚Å†of thousands ‚Äãhave been forced to leave their homes as Israeli ‚Äãstrikes have pounded Lebanon for over a week.
HRW previously accused Israel of using white phosphorus during Israel's 2023 operations against ‚ÄãLebanon, a charge Israel's military denied at the time.
(This story has been refiled to correct the spelling of 'phosphorus' in the headline, and to add the missing word 'of' in paragraph 8)

item.title: Iran not more formidable than thought, top U.S. general says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The top U.S. general said on Tuesday that while Iran was fighting, ‚Äãit was not more formidable than Washington had thought, as ‚Äåthe United States geared up for the most intense day of strikes against Iran in the war so far.
General Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint ‚ÄãChiefs of Staff, told reporters that the United States was ‚Äãcarrying out strikes against Iranian mine-laying vessels and the ‚Å†Pentagon would look at a range of options if it was ‚Äãtasked with escorting ships through the Strait of Hormuz.
The war has effectively ‚Äãshut the Strait of Hormuz, a chokepoint for global oil and liquefied natural gas transport, leaving tankers unable to sail for more than a week and ‚Äãforcing producers to halt pumping as storage fills.
"I think they're fighting, ‚Äãand I respect that, but I don't think they're more formidable than what we ‚Äåthought," ‚Å†Caine said.
U.S. President Donald Trump on Monday threatened to escalate the war with Iran if it blocked oil shipments from the Middle East, even as he predicted a quick end to the conflict.
‚ÄãDuring the press ‚Äãconference at the ‚Å†Pentagon, U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said the United States would carry out the most intense day ‚Äãof strikes against Iran on Tuesday.
Hegseth reiterated that ‚Äãthis would ‚Å†not be an endless war and said Trump would decide when the U.S. campaign would end.
The United States has carried out strikes ‚Å†against more ‚Äãthan 5,000 targets in the first ‚Äã10 days of the campaign, including against more than 50 naval ships, Caine said.

item.title: Shots fired at US consulate in Toronto, no injuries reported
item.description:
TORONTO/WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Shots were ‚Äãfired at the U.S. consulate in Toronto on Tuesday, ‚Äåpolice said, adding that evidence was found of a discharged firearm and that no injuries were ‚Äãreported.
Toronto police said on X they responded to ‚Äãreports of shots at 5:29 a.m. (0929 GMT).
Representatives for ‚Å†the U.S. consulate in Toronto and ‚Äãthe U.S. Department of State did not ‚Äãimmediately respond to requests for comment.
In another post, police said they had no information on a potential ‚Äãsuspect and were on scene investigating the ‚Äãincident. They did not provide details of any motive.
Separately, ‚Äåon ‚Å†Sunday, an improvised device exploded in Norway at the U.S. embassy in Oslo , and police were still searching for a suspect, with ‚Äãa possible ‚Äãlink to ‚Å†the Iran war among the lines of inquiry.
In New York ‚ÄãCity, two men have been charged with ‚Äãterrorism ‚Å†after throwing a homemade bomb at anti-Islam protesters over the weekend.

item.title: Britain working with allies to support shipping through Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - Britain is working with allies on a range ‚Äãof options to support commercial ‚Äåshipping through the Strait of Hormuz in the face of Iranian ‚Äãthreats, Prime Minister Keir ‚ÄãStarmer's spokesperson said on Tuesday ‚Å†after calls with the leaders ‚Äãof Italy and Germany.
After speaking ‚Äãto Germany's Friedrich Merz and Italy's Giorgia Meloni late on Monday, Downing ‚ÄãStreet said in a readout ‚Äãthat they agreed on the "vital importance of ‚Äåfreedom ‚Å†of navigation" through the Strait and "agreed to work closely together in the coming days ‚Äãin the ‚Äãface ‚Å†of Iranian threats."
"We're working with our allies on ‚Äãa range of options ‚Äãto ‚Å†support commercial shipping through the Strait as the threat picture ‚Å†develops," ‚Äãthe spokesperson told ‚Äãreporters when asked about the readout.

item.title: Russia's deportations of Ukrainian children amount to crimes against humanity, UN inquiry finds
item.description:
GENEVA, March 10 (Reuters) - A U.N. investigation found on Tuesday that the deportation and transfer of ‚ÄãUkrainian children by Russian authorities since Moscow's full-scale invasion of ‚Äåthe country in 2022 amounted to crimes against humanity.
"In this report, the Commission concluded that crimes against humanity and war crimes ‚Äãby Russian authorities have targeted children, who are ‚Äãamong the most vulnerable victims. These crimes have ‚Å†irreversible consequences on their lives and their future," according ‚Äãto the report to be presented to the U.N. Human ‚ÄãRights Council in Geneva on Thursday.
The report by the Independent International Commission of Inquiry on Ukraine studied the cases of 1,205 children ‚Äãfrom five oblasts, or regions, in Ukraine and said ‚Äãthat 80% of them have yet to return to Ukraine.
Ukrainian authorities say ‚ÄåRussia ‚Å†has illegally deported or forcibly displaced more than 19,500 children to Russia and Belarus in violation of the Geneva Conventions.
U.S.-funded research last year showed Russia expanded its forced ‚Äãre-education programmes of ‚Äãdeported children.
"The ‚Å†deportations and transfers have originated from various locations across a wide geographic area in ‚ÄãRussian-occupied areas in Ukraine, following a well-established ‚Äãpattern ‚Å†of conduct, indicating that these acts have been widespread and systematic," the U.N. report said.
Russia denies it is taking ‚Å†children against ‚Äãtheir will and says it ‚Äãhas been evacuating people voluntarily to remove them from the war zone.

item.title: Pope Leo fires San Diego bishop accused of stealing $250,000
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 10 (Reuters) - Pope Leo has accepted the resignation of a Catholic bishop in ‚ÄãSan Diego who was arrested by ‚Äålocal authorities on suspicion of stealing $250,000 from his congregation, the Vatican announced on Tuesday.
Bishop Emanuel Hana Shaleta had ‚Äãled the small Chaldean Catholic community ‚Äãin the California city since 2017. He ‚Å†pleaded not guilty to 16 counts ‚Äãof embezzlement and money laundering at a hearing ‚Äãon Monday, according to local media reports.
San Diego Deputy District Attorney Joel Madero said the alleged crimes ‚Äãtook place in 2024 and were reported ‚Äãby a church employee who noticed the missing funds, ‚Äåaccording ‚Å†to KGTV, a local ABC affiliate.
Hana Shaleta was arrested on March 5 at San Diego's international airport while attempting to leave ‚Äãthe U.S., according ‚Äãto ‚Å†a sheriff's office statement.
The bishop's lawyer said at Monday's hearing that ‚ÄãHana Shaleta was taking a planned ‚Äãtrip ‚Å†to Germany.
Chaldean Catholics recognise the authority of the pope but worship according to an eastern ‚Å†Christian ‚Äãliturgical rite. There are about ‚Äã71,000 Chaldean Catholics in San Diego, according to Vatican ‚Äãstatistics.

item.title: Ex-Syrian intelligence officer appears in UK court charged with crimes against humanity
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - A former member of Syria's Air Force Intelligence attended a British court hearing via videolink on Tuesday charged with crimes against humanity and torture relating to the ‚Äãsuppression of pro-democracy demonstrations in Damascus in 2011.
Salem Michel Al-Salem, 58, who now ‚Äålives in Britain, appeared virtually at the hearing at London's Westminster Magistrates' Court from his home. He was wearing a breathing apparatus mask and the court was told he suffered from degenerative motor neurone disease.
Al-Salem is charged ‚Äãwith three counts of murder as a crime against humanity relating to deaths in ‚ÄãApril and July 2011 "as part of a widespread or systematic attack against a ‚Å†civilian population with knowledge of the attack".
He is also accused of three charges of torture ‚Äãrelating to incidents in 2011 and 2012, and one of conduct ancillary to murder as a crime ‚Äãagainst humanity. He did not speak during the hearing and there was no indication as to how he would plead.
His lawyer Sean Caulfield told the court that Al-Salem was too unwell to confirm his name.
The seven charges were ‚Äãbrought under a British law that allows the prosecution of serious international crimes committed abroad. ‚ÄãThe Crown Prosecution Service said it was the first time it had brought charges of murder as crimes against ‚Äåhumanity.
In ‚Å†2005, Afghan warlord Faryadi Zardad was convicted by a British court of torture that had taken place in Afghanistan.
Al-Salem, who has sought indefinite leave to remain in Britain, was a colonel in the Syrian Air Force Intelligence department with oversight of the Information Branch in the district of ‚ÄãJobar, to the east of ‚Äãcentral Damascus, British ‚Å†prosecutors say.
He is accused of leading a group tasked with quelling the demonstrations, which mostly occurred during Friday afternoon prayers. Prosecutors say he gave ‚Äãhis men orders to open fire on protesters, which led to the ‚Äãdeaths of ‚Å†some individuals.
Prosecutors say he was also present at, or took part in, the torture of men at the Information Branch building.
Al-Salem was first arrested in central England in December 2021. His lawyer had sought ‚Å†an order ‚Äãto withhold his name, arguing it could pose a ‚Äãrisk to his safety. England's Chief Magistrate Paul Goldspring rejected the application but ordered that his address not be made ‚Äãpublic.
He will next appear on Friday at London's Old Bailey court.

item.title: Iran shock creates problematic fiscal loop
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters Breakingviews) - It‚Äôs never a convenient time for war-induced spikes in oil and gas prices, as Russia‚Äôs invasion of Ukraine in 2022 can attest. But today‚Äôs flare up in the Middle East hits Western economies at a ‚Äãmore fragile moment. Labour markets are looser, corporate margins thinner, and interest rates much higher than they were four years ago. Whether ‚Äågovernments shield households and firms from costlier energy or leave them exposed, the result is likely the same: borrowing costs will climb.
Bond markets are already tense. Even after Donald Trump on Tuesday predicted rapid de escalation with Iran , 10 year yields in Germany, France, the United Kingdom and Japan remain well above their pre crisis levels. Unlike the United States, all are large ‚Äãnet importers of energy and particularly natural gas, which plays a key role in setting electricity prices.
Yet, if disruption at the Strait of Hormuz ‚Äãpersists, governments may be forced to brave hostile debt markets and once again fund energy price caps, subsidies and tax ‚Å†cuts. South Korea has already moved in that direction, and UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer signalled on Monday that Britain may follow. Bruegel‚Äôs data on Europe‚Äôs last ‚Äãenergy shock shows how expensive such measures can be: between September 2021 and January 2023, they cost European countries -- including the UK -- 3.7% of GDP. This would ‚Äãsit atop the 3.4% deficit that official projections , opens new tab had already pinned on the European Union for this year.
But inaction would lead to a similar place. The post-pandemic experience showed that companies react to energy shocks by coordinating price hikes to protect their margins , opens new tab , which is why even ‚Äúcore‚Äù inflation in the U.S., the eurozone and Britain ‚Äì which excludes the price of fuel and food ‚Äì peaked ‚Äãat range of between 5.7% and 7.1% in 2023. In theory, central banks should look through one off energy shocks; in practice, this so-called pass through ‚Äãeffect forces them to respond. Markets have already shifted from expecting year end rate cuts at the Bank of England and the European Central Bank to pricing in a hike ‚Äåfrom the ‚Å†former and no change from the latter, according to derivative prices collected by LSEG.
The inflation hit should still be smaller this time. European natural gas trades around 50 euros per megawatt hour, far below the 300-euro peak of 2022. Deutsche Bank‚Äôs Sanjay Raja estimates that even a generous UK support package would total only 14 billion pounds, or 0.4% of GDP, compared with 90 billion pounds previously. Still, that extra fiscal hit comes at a time when government tax revenues are already ‚Äãstrained by low growth. The danger is ‚Äãthat more, potentially open-ended financial commitments ‚Å†push up bond yields, making the fiscal math even trickier.
Central banks could help break the feedback loop with a clear pledge to backstop sovereign bond markets, as the ECB did during the pandemic. At the margin, energy subsidies might help ease ‚Äãfears of a price spiral. But the current level of inflation, which is at or above target in the ‚ÄãEU and UK, means ‚Å†ratesetters probably won‚Äôt act unless bond markets are under extreme duress. If the Iran war continues, government coffers could become a pain point.
Follow Jon Sindreu on X , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Critical Metals approves $30 million for Greenland rare earth project development
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Critical Metals (CRML.O) , opens new tab said on Tuesday its board approved a $30 ‚Äãmillion acceleration program for the ‚Äådevelopment of the Tanbreez heavy rare earth project in Greenland.
The push ‚Äãcomes as the United ‚ÄãStates ramps up efforts to build ‚Å†domestic rare earth and ‚Äãcritical minerals supply chains amid concerns ‚Äãover reliance on China for these materials used in electronics, defense and ‚Äãclean energy technologies.
The program for ‚Äãone of the world's largest known rare ‚Äåearth ‚Å†deposits will advance drilling, infrastructure, engineering design and metallurgical programs.
It expects first ore production in ‚Äãfourth ‚Äãquarter of ‚Å†2028 or first quarter of 2029, with concentrate ‚Äãexport beginning by third ‚Äãquarter ‚Å†of 2029.
Last year, Reuters reported Trump administration officials had discussed the ‚Å†possibility ‚Äãof taking a ‚Äãstake in the company.

item.title: WHO warns of health risks from 'black rain' in Iran
item.description:
GENEVA, March 10 (Reuters) - The World Health Organization warned on Tuesday that the "black rain" falling in Iran ‚Äãafter strikes on oil facilities could cause respiratory ‚Äåproblems, and it backed Iran‚Äôs advisory urging people to remain indoors.
The U.N. health agency, which has an office in ‚ÄãIran and works with authorities on health emergencies, ‚Äãsaid it has received multiple reports of oil-laden ‚Å†rain this week. Tehran was choked in black ‚Äãsmoke on Monday after an oil refinery was hit, ‚Äãin an escalation in strikes on Iran's domestic energy supplies as part of the U.S.-Israeli campaign.
"The black rain and the acidic rain ‚Äãcoming with it is indeed a danger for ‚Äãthe population, respiratory mainly," WHO spokesperson Christian Lindmeier told a press ‚Äåbriefing ‚Å†in Geneva, adding that Iran had advised people to stay indoors.
Asked whether the WHO backed that advice, he said: "Given what is at risk right now, the ‚Äãoil storage facilities, ‚Äãthe refineries ‚Å†that have been struck, triggering fires, bringing serious air quality concerns, that is ‚Äãdefinitely a good idea."
One video sent to ‚ÄãReuters by ‚Å†a WHO staff member showed what they said was a cleaner mopping up black liquid at its office ‚Å†entrance ‚Äãin Tehran on March 8. ‚ÄãReuters was not able to independently verify the footage.

item.title: Decision to turn back on nuclear was a strategic mistake, EU's Von der Leyen says
item.description:
PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - The decision to reduce the share of nuclear energy in the electricity production mix in the European Union ‚Äãwas a strategic mistake given that the bloc is ‚Äåneither an oil or gas producer, EU Commission President Ursula Von der Leyen said on Tuesday.
"This reduction in the share of nuclear was ‚Äãa choice, I believe that it was a strategic ‚Äãmistake for Europe to turn its back on a ‚Å†reliable, affordable source of low-emissions power," she said in ‚Äãa speech at an event on nuclear energy held in Paris.
The ‚ÄãEU relied on nuclear for one third of its electricity in 1990 and now the share is only close to 15%, she said.
The European ‚ÄãUnion aims to stimulate the development of small nuclear reactors, ‚Äãto make Europe less dependent on imported fossil fuels, she added.
"We see ‚Äåa ‚Å†global revival of nuclear energy. And Europe wants to be part of it," von der Leyen said as she announced a 200 million euros ($233 million) guarantee by the EU to stimulate ‚Äãinvestments in the ‚Äãdevelopment of ‚Å†small modular reactors.
The money would come from the EU's Emissions Trading System, she said.
"We want this ‚Äãnew technology to be operational by the early ‚Äã2030s," ‚Å†she said.
Von der Leyen said soaring energy prices caused by the war in the Middle east were a "stark reminder" of Europe's ‚Å†vulnerability as ‚Äãan importer of fossil fuels and ‚Äãunderlined the importance of increasing power production from renewable sources and nuclear reactors.
($1 = ‚Äã0.8584 euros)

item.title: Spanish researchers develop low-cost artificial cornea from fish scales
item.description:
MADRID, March 10 (Reuters) - Researchers at Spain's University of Granada have developed an artificial cornea made from the scales of several fish ‚Äãspecies commonly found in markets, which could become a ‚Äålower-cost alternative to donor transplants for severe eye diseases.
The cornea - the eye's transparent front layer - is difficult to repair when badly damaged because it ‚Äãhas no blood vessels and limited regenerative capacity. Severe ‚Äãcorneal disease is often treated with donor transplants, which ‚Å†can be constrained by organ availability and waiting lists.
Scientists from ‚Äãthe university's Tissue Engineering Group and the ibs.GRANADA biomedical research institute ‚Äãsaid in a press release they had created corneal implants with fish scales that were highly biocompatible, transparent and resistant.
"Due to its origin, this product ‚Äãis very accessible, easy to obtain and inexpensive, and could ‚Äãhelp boost the local fishing industry," said Ingrid Garzon, professor of histology ‚Äåat ‚Å†the southern Spanish university, who co-authored the research.
While testing suggested they could be useful for corneal repair and regeneration, the technology remains far from possible clinical use in humans pending human trials.
The ‚Äãteam said the ‚Äãmaterial produced ‚Å†good functional results in laboratory studies and in animals that received the implants.
"Although the standard transplant ‚Äãusually offers good results, it's necessary to develop ‚Äãnew effective ‚Å†regeneration methods that do not depend on organ donation, which is subject to waiting lists," Miguel Alaminos, also a histology professor at ‚Å†the ‚Äãsame university, said in the release.
A ‚Äã2016 peer-reviewed paper by researchers at Antwerp University found that fish scale-derived artificial corneas ‚Äãwere biocompatible with humans.

item.title: Argentina's Milei is pitching to Wall Street as Middle East spooks investors
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 10 (Reuters) - President Javier Milei aims on Tuesday to persuade investors that Argentina's economic turnaround can stay on track even as war ‚Äãin Iran pushes oil prices higher, strengthens the dollar and rattles emerging markets.
Milei is due to speak to investors and ‚Äåexecutives inside JPMorgan's new headquarters in Midtown Manhattan as part of "Argentina Week," a New York roadshow to convince financiers that the South American country's stabilization effort remains an attractive investment story despite the global backdrop.
"We're talking about making connections, about creating the right environment for these conversations to be productive," said Manuel Adorni, chief of Milei's ‚Äãcabinet and his spokesperson, addressing investors at a reception on Monday night in the Argentine consulate in New York. "We want to ‚Äãbuild trust, we want to lay the foundations so that we can establish a long-term relationship."
U.S. government backing has ‚Å†become a key part of that pitch. President Donald Trump 's administration publicly supported Milei ahead of Argentina's October 2025 midterm elections and expanded financial ‚Äãcooperation with Buenos Aires. A liquidity facility championed by the United States helped avoid a run on the peso ahead of that vote.
The two countries ‚Äãdeepened economic ties in February, when the United States and Argentina signed a reciprocal trade and investment agreement designed to facilitate U.S. investment, including in the critical minerals sector.
"They're simply reiterating the investment opportunities in Argentina and sending a message of macroeconomic and political stability," Armando Armenta, a senior economist and strategist at AllianceBernstein, said of ‚Äãthe roadshow. "It's good that they're doing this because Argentina has been out of the spotlight for a long time."
Argentina's government says aggressive spending cuts, ‚Äãderegulation and fiscal tightening are beginning to restore macroeconomic stability after years of deficits, currency crises and galloping inflation.
Investors have cautiously welcomed the adjustment and recent reforms, ‚Äåincluding a labor ‚Å†overhaul approved by Congress that marked a major legislative victory for Milei.
Still, Argentina must rebuild foreign-exchange reserves, attract longer-term investment and regain reliable access to international capital markets after years of default and capital controls -some of which remain in place.
Officials hope Argentina Week, which will also host Economy Minister Luis Caputo, central bank governor Santiago Bausili and deregulation minister Federico Sturzenegger, will help demonstrate that reforms are translating into investment opportunities, particularly in ‚Äãsectors such as energy, mining, agriculture ‚Äãand technology.
The closer alignment with the ‚Å†United States marks a shift after years in which China expanded its economic footprint across South America. Yet China remains one of the country's largest trading partners and a major creditor.
Global conditions may complicate the message.
Oil ‚Äãprices are up nearly 30% this month to about $90 per barrel as the United States and Israel ‚Äãattack Iran, while a ‚Å†flight to safety has strengthened the dollar and drawn some investors away from emerging markets. The dollar (.DXY) , opens new tab has gained more than 4% against developed currencies since late January, while the EM currency index (.MIEM00000CUS) , opens new tab has surrendered its gains for the year.
Last week, the Argentine local stock benchmark (.MERV) , opens new tab touched its lowest level since ‚Å†October, while ‚Äãthe dollar bond yield spread to U.S. Treasuries widened in line with the global ‚Äãbenchmark (.JPMEGD) , opens new tab .
For Milei, the challenge is convincing investors that Argentina's reforms deserve attention even amid a flight to safety.

item.title: China's leaders mourn the death of 109-year-old Communist Party elder
item.description:
BEIJING, March 10 - Chinese leaders on Tuesday paid their respects to Song Ping, the longest-lived member of the most powerful decision-making body in the Communist Party, who died last week in Beijing at the age of 109.
President Xi ‚ÄãJinping, Premier Li Qiang and other members of the party's current Politburo Standing Committee attended a ceremony ‚Äåthat closed the chapter on a political career that spanned five generations of leaders of the People's Republic.
They stood in silent tribute then bowed three times in front of his remains, which were draped in the red flag of the Communist Party, before he ‚Äãwas cremated. The event was shown on state broadcaster CCTV.
"The life of Comrade Song Ping was a ‚Äãlife of revolution, of struggle and of glory, a life spent in the pursuit ‚Å†of truth and progress and in dedication to the communist cause," read an official account of his life released ‚Äãby state-run Xinhua news agency on Tuesday.
Born in 1917 into a family of farmers in the eastern province of ‚ÄãShandong, Song became a revolutionary youth when he joined the Communist Party in 1937.
He briefly served as a personal secretary of Zhou Enlai, who became communist China's first premier after 1949.
Song, as an economic planner and the party's personnel chief, was a prominent member ‚Äãof China's second generation of leadership, an era when reform-minded Deng Xiaoping remade China after the 27-year rule ‚Äãof Mao Zedong ended in 1976 with Mao's death.
He rose to the apex of power in China's political system - the Standing Committee ‚Äåof ‚Å†the Politburo, comprised of only a handful of people - in June 1989, following the bloody crackdown on pro-democracy demonstrations in and around Tiananmen Square in Beijing.
Song was elevated by Deng to the top leadership in 1989, as Jiang Zemin emerged as the head of China's third generation of leaders - a move partly made in recognition of Song's commitment to Deng's ‚Äãreform programme.
In the 1970s, ‚Äãwhile serving as the party ‚Å†chief of Gansu, a province in the country's barren northwest that housed the young republic's space and atomic programmes, Song sided with Deng in the tumultuous years following ‚ÄãMao's death, as party elites debated whether to continue on the path set by ‚ÄãMao or to ‚Å†follow Deng's call to modernise, reform and open up.
Song was also widely credited with bringing Hu Jintao, China's leader before Xi, to the attention of Deng.
"Hu Jintao sent a wreath to express his condolences on the passing of Comrade Song ‚Å†Ping," ‚ÄãXinhua reported on Tuesday.
Song retired from the Politburo in 1992, aged 75.
Popular ‚Äãand respected within the party for his seniority, Song remained politically active after his retirement. In 2022, already aged 105, he was seen on ‚Äãstage at a Communist Party meeting flanked by other retired senior officials.

item.title: Germany's Merz sees no plan for bringing Iran war to swift end
item.description:
BERLIN, March 10 (Reuters) - German Chancellor Friedrich Merz underlined growing concern in Europe at the U.S. and Israeli war against Iran on Tuesday ‚Äãsaying a "dangerous escalation" was underway with "clearly no joint plan" for bringing ‚Äåit to an end.
Merz's comments, which echoed remarks he made at the end of last week, came after U.S. President Donald Trump said the war could end "soon" ‚Äãbut also said the U.S. could escalate its attacks if Iran ‚Äãsought to block tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz.
Merz ‚Å†said that Germany shared "many of the goals" of the U.S. and Israeli ‚Äãoperation, which killed Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamanei, but he said questions ‚Äãwere arising with each day that passed, amid signs of a dangerous escalation.
"We are particularly concerned that there is clearly no joint plan for bringing this war to ‚Äãa swift and convincing end," Merz said at a joint press conference ‚Äãin Berlin with Czech Prime Minister Andrej Babis.
"We have no interest in a war ‚Äåwithout ‚Å†end. We have no interest in the dissolution of Iran's territorial integrity, statehood or economic viability," he added.
The world needed "a stable, viable Iran as part of a regional peace and security order in which neither Israel nor other ‚Äãpartners are threatened," ‚Äãhe said.
The remarks ‚Å†reflect the concern in Europe at the economic damage inflicted by any prolonged closure of the Strait of ‚ÄãHormuz, through which a fifth of the world's oil ‚Äãpasses, as ‚Å†well as by fears of the disorder that would follow a breakdown in Iran's state institutions.
"A scenario such as we have seen in Libya, Iraq ‚Å†or other ‚Äãcountries in the region would also harm ‚Äãus all," he said. "This affects our security, our energy supply and possibly also the situation ‚Äãsurrounding migration."

item.title: Brazil's Haddad says Durigan likely to replace him as finance minister
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 10 (Reuters) - Brazil's Finance ‚ÄåMinister Fernando Haddad confirmed on Tuesday that he will step down next week ‚Äãand said that ‚Äãhis deputy, Dario Durigan, is ‚Å†likely to succeed him.
A ‚Äãformal announcement on who will take over ‚Äãthe ministry, however, is up to President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, Haddad told reporters ‚Äãin Brasilia.
"Dario has a ‚Äãvery good relationship with the president, ‚Äåone ‚Å†of great trust... He is a great public administrator," said Haddad, who had ‚Äãpreviously announced ‚Äãhe would leave ‚Å†his role ahead of Brazil's general elections ‚Äãin October.
Haddad is ‚Äãexpected ‚Å†to run for governor of Sao Paulo state, four sources ‚Å†told ‚ÄãReuters earlier this ‚Äãweek.

item.title: Netherlands moves Iran embassy staff to Azerbaijan
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 10 (Reuters) - The Netherlands will ‚Äãtemporarily move its ‚Äåembassy staff in Iran to ‚ÄãAzerbaijan, Foreign ‚ÄãMinister Tom Berendsen said ‚Å†on Tuesday, ‚Äãciting safety concerns ‚Äãover the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
"Due to ‚Äãincreasing risks ‚Äãto the safety of ‚Äåour ‚Å†staff, it has been decided to temporarily relocate ‚Äãthe ‚Äãactivities ‚Å†of the Dutch embassy ‚Äãin Iran ‚Äãto ‚Å†Baku, Azerbaijan," Berendsen wrote ‚Å†on ‚ÄãX.

item.title: Private credit alarm bells echo 2007 subprime warnings
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 10 (Reuters) - Every financial market crisis is different, but they do rhyme, and parallels are beginning to emerge between the tremors now rippling through private credit and those in U.S. subprime housing that led to the 2007-09 Global Financial Crisis.
This isn't to say a repeat of that historic crash is in the cards. ‚ÄãBut there is a growing risk that the mounting stress in private credit - scarce or nonexistent liquidity, opaque pricing, and spiking redemptions - could spill over into the public ‚Äåsecurities markets.
BlackRock (BLK.N) , opens new tab , the world's biggest asset manager with some $14 trillion under management, said on Friday it had limited withdrawals from a flagship debt fund after a surge in redemption requests. A few days earlier, alternative asset manager Blackstone (BX.N) , opens new tab said it had raised the redemption cap on its BCRED private credit fund to meet record withdrawal requests.
The alarm bells at these two behemoths come after a similar event at smaller alternative asset manager Blue Owl (OWL.N) , opens new tab last month, and the bankruptcies of U.S. auto-parts supplier First Brands and car dealership Tricolor late last year ‚Äãthat prompted JPMorgan Chase (JPM.N) , opens new tab CEO Jamie Dimon to warn: "When you see one cockroach, there are probably more."
Investors with a sense of history, or who were around in the 2000s, may think this ‚Äãall sounds a bit familiar. In 2007, BNP Paribas, Bear Stearns and HSBC blocked redemptions from U.S. subprime funds or warned that they were in trouble ‚Äì ‚Å†a seemingly small risk that metastasized into a global financial meltdown.
Of course, the GFC didn't fully explode until September 2008 when U.S. authorities allowed Lehman Brothers to go under. But the crisis had been building ‚Äãup steadily for at least 18 months, with tremors at those subprime funds offering investors early warnings that trouble was brewing.
The rationale for not letting investors access their own money today is likely similar to ‚Äãthe justifications in 2007: assets have probably fallen significantly in value and thus would need to be sold at a heavy loss; the asset manager may fear triggering a fire sale in other assets to raise the cash being demanded; or the fund may be struggling to unload illiquid assets. Or it may be a bit of all three.
Either way, as was the case with subprime mortgages and related derivatives in 2007, it is hard to know what private credit ‚Äãassets today are truly worth because the market is so opaque and illiquid. When price discovery evaporates, the more bearish assumptions often win out.
Another similarity with subprime in 2007 is that private credit and private ‚Äãmarkets more broadly are not believed to pose a systemic financial stability risk. As we all know, that turned out to be wishful thinking back then.
Is it different this time?
Probably, if we're looking at sheer ‚Äåsize. The mortgage-backed ‚Å†securities market, the root of the GFC, was worth around $7.2 trillion in 2007, or 5% of the total value of global securities at the time, according to Investec. The private credit market today is worth around $2 trillion, less than 1% of all global securities.
On the other hand, like subprime in 2007, private credit today is loosely regulated, certainly relative to traditional bank lenders, meaning its true reach isn't easily ascertained.
What's more, ordinary mom-and-pop investors are getting more involved. Retail investors represented 16.6% of holdings in private credit funds at the end of 2024, up from 5.5% in 2020, according to Investec.
Meanwhile, private credit default rates are ‚Äãrising, hitting a record 9.2% in 2025, credit rating ‚Äãagency Fitch Ratings said last week. That's ‚Å†up from the previous record of 8.1% in 2024.
Perhaps ominously, these defaults didn't include any software companies, which have become major private borrowers. The software sector has been hammered this year by fears of artificial intelligence-related disruption, which has slammed shares in private credit giants Blackstone, KKR (KKR.N) , opens new tab and Apollo (APO.N) , opens new tab down 30-45% in recent months.
The broader risks ‚Äãfrom private credit seem skewed to the downside. The U.S. economy is at a delicate juncture, facing a shaky labor market and the fallout from ‚Äãthe war in the Middle East , ‚Å†including wild volatility in oil markets and the specter of modern-day "stagflation."
True, the consensus view is that the economy's underlying fundamentals are solid and that private credit isn't large enough or well-integrated enough to torpedo GDP growth or broader asset markets. As strategists at Barclays note, private credit has problems, but they aren't big enough to push the U.S. into recession.
Of course, this is exactly how subprime was viewed in 2007.
To paraphrase Warren Buffett, when ‚Å†the liquidity tide ‚Äãgoes out, you see who's been swimming naked. Recent events in the private credit market suggest more funds may soon ‚Äãbe exposed.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for Reuters)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to ‚Äãhear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Exclusive: Exxon CEO says non-essential employees in Middle East have been evacuated
item.description:
HOUSTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Exxon Mobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab has evacuated ‚Äãnon-essential employees from its operations in the ‚ÄåMiddle East, CEO Darren Woods said in an interview on Tuesday, as the U.S.-Israel war ‚Äãon Iran continues.
Some operations have been ‚Äãscaled back to manage inventory levels as ‚Å†traffic through the Strait of Hormuz ‚Äãhas been challenged, he said. Exxon is a ‚Äãminority partner in oil projects in the UAE, Qatar and Saudi Arabia.
"Our first and highest priority ‚Äãis making sure our people remain safe, ‚Äãand we evacuated folks who weren't critical or essential ‚Äåto ‚Å†the operations that we were providing support for," Woods said.
The Strait of Hormuz, an important waterway that handles one-fifth of ‚Äãthe world's oil ‚Äãsupply, ‚Å†has been strained as Iran has threatened to attack tankers that ‚Äãnavigate through it. U.S. President Donald ‚ÄãTrump ‚Å†on Monday threatened to escalate the war with Iran if it blocked oil shipments from ‚Å†the ‚ÄãMiddle East, even as ‚Äãhe predicted a quick end to the conflict.

item.title: Denmark's Vestas receives UK order for 92 wind turbines
item.description:
COPENHAGEN, March 10 (Reuters) - Denmark's Vestas (VWS.CO) , opens new tab said on Tuesday it has received a ‚Äãfirm order for German utility ‚ÄåRWE's 1.38 gigawatt (GW) Vanguard East offshore wind project in Britain.
The order supports ‚ÄãBritain's advance towards its 2030 ‚Äãclean power targets and the wider ‚Å†energy transition across Europe, Vestas ‚Äãsaid in a statement.

item.title: Zambia seeking global investors to help triple copper output by 2031
item.description:
LUSAKA, March 10 (Reuters) - Zambia is courting global investors, including from the ‚ÄåUnited States, as it aims to more than triple its copper output to 3 million metric tons by 2031, its mines minister said on Tuesday.
Africa's second-largest copper producer after Democratic Republic ‚Äãof Congo is seen as one of the countries Washington is keen ‚Äãto partner with as the U.S. escalates efforts to loosen China's ‚Å†grip on materials crucial to advanced manufacturing.
Zambia produced 890,346 tons of copper ‚Äãlast year, missing an annual target of 1 million tons. The red metal is ‚Äãa key material in electric vehicles and renewable energy infrastructure vital for the transition to a low-carbon economy.
Zambian Mines Minister Paul Kabuswe said investment talks extend to various countries as part ‚Äãof a broader strategy to meet the 2031 target.
"The U.S. is part of ‚Äãit," he said, without providing details of specific discussions.
Negotiations between Lusaka and Washington are also underway ‚Äåfor ‚Å†a more than $1 billion health aid deal that health advocates have warned links the money to mining access and has data-sharing risks.
"There is nothing linked to anything," Kabuswe told Reuters when asked whether ‚ÄãU.S. health funding ‚Äãwill be linked ‚Å†to mining collaboration, adding: "Zambia is very mindful when seated on the table to do negotiations over any matter."
Kabuswe emphasized the ‚Äãimportance of fair deals, adding that "investors must also take their share". "It ‚Äãhas ‚Å†to be a win-win situation for Zambia and for the investors," he said.
In addition to copper, Zambia is also rich in cobalt, nickel, manganese, graphite, lithium and rare-earth elements.
Mining ‚Å†firms ‚Äãoperating in Zambia include China's JCHX Mining (603979.SS) , opens new tab , Canada's ‚ÄãBarrick Gold (ABX.TO) , opens new tab and First Quantum Minerals (FM.TO) , opens new tab , India's Vedanta Resources (VDAN.NS) , opens new tab , United Arab Emirates' International Resources Holding and KoBold ‚ÄãMetals, which is backed by U.S. investors.

item.title: Reducing Europe's nuclear energy sector was 'strategic mistake', EU chief says
item.description:
PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - Reducing Europe's nuclear energy sector was a "strategic mistake", European Commission chief Ursula von der Leyen said on Tuesday, as governments grapple ‚Äåwith an energy crunch from the Iran war .
Europe produced around a third of electricity from nuclear power in 1990 but that has fallen to 15%, she told an event in Paris, leaving it reliant on oil and gas imports whose prices have surged in recent days.
Being "completely dependent on expensive and volatile imports" of fossil fuels ‚Äãputs Europe at a disadvantage to other regions, von der Leyen said in a speech.
"This reduction in the share of nuclear ‚Äãwas a choice. I believe that it was a strategic mistake for Europe to turn its back ‚Å†on a reliable, affordable source of low-emissions power."
Von der Leyen's native Germany took a political decision under then-Chancellor Angela Merkel to phase out nuclear power ‚Äãplants owing to public opposition and safety concerns after the 2011 Fukushima disaster. Von der Leyen was a minister in Merkel's government when that decision ‚Äãwas made.
Germany's Environment Minister Carsten Schneider on Tuesday criticised von der Leyen's "backward-looking strategy" on nuclear power.
"Cleaner, safer electricity from wind and sun is cheaper, has long been driving the energy transition, and produces no radioactive waste," Schneider said in a statement.
The EU has rapidly expanded renewable energy, but gas power plants still form an ‚Äãimportant part of its power mix, and fossil fuels still dominate energy consumption in sectors such as transport and heating.
The continued heavy reliance on imported oil ‚Äãand gas exposed European countries to soaring energy prices in 2022, when Russia cut gas deliveries after the invasion of Ukraine .
The EU budget does not directly ‚Äåfund nuclear ‚Å†energy projects because they are not unanimously supported by its 27 member governments.
In a sign of the EU's increasing acceptance of the technology, von der Leyen said the executive Commission would offer a 200-million-euro guarantee for private investments in innovative nuclear technologies.
She said the money would come from the EU's carbon market.
Some EU countries which previously opposed nuclear, such as Denmark and the Netherlands, have recently softened their stance, as they ‚Äãhunt for ways to secure large ‚Äãamounts of stable, low-carbon electricity ‚Å†for heavy industry.
Others, including Austria and Luxembourg, remain opposed.
France, Europe's biggest nuclear energy producer, argues that stable, low-carbon power from nuclear plants is key to industrial competitiveness.
French President Emmanuel Macron said the EU - where nuclear power producers still imported ‚Äã15% of their uranium from Russia in 2024 - needed to move towards other suppliers.
"We need to ‚Äãcooperate internationally to make ‚Å†progress on this issue, to diversify our supply sources," he told the Paris event, adding that France planned to increase its own enrichment capacity.
France imported 39% of its enriched uranium from Russia in 2025, customs data showed.
Macron also proposed standardising reactor designs across Europe. That could benefit France's state-owned ‚Å†nuclear giant ‚ÄãEDF, which has struggled to win recent tenders for new projects.
In 2024 South ‚ÄãKorea's KHNP won a tender worth at least $18 billion to build a new nuclear power plant in the Czech Republic, a decision which losing bidder EDF sought to block in the ‚Äãcourts.
($1 = 0.8584 euros)

item.title: Western nations bolster military presence in Eastern Mediterranean during Iran conflict
item.description:
ATHENS, March 10 - Western nations have bolstered their military presence in the eastern Mediterranean during the conflict in Iran , focusing on the security of Cyprus after an Iranian-made drone hit a British military ‚Äãbase on the island on March 2.
Here is a snapshot of military assets ‚Äåthat have been sent to the region in addition to those that had been there long-term before the conflict began on February 28.
USS Gerald R. Ford, the biggest U.S. aircraft carrier, docked at the ‚ÄãSouda base on the Greek island of Crete two weeks ago, before sailing ‚Äãfurther east with escort ships carrying missiles.
Britain dispatched Wildcat helicopters on March ‚Å†6, equipped with counter-drone technology. As speculation mounted in February on U.S.-led strikes on Iran, ‚ÄãBritain stationed additional F-35B jets at its base in Cyprus, joining the Typhoon FGR4 aircraft ‚Äãalready there. Britain has said it will also send its destroyer, the HMS Dragon, to Cyprus.
France sent its flagship aircraft carrier Charles de Gaulle to the East Mediterranean last week, deploying a dozen naval vessels around ‚Äãit. It was off the Greek island of Crete on Tuesday and was heading ‚Äãto Cyprus. Another French frigate was docked in Souda base on Tuesday.
German navy frigate FGS Nordrhein-Westfalen arrived in ‚Äåthe ‚Å†Cyprus region on March 8.
Greece has sent its new Belharra frigate, Kimon, and MEKO 200 frigate called Psara, equipped with the Centauros anti-drone system, to protect the island's airspace. It also sent 4 F-16 Viper fighter jets to be stationed in western Cyprus and deployed a ‚ÄãPatriot anti-aircraft missile system ‚Äãon the southeastern ‚Å†Greek island of Karpathos to protect eastern Crete.
Italy deployed the Martinengo frigate to the region as part of a coordinated mission with EU ‚Äãpartners. It was docked in Souda base on Tuesday and was ‚Äãexpected to sail ‚Å†to Cyprus in the coming days.
Spain sent its most advanced warship, the √Ålvaro de Baz√°n-class frigate Cristobal Colon, to the eastern Mediterranean. It was docked in Souda Bay on Tuesday.
The Netherlands ‚Å†is preparing ‚Äãto send the air defence frigate HNLMS Evertsen to ‚Äãthe region.
Turkey has sent six F-16 aircraft and air-defence systems to northern Cyprus, a breakaway Turkish Cypriot state recognized ‚Äãonly by Ankara.

item.title: Governments plan repatriations as Middle East flights stop
item.description:
March 10 - Commercial flights in parts of the Middle East have been grounded due to the escalating conflict following U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran , stranding foreign nationals and prompting governments to plan repatriations.
Below is what governments have said about repatriation plans:
Austria's foreign ministry said it had helped 117 vulnerable citizens leave the UAE and Israel via neighbouring states and had planned a first charter flight from Muscat on March 4 for 170 people, while warning that land departures would be ‚Äãundertaken at travellers' own risk.
Three flights by GullivAir, Bulgaria Air and the State Aviation Operator planned to return Bulgarian citizens from Dubai, Abu Dhabi and Oman on March 4 and March 5, including a 326‚Äëseat GullivAir ‚Äåflight from Dubai, a Bulgaria Air Boeing 737 rotation via Oman, and a 90‚Äëseat government aircraft from Abu Dhabi.
The Czech government has arranged 10 repatriation flights to return more than 1,500 stranded citizens, the Foreign Ministry said on March 9.
Estonia's foreign ministry on March 4 said it had organised a 180-seat flight from Muscat the following day, available to its citizens in Oman and the United Arab Emirates.
The European Commission has said it is coordinating repatriation flights under the EU Civil Protection Mechanism as more member states request help.
The EU has supported so far more than 42 flights, bringing ‚Äãover 4,100 Europeans safely back to Belgium, Bulgaria, Czechia, Italy, Cyprus, Latvia, Lithuania, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, Austria, Portugal, Romania, Slovakia, and Sweden, a statement from the European Commission said on March 9.
Finland planned a single repatriation flight with about ‚Äã160 seats on March 8 for the roughly 3,000 Finnish citizens stranded in the United Arab Emirates, the foreign ministry said.
Finnair said on March 6 that it was preparing to operate ‚Å†flights from Muscat, Oman to Helsinki to bring home customers who have been stranded in Dubai. The first flight is scheduled for March 10, with more flights planned later in the week.
The French foreign minister said several repatriation flights for French nationals, around ‚Äã400,000 of whom are in the region, were planned from March 4.
