France said it had deployed consular teams at Israel's borders with Egypt and Jordan to facilitate land exits so people can fly onward, and has put in place a similar mechanism in the ‚ÄãUAE at borders with Oman and Saudi Arabia, where airspace has remained open.
Two flights with about 250 people were scheduled for March 5 and March 6 from the Gulf, after the first landed March 5 in Frankfurt, said German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul.
Greek foreign ministry said it has repatriated hundreds of citizens from Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Jerusalem, Oman and the UAE during last week.
87 people were repatriated on March 4 from Amman, Jordan, with another flight carrying 88 people planned for March 5, Hungary's foreign ministry said in a statement.
Another flight was scheduled for March 6 from Sharm-el Sheikh, Egypt. ‚ÄãHungary rented a Flydubai plane for March 5 and March 6 to repatriate Hungarians from Dubai. Hungary had two flights each on March 7 and March 8 leaving from Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
Italy's foreign ministry said about 25,000 Italians have returned from the Middle ‚ÄãEast on commercial flights facilitated by the ministry.
It has expanded consular staffing in Oman and the UAE and is coordinating returns across multiple countries, with large groups moving from the UAE and others travelling by land through Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain and Saudi Arabia. Italians in Oman, Israel, ‚Äåthe Maldives and ‚Å†Thailand are also getting help.
The Dutch government on Friday said it was preparing to use its military to repatriate its citizens from countries they cannot leave on their own. No details on specific countries and timing were given.
The first group of Polish citizens evacuated by military airlift from the Middle East arrived in Poland on March 6, the Operational Command of the Polish Armed Forces said.
A charter TAP flight carrying 139 Portuguese citizens and eight foreigners landed in Lisbon on March 6, with Portugal's foreign ministry planning further repatriations.
Romania's foreign ministry said on March 4 airline FlyDubai has scheduled two flights to Bucharest on March 4 and March 5. It had received more than 3,000 repatriation requests and had around 16,000 nationals registered in the region.
An Air Serbia flight from Sharm el-Sheikh landed ‚Äãin Belgrade early on March 4, carrying 67 passengers, all ‚Äãevacuated from Israel.
The government will deploy an air force A330 ‚Å†multi-role aircraft to support the assisted departure of Singaporeans from Riyadh on March 10, with a second flight from Saudi Arabia planned for March 12, the foreign affairs ministry said on Facebook.
Slovakia's government has carried out 15 repatriation flights since evacuations started, transporting 641 people, most of whom were Slovak nationals, the Foreign Ministry said on March 9. It is planning more flights.
Slovenia organised four ‚Äãbuses on March 3, escorted by police, to take Slovenian citizens and families with children from Dubai to Muscat airport in Oman, the prime minister's office said in a statement.
The first ‚Äãflight for Slovenia was organised on ‚Å†March 3, with two more flights scheduled for March 4.
Spain has begun evacuating its citizens from the Middle East on March 3, with more than 175 Spaniards arriving on a flight from Abu Dhabi and further flights expected from the UAE via Istanbul, according to Foreign Minister Jose Manuel Albares.
Spain was also reinforcing its embassies in the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Oman and Bahrain to provide support and facilitate further repatriations, Albares said.
Sweden chartered a plane to repatriate on Saturday 180 stranded Swedes identified as vulnerable.
Thailand plans to evacuate its nationals from Iran by land ‚Å†to Turkey on ‚ÄãMarch 7 and March 10, while others stranded in Iraq, Qatar, Bahrain, the UAE and Jordan are returning or will return once airspace reopens.
The UAE civil aviation authority plans to begin operating "special flights" across the country's airports to help some of the tens of thousands of passengers stranded in the region leave, state news agency WAM reported.
British chartered flights left Oman on March 5 after technical delays on March 4, prioritising vulnerable UK nationals wanting to ‚Äãleave the region, the British Foreign Office said, after some 130,000 citizens registered their presence in the region.
($1 = 0.8615 euros)

item.title: Iran's intelligence ministry arrests foreign agents, state media reports
item.description:
DUBAI, March 10 (Reuters) - Iran's intelligence ministry ‚Äãhas arrested a ‚Äåforeign national it said was conducting ‚Äãespionage on ‚Äãbehalf of the ‚Å†United States and ‚ÄãIsrael, and ‚Äãacting as a proxy for two Gulf ‚Äãcountries, state ‚Äãmedia cited the ministry ‚Äåas ‚Å†saying on Tuesday.
The ministry also said it had ‚Äãarrested ‚Äã30 ‚Å†spies, internal mercenaries, and ‚Äãoperational agents ‚Äãof ‚Å†Israel and the U.S. over ‚Å†the ‚Äãpast few ‚Äãdays.

item.title: EU leaders to demand carbon market reform by July, draft shows
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 10 (Reuters) - European Union governments are set to ask the European Commission to ‚Äãpropose reforms to the bloc's carbon ‚Äåmarket by July, draft conclusions for a summit of EU leaders next week showed.
At ‚Äãtheir summit on 19 March, EU ‚Äãleaders will ask Brussels "to present a ‚Å†review of the emissions trading system (ETS) ‚Äãat the latest by July 2026, to ‚Äãboth reduce the volatility of the carbon price and mitigate its impact on electricity prices," ‚Äãaccording to the draft conclusions, seen ‚Äãby Reuters.
The draft conclusions said the reform should preserve ‚Äåthe ‚Å†central role of the ETS in the EU's energy transition. That's despite calls from some governments, including Slovakia and ‚Äãthe Czech ‚ÄãRepublic, for ‚Å†the EU to suspend or weaken the system, as a ‚Äãway to cut energy bills.
The ‚ÄãEuropean ‚Å†Commission has said it plans to propose the ETS reform in the third ‚Å†quarter ‚Äãof the year, but ‚Äãhas not given a specific date.

item.title: Spain's Repsol may bring new investors into US renewables unit
item.description:
MADRID, March 10 (Reuters) - Spanish energy group Repsol (REP.MC) , opens new tab is considering welcoming new investors in its renewables business in ‚Äãthe United States as it reviews its approach ‚Äåto renewable power generation, CEO Josu Jon Imaz said on Tuesday.
Since 2018, Repsol has built up a portfolio of wind, ‚Äãsolar and hydroelectric projects as part of its ‚Äãbroader strategy of transitioning from a traditional oil ‚Å†and gas company to a multi-energy player. It has ‚Äãalso been investing in the production of green hydrogen and ‚Äãlow-carbon fuels.
The United States, alongside Spain, is a key market for its low‚Äëcarbon division. In the U.S., the company has about ‚Äã2.8 gigawatts (GW) of capacity operating or under construction, plus ‚Äãa development pipeline of more than 15 GW.
"We are evaluating the ‚Äåpotential ‚Å†incorporation of additional investors at the platform level in the U.S.," Imaz told analysts and investors at an event presenting the company's strategic update .
"The key message for this ‚Äãdivision is clear - ‚Äãthis business ‚Å†will be self-financed over the next three years," Imaz said.
"This represents a critical inflection ‚Äãpoint as the business transitions from cash ‚Äãconsumer to ‚Å†cash generator, moving from a build-out phase to disciplined growth," he said.
Repsol, which also cut its renewable power generation target, ‚Å†will ‚Äãfocus on green energy projects ‚Äãoffering the best returns while limiting exposure thanks to asset sales and ‚Äãpartnerships, he said.

item.title: Exclusive: Hezbollah returns to guerrilla roots, awaits Israeli invasion
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 10 (Reuters) - Lebanon's Hezbollah is applying lessons from its last war with Israel as it braces ‚Äãfor a possible full-scale Israeli invasion and protracted conflict, returning to its roots in guerrilla warfare in south Lebanon, four Lebanese sources said.
Operating in ‚Äåsmall units, fighters from the Iran-backed group are avoiding the use of communication devices that could be at risk of Israeli tapping, and are rationing the use of key anti-tank rockets as they engage Israeli troops, said the sources, who are familiar with Hezbollah military activities.
Some 15 months since Israel pounded Hezbollah in their last war, the group sparked a new Israeli offensive last week by ‚Äãopening fire to avenge the killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei at the start of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
Heavily criticised in Lebanon for dragging ‚Äãthe country into a war that has displaced 700,000 people, Hezbollah has described its actions as "existential defence", framing it as a response to Israeli ‚Å†attacks that have continued since a 2024 ceasefire.
While Israel plans for the likely continuation of its Lebanon offensive after the Iran war, the four sources said Hezbollah's calculations ‚Äãare based on Iran's clerical leadership surviving the war, leading to a regional ceasefire of which it would be part.
The sources who ‚Äãare familiar with Hezbollah thinking declined to be named due to the sensitivity of the matter. The details of how Hezbollah is operating in the field have not previously been reported.
Hezbollah's media office didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.
Hezbollah, a Shi'ite Muslim group founded by Iran's Revolutionary Guards in 1982, was the only Lebanese group to keep its weapons at the end of ‚Äãthe 1975-90 civil war to fight Israeli troops who occupied the south until withdrawing in 2000.
Hezbollah's role in driving them out has underpinned its popularity among many Shi'ites, ‚Äãthough its decision to enter the Iran war has drawn criticism from within the Shi'ite community.
This war has come at a critical juncture for Hezbollah.
Greatly weakened during the 2024 war, Hezbollah has ‚Äåfaced pressure ‚Å†from the Lebanese state to disarm. The Beirut government last week banned Hezbollah's military activities.
Adding to the pressures on Hezbollah since the 2024 war, its Syrian ally President Bashar al-Assad was toppled in December of that year, severing the main supply route from Iran.
The sources said much of Hezbollah's fighting on the ground had been focused so far near the town of Khiyam, near the intersection of Lebanon's border with Israel and Syria.
This is one area where Hezbollah believes any Israeli land invasion could begin.
Reuters reported last week ‚Äãthat Hezbollah's elite Radwan fighters, who withdrew from ‚Äãthe south following the 2024 ceasefire, ‚Å†had returned to the area.
ISRAELI SOURCE: HEZBOLLAH STABILIZING RANKS DESPITE BLOWS
An Israeli security source said there was no sign that Hezbollah was looking to de-escalate - quite the opposite. While Israel had eliminated a few of Hezbollah's very senior commanders, it seemed that the ‚Äãgroup was managing to stabilize its ranks and make and execute decisions.
Two of the Lebanese sources said four deputies had ‚Äãbeen appointed for every ‚Å†Hezbollah commander, to ensure continued operations.
The Israeli military says it has struck hundreds of Hezbollah targets since March 2, launching airstrikes in the south, Beirut's Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs, and the eastern Bekaa Valley.
The Israeli military has also sent more soldiers into south Lebanon, where some of its troops had remained since 2024, establishing what it has called forward defensive positions ‚Å†to guard ‚Äãagainst the risk of Hezbollah attacks on northern Israel.
Two Israeli soldiers have been killed in Lebanon.
Hezbollah has ‚Äãlaunched daily drone and rocket attacks at Israel.
In 2024, not only did Israel booby-trap hundreds of pagers used by Hezbollah, but it also penetrated the group's private phone network, according to Lebanese officials familiar with ‚ÄãHezbollah's post-war investigation into breaches.
The sources said Hezbollah was avoiding any devices that could be susceptible to eavesdropping.

item.title: Israeli strikes hit near Beirut as envoy says disarming Hezbollah could end war
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 10 (Reuters) - Israel's military pounded the Lebanese capital's southern suburbs with air strikes on Tuesday and its troops ‚Äãpushed deeper into the country's south, as an Israeli envoy said the key to ending the war ‚Äåwas disarming Lebanese militant group Hezbollah.
Lebanon was pulled deep into the war in the Middle East last week, when Iran-backed Hezbollah opened fire on Israel to avenge the killing of Iran's supreme leader.
Israel has since launched air strikes across Lebanon's south, east and Beirut's suburbs, killing nearly 500 people including more ‚Äãthan 80 children, according to Lebanon's health ministry.
Strikes on Beirut's southern suburbs on Tuesday afternoon sent thick columns of ‚Äãsmoke over the city. Two hours before they began, an Israeli military spokesperson ordered residents to ‚Å†leave immediately, specifying three new districts that should be evacuated.
A member of the municipal council for the area told Reuters ‚Äãfamilies there were fleeing, adding to the nearly 700,000 that Lebanese authorities say have already been displaced by the war.
Lebanon's Minister of ‚ÄãSocial Affairs Haneen Sayed said on Tuesday that the state was bracing itself for higher displacement figures than in 2024, when the last war between Israel and Hezbollah pushed more than a million people out of their homes.
"So we expect that the needs, the numbers of displacement, will ‚Äãbe higher than in 2024. Now on the other side in terms of resources, there's far less resources this year ‚Äãgiven the global situation, the regional war that's happening," she said.
Sayed spoke to Reuters at Beirut's airport, ‚Äåwhere ‚Å†the European Union was delivering 45 tons of emergency supplies including medical kits and blankets.
"Our traditional partners and friends in the Gulf are of course under stress themselves. So we're appealing to the international community to be with us at this moment to help stabilize the situation in terms of humanitarian needs," Sayed said.
Israeli troops made advances on Tuesday in additional towns in ‚Äãsoutheastern Lebanon, including with armoured columns, ‚ÄãLebanese security sources told ‚Å†Reuters.
Lebanese President Joseph Aoun on Monday had signaled his openness to enter direct negotiations with Israel to end the war.
But Israel's ambassador to France Joshua Zarka said on Tuesday that words ‚Äãwere not enough.
"At this stage, I‚Äôm not aware of any decision to enter negotiations to end ‚Äãthis war," Zarka ‚Å†said. "What would end it is the disarmament of Hezbollah ‚Äî and that is a choice for the Lebanese government," he said.
Zarka said Lebanon's government was "making very good statements, but to these comments they need to add actions."
Lebanon's government last year vowed to establish a ‚Å†state monopoly ‚Äãon arms and confiscated part of Hezbollah's arsenal in the country's south, without ‚Äãobjections from the group.
But Hezbollah has refused to disarm in full, and Lebanese authorities were fearful that taking its arms by force could ignite a civil ‚Äãconflict.

item.title: British warship HMS Dragon departs for Eastern Mediterranean
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - British warship HMS Dragon departed for the Eastern Mediterranean on Tuesday, more than a week after Britain's air base in ‚ÄãCyprus was attacked by a drone in the aftermath of ‚Äåthe United States and Israel launching attacks against Iran.
Britain's government has been criticised for not being able to send a warship to the region sooner, ‚Äãwith military vessels from allies Greece and France arriving within days ‚Äãof Iran launching retaliatory attacks across the Gulf.
On March ‚Å†1 RAF Akrotiri in Cyprus was hit by an Iranian-made drone, ‚Äãthought to have been launched from Lebanon or Iraq.
The decision to ‚Äãdeploy the destroyer was made a week ago but it has taken days to prepare it for the journey, adding to scrutiny of Britain's military readiness .
The government ‚Äãhas defended its response, saying it had pre-deployed other assets to ‚Äãthe region including radar systems, air defence, and F-35 jets.
HMS Dragon is a Type ‚Äå45 ‚Å†air-defence destroyer equipped with the Sea Viper missile system and advanced radar designed to track and neutralise airborne threats, according to the Royal Navy's website.
The government has also cited its close cooperation with ‚Äãthe United States in ‚Äãpreparation and ‚Å†in response to the conflict, despite criticism from Trump over Britain's support.
Trump has lambasted Prime Minister Keir ‚ÄãStarmer for limiting Britain's military support for the ‚Äãstrikes on ‚Å†Iran, plunging the relationship between the historically close military allies into crisis.
Starmer has allowed the U.S. to use British bases for defensive action ‚Å†but ‚Äãhas said that he would not approve ‚ÄãUK involvement in offensive actions unless he was sure they were legal and were ‚Äãpart of a clear plan.

item.title: US State Department OKs potential sale of rocket systems to Sweden
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The ‚ÄãU.S. State ‚ÄåDepartment said on ‚ÄãTuesday ‚Äãthat it had ‚Å†approved ‚Äãthe potential ‚Äãsale of M142 High ‚ÄãMobility ‚ÄãArtillery Rocket Systems ‚Äåand ‚Å†related equipment to Sweden.
The ‚Äãprincipal ‚Äãcontractor ‚Å†will be ‚ÄãLockheed ‚ÄãMartin (LMT.N) , opens new tab .

item.title: Russia told Trump it isn't sharing US military asset info with Iran, says Witkoff
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Russia has denied sharing ‚Äãintelligence with Iran on U.S. military ‚Äåassets in the Middle East, U.S. special envoy Steve Witkoff said on Tuesday in a CNBC ‚Äãinterview.
Witkoff said the denial came during ‚Äãa phone call that U.S. President Donald ‚Å†Trump had with Russian President Vladimir ‚ÄãPutin on Monday.
The Washington Post reported on ‚ÄãFriday that Russia was providing Iran with targeting information that included locations of U.S. warships and aircraft ‚Äãin the Middle East.
"Yesterday on the call ‚Äãwith the president, the Russians said that they have ‚Äånot ‚Å†been sharing," Witkoff said when asked if Washington thought Russia had shared with Tehran intelligence about the location of U.S. military ‚Äãassets.
"We can ‚Äãtake them ‚Å†at their word. But they did say that. And yesterday ‚Äãmorning, independently, Jared (Kushner) and I had ‚Äãa ‚Å†call with (Kremlin foreign policy aide Yuri) Ushakov who reiterated the same," said Witkoff.
He added: "That's a ‚Å†better ‚Äãquestion for the intel ‚Äãpeople, but let's hope that they're not sharing."

item.title: Ukrainian missile strike on Russia's Bryansk causes casualties, local governor says
item.description: MOSCOW, March 10 (Reuters) - Ukraine ‚Äãhas ‚Äålaunched a ‚Äãmissile ‚Äãstrike on the ‚Å†city ‚Äãof ‚ÄãBryansk in south-western ‚ÄãRussia, ‚Äãcausing casualties and ‚Äåinjuries, ‚Å†regional governor Alexander ‚ÄãBogomaz ‚Äãsaid ‚Å†on ‚ÄãTuesday.

item.title: Trump administration estimates cost of Iran war's first two days at $5.6 bln, source says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump's administration said it used $5.6 billion in munitions during the ‚Äãfirst two days of strikes against ‚ÄåIran in a report provided to U.S. congressional committees, a source familiar with the information said on Tuesday.
Members ‚Äãof Congress, who may soon have ‚Äãto approve additional funding for the war, ‚Å†have expressed concern that the conflict will deplete ‚ÄãU.S. military stocks at a time when the ‚Äãdefense industry was already struggling to keep up with demand.
Trump met executives from seven defense contractors on Friday as ‚Äãthe Pentagon worked to replenish supplies.
The administration ‚Äãhas not provided a public assessment of the cost of ‚Äåthe ‚Å†conflict it launched on Feb. 28 along with ally Israel.
Members of Congress have clamored for more information, including public testimony from officials about ‚Äãissues including ‚Äãhow the ‚Å†conflict might affect the U.S. military's readiness to defend the country.
Several ‚Äãcongressional aides have said they expect ‚Äãthe ‚Å†White House to soon submit a request to Congress for additional funding for the war. Some ‚Å†officials ‚Äãhave said the request could ‚Äãbe for $50 billion, but others have said that estimate seems ‚Äãlow.

item.title: Zelenskiy says Turkey ready to host next trilateral peace talks
item.description:
KYIV, March 10 (Reuters) - Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy ‚Äãsaid Turkey was ‚Äåready to host the next ‚Äãround of trilateral ‚Äãpeace talks between ‚Å†Kyiv, Moscow and ‚ÄãWashington, after speaking ‚Äãto President Tayyip Erdogan on Tuesday.
"The President ‚Äãnoted that ‚ÄãT√ºrkiye is ready to host ‚Äåthe ‚Å†next round of talks in a trilateral format. ‚ÄãWe ‚Äãappreciate ‚Å†this initiative and hope ‚Äãit can ‚Äãproduce ‚Å†results," he wrote on Facebook.

item.title: Trump envoy Witkoff says he will probably travel to Israel next week-CNBC interview
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - U.S. ‚Äãspecial envoy ‚ÄåSteve Witkoff, a ‚Äãtop adviser ‚Äãto President Donald ‚Å†Trump, ‚Äãsaid in ‚Äãa CNBC interview on ‚ÄãTuesday ‚Äãthat he probably ‚Äåwill ‚Å†travel to Israel next week ‚Äãto ‚Äãcoordinate ‚Å†on Iran ‚Äãwar ‚Äãplans.

item.title: Number of Iranian missiles and drones fired at Gulf countries
item.description:
March 10 - Here are statistics released by Gulf ‚Äãdefence ministries and militaries on drones ‚Äåand missiles that Iran has fired at their territories since the start of the U.S.-Israeli ‚Äãair war against Iran , as ‚Äãof March 9:
Ballistic missiles:
Drones:
Cruise missiles:
8 detected and destroyed
QATAR
Ballistic missiles:
Drones:
Cruise ‚Äãmissiles:

BAHRAIN

KUWAIT

Drones:
Kuwait reported missile and drone attacks from March 2-5 ‚Äãbut gave no specific data. No ‚Äãdata ‚Å†was available for Saudi Arabia and Oman.

item.title: Israel not seeking endless war with Iran, foreign minister says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 10 (Reuters) - Israel is not seeking an endless war with Iran and will coordinate ‚Äåwith the United States on when to end the fighting, Foreign Minister Gideon Saar said on Tuesday, declining to publicly state a timeline for when the conflict could end.
The U.S.-Israeli war with Iran, now in its ‚Äã11th day, has engulfed the Middle East, with Iranian strikes hitting neighbouring states, including ‚Äãthe United Arab Emirates, and Israel fighting Hezbollah in Lebanon as well as ‚Å†striking Iran.
"We will continue until the minute that we and our partners think that is ‚Äãappropriate to stop," Saar told journalists in Jerusalem alongside his German counterpart.
"We are not looking for ‚Äãan endless war," he said.
German Chancellor Friedrich Merz said earlier in Berlin that there was growing concern in Europe over the war and there appeared to be no plan to bring it to an end.
"We want ‚Äãto remove, for the long-term, existential threats from Iran to Israel," Saar said in response to ‚Äãa Reuters question on what victory would look like to the government.
He described Iran's newly appointed supreme leader ‚ÄåMojtaba ‚Å†Khamanei - son of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei who was killed by the Israeli military on the first day of the war - as an extremist.
Israel has said it aims to eliminate Iran's clerical rule by destroying its nuclear and ballistic missile programs and creating conditions for Iranians to overthrow their ‚Äãrulers.
Saar said there was ‚Äãan opportunity to ‚Å†create conditions for Iranians to "regain their freedom," while acknowledging that it may not happen during the war and could come afterward.
"We must not miss ‚Äãthis opportunity with partial results."
German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul, the first senior ‚Äãforeign official ‚Å†to publicly visit Israel since the war began, said he was confident Israel and Washington were open to a diplomatic solution that could lead to an end to the war.
But any such solution ‚Å†would need ‚Äãto include agreements with Iran on its nuclear and ‚Äãmissile programs, as well as its support for regional militias, terms that Wadephul said Tehran had made clear that it ‚Äãwas not currently prepared to accept.

item.title: Spain's LNG imports rise almost 20% from last year, with US as main supplier
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Liquefied natural gas from the United States made up 33.8% of Spain's ‚Äãtotal gas imports in February, as ‚Äåthe country's gas imports rose almost 20% from the same month last year, data ‚Äãshowed on Tuesday.
The U.S. has become Europe's ‚Äãdominant supplier of LNG as the region ‚Å†pushed to phase out energy imports ‚Äãfrom Russia in the wake of ‚Äãits invasion of Ukraine.
In February, Spain imported the equivalent of 31,422 gigawatt-hours (GWh) of gas, sharply up ‚Äãfrom the 26,318 GWh it imported ‚Äãa year earlier, according to data from Spanish gas ‚Äågrid ‚Å†operator Enagas.
Spain imported 10,612 GWh of gas from the United States, while Algeria, traditionally Spain's largest gas supplier, which ‚Äãmostly pumps ‚Äãgas directly ‚Å†to Spain through pipelines, was the second biggest supplier with ‚Äã9,151 GWh of gas, about ‚Äã29% ‚Å†of the total imported.
Natural gas imports from Russia accounted for 14.6% of supply in ‚Å†February, ‚Äãdeclining roughly 52% in ‚Äãabsolute terms from a year ago.

item.title: Higher gasoline prices, volatile stocks, could hit both low- and high-income US consumers as war persists
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Rising gasoline prices and a wobbly stock market are increasing the risk that the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran could hit hard among consumers across the economic spectrum in the United States, undercutting a key prop of economic growth that had been expected to surge this year on the basis of beefed-up income tax refunds, ‚Äãrelatively low unemployment, and rising asset values.
Heading into the year, analysts felt there was reason both "spurs" of the U.S.' so-called K-shaped economy could sustain or even grow spending in coming months, with wealth effects ‚Äåleading better-off families to consume more and windfall tax refunds from new exemptions on overtime and tip income helping hourly and some service industry workers.
Instead, both sides could be under different sorts of pressure. Average national gasoline prices topped $3.50 a gallon as of Tuesday morning according to American Automobile Association data, a 17% jump from the roughly $3 average from before the beginning of the conflict. Prices are now above $3 in every state except Kansas, where the average was $2.96, and with oil markets still volatile given shipping disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz analysts see $4 gas as ‚Äãa possibility if the conflict persists.
Stocks have come off their highs and the uncertainty about what's next, a factor in household spending based on rising net worth, was on display when President Donald Trump on Monday hinted at ‚Äãa possible quick end to the conflict and sent stocks higher, only to temper his language overnight and keep open the chance of an extended conflict with all of the attendant ‚Å†risks to global supply chains, commodity markets, and corporate earnings.
Major U.S. stock indices were little changed when trading opened on Tuesday morning.
For lower-income households, rising gasoline costs could pull money from other categories of spending, spreading the pain across businesses that ‚Äãcould in turn begin to lower job and investment plans in a suddenly uncertain environment.
"The higher the price and the longer it goes the more you shift from higher prices benefitting some companies who boost oil production and get more revenue...to really ‚Äãpinching consumers and being a drag on the economy. There is some point of prices being at a certain level for a certain amount of time that it flips from being a plus to GDP to a drag on GDP and increasing the likelihood of a downturn," said Luke Tilley, chief economist at Wilmington Trust, with oil in the $85 to $100 per barrel range for several months likely to "materially increase the risk of recession because the labor market is already in such a challenging state."
Benchmark Brent crude topped $116 a barrel on Monday before falling below $90 ‚Äãa barrel, then rising again on Tuesday morning. Far from an offramp, U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said Tuesday would see the most intense strikes yet on Iran, even as a top general talked of targeting Iran's capacity to lay mines ‚Äãin the Strait of Hormuz, a possible step towards reopening shipping through a chokepoint where the movement of oil from the Middle East has all but stopped.
The sudden shift in risks to the U.S. and global economy has posed a challenge to central bank policymakers, particularly ‚Äåin the U.S., ‚Å†where officials saw an essentially strong economy facing a balanced set of risks between inflation that was lodged about a percentage point above their 2% target but expected to fall, and an unemployment rate seemingly stable in a range around 4.3% with no clear consensus that it was about to spike higher.
That view has now been hit from both sides: with the economy already having shed jobs unexpectedly in February, there is now the added risk that uncertainty stemming from the conflict could cause businesses to become more cautious about hiring, while inflation could rise more broadly if higher oil prices drive up costs for other things, like shipping rates or home heating bills.
Even though higher fuel prices may have only a temporary influence on inflation, ‚Äãor could even ease broader price pressures on net if ‚Äãconsumers shift spending away from other areas and overall ‚Å†growth slows, the current context poses other risks.
In analysis conducted after oil costs jumped in 2022 following Russia's invasion of Ukraine, Kansas City Fed researchers concluded that increases in high-profile consumer prices , opens new tab like those for gasoline can have an outsized effect on household inflation expectations if those are already elevated by a previous inflation shock - a dynamic Fed policymakers continue to worry about and ‚Äãhave cited as a reason to keep monetary policy restrictive in order to hold expectations in check.
Investors still expect the Fed to cut interest rates this year, but the ‚Äãtiming shifted back after the ‚Å†start of U.S. military action, with a potential standoff developing among policymakers between inflation and growth concerns.
The Fed meets next week and is expected to keep its policy rate steady in the current 3.5% to 3.75% range.
Vincent Reinhart, chief economist at BNY Investments and a former top Fed staffer, said it was far too early for the central bank to draw any conclusions about the likely economic fallout from the conflict. Even concerns about the impact on growth have to be tempered by the fact that the U.S.' role ‚Å†as a net ‚Äãenergy producer means that while higher global prices may mean higher prices at the pump for U.S. consumers, it also means higher income, and ‚Äãpotentially more jobs and investment, for U.S.-based energy firms.
But the longer prices remain elevated the greater the risks, with per-barrel costs in the upper $90 range for a month or longer the sort of material shock that could start to undercut consumption and growth.
"You have to have prices meaningfully higher than what ‚Äãpeople are used to," for an oil shock to change the course of the U.S. economy, Reinhart said. "It's got to be big enough. We're not at the big enough stage."

item.title: Brent oil prices to remain above $95/bbl over next 2 months on Mideast conflict, EIA says
item.description:
HOUSTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Brent oil prices are set to remain ‚Äãabove $95 a barrel over the ‚Äånext two months as conflict in the Middle East rages ‚Äãon, the Energy Information ‚ÄãAdministration said on Tuesday in ‚Å†its monthly short term ‚Äãenergy outlook report.
Brent crude futures ‚Äãwill then fall below $80 a barrel in the third quarter of 2026, ‚Äãbefore slipping to around $70 ‚Äãby the end of the year.
The ‚Äåeffective ‚Å†closure of the critical chokepoint, the Strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of ‚Äãglobal oil ‚Äãflows ‚Å†every day, will cause Mideast oil output ‚Äãto fall further in ‚Äãthe ‚Å†coming weeks.
Those production shut-ins will gradually ease as transit ‚Å†resumes, ‚Äãthe EIA added.

item.title: Elected in an economic upswing, Chile's Kast takes office as global turmoil rattles markets
item.description:
SANTIAGO, March 10 (Reuters) - Elected on promises of economic growth, deregulation and public spending cuts, local markets rallied after Chile elected far-right Jose Antonio Kast as president in December, but the economic tailwinds have turned turbulent as the Iran war has sent global markets into a tailspin.
Kast, who ‚Äãtakes power on Wednesday, is now in charge of managing the turmoil. A spokesperson for Kast's economic team said that no economic ‚Äåcontingencies were planned at the moment, but did not provide further details of how recent developments would impact their economic agenda.
"(Kast) was elected on a promise of being an 'emergency government' that was going to fix things that mattered to Chileans quickly," said Kenneth Bunker, a political analyst and academic at University of San Sebastian, adding that one of Kast's main promises ‚Äãwas economic growth.
"Kast's priorities will probably remain stable, but his ability to carry them out will probably be affected by the exchange rate, inflation ‚Äãand economic growth," Bunker said, adding that if his government can't achieve the growth it was expecting, several of its ‚Å†plans could be delayed.
Chile, the world's largest copper producer and second-largest lithium producer, is sensitive to fluctuations in international markets.
Copper, Chile's main export and the country's ‚Äãmain source of revenue, climbed from less than $10,000 per ton last June to a peak of $13,618 at the end of January and every cent increase represents ‚Äãan additional $27 million to $35 million for the Chilean treasury, according to the outgoing government.
Before the Iran war, experts estimated Chile could receive up to $4 billion in additional revenue from the soaring prices of the red metal but the price has been volatile in recent weeks, dropping up to 8% from recent highs. As of Tuesday, the price has risen back up ‚Äãto $13,098.
Chile is also one of the largest oil importers in Latin America due to its lack of domestic production, increasing the impact of rising oil prices ‚Äãwhich hit nearly $120 a barrel since the start of the war.
"The Iran war has increased inflation risks considerably," Oxford Economics said in a report surveying emerging markets published on Monday.
The ‚Äåreport added ‚Å†Central and Eastern Europe, Chile and India would be the hardest hit in its oil shock simulation, which showed Q2 inflation estimates rising between 0.4ppts to 1.7ppts.
"(Chile) is very sensitive to external shocks, very sensitive, whether they be geopolitical, military, or fluctuations in global markets," said Marcela Vera, an economist at the University of Santiago.
"The economy has very few financial protection barriers and it has a lot of free trade agreements, so its model is based on a primary export system."
After ‚Äãmonths of setting new highs, Chile's ‚ÄãIPSA stock market continued to rise ‚Å†following the election and peaked in late January, with about a 65% surge from a year earlier.
Chile's peso, boosted by strong copper prices, had been gaining steam since July and reached its strongest point in years in early February.
But since ‚Äãthose recent highs, the stock market has dropped over 10% and the currency has depreciated about 5% as spiking ‚Äãoil prices and global ‚Å†economic uncertainty rock the nation.
Vera noted that Chile has a fuel stabilization fund, MEPCO, that operates on three-week cycles to help smooth out price spikes.
"If the war continues for months, then we'll have a chronic effect on our economy," Vera said. "It won't just be the price of oil going up, but the cost of logistics and ‚Å†the price ‚Äãof the dollar."
A JPMorgan report released Friday said that while MEPCO dampens the impacts of spiking ‚Äãoil prices, it "does not eliminate pass-through effects" and raised its inflation forecast by 20 bp to 3.6% for December with "risks tilted to the upside."

item.title: Brazil's Bolsonaro seeks court approval for prison visit by Trump adviser
item.description: BRASILIA, March 10 (Reuters) - Lawyers ‚Äãfor former ‚ÄåBrazilian President Jair ‚ÄãBolsonaro have ‚Äãasked the country's ‚Å†Supreme ‚ÄãCourt to ‚Äãallow Darren Beattie, who ‚Äãwas ‚Äãrecently tapped by ‚Äåthe ‚Å†Trump administration for a senior advisory ‚Äãrole ‚Äãoverseeing ‚Å†Brazil , to ‚Äãvisit ‚Äãhim ‚Å†in prison, a ‚Å†document ‚Äãshowed ‚Äãon Tuesday.

item.title: US urges Israel to stop attacks on Iran energy sites, Axios reports
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The ‚ÄãUnited States has asked Israel ‚Äåto halt strikes on Iran's energy infrastructure, Axios reported on Tuesday, ‚Äãciting three sources familiar ‚Äãwith the matter.
Washington sent the message ‚Å†at a senior political ‚Äãlevel and to IDF Chief of ‚ÄãStaff Eyal Zamir, Axios reported, citing an Israeli official.
The Trump administration cited ‚Äãthree reasons, including a goal ‚Äãto cooperate with Iran's oil sector after ‚Äåthe ‚Å†war, according to the report.
Other reasons included a fear that such strikes would harm the ‚ÄãIranian ‚Äãpublic and ‚Å†trigger massive Iranian retaliatory attacks on energy infrastructure ‚Äãacross Gulf states, Axios ‚Äãreported.
The ‚Å†White House, U.S. State Department and Israeli Embassy in Washington ‚Å†did ‚Äãnot immediately respond ‚Äãto requests for comments.

item.title: Ukraine sends drone experts to three countries in Middle East, Zelenskiy says
item.description:
KYIV, March 10 (Reuters) - Ukraine has sent air defence teams to Qatar, the United Arab Emirates and ‚ÄåSaudi Arabia to help them in their fight against Iran's aerial attacks, President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Tuesday.
Gulf states have expended large quantities of precious air-defence missiles to counter Iran‚Äôs attack drones and have sought Ukraine's expertise in downing ‚Äãthem. Kyiv downs Russian drones every night using an array of weaponry including cheaper, ‚Äãsmaller drones or jamming equipment.
"Regarding the situation in the Middle East, we have ‚Å†sent our teams: three professional, equipped teams," Zelenskiy told reporters. He did not give details ‚Äãon the teams but said they should all be in place this week.
His communications adviser also said ‚ÄãUkrainian specialists had already been deployed to a U.S. military base in Jordan, without providing details.
Zelenskiy said the presence of Ukrainian pilots was key to the effectiveness of interceptor drone operations.
"Even those countries that were quietly buying ‚Äãinterceptors have, I think, realised that without our military, our operators, our software... without all of ‚Äãthat, the interceptors simply don't work."
Since the U.S. and Israel began airstrikes on Iran on February 28, ‚ÄåUkraine has ‚Å†sought to leverage its long experience of countering Russia's Iranian-designed Shahed attack drones.
Kyiv says Russia - which now manufactures them itself - has launched more than 57,000 Shahed-type drones at Ukraine so far.
Reuters reported last week that the U.S. and Qatar were looking to buy interceptor drones from Ukraine as a ‚Äãmuch cheaper alternative to missiles ‚Äãfor downing Shaheds.
Asked ‚Å†what Kyiv would get in return for the deployment of the specialist teams, Zelenskiy said Ukraine was seeking air defence missiles above all else.
Kyiv has ‚Äãcomplained for months of critical shortages of those munitions, particularly for the ‚ÄãU.S.-made Patriot ‚Å†systems which are the Ukraine's only effective tool to intercept ballistic missiles.
The Iran war has delayed a fresh round of peace talks brokered by Washington between Russia and Ukraine.
Zelenskiy told reporters on Tuesday the ‚Å†next ‚Äãtrilateral meeting had been postponed to next week, adding that ‚Äãhe wanted more swaps of prisoners of war and discussion of a possible meeting between himself, U.S. President Donald Trump ‚Äãand Russia's Vladimir Putin.

item.title: Exclusive: As many as 150 US troops wounded so far in Iran war, sources say
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - As many as 150 U.S. troops have been wounded in the 10-day-old ‚Äãwar with Iran, two people familiar with the matter told ‚ÄåReuters on Tuesday.
The casualty figure has not been previously reported. Prior to Reuters' publication of the figure, the Pentagon had only disclosed eight U.S. personnel seriously injured.
In a statement after ‚ÄãReuters published its report, the Pentagon estimated the figure to be approximately ‚Äã140 wounded and said the vast majority of them were ‚Å†minor.
"Since the start of Operation Epic Fury, approximately 140 U.S. service members ‚Äãhave been wounded over 10 days of sustained attacks," said chief Pentagon ‚Äãspokesperson Sean Parnell.
He said 108 of the wounded service members had already returned to duty.
Parnell said the eight seriously wounded service members were receiving the highest level of medical ‚Äãcare.
Reuters could not determine the types of injuries and whether they include traumatic brain ‚Äãinjuries, which are common after exposure to blasts.
Iran has launched retaliatory strikes against U.S. military bases ‚Äåsince ‚Å†the start of the conflict on Feb. 28. It has also struck diplomatic missions in Arab Gulf states as well as hotels and airports and damaged oil infrastructure.
The Pentagon says the number of Iranian strikes has fallen sharply ‚Äãsince the start of ‚Äãthe war, as ‚Å†the U.S. military bombs Iran's weapons inventories and targets Iran's more limited number of missile launchers.
Asked if ‚ÄãIran was a stronger adversary than he expected when the ‚ÄãU.S. military ‚Å†drew up its war plans, General Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, told reporters earlier on Tuesday that the fight was not harder than ‚Å†expected.
"I think ‚Äãthey're fighting, and I respect that, but ‚ÄãI don't think they are more formidable than what we thought," Caine told a Pentagon briefing.

item.title: US Senate confirms National Security Agency director, ending long vacancy
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The U.S. Senate confirmed General Joshua Rudd on Tuesday to lead the National Security Agency and the ‚Äãnation's Cyber Command, filling a position that had been vacant ‚Äåsince President Donald Trump abruptly fired his predecessor in a national security purge.
The Senate voted 71-29 to confirm Rudd, making him a four-star general.
Trump nominated Rudd, then ‚Äãa lieutenant general and No. 2 at the military's ‚ÄãIndo-Pacific Command, for the so-called dual-hat position in December.
The NSA ‚Å†monitors, collects and processes information and data for global intelligence ‚Äãand counterintelligence purposes. Cyber Command conducts offensive and defensive cyber operations, ‚Äãan increasingly important part of military campaigns including the current Iran war .
The agency was shaken up in April, when Trump fired General Timothy Haugh along with Wendy ‚ÄãNoble, his deputy.
No reason was given for the firings, which took ‚Äãplace after the Republican president had an Oval Office meeting with Laura Loomer, ‚Äåa ‚Å†far-right political activist known as a conspiracy theorist, who said on X that she had given Trump a list of officials she considered disloyal to him.
In a statement, Republican U.S. Senator Tom Cotton ‚Äãof Arkansas, chairman ‚Äãof the Senate ‚Å†Intelligence Committee, praised the confirmation.
"General Rudd is a war hero with a lifetime of service to ‚Äãour nation. He is the right choice to lead ‚Äãthe ‚Å†protection of our nation from cyberattacks by Iran , Russia, and China," said Cotton, who criticized Democrats for not acting more quickly.
Senator Ron Wyden of ‚Å†Oregon, a ‚Äãsenior member of the intelligence panel, ‚Äãsaid in a letter to Trump , opens new tab that he did not feel Rudd was qualified and ‚Äãblocked efforts to fast-track his confirmation.

item.title: U.S. Navy has not yet escorted ships through Strait of Hormuz, White House says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The U.S. military has not yet escorted any commercial ships through the Strait of ‚ÄãHormuz, the White House said on Tuesday, just after U.S. Secretary of ‚ÄåEnergy Chris Wright deleted a post on X in which had said the U.S. Navy successfully escorted an oil tanker through the key water way.
The U.S.-Israel war against Iran has already effectively halted ‚Äãshipments through the Strait along Iran's coast, where a fifth of global ‚Äãoil and liquefied natural gas normally passes, and Middle East oil ‚Å†producers have run out of storage and stopped pumping.
U.S. President Donald Trump said ‚Äãon March 3 that the U.S. would provide protection through the Strait for oil ‚Äãtankers. The Pentagon on Tuesday renewed threats to hit Iran harder unless shipments can flow through and said it was striking Iranian mine-laying vessels and mine storage facilities.
Wright then posted on X ‚Äãthat the U.S. Navy had escorted an oil tanker through the Strait of ‚ÄãHormuz "to ensure oil remains flowing to global markets."
Soon after, he deleted the post for reasons that ‚Äåwere ‚Å†unclear.
The United States has not yet escorted any oil tankers or vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters when asked about the issue at a press conference later on Tuesday.
Commenting on Wright's remarks, a ‚Äãspokesperson for Iran's Revolutionary ‚ÄãGuards denied an ‚Å†oil ship had been escorted.
"Any movement of the U.S. fleet and its allies will be stopped by our missiles and ‚Äãdrones," Ali Mohammad Naini said in comments carried by Iranian ‚Äãstate media.
The ‚Å†top U.S. general earlier on Tuesday said the U.S. military has started looking at ways to potentially escort ships through the Strait of Hormuz, should it be ordered to ‚Å†do ‚Äãso.
"We're looking at a range of options there," General ‚ÄãDan Caine told reporters at the Pentagon.

item.title: White House to Americans: Rise in energy prices over Iran is temporary
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The American public will see oil and gas ‚Äãprices drop rapidly, White House press secretary ‚ÄåKaroline Leavitt said on Tuesday, once the objectives of the joint Israeli-U.S. air war against Iran are fully achieved.
"Rest ‚Äãassured, to the American people, the recent ‚Äãincrease in oil and gas prices is temporary, and ‚Å†this operation will result in lower gas ‚Äãprices in the long term," Leavitt told reporters.
Oil ‚Äãprices on Monday surged to more than $119 a barrel - their highest since June 2022 - as supply cuts by Saudi Arabia ‚Äãand other producers stoked fears of major ‚Äãdisruptions to global supplies.
Leavitt added that Trump and his energy team ‚Äåwere ‚Å†closely watching the markets, speaking with industry leaders, and that the U.S. military was drawing up additional options in line with the president's directive ‚Äãto keep the ‚ÄãStrait of ‚Å†Hormuz open.
The deliberations reflect White House worries that the surge in ‚Äãoil prices - following more than a week ‚Äãof ‚Å†U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran - will hurt U.S. businesses and consumers ahead of the November midterm ‚Å†elections, ‚Äãwhen Trump's fellow Republicans hope ‚Äãto retain control of Congress.

item.title: Nigeria prioritises local gasoline supply, suspends import licences
item.description:
LAGOS, March 10 (Reuters) - Nigeria has suspended the issuance of gasoline import licences for a second straight month as regulators begin enforcing provisions of ‚Äãthe Petroleum Industry Act (PIA) that only allow imports when the African ‚Äåcountry's domestic supply falls short.
Data from the Nigerian Midstream and Downstream Petroleum Regulatory Authority (NMDPRA) show no import licences were issued in February, while the Crude Oil Refineries Association of ‚ÄãNigeria (CORAN) confirmed that none has been issued so far in March, signalling ‚Äãa shift towards prioritising local output.
This shift highlights a stronger ‚Å†intent by Nigerian authorities to protect domestic refining and marks a win ‚Äãfor the Dangote Refinery, which last year sued the regulator and the state ‚Äãoil company to force a halt to imports.
Under the PIA, the regulator may grant import permits only when domestic production is not enough to meet national demand.
The previous regulator, who quit ‚Äãlast year, argued that issuing licences was necessary to maintain competition and ‚Äãprevent market dominance.
Fuel pump prices have surged by more than 54% since the U.S. and ‚ÄåIsrael ‚Å†began strikes on Iran last week, pushing global oil markets higher. NMDPRA spokesperson George Ene‚ÄëIta blamed the sharp rise in prices on escalating conflict in the Middle East.
Nigeria's average daily petrol consumption fell to 56.9 million litres per day ‚Äãin February 2026, ‚Äãdown from 60.2 ‚Å†million litres in January.
In February, the Dangote Refinery supplied 36.5 million litres of petrol and 8 million litres of ‚Äãdiesel to the local market. The regulator deemed these volumes ‚Äãwere sufficient, ‚Å†leading to its decision to withhold import licences.
Eche Idoko, spokesperson for the Crude Oil Refiners Association of Nigeria (CORAN), which has long urged the government to stop ‚Å†issuing import ‚Äãlicences that undermine local refiners' margins, welcomed the ‚Äãregulator's stance.
"For us, anything that protects local production is a good move. The challenge now is ‚Äãto sustain the momentum," Idoko said.

item.title: Rising gas prices from Iran war imperil Trump's Republican majority in Congress
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Minutes into Donald Trump 's State of the Union address laying out his argument for why voters should keep Republicans in power in November's midterm elections, the president boasted that falling gasoline prices had ‚Äãsolved a "disaster" left behind by his predecessor.
Two weeks later, gas prices have risen by nearly 60 cents a gallon, part of a burgeoning energy crisis that began when ‚ÄåTrump and Israel launched air strikes against Iran on February 28 and set off a regional conflict that has expanded well beyond Iran's borders.
The pain at the pump is quickly turning into a political headache for Trump and his fellow Republicans, whose narrow congressional majority looks increasingly fragile ahead of the midterms.
Crude oil prices on Tuesday were easing off Monday's dramatic gains, and it remains to be seen where gasoline prices will head in the nearly nine months ‚Äãleading up to the November 3 election. But even before the Iran war, U.S. voters were already angry about the high cost of living and frustrated that Trump has not done ‚Äãmore to address it, Reuters/Ipsos polls show .
"You can't hide gas prices," said Jacob Perry, a Republican strategist. "You can lie about all this other stuff and claim ‚Å†everything is fake news. But there's a giant sign on every street corner saying how bad things are. Literally every commute to work is a reminder."
Democrats have promised to put ‚Äãaffordability center stage in their campaigns. The party needs to flip only three Republican-held House of Representatives seats to win a majority, though they face a tougher path in the Senate.
"Trump promised a Golden ‚ÄãAge in America," House Democratic leader Hakeem Jeffries said on X. "Meanwhile, Republicans are crashing the economy, gas prices are out of control and the extremists are spending billions dropping bombs in the Middle East."
Some House Republicans, who are huddling in Florida this week at a policy retreat to discuss their legislative agenda, acknowledged that surging gas prices are a worry to voters, but cited Trump's assertion that the increase would be short-lived and said the administration's efforts ‚Äãto increase domestic energy production would lessen the sting.
"Every day that people pull into the gas station and pay what they're paying for fuel, I mean, it hurts, and we know it hurts," ‚Äãsaid Representative Austin Scott, of Georgia. "We're going to do what we can to fix it. They understand it's a short-term issue."
The average gas price nationwide on Tuesday was $3.54 a gallon, up about 19% ‚Äåsince the ‚Å†war began, according to AAA.
In a sign of the White House‚Äôs concern, Trump planned to review several options aimed at curbing oil prices, Reuters reported on Monday, including a possible release of crude oil from strategic reserves, restricting U.S. exports and waiving some federal taxes. Trump said on Monday evening that the U.S. would waive some oil-related sanctions, though he did not offer details.
Trump plans to discuss the economy when he travels to Ohio and Kentucky on Wednesday, the White House said.
Higher gas prices may be particularly damaging for a president who won the White House in 2024 largely on the promise that ‚Äãhe would curb inflation, lower energy rates and ‚Äãbring down the cost of living.
Representative Warren Davidson, ‚Å†an Ohio Republican, said the rise in gas prices is a "concern" for the midterms.
"The economy is always going to be one of the things on the ballot," he said. "The other things the president's done on the economy have been tremendous, so we'll see how it turns out."
Trump's own response to the spiking prices seems unlikely to assuage Americans' anxiety. In an interview with Reuters last week, Trump said he had no ‚Äãconcern about gas prices and ‚Å†predicted they would drop once the Iran conflict ends, adding, ‚ÄúIf they rise, they rise.‚Äù
He repeated his contention on social media over the weekend, saying that ‚Äúshort term oil prices‚Äù were a ‚Äúvery small price to pay‚Äù for global security.
Trump‚Äôs shifting rationales for the Iran war, however, have made it more difficult for him to argue that the disruption to the U.S. economy is worthwhile. Administration officials have not presented evidence that Iran posed an ‚Å†immediate threat, and ‚ÄãTrump himself had insisted that U.S. strikes last year ‚Äúobliterated‚Äù the country‚Äôs nuclear weapons program.
"I don‚Äôt think that people care that ‚Äãmuch about Iran," said John Feehery, a Republican strategist. "I think they care about their gas prices. Any president that spends the majority of his time talking about international issues and not talking about dinner table issues, they run a risk of ‚Äãalienating voters.
"This better be pretty quick, that‚Äôs all I can say," he added of the war.

item.title: US power use to beat record highs in 2026 and 2027 as AI use surges, EIA says
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 10 (Reuters) - U.S. ‚Äåpower consumption, which hit its second straight record high in 2025, will rise further in 2026 and 2027, the Energy Information Administration (EIA) said in its Short-Term Energy Outlook (STEO) on Tuesday.
The EIA projected power demand ‚Äãwill rise from a record 4,195 billion kilowatt-hours (kWh) in 2025 to 4,260 billion ‚ÄãkWh in 2026 and 4,388 billion kWh in 2027.
Demand is surging due ‚Å†in large part to data centers dedicated to artificial intelligence and cryptocurrency, and as ‚Äãhomes and businesses use more electricity and less fossil fuel for heat and transportation.
The EIA ‚Äãforecast power sales in 2026 will rise to 1,535 billion kWh for residential consumers, 1,525 billion kWh for commercial customers and 1,056 billion kWh for industrial customers.
The forecasts compare with all-time highs of 1,517 ‚Äãbillion kWh for residential consumers in 2025, 1,486 billion kWh in 2025 for commercial ‚Äãcustomers and 1,064 billion kWh for industrial customers in 2000.
As renewable output rises, the EIA said the ‚Äåshare ‚Å†of power generation from natural gas would hold at 40% in 2026, the same as 2025, before sliding to 39% in 2027. Coal's share will slide from 17% in 2025 to 16% in 2026 and 15% in 2027.
The percentage of renewable generation will rise ‚Äãfrom around 24% in ‚Äã2025 to 25% ‚Å†in 2026 and 27% in 2027, while nuclear power's share will rise from 18% in 2025 to 19% in 2026 before easing ‚Äãback to 18% in 2027, according to the outlook.
The EIA projected ‚Äãgas sales ‚Å†in 2026 would slide to 13.0 billion cubic feet per day (bcfd) for residential consumers, 9.6 bcfd for commercial customers and 23.3 bcfd for industrial customers, but rise to 36.2 bcfd for ‚Å†power generation.
Those ‚Äãfigures compare with all-time highs of 14.3 bcfd ‚Äãin 1996 for residential consumers, 9.9 bcfd in 2025 for commercial customers, 23.8 bcfd in 1973 for industrial customers, ‚Äãand 36.8 bcfd in 2024 for power generation.

item.title: Russian oil prices soar though tanker costs eat into gains
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - The price of Russian Urals oil, shunned by many buyers ‚Äåamid Ukraine-related sanctions, has surged on the war in Iran, though costs are also rising, traders said on Tuesday.
With tankers from top exporter Saudi Arabia effectively blocked by Iranian ‚Äãthreats to Gulf shipping, attention has returned to Russia, the world's ‚Äãsecond-largest seller.
Urals crude from Russian ports was being offered at around $76 ‚Å†per barrel on Monday on a free on board (FOB) basis, Reuters data ‚Äãshowed, up from $45 just two weeks ago.
The price of a Urals cargo loading ‚Äãfrom the Baltic Sea port of Primorsk has jumped to around $54 million this week from about $35 million in February, according to Reuters calculations.
Russian sellers faced near negative margins on exports earlier this ‚Äãyear, traders said.
Despite G7 and other efforts to cap Russian seaborne oil ‚Äãprices, Urals for delivery to Indian ports is now selling at a premium to benchmark Brent ‚Äåcrude ‚Å†for the first time ever.
The United States last week granted Indian refiners a temporary waiver to resume imports of Russian crude already aboard tankers.
Yet traders said Russian sellers also face sharply higher costs. They said several vessels were fixed ‚Äãat around $22-23 million from ‚ÄãRussia's Baltic ‚Å†Sea ports to India amid a scarcity of the vessels.
That's nearly double rates seen in early February and up about $5-8 ‚Äãmillion from late last week.
From Russia's Black Sea port ‚Äãof Novorossiysk, ‚Å†which resumed loadings on Friday after recent drone damage, freight costs also rose above $20 million to India, traders said.
Brent oil surged to more than $119 a barrel on ‚Å†Monday ‚Äãto its highest since mid-2022 amid supply cuts by ‚ÄãSaudi Arabia and other Gulf producers.
Prices pared gains after U.S. President Donald Trump predicted the war in the ‚ÄãMiddle East could end soon .

item.title: US Navy tells shipping industry Hormuz escorts not possible for now
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - The U.S. Navy has refused near-daily requests from the shipping industry for military escorts through the Strait of Hormuz since ‚Äãthe start of the war on Iran, saying the risk of attacks is too high for now, according to sources familiar with the matter.
The ‚ÄåNavy's assessments spell continued disruption to Middle East oil exports and reflect a divergence from President Donald Trump‚Äôs statements that the U.S. is prepared to provide naval escorts whenever needed to restart regular shipments along the key waterway.
Shipping along the narrow strait has all but halted since the start of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran more than a week ago, preventing exports of around a fifth of the world‚Äôs oil supply ‚Äãand sending global oil prices surging to highs not seen since 2022.
A senior official with Iran's Revolutionary Guards has said the strait is closed and Iran will ‚Äãfire on any ship trying to pass, Iranian media reported last week . Several ships have already been hit.
The U.S. Navy has held regular ‚Å†briefings with shipping and oil industry counterparts and has said during those briefings it is unable to provide escorts for the time being, three shipping industry sources familiar with ‚Äãthe matter said.
The sources, who declined to be identified due to the sensitivity of the matter, said the shipping industry has been making requests almost daily during the calls for naval ‚Äãescorts through the strait.
One of the sources said the Navy‚Äôs assessment during Tuesday‚Äôs briefing had not changed and that escorts would only be possible once the risk of attack was reduced.
The Pentagon did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Trump has said repeatedly in recent days that the United States is prepared to escort tankers through the Strait of Hormuz when necessary.
"When the time comes, ‚Äãthe U.S. Navy and its partners will escort tankers through the strait, if needed. I hope it's not going to be needed, but if it's needed, we'll escort them ‚Äãright through," he said on Monday during a press conference at his Mar-a-Lago resort in Florida.
The U.S. military has started looking at options to potentially escort ships through the strait, should it be ‚Äåordered to do ‚Å†so, General Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, said on Tuesday.
"We're looking at a range of options there," Caine told reporters at the Pentagon.
A U.S. official told Reuters the U.S. military has not yet escorted any commercial ships through the strait. Earlier in the day, U.S. Secretary of Energy Chris Wright deleted a post on X in which he said the Navy had successfully escorted one through.
While there have been some voyages through the waterway in recent days , the majority of shipping traffic remains on hold with hundreds of ships ‚Äãanchored.
Saudi Arabia's Aramco, the ‚Äãworld's top oil exporter, said on ‚Å†Tuesday there would be "catastrophic consequences" for the world's oil markets if the war on Iran continues to disrupt shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.
Maritime security specialists and analysts said securing the strait will be difficult, even if the effort involves an international coalition, because of ‚ÄãIran‚Äôs ability to deploy mines or cheaply made attack drones.
"Neither France, the United States, an international coalition or anybody is in a ‚Äãposition to secure the ‚Å†Strait of Hormuz," said Adel Bakawan, director of the European Institute for Studies on the Middle East and North Africa.
Last week, Iran used a remote-controlled boat laden with explosives to damage a crude oil tanker anchored in Iraqi waters, according to initial assessments from two Iraqi port security sources.
A maritime security source said securing the strait could require the U.S. to take control of Iran's ‚Å†vast coast.
"There ‚Äãare not enough naval vessels to do that and the risks remain high even with an escort. One ‚Äãor two vessels can be overwhelmed by a swarm (of fast boats or drones)," the source said.
The Pentagon renewed threats on Tuesday to hit Iran harder unless shipments can flow through and said it was striking Iranian mine-laying ‚Äãvessels and mine-storage facilities.

item.title: Zelenskiy says Ukraine's military hit key Russian missile plant in Bryansk region
item.description:
KYIV, March 10 (Reuters) - President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said Ukraine's military struck a key ‚Äåplant in Russia's Bryansk region bordering Ukraine on Tuesday that produces components for missiles .
Ukraine's military said British Storm Shadow missiles were deployed against the Kremniy bfactory ‚Äãand posted a video which it said showed explosions ‚Äãand a fire at the plant. It said the facility ‚Å†produced critical missile components.
"Our fighters struck one of the important ‚ÄãRussian military factories in Bryansk," Zelenskiy said in his nightly video ‚Äãaddress, referring to a report from top Ukrainian commander Oleksandr Syrskyi.
"The plant produced electronics and components for Russian missiles. The very missiles that strike our ‚Äãcities, our villages and civilians," he said.
The governor of Bryansk region, ‚ÄãAlexander Bogomaz, said on Telegram that six civilians were killed and 37 injured ‚Äåand ‚Å†posted a video showing him at the scene.
Bogomaz made no mention of the plant in what he described as a "terrorist missile attack". The injured were being treated in hospital.
The Ukrainian military's general ‚Äãstaff, posting on ‚ÄãTelegram, described the ‚Å†plant as "a critically important link in the chain of production of Russian high precision weapons" producing ‚Äãsemiconductor devices and integrated micro chips.
"The target was hit ‚Äãand ‚Å†significant damage to production facilities was recorded," the General Staff said. "The extent of the damage is being clarified."
The video posted on Telegram shows ‚Å†aerial ‚Äãimages of a series of explosions and ‚Äãlarge fires over a large area near woods.

item.title: Israeli president tells Bild: War with Iran needs 'end result', not exact timetable
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Israel's President Isaac Herzog on Tuesday did not offer a timetable on when the war ‚Äãwith Iran could end, telling Germany's Bild newspaper: "We ‚Äåneed to take a deep breath and get to the end result."
Herzog said the U.S. and Israeli attacks on ‚ÄãIran were changing the whole configuration of the ‚ÄãMiddle East. He defended strikes on Iranian ‚Å†oil sites as a way of taking away ‚Äãmoney from Tehran's "war machine".
The interview was published as the ‚ÄãU.S. and Israel pounded Iran with what the Pentagon and Iranians on the ground said were the most intense airstrikes of ‚Äãthe war, despite global markets betting that President ‚ÄãDonald Trump will seek to end the conflict soon.
Israel's foreign minister, ‚ÄåGideon ‚Å†Saar, had earlier said his country was not planning for an endless war and was consulting with Washington about when to stop it.
‚ÄúThe Iranians are ‚Äãthe ones spreading ‚Äãchaos and ‚Å†terror throughout the region and the world. So I think if we measure ‚Äãeverything by a speedometer, we won‚Äôt get ‚Äãanywhere. ‚Å†We need to take a deep breath and get to the end result,‚Äù Herzog told Bild.
Eliminating the ‚Å†Iranian threat ‚Äãwould "enable the entire system in ‚Äãthe region to suddenly breathe again and develop further. That‚Äôs fantastic,‚Äù ‚Äãhe added.

item.title: Anthropic gives lesson in AI revenue hallucination
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Anthropic‚Äôs battle ‚Äãwith the Pentagon carries immensely high stakes for the future of artificial intelligence. Along the way, though, it ‚Äåhas revealed something just as interesting about the prosaic realities of accounting.
In a court filing , opens new tab , Anthropic Chief Financial Officer Krishna Rao said revenue has exceeded ‚Äú$5 billion to date.‚Äù This figure sits awkwardly beside the Claude developer‚Äôs ‚Äúrun-rate‚Äù claims: $14 billion , opens new tab as of February 12, rising to $19 billion by month‚Äôs end. The gap reflects ‚ÄãSilicon Valley‚Äôs habit of touting metrics that assume a lot about the future.
The $5 billion figure refers to GAAP revenue generated ‚Äãfrom 2023 through to December 2025, a person familiar with the matter told Breakingviews; the $19 billion ‚Å†is an extrapolation. Anthropic defines ‚Äúrun-rate revenue‚Äù in two parts. Use the last 28 days of sales from customers charged on a ‚Äãconsumption basis and multiply it by 13. Then, multiply the monthly subscription take by 12, and add the two together.
These snapshots are ‚Äãinexact, capturing or missing sudden consumption spikes or dips. It helps explain how $14 billion can become $19 billion within weeks.
Big businesses account for 80% of Anthropic‚Äôs revenue, and they tend to be billed for consumption, making the headline run-rate highly sensitive. Pricing changes, promotional credits or attempts to optimize usage ‚Äãall have an impact. This makes calculating trailing revenue from reported numbers error-prone. Still, working backwards from Anthropic's run-rate at various ‚Äãtimes, it's clear most sales have been generated in recent months.
How metrics are defined also complicates comparisons. OpenAI‚Äôs annual recurring revenue, which it said , opens new tab reached $20 ‚Äåbillion as ‚Å†of the end of December, is meant to specifically capture subscriptions rather than metered sales. Further twists include revenue-sharing agreements with partners such as Microsoft (MSFT.O) , opens new tab .
Run-rate figures can be useful when companies start small and are growing quickly. But it also makes it easier to tout brief, rapid momentum in support of projections showing astronomical growth far out into the future.
Because the Pentagon has moved to ‚Äãblacklist Anthropic, these dangers are ‚Äãparamount. Anthropic Chief Commercial Officer ‚Å†Paul Smith said , opens new tab one customer paused discussions on a $15 million contract after the company was labeled a supply-chain risk, while two financial-services companies refused to finalize agreements worth a combined $80 million unless they secured ‚Äãbroad cancellation rights. Anthropic has sued to challenge the designation.
No one is being misled. Until ‚ÄãAI companies standardize ‚Å†how they report revenue and are upfront about potential volatility, however, their metrics risk looking like a plausible hallucination.
Follow Karen Kwok on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: LNG Canada ramps up output as Iran war threatens global gas supplies
item.description:
HOUSTON, March 10 (Reuters) - LNG Canada, a Shell-led (SHEL.L) , opens new tab venture, has ramped up production and exports to Asia this ‚Äåmonth, LSEG data show, as the Iran war threatens Asian natural gas supplies which are particularly vulnerable to global disruptions.
The LNG project in Kitimat, British Columbia, which began operations in June 2025, has exported five cargoes in the first 11 days of March, already exceeding half its total February ‚Äãvolume, the data shows. A sixth shipment is due to depart on Tuesday.
All cargoes have been sent to ‚ÄãAsia, with two heading to Japan, two to South Korea and one to the Philippines. ‚Å†The plant appears to be operating close to its full capacity of 14 million metric tons per year, according to ‚Äãthe LSEG data.
An LNG Canada spokesperson did not comment on the facility's current production volumes but said the company continues to ‚Äãadvance early operations at the site safely and responsibly.
"A 58th cargo is scheduled to depart in the coming days," the spokesperson said in an email.
The venture can export just under 1.2 million metric tons per month. In the first one-third of this month it has loaded ‚Äãmore than 400,000 tons, the data showed.
Global markets have rushed to adapt after Qatar, which supplies about 20% of globally traded ‚ÄãLNG, was forced to halt production and declare force majeure when the conflict blocked tankers from transiting the Strait of Hormuz.
"They are ‚Å†further ramping up activity to push toward full capacity, as well as trying to make a quick surge in LNG output to get more LNG on the water to Asia and take advantage of higher prices in the region," said Martin King, an analyst with RBN Energy.
LNG Canada is the first large-scale Canadian LNG facility to start production and the first major ‚ÄãNorth American plant with direct ‚Äãaccess to the Pacific, shortening ‚Å†sailing time to Asian buyers compared with U.S. Gulf Coast exporters.
The plant has faced operational challenges since startup but has gradually increased output since January, LSEG data shows.
Canadian natural gas producers ‚Äãhad stepped up production significantly in anticipation of LNG Canada's startup last summer, but then ‚Äãfaced a slump ‚Å†in domestic prices when the project did not draw down supplies as quickly as the markets had expected.
"It seems like they are pretty close, for the last two weeks, to capacity," said Mike Belenkie, CEO of Advantage Energy (AAV.TO) , opens new tab , which was one of several ‚Å†companies that ‚Äãtemporarily curtailed production in September when Western Canadian natural gas prices temporarily ‚Äãwent negative.
Daily spot prices at the Alberta Energy Company (AECO) storage hub hovered close to $2 per million British thermal units on Tuesday, a $1.25 discount to the ‚ÄãU.S. Henry Hub benchmark.

item.title: US lawmakers worry Trump may put 'boots on the ground' in Iran
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Democratic U.S. senators expressed alarm on Tuesday about the Iran war , saying they were worried that President Donald Trump could deploy U.S. ground forces and noting the high risks ‚Äãgiven Russian support for Tehran's military.
"We know that Russia is already helping with ‚Äåintel, providing that to Iran , and that there's an axis with Russia and China , Iran and North Korea that puts at greater risk the United States and our national security," Democratic Senator Jeanne Shaheen of New Hampshire said on ‚ÄãCNN.
Speaking after a classified briefing from administration officials to the Senate Armed Services ‚ÄãCommittee, Shaheen called on the administration to send officials to testify publicly in ‚Å†Congress.
Two sources familiar with U.S. intelligence reports have said that Russia is providing Iran ‚Äãwith targeting intelligence on U.S. bases and ships.
Shaheen and other senators said a series of closed-door ‚Äãbriefings from Trump aides have left them with more questions than answers, especially about the cost and expected duration of the war, and whether U.S. forces would be sent into Tehran.
"We seem to be on a ‚Äãpath toward deploying American troops on the ground in Iran to accomplish any of the ‚Äãpotential objectives here," Senator Richard Blumenthal of Connecticut told reporters after Tuesday's classified briefing.
Trump has not ruled ‚Äåout sending ‚Å†U.S. ground troops into Iran. His Republicans, who have slim majorities in both houses of Congress, have almost unanimously backed his strategy on Iran, with only a handful expressing doubt about the war.
Blumenthal also cited the risk of Russian involvement putting American lives at risk. "Literally, Russia ‚Äãseems to be aiding ‚Äãour enemy actively and ‚Å†intensively with intelligence and perhaps with other means and China also may be assisting Iran," Blumenthal said.
"So the American people deserve to know ‚Äãmuch more than this administration has told them about the cost ‚Äãof the war, ‚Å†the danger to our sons and daughters in uniform and the potential for further escalation and widening of this war," he said.
The lawmakers' comments came as they awaited a request the White ‚Å†House ‚Äãis expected to make for more funding for the war. ‚ÄãSeveral congressional aides have said they expect Trump to ask for $50 billion, although others have said that estimate seemed ‚Äãlow.

item.title: Boeing signs $289 million Israel contract for 5,000 smart bombs, source says
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Boeing (BA.N) , opens new tab has signed a new $289 million contract ‚Äãwith Israel to deliver ‚Äãas many as 5,000 new ‚Å†air-launched smart bombs, a ‚Äãsource told Reuters on Tuesday.
The ‚Äãnew contract is not related to the ongoing U.S.-Israeli air strikes on ‚ÄãIran, with deliveries not ‚Äãscheduled to start for 36 months, Bloomberg ‚ÄåNews ‚Å†reported earlier, citing a person familiar with the matter.
Boeing declined to comment when contacted ‚Äãby Reuters.
The ‚Äãcompany's ‚Å†Small Diameter Bomb is a guided munition ‚Äãthat can be launched ‚Äãby ‚Å†Israeli jets at targets more than 40 miles (64 kilometers) away.

item.title: Trading Day: Role reversal, as Wall Street lags
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 10 (Reuters) - Oil slumped more than 10% on Tuesday, the biggest one-day fall since 2022, on hopes that conflict in the Middle East will de-escalate. Asian and European stocks rose sharply, but in a rare instance since the war broke out, Wall Street lagged and U.S. stocks posted mild losses.
In my ‚Äãcolumn today I look at parallels between the stresses emerging in private credit markets today and U.S. subprime mortgages in 2007. Of course, ‚Äåthat doesn't mean a global crash will follow, but investors should be aware of the potential risks under the hood.
If you have more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* Timing is everything
Like many things in life, timing is everything in investing. In trading, it's even more crucial. In that light, the ‚Äãlast 24 hours have been particularly challenging for oil traders, to put it mildly, with crude prices notching their biggest intraday swings on record.
With oil trading in ‚Äãa $36 intraday range, as it did on Monday, fortunes and careers can be made - or lost - in minutes. Leveraged positions will be particularly exposed, and it wouldn't be a total ‚Äåshock if ‚Å†it soon emerges that some hedge funds suffered some big losses.
* Fake news?
Underlining how sensitive markets are right now to headlines, oil extended heavy losses on Tuesday after U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright posted on X that the U.S. navy escorted a tanker through Strait of Hormuz, indicating that supply constraints may be easing.
But the post was deleted minutes later, and oil rebounded around $10, a bounce boosted by a CBS report that U.S. intelligence detected signs Iran may be considering steps to deploy mines ‚Äãin the Strait of Hormuz. Headlines can ‚Äãalways move markets. But these are ‚Å†extraordinary times.
* Trading places
China's powerful export machine is racing up through the gears. Exports in the first two months of this year soared 22%, more than three times the pace of growth in December and the Reuters poll forecast. The January-February ‚Äãtrade surplus was $213 billion.
With tariffs crimping shipments to the U.S., trade with the rest of the world is booming. Last ‚Äãyear's record $1.2 trillion trade ‚Å†surplus, which revived complaints about China's FX regime, could be broken this year. Meanwhile, figures from Berlin on Tuesday showed that German exports in January shrank at their fastest rate since May 2024.
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want to receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views ‚Äãof Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Brazil's Petrobras to auction 20 million liters of diesel amid shortage reports, sources say
item.description:
RIO DE JANEIRO, March 10 (Reuters) - Brazilian state-run oil company ‚ÄãPetrobras (PETR3.SA) , opens new tab plans to ‚Äåhold an auction to sell 20 million liters of ‚Äãdiesel in the ‚Äãsouthern Rio Grande do Sul ‚Å†state amid reports ‚Äãof shortages, sources familiar ‚Äãwith the issue told Reuters on Tuesday.
The auction will take ‚Äãplace on Wednesday, ‚Äãthey said.
A spike in diesel prices is ‚Äåemerging ‚Å†as the first and most immediate threat to Brazil's farm sector ‚Äãfrom the ‚ÄãU.S.-Israeli ‚Å†attacks on Iran, raising costs for ‚Äãproducers harvesting a ‚Äãrecord ‚Å†soybean crop and planting corn they cannot afford ‚Å†to ‚Äãdelay.

item.title: Oil dives, settles down 11% after Trump predicts Middle East de-escalation
item.description:
NEW YORK/LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - Oil prices plunged by more than 11% on Tuesday, the steepest percentage drop of any session since ‚Äã2022, a day after U.S. President Donald Trump predicted a quick end to the war with Iran that has disrupted global crude flows .
Brent futures fell $11.16, or 11%, to settle at $87.80 a barrel. U.S. West Texas ‚ÄåIntermediate (WTI) crude settled at $83.45 a barrel, down $11.32, or 11.9%. Both benchmarks logged the biggest single-day percentage loss since March 2022, after rocketing to four-year highs a day earlier.
