In a note labelled "Advice to the prime minister", a due diligence checklist said Mandelson's relationship with ‚ÄãEpstein posed a "reputational risk".
The document, which said it was based on checks conducted on December 4, ‚Äã2024, said: "After Epstein was first convicted of procuring an underage girl in 2008, ‚Å†their relationship continued across 2009-2011, beginning when Lord Mandelson was Business Minister and continuing after the end ‚Äãof the Labour government".
"Mandelson reportedly stayed in Epstein's house while he was in jail in June 2009," ‚Äãthe note said.
Alongside the due diligence checklist, a briefing note on the decision added that Starmer's then-Chief of Staff Morgan McSweeney had discussed Mandelson's relationship with Epstein but Director of Communications Matthew Doyle had been satisfied with Mandelson's responses to ‚Äãquestions about contact.
Further correspondence and follow-up questions to Mandelson about the relationship were not part of this ‚Äãdisclosure.
Government officials told Starmer that he would be "exposed" if anything went wrong if he chose to make a political ‚Äåappointment ‚Å†such as Mandelson.
"If anything goes wrong, you could be more exposed as the individual is more connected to you personally," the officials said prior to the appointment.
Following the allegations about Mandelson's ties to Epstein which led to his dismissal last September, Downing Street began a "fact-finding" mission to establish what happened around his appointment.
In ‚Äãa summary of a ‚Äãfact-finding call between Starmer's ‚Å†general counsel and the government's National Security Adviser Jonathan Powell in September, it says that Powell found the appointment process "unusual" and "weirdly rushed".
Powell said he had concerns ‚Äãabout Mandelson's "reputation" in conversations with McSweeney.
According to the note Philip Barton, then the ‚Äãmost senior ‚Å†government official at the Foreign Office, "also had reservations around the appointment".
Documents were published on discussions over Mandelson's severance pay after he was fired. Officials said these began with a request from Mandelson to be paid the ‚Å†remainder ‚Äãof "the 4-year salary costs of the fixed term appointment", amounting ‚Äãto 547,201 pounds ($733,523), the document said.
The government eventually approved 34,670 pounds in discretionary payments plus 40,330 pounds he was legally entitled to, ‚Äãtotalling 75,000 pounds.
($1 = 0.7460 pounds)

item.title: Norway police apprehend three suspects in U.S. embassy bombing
item.description:
OSLO, March 11 (Reuters) - Norwegian ‚Äãpolice said on ‚ÄåWednesday they had apprehended three suspects following Sunday's bombing ‚Äãat the U.S. ‚Äãembassy in Oslo.
The powerful ‚Å†early morning blast from ‚Äãan improvised explosive device (IED) ‚Äãdamaged the entrance to the embassy's consular section but ‚Äãcaused no injuries, Norwegian ‚Äãauthorities have said.
"They are suspected ‚Äåof ‚Å†a terror bombing," Police Attorney Christian Hatlo told reporters.
Images of one ‚Äãof ‚Äãthe ‚Å†suspects released by police on Monday ‚Äãshowed a hooded ‚Äãperson, ‚Å†whose face was not visible, wearing dark clothes ‚Å†and ‚Äãcarrying a ‚Äãbag or rucksack.

item.title: Historic oil reserve release is only a band-aid on a gaping supply shock
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - The International Energy Agency‚Äôs plan to release 400 million barrels of oil reserves is unprecedented in scale and desperately needed to blunt the devastating supply shock triggered by the Iran war . But it will offer only limited relief as long as energy exports from the Middle East remain blocked.
The IEA said ‚Äãon Wednesday that its 32 member countries unanimously agreed to move forward with the biggest collective drawdown ever from their strategic petroleum reserves (SPR).
The release is more than double ‚Äåthe scale of the previous ‚Äì and until now, largest-ever ‚Äì coordinated drawdown in March 2022 following Russia‚Äôs invasion of Ukraine , when the U.S. released 180 million barrels .
Yet the enormity of the crisis confronting the global oil market today certainly warrants these record-breaking volumes ‚Äì if not more. Nearly 20 million barrels per day (bpd) of supply ‚Äì roughly a fifth of global output ‚Äì have been trapped inside the Gulf since the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz shortly after the launch of the joint Israel‚ÄëU.S. war against Iran ‚Äãon February 28.
Measured against that disruption, the announced release looks a lot less impressive. After only 11 days of conflict, the current market deficit has already reached roughly 220 million barrels. Saudi Arabia ‚Äãand the United Arab Emirates are working to divert some exports to ports outside the Gulf, which should provide additional relief, but those flows remain limited ‚Å†and vulnerable.
The duration of the Iran conflict and the continued closure of the Strait of Hormuz are therefore critical in determining whether the release can meaningfully stabilise markets or merely slow the pace of ‚Äãdamage.
The IEA has coordinated emergency stock releases only four times since it was created by OECD members in the mid‚Äë1970s in response to the 1973 OPEC oil embargo. That crisis caused severe fuel ‚Äãshortages and a quadrupling of oil prices.
The infrequency underscores the gravity of the current situation ‚Äì but also potentially the limits of the tool being deployed.
Several key details of the release have yet to be announced, and they are crucial to assessing its likely impact.
First, the pace of the drawdown is unknown. The IEA said that members will determine the timing of their release and, more importantly, how quickly oil will actually reach the market.
JPMorgan estimates that a coordinated ‚ÄãSPR release could flow at a maximum rate of about 1.2 million bpd, based on past precedents. The 2022 drawdown equated to roughly 1 million bpd. At those rates, the release would offset only a ‚Äãsmall fraction of the current supply disruption, leaving the market heavily undersupplied.
Where the oil is released matters almost as much as the quantity. The region most heavily hit by the supply shock so far is Asia , ‚Äåwhich relies on ‚Å†the Gulf for about 60% of its oil imports. Refiners there have already started cutting operating rates, while several countries have begun rationing fuel to conserve dwindling stocks.
Japan, which holds the second‚Äëlargest strategic reserves in the OECD, said it will release around 80 million barrels from both state and private inventories, equivalent to 45 days of supply, starting as early as March 16. That should provide Japanese refiners with rapid, tangible relief.
The U.S., which has yet to spell out its contribution, will almost certainly account for the largest share of the release. Its SPR stands at about 415 million barrels stored in dozens of salt caverns along the ‚ÄãGulf Coast. That‚Äôs less than 60% of total ‚Äãcapacity of roughly 713 million barrels, according ‚Å†to Energy Information Administration data.
For refiners in Asia, however, the release of U.S. barrels might not be that helpful. A tanker journey from the U.S. Gulf Coast to Asia takes 40 to 60 days depending on the route, more than twice as long as shipments from the Middle East.
Those delays will be ‚Äãcompounded by sharply higher freight costs. The recent surge in tanker rates due to the conflict adds an extra $10 to $12 per barrel for a ‚Äãvoyage from the U.S. Gulf ‚Å†Coast to Singapore ‚Äì around 15% of the cargo‚Äôs value ‚Äì compared with just 2% to 3% before the war, according to Reuters calculations.
There is also a longer‚Äëterm cost. A release of this magnitude will drain a substantial share of the world‚Äôs emergency reserves, leaving consuming nations with a far thinner cushion should the conflict drag on or escalate further.
The SPR release will unquestionably offer the global oil market vital breathing room as governments ‚Å†grapple with the ‚Äãthreat of surging inflation, economic slowdown and social strain.
But without a rapid resumption of energy flows from the Middle East, ‚Äãeven this historic intervention could quickly become a drop in the ocean.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Ron Bousso , a columnist for Reuters.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen ‚Äãto the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: "We are having a tremendous impact," Trump says in G7 call after IEA decision on oil stocks
item.description:
PARIS, March 11 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump praised the "tremendous impact" of decisions ‚Äãbeing made on Wednesday as ‚Äåleaders of the Group of Seven Nations met to discuss war in Iran ‚Äãand its economic consequences, ‚Äãaccording to a short videoclip shared ‚Å†by the French presidency.
"I think ‚Äãwe are having a tremendous impact, ‚Äãunbelievable actually, on the world," Trump said, after being given the floor by French ‚ÄãPresident Emmanuel Macron, who chaired ‚Äãthe G7 meeting. It was not immediately ‚Äåclear ‚Å†whether Trump was talking about the release of oil stocks decided minutes earlier.
Macron and Trump were speaking ‚Äãafter a ‚Äãrecommendation ‚Å†by the International Energy Agency (IEA) to release 400 million ‚Äãbarrels of oil, the ‚Äãlargest ‚Å†such move in the IEA history, to try to restrain soaring crude ‚Å†prices ‚Äãamid the U.S.-Israeli ‚Äãwar with Iran.

item.title: China scientists rush to climate-proof potatoes
item.description:
By Florence Lo
Filed Nov. 18, 2024, 5:06 p.m. GMT
Photography and reporting by¬†Florence Lo
Reporting by Mei Mei Chu and ¬†Xiaoyu Yin
Filed November 27, 2024, 11:00 p.m.¬†GMT
In a research facility in the northwest of Beijing, molecular biologist Li Jieping and his team harvest a cluster of seven unusually small potatoes, one as tiny as a quail‚Äôs egg, from a potted plant.
Grown under conditions that simulate predictions of higher temperatures at the end of the century, the potatoes provide an ominous sign of future food security.
At just 136 grams (4.8 oz), the tubers weigh less than half that of a typical potato in China, where the most popular varieties are often twice the size of a baseball.
China is the world‚Äôs biggest producer of potatoes, which are crucial to global food security because of their high yield relative to other staple crops.
But they are particularly vulnerable to heat, and climate change, driven by fossil fuel emissions, is pushing temperatures to dangerous new heights while also worsening drought and flooding.
With an urgent need to protect food supplies, Li, a researcher at the International Potato Center (CIP) in Beijing, is leading a three-year study into the effects of higher temperatures on the vegetable. His team is focusing on China‚Äôs two most common varieties.
‚ÄúI worry about what will happen in the future,‚Äù Li said. ‚ÄúFarmers will harvest fewer potato tubers, it will influence food security.‚Äù
Li‚Äôs team grew their crop over three months in a walk-in chamber set at 3 degrees Celsius above the current average temperature in northern Hebei and Inner Mongolia, the higher altitude provinces where potatoes are usually grown in China.
Their research, published in the journal Climate Smart Agriculture this month, found the higher temperatures accelerated tuber growth by 10 days, but cut potato yields by more than half.
Under current climate policies, the world is facing as much as 3.1 C of warming above pre-industrial levels by 2100, according to a United Nations report released in October.
In Inner Mongolia, dozens of workers clutching white sacks rush to gather potatoes dug up from the soil before the next downpour.
‚ÄúThe biggest challenge for potatoes this year is the heavy rain,‚Äù said manager Wang Shiyi. ‚ÄúIt has caused various diseases... and greatly slowed down the harvest progress.‚Äù
Meanwhile, seed potato producer Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company has invested in aeroponic systems where plants are grown in the air under controlled conditions.
Farmers are increasingly demanding potato varieties that are higher-yielding and less susceptible to disease, particularly late blight, which caused the Irish Potato Famine of the mid-19th century and thrives in warm and humid conditions.
‚ÄúSome new and more aggressive (late blight) strains have begun to appear, and they are more resistant to traditional prevention and control methods,‚Äù said general manager Li Xuemin, explaining the Inner Mongolia-based company‚Äôs strategy.
The research by CIP, which is headquartered in Lima, is part of a collaborative effort with the Chinese government to help farmers adapt to the warmer, wetter conditions.
In the greenhouse outside Li's lab, workers swab pollen on white potato flowers to develop heat-tolerant varieties.
Li says Chinese farmers will need to make changes within the next decade, planting during spring instead of the start of summer, or moving to even higher altitudes to escape the heat.
‚ÄúFarmers have to start preparing for climate change,‚Äù Li said. ‚ÄúIf we don‚Äôt find a solution, they will make less money from lower yields and the price of potatoes may rise.‚Äù
A Chinese flag flutters next to sacks of newly harvested potatoes, at a farm under Hebei Jiuen Agricultural Development Company, in Xilingol League, Inner Mongolia, China, September 24, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers load a lorry with sacks of newly harvested potatoes at a farm under Hebei Jiuen Agricultural Development Company, in Xilingol League, Inner Mongolia, China, September 24, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Sprinklers on a centre pivot irrigation system water growing potato plants, at a farm under Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company, in Old Barag Banner, Inner Mongolia, China, June 17, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Potato tubers dug up by harvesters lie on the ground, at a farm under Hebei Jiuen Agricultural Development Company, in Xilingol League, Inner Mongolia, China, September 24, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Researcher Li Yafei rinses a potato plant, before separating the tubers and weighing them, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 14, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Lab workers sort vessels with potato plantlets as they prepare them for propagation, in a tissue culture laboratory at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, April 17, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Potato plantlets propagated in a laboratory lie on the potting mix as workers transplant them in a greenhouse, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
A lab worker holds potato plantlets propagated in a laboratory to transplant them in a greenhouse, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Researcher Li Yafei sets up a device to measure the photosynthesis rates of potato plants grown inside a heat chamber, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, April 2, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Potato plants grow in a pot at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 14, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
A worker adjusts the hose as she waters potato plantlets transplanted from a laboratory, in a greenhouse at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Researchers transport a cart of potato plantlets to a greenhouse, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
A worker places clasps on potato plants to help them grow vertically, at a greenhouse under Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company, in Yakeshi, Inner Mongolia, China, June 15, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Potato flowers grow in a greenhouse at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers pack potato plants into containers to transplant them to the field, in a greenhouse at a farm under Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company, in Yakeshi, Inner Mongolia, China, June 17, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers sort sacks of seed potatoes to be distributed to other facilities, in a warehouse at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, April 2, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers rest on mats placed outside a greenhouse during their lunch break, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers sit in a bus for a lunch break Workers sit in a bus for a lunch break, after their work to transplant greenhouse-grown potato plants to the field was disrupted by the rain, at a farm under Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company in Yakeshi, Inner Mongolia, China, June 16, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
The Wider Image
Photography: Florence Lo
Reporting: Mei Mei Chu, Florence Lo, Xiaoyu Yin
Writing: Mei Mei Chu
Photo editing: Maye-E Wong and Marta Montana Gomez
Text editing: Kate Mayberry
Design: Marta Montana Gomez
LICENSE THIS STORY
Amid Israel‚Äôs military assault, the world‚Äôs hunger monitor has struggled to access key data to determine whether conditions in Gaza constitute a famine.
OnlyFans is among the world's most successful social media platforms ‚Äì but a secretive one. Reuters traces its journey from an obscure, porn-free site to an adults-only phenomenon turbocharged by erotic performers and influencers.
It‚Äôs a simple but brutal equation: The number of people going hungry or otherwise struggling around the world is rising, while the amount of money the world‚Äôs wealthiest nations are contributing toward helping them is dropping.
Multiple OnlyFans accounts featured suspected child sex abuse, investigator reports.

Reuters provides business, financial, national and international news to professionals via desktop terminals, the world's media organizations, industry events and directly to consumers.

item.title: Buffett‚Äôs Berkshire Hathaway operates the dirtiest set of coal-fired power plants in the US
item.description:
In his letter to investors last year, Berkshire Hathaway Chairman Warren Buffett urged readers to come ‚Äúinhale the air, drink the water‚Äù and attend the company‚Äôs annual shareholder meeting in Omaha, his hometown.
The letter, a document pored over by shareholders, analysts and press every year for insights into the legendary investor‚Äôs thinking, playfully sought to link the success of his conglomerate with the setting of the heartland city, in eastern Nebraska along the Iowa border.
One detail omitted: Federal emissions data show that Omaha‚Äôs air quality ranks in the bottom third of U.S. cities, fouled in part by coal-fired power plants owned by Berkshire in neighboring Iowa. A modeling tool of the Environmental Protection Agency, or EPA, shows that pollution from the plants each year causes up to seven premature deaths, $104 million in healthcare expenses and 1,800 lost school days in the metropolitan area of Omaha, with nearly 1 million people.
The calculations, performed by Reuters using the EPA‚Äôs modeling data for 2023, were reviewed and confirmed by three outside emissions scientists consulted for this report. The scientists are all independent research experts with no affiliation with the news agency or the electricity industry.
The air over Omaha, and other areas near Berkshire‚Äôs dozen coal-fired power plants nationwide, casts a pall over the environmental record of a company that touts itself at the vanguard of clean energy. Despite investments to date of $41 billion in renewable energies, mostly wind and solar power, Berkshire operates the dirtiest corporate coal fleet in the U.S. by at least one important measure, a Reuters investigation shows.
Berkshire‚Äôs coal plant fleet, operated by three utility companies it has acquired in recent decades, emits more nitrogen oxides than any other coal fleet in the country, federal emissions data show. Reuters analyzed the most recent full calendar year of EPA emissions data available for coal plants, from 2023.
Known as NOx, nitrogen oxides are poisonous gases and key components of smog and haze. They help form lung-damaging ozone and tiny soot particles that can cause respiratory illness and premature death in communities up to hundreds of miles away.
Berkshire has lobbied regulators and filed lawsuits to avoid installing expensive pollution controls, known as SCR scrubbers, that can reduce NOx and that are more commonly employed at coal plants owned by rivals. And the company has said it plans to operate some of its coal-fired plants ‚Äì aging, decades-old facilities in Iowa, Nevada, Utah and Wyoming ‚Äì for years after many of its corporate competitors plan to shut theirs, company disclosures show.
In an email response to detailed questions from Reuters, Berkshire said its electric utilities operate their coal plants ‚Äúin full compliance with state and federal environmental laws, regulations and requirements.‚Äù The company said it hasn‚Äôt employed more SCR scrubbers because it is ‚Äúan expensive technology for our customers,‚Äù but that it has reduced emissions through other methods, retired some coal plants early and converted others to gas. Berkshire didn‚Äôt address the toll its coal plants have exacted on the health of residents affected by their emissions.
Its efforts to reduce pollution, the company added, have sharply cut NOx emissions from its electricity portfolio over the last two decades. Coal power now accounts for only 22% of Berkshire‚Äôs power generation, it said, down from 71% in 2005. Berkshire said it plans to exit coal power entirely by 2049.
The Reuters analysis used federal emissions data ‚Äì submitted to the EPA by plant operators themselves ‚Äì to determine the NOx pollution produced by Berkshire‚Äôs coal fleet and compare it against industry peers. Reporters also used an EPA tool known as Co-Benefits Risk Assessment, or COBRA, to see how Berkshire‚Äôs coal plant pollution affects the health of communities.
Widely used by scientists and regulators, COBRA assigns an economic value to pollution variables, calculating the economic and public health costs of contaminated air. Reuters also reviewed thousands of pages of regulatory and corporate documents and interviewed more than three dozen energy and medical experts as well as people living near Berkshire‚Äôs plants.
The examination found:
Abel declined to comment for this story.
The EPA didn‚Äôt respond to requests from Reuters for comment on the health tolls of emissions from Berkshire‚Äôs plants.
Despite advances in renewable energy across the U.S., electricity generation still creates significant costs for the environment and public health. At a time of surging electricity demand because of intense heat waves, power-hungry data centers for artificial intelligence and the ever-growing needs of the U.S. economy, Berkshire‚Äôs resistance to clean up its coal-fired plants worries doctors and environmental scientists.
‚ÄúWarren Buffett is making a multi-layered environmental mess he‚Äôs not being required to clean up,‚Äù said Brian Moench, a doctor whose group, Utah Physicians for a Healthy Environment, has studied the impact of Berkshire‚Äôs coal plants.
Known for his folksy demeanor, frugal lifestyle and generous philanthropy, Buffet over more than half a century built Berkshire into a champion of American industry. Its trillion-dollar portfolio, forged through acquisitions such as the power plants, ranges from railways to insurance, fast food to real estate. Buffett, 94, is known for his hands-off approach to Berkshire companies, allowing managers to navigate the disparate industry and regulatory environments each confronts.
Berkshire‚Äôs late vice chairman, Charlie Munger, told shareholders at the 2019 annual meeting that ‚Äúthe environmental stuff is done one level down from us. I think Greg Abel is just terrific at it.‚Äù Pushing back at the time against shareholder proposals for Berkshire to share more information with the public about its emissions, Munger added: ‚ÄúWe are not going to do reports like everyone else.‚Äù
The energy policies are likely to outlast Buffet‚Äôs tenure. Abel, who has led energy operations since the early 2000s, is widely expected to succeed the billionaire at Berkshire‚Äôs helm.
Electricity has been fundamental to modern life and economic growth for more than a century. The risks and costs associated with its production are an afterthought for some users. As with cars and industrialized foods, convenience often outweighs concerns about downsides, particularly because much of the toll on health happens slowly and not always in plain sight.
‚ÄúWe all know coal plants have this continuous impact that is going to elevate the risk of asthma, heart attacks and death,‚Äù said Elena Krieger, who oversees scientific research at PSE Healthy Energy, a California-based policy institute. ‚ÄúBut it is very, very difficult to pinpoint a single death. And that makes it very hard to point the finger at any given facility.‚Äù
The harm is considerable.
According to Reuters‚Äô COBRA analysis, the roughly 200 coal plants still operating in the U.S. each year cause up to 8,400 premature deaths and $130 billion in excess healthcare costs. Berkshire‚Äôs coal plants are estimated to cause up to 260 deaths and $3.9 billion in health expenditures, the model shows. Reuters‚Äô analysis, Krieger said, offers ‚Äúa reasonable portrait of the magnitude of the health impacts from these coal plants.‚Äù
The news agency‚Äôs COBRA calculations were also reviewed by Nicholas Mailloux, an expert in the health effects of energy systems at the University of Wisconsin. Tim Canty, a University of Maryland professor who is an expert on pollution at coal plants, reviewed Reuters‚Äô calculations of NOx emissions.
NOx, among the worst air pollutants, is a precursor for hazardous ground-level ozone and fine particulate matter. Wildfires and vehicle tailpipes are also large sources of NOx, which can travel over 1,000 miles to cause faraway smog and soot. The particulate pollution NOx helps form can penetrate the bloodstream and brain, contributing to respiratory issues, cancers and other illnesses. Children are particularly vulnerable.
Joel Schwartz, a Harvard University epidemiologist who studies pollution and human health, said there is no known safe threshold for exposure to soot particles formed from NOx. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs affecting systems that do bad things all over the human body,‚Äù he told Reuters.
Coal plants are also big emitters of climate-warming carbon dioxide, or CO2, a gas not removed by SCR scrubbers. A research report from the University of Massachusetts-Amherst last year found that Berkshire ranked fourth among the top CO2 emitting companies ‚Äì from any industry ‚Äì in the U.S. The report relied on data from 2022.
Unlike many big industrial firms, Berkshire doesn‚Äôt issue annual reports breaking out its company-wide CO2 emissions. The company says it is aiming for net zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050.
Coal-fired electricity is largely in its twilight in the U.S.
The country‚Äôs remaining coal plants generate about 16% of its electricity. That‚Äôs almost twice the level of the European Union, which has more aggressively pursued renewable energy. But it‚Äôs a fraction of the 52% reliance on coal in 1990, when the U.S. had nearly 500 coal plants, according to data from the U.S. Energy Information Administration and from Eurelectric, a Brussels-based industry group.
Wind and natural gas, now the leading fuel for U.S. power generation, account for much of Berkshire‚Äôs electrical output. But the company still relies on coal for over a fifth of its generation, according to company data.
Berkshire recently slowed some initiatives to wean itself further.
In Utah, its PacifiCorp unit, after announcing plans to invest heavily in renewable energy, last year changed course and said it would prolong the life of its two coal plants there. In an April statement, PacifiCorp said slower-than-expected changes to regulation made it attractive to do so.
‚ÄúThe twilight of coal seems to be turning into an odd sunrise,‚Äù said Ulla Reeves, director of the clean air program at the National Parks Conservation Association, one of several environmental groups that in federal court have sought stricter regulations against the Utah plants.
Berkshire began adding electric utilities to its portfolio in 2000, when it purchased Iowa-based MidAmerican Energy. Among that company‚Äôs five coal-fired plants is Walter Scott Jr Energy Center, a 50-year-old facility in Council Bluffs, Iowa, 10 miles down the Missouri River from Berkshire‚Äôs Omaha headquarters. MidAmerican‚Äôs four other Iowa coal plants all add to Berkshire‚Äôs NOx tally, but Walter Scott, EPA data show, is the largest industrial source of NOx pollution in the Omaha area, emitting 4,000 tons in 2023 alone.
The facility is named in honor of one of Buffett‚Äôs childhood friends, who was also a big investor in MidAmerican. It operates with two boilers, one of which doesn‚Äôt have an SCR scrubber. Pollution from the plant settles over Omaha on days when winds blow from the south and southeast, a typical weather pattern, according to Dan Goldberg, a George Washington University professor who uses satellite data to examine real-time changes in air quality.
In 2023, metro Omaha residents had ‚Äúgood‚Äù air to breathe during only half the days of the year, according to standards used by the EPA‚Äôs Air Quality Index. It ranked 299 in air quality out of 416 small and large U.S. metro areas, according to a Reuters analysis of EPA data. Metro Omaha had 25 days in 2023 when the air quality was deemed unhealthy for sensitive groups, ranking in the bottom 10% among U.S. metro areas by this metric. That was largely caused by lung-damaging ozone topping EPA limits on 23 days, the most so far for Omaha this century.
‚ÄúIf I had an asthmatic child, I would definitely not want to live in Omaha,‚Äù said Wilma Subra, a Louisiana-based scientist and environmental consultant who formerly served on the EPA‚Äôs National Environmental Justice Advisory Council.
Omaha Mayor Jean Stothert didn‚Äôt respond to messages seeking comment on the city‚Äôs air quality.
In its email to Reuters, Berkshire said MidAmerican is investing billions in renewable energy. Its Iowa coal plants operate under the ‚Äúdetailed oversight of local regulatory agencies‚Äù and as part of MidAmerican‚Äôs ‚Äúpromise to customers to deliver safe, reliable and affordable energy.‚Äù
One day last year, a massive coal heap sat outside the Walter Scott plant. When wind kicks up over the Missouri River, black dust flies from the stockpile into nearby neighborhoods. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs like a black cloud rolling through,‚Äù said Treg Hecker, who lives two miles away and says he sometimes has to hose the powder off the sides of his house. Medical research shows that communities near coal stockpiles can have increased risk of lung, kidney and other ailments because of such dust.
‚ÄúOur facilities employ methods to eliminate coal dust from leaving the facility,‚Äù Berkshire said in its email, ‚Äúand our current leadership team is not aware of any formal complaints that have been filed with regulators.‚Äù
About 11 miles northwest of Walter Scott lies North Omaha, a predominantly Black area with the highest prevalence of asthma in Nebraska. The asthma rate in North Omaha is nearly 13% of the population, according to 2021 data from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, outpacing the state average of 8.8% and the national average of 7.7%.
For years, members of Omaha‚Äôs Black community have complained about pollution from another nearby coal plant, North Omaha Station. The plant, which operates without an SCR scrubber, is just two miles from the neighborhood. Operated by Omaha Public Power District, a state-run utility, it has been targeted at public hearings by neighborhood activists pressing for stricter pollution curbs there.
OPPD declined to comment.
Over the past 20 years, EPA data show, Walter Scott has produced 403,000 tons of NOx and soot-producing sulfur dioxide pollution, 22% more than North Omaha Station. Preston Love Jr, a longtime advocate for North Omaha residents, said the risks from Berkshire‚Äôs plant have largely escaped community notice.
‚ÄúPeople are not aware of what is happening to them,‚Äù Love told Reuters. ‚ÄúThey live and die without ever knowing.‚Äù
In September 2023, Memphis Zabawa, a 12-year-old boy from West Omaha, another of the city‚Äôs neighborhoods, competed in a local cross-country race. Despite an asthma diagnosis, Memphis was running at a time of high pollution in Omaha. The city‚Äôs Air Quality Index, a metric public officials use to warn the public about unhealthy air, that week topped 154 for tiny soot pollution, a measure of fine particle emissions, the highest score of the year. Soot pollution on race day registered a score of 75, a ‚Äúmoderate‚Äù level that can still pose risks for some asthmatics.
During the race, Memphis carried his asthma inhaler and used it at least once, an Omaha Police Department report shows. But while running down a hill, he collapsed. His heart stopped beating. A nurse raced to Memphis‚Äô side and started CPR, police said. Paramedics rushed him to a local hospital, where he recovered.
Medical research has shown that coal plant pollution can exacerbate asthma and that tiny soot particles can trigger heart attacks. There‚Äôs no way to know, though, whether that week‚Äôs air quality, let alone emissions from local power plants, caused Memphis‚Äô cardiac arrest. Linking a precise cause to any individual‚Äôs illness is often difficult, even impossible. Memphis‚Äô parents, Erin and Justin Zabawa, told Reuters they can‚Äôt assign blame for their son‚Äôs collapse.
About 1,000 miles to the west, Utah is known for Rocky Mountain slopes, red rock arches and national parks with panoramic vistas. Less advertised are the state‚Äôs air quality problems, which experts say in part stem from heavy reliance on coal power ‚Äì source of nearly half of Utah‚Äôs electricity, federal energy data show, around three times the national average.
Ringed by mountains, Salt Lake City, the state‚Äôs fast-growing capital, often ranks among the worst cities on the American Lung Association‚Äôs list for ozone pollution and dangerous particulate matter. The valley‚Äôs topography traps smog from industrial emissions, vehicles and other sources, sometimes creating a bowl of hazy pollution.
Hunter and Huntington, the two coal plants Berkshire owns more than 100 miles to the south of Salt Lake City, generate enough electricity to power about 800,000 homes. Built in the 1970s, during a coal mining boom in central Utah, the plants became part of Berkshire‚Äôs portfolio in 2006, when it acquired PacifiCorp, owner of utility companies in several western states. Neither of the plants is equipped with SCR scrubbers.
In 2023, EPA data show, Hunter and Huntington had NOx pollution rates around 60% higher than the national average for coal plants, and more than twice the average rate of plants with SCR scrubbers. The two plants are responsible for some of Salt Lake‚Äôs smog, causing up to $8.4 million a year in healthcare costs there, according to EPA data.
Much of their emissions also fuel regional haze pollution elsewhere, notably at Arches and Canyonlands national parks. The natural range of visibility at Canyonlands, which attracts hundreds of thousands of visitors to its gorges and buttes each year, on a clear day can span a majestic 200 miles. But in 2023, pollution some days dimmed the range by nearly 40%, according to federal visibility data for national parks.
The pollution is caused by a variety of industrial sources and vehicles. Within five years, however, the Hunter and Huntington coal plants are projected to be the top two emitters of the NOx particle pollution at Canyonlands, according to the EPA. The plants‚Äô NOx output also helps form health-harming ozone pollution. Canyonlands and nearby wilderness areas have exceeded the federal health limit for ozone on 19 different days since 2018, including three in 2024, according to EPA air monitor data.
Hunter and Huntington also embody Berkshire‚Äôs success opposing stricter NOx curbs, including a well-documented legal battle that enabled it to avoid the installation of SCR scrubbers there. Led by Abel, the Buffett protege and Berkshire energy boss, pushback against regulators has been driven by his focus on the bottom line, according to people familiar with the company‚Äôs tactics.
Abel is ‚Äúa consummate numbers guy,‚Äù said Steven Guyer, an attorney and former environmental director at MidAmerican, Berkshire‚Äôs first utility. ‚ÄúEverything is about the earnings.‚Äù Guyer left the company in 2008 and is now an outspoken critic of Berkshire‚Äôs environmental policies at the Iowa Environmental Council, an organization that advocates for clean energy.
In its email to Reuters, Berkshire said it has made some of PacifiCorp‚Äôs plants cleaner even without SCR scrubbers. Last year, for instance, PacifiCorp converted two of the four coal boilers at its Jim Bridger plant, in Wyoming, to run on natural gas.
In 2015, during the administration of former President Barack Obama, the EPA was evaluating tougher ozone pollution standards. That year, according to agency memos, Abel met twice with Gina McCarthy, EPA administrator at the time. The memos didn‚Äôt disclose details about the meetings.
The EPA declined to comment on the meetings.
McCarthy didn‚Äôt respond to requests for comment.
The next year, the EPA ordered PacifiCorp to install SCR scrubbers at Hunter and Huntington. Within five years, the agency said, the scrubbers would cut NOx pollution from the two plants by around 80%. ‚ÄúEPA acts to reduce regional haze in Utah and improve visibility in National Parks,‚Äù read a press release about the decision at the time.
Months later, PacifiCorp filed suit to block the plan in a Denver federal court. During two years of litigation, Berkshire resisted the EPA‚Äôs orders, arguing that the steep price tag for two SCR scrubbers at each of the plants would outweigh the benefits. The estimated total cost of $711 million, it said, would force PacifiCorp to hike rates for customers.
The EPA hired Andover Technology Partners, a Boston-area pollution control consultant, to audit Berkshire‚Äôs estimate. Andover said SCR projects at the two plants could be done for $517 million, or about 30% less.
PacifiCorp, the consultancy found, overestimated costs by including items like parking lot signs, crushed rock for roadways and employee eye-wash stations. Those expenses, Andover said, have nothing to do with SCR controls, adding that PacifiCorp also inflated estimates for property taxes and the fuel needed to generate steam. ‚ÄúThey make the projects look as expensive as possible,‚Äù said James Staudt, Andover Technology‚Äôs owner.
In 2016 comments published by the EPA, PacifiCorp described Andover‚Äôs review as ‚Äúgrossly flawed.‚Äù The EPA, in the same comments, defended the report.
In 2020, under the administration of former President Donald Trump, the EPA reversed the Obama-era order to install SCR scrubbers at Hunter and Huntington. Because PacifiCorp in 2015 had shut another Utah plant, also coal-fired, it had helped the government meet long-term goals, the agency said.
As of 2023, Berkshire had avoided installing SCR scrubbers on all but four of 24 coal-fired boilers at a dozen power plants, EPA data show. In Utah and Wyoming alone, according to a letter PacifiCorp sent the EPA in 2022, Berkshire has saved up to $2 billion by not having to install more advanced pollution controls.
In recent years, air quality experts and clean power advocates had some new reasons to be hopeful about Utah. In a May 2023 regulatory filing, PacifiCorp said it planned to shutter Hunter and Huntington by 2032. In their place, the company wrote, it was considering construction of two small nuclear plants on the sites.
The company was planning other clean energy investments over the next decade, too. In regulatory filings, PacifiCorp said the investments would create 13 gigawatts of renewable wind and solar power infrastructure in various Western states, enough electricity to power roughly 10 million average U.S. homes.
But optimism was short-lived.
In June 2023, PacifiCorp lost a high-profile lawsuit filed by fire survivors in Oregon, where a jury found its power lines responsible for sparking a wildfire. The judgment raised the specter that Berkshire utilities could face billions more in similar liabilities. Forced to set aside cash, regulatory filings show, PacifiCorp pared back many of its planned investments in renewable power.
In April of last year, PacifiCorp said in a regulatory filing that it would keep its Utah coal plants running for years longer, still with no SCR scrubbers. Huntington‚Äôs operations are now expected to last an additional four years, until 2036, and Hunter‚Äôs a full decade longer than previously foreseen, until 2042, the filing said.
Pollution from the plants doesn‚Äôt just stay in Utah. COBRA modeling shows it also increases health harms hundreds of miles away, in big cities like Denver, Los Angeles and Phoenix.
Separately, the EPA recently suffered a setback to its ‚ÄúGood Neighbor Plan,‚Äù part of a longstanding initiative to limit the impact that coal plant emissions have on surrounding states. Under the plan, Utah‚Äôs coal plants would be subject to new rules that crack down on interstate ozone pollution. In June, however, the U.S. Supreme Court blocked the plan , pending further litigation from opponents including Berkshire. Among utility rivals, the industrial giant has been the most active in its opposition, submitting 125 different written objections to the initiative, according to an EPA summary document.
The decision leaves ‚Äúupwind States free to keep contributing significantly to their downwind neighbors‚Äô ozone problems,‚Äù wrote Justice Amy Coney Barrett in a dissenting opinion. It‚Äôs unclear what changes may occur to the federal regulatory landscape in the next Trump administration, but the incoming president campaigned on promises to curb environmental rules and encourage energy companies to further tap fossil fuels.
State officials in Utah have been friendly to the industry. As with other supporters of continued coal generation, Utah legislators have cited the fuel‚Äôs reliability compared with wind and solar power, whose output can vary depending on weather and daylight. ‚ÄúMany people sleep better knowing these coal plants can still supply the grid,‚Äù said James Scott Holladay, an energy economist at the University of Tennessee.
In March, the Utah State Legislature passed a law that could preserve coal as a preferential power source there. Utah lawmakers also offered PacifiCorp protection against wildfire liabilities, allocating taxpayer money to a fund that could cover costs from blazes sparked by power lines.
At last year‚Äôs shareholder meeting, Buffett and Abel lauded the fund and praised Utah for providing electric utilities with a favorable regulatory environment. Utah, Abel said, is ‚Äúthe best example we have. It‚Äôs the gold standard across the country.‚Äù
By Tim McLaughlin and Joshua Schneyer
To determine Berkshire‚Äôs NOx emissions from its fleet of 12 U.S. coal plants, and to compare their NOx emissions rate against those of other large corporate coal power plant operators, Reuters analyzed public EPA emissions data for 2023, the most recent full calendar year available.
The EPA breaks out the data for the 200 or so coal-powered plants that still supply electricity to the U.S. power grid.
The data show coal boilers at plants Berkshire controls released 35,891 tons of NOx in 2023, more than any other single coal plant fleet operator in the country. Reuters also compared Berkshire plants‚Äô average NOx emissions rate to that of other large U.S. corporate coal plant fleets ‚Äì those owned by the 20 utility operators with coal plants in the Standard and Poor‚Äôs Utilities Index.
The NOx rate takes into account a coal fleet‚Äôs NOx emissions by weight relative to the heat energy it produces from burning coal, in British thermal units, or BTUs, a standard methodology used by the EPA.
Berkshire‚Äôs coal fleet had a higher average NOx emissions rate ‚Äì 0.163 pounds per million BTUs ‚Äì than any of its big corporate utility peers. It did not have advanced pollution controls ‚Äì selective catalytic reduction, or SCR, scrubbers ‚Äì on most of its coal-fired boilers.
To estimate the healthcare costs and negative health outcomes attributable to Berkshire‚Äôs coal plant emissions in the Omaha metropolitan area and nationwide, Reuters used the EPA‚Äôs Co-Benefits Risk Assessment Health Impacts Screening and Mapping Tool, or COBRA.
COBRA provides the range of estimated health costs associated with coal plant pollution in a given area. Reuters used the EPA‚Äôs baseline emissions data estimates for 2023. COBRA results for metro Omaha include the upper range of estimated impacts within the "Omaha, NE‚ÄìIA, Metropolitan Statistical Area (MSA)," an eight county, bi-state area including the City of Omaha and surrounding areas.
To explore the air quality metrics in metro Omaha and elsewhere, Reuters analyzed the EPA‚Äôs Air Quality Index data, which relies on air monitoring equipment nationwide. Reuters analyzed air quality data from 416 U.S. urban areas. The analysis excluded areas where air monitoring readings were incomplete.

item.title: Is climate change lighting a fuse under Iceland's volcanoes?
item.description:
Scientists are racing to find out whether the rapid retreat of glaciers could drive a surge in eruptions as magma builds under the island nation ‚Äî and if so, whether the same might occur at ice-covered volcanoes around the world, putting many lives at risk.
By GLORIA DICKIE Photography by STOYAN NENOV Graphics by SAM HART and ANURAG RAO Video by KUBA STƒò≈ªYCKI
Filed Oct. 26, 2024, 10 a.m. GMT
Toxic sulphurous¬†gas, carrying¬†the¬†telltale reek of rotten eggs, wafted through vents in the steep walls of Iceland‚Äôs Viti crater, while carbon dioxide bubbled to the surface of the milky blue crater lake. Veils of steam wreathed the landscape of loose rock in eerie half-light.
Through this forbidding terrain ‚Äì ‚ÄúViti‚Äù is derived from the Icelandic for ‚Äúhell‚Äù ‚Äì Michelle Parks, a volcanologist with the Icelandic Meteorological Office, picked her way toward the water‚Äôs edge one day last August. With a monitor strapped to her hip to warn her if the gases¬†reached dangerous levels, she stooped to submerge a temperature probe in the lake ‚Äì 26.4 degrees Celsius (79.5 degrees Fahrenheit), consistent with recent readings.
That was reassuring, at least for the time being. The crater was formed when Askja, a volcano in Vatnajokull National Park in Iceland‚Äôs central highlands, uncorked in an explosive eruption in 1875. Askja‚Äôs last eruption, in 1961, was milder, and for decades after, the volcano was quiet. But in 2021, Parks and other scientists keeping tabs on it were shocked to find that in just a few months, the volcano had rapidly expanded, uplifting by 11 centimetres¬†(4.3 inches). This phenomenon, called inflation, occurs when magma or pressurised¬†gases¬†accumulate under a volcano, pushing the ground upwards and outwards.
In the three years since, Askja‚Äôs bloat has reached about 80 cm (32 inches). That uplift, scientists estimate, is the result of 44 million cubic metres¬†(1.6 billion cubic feet) of magma flowing into the existing reservoir around three km (two miles) beneath the surface.
Volcanologists have established a correlation between magma buildup under a volcano and subsequent eruption. But they don‚Äôt know exactly how much magma is needed to help trip an eruption.¬†That is why Parks and her colleagues are closely monitoring the temperature and acidity of Viti‚Äôs crater lake. A jump in either, indicating that more gases¬†are pushing in from below, would suggest the volcano is moving closer to an eruption. So far, those metrics have remained stable, but the scientists watching Askja don‚Äôt take that for granted.
‚ÄúThere‚Äôs so much uncertainty on when this will erupt ‚Äî if it will erupt,‚Äù Parks said. It could be years, or decades, she said, or ‚Äúit could be sooner.‚Äù
An eruption could be catastrophic, though smaller ones are much more likely. On the scale volcanologists use to measure the size of explosive eruptions, Askja is capable of one equivalent to that of Mount St. Helens in the U.S. state of Washington in 1980, although its eruptive style would differ. That eruption, which sparked the Australia-born Parks‚Äô lifelong passion for volcanoes, unleashed a force equivalent to 25,000 Hiroshima-type atomic bombs.
The aim of the volcano observatory at the Icelandic Meteorological Office is to monitor the country‚Äôs volcanoes for changes in activity, like those now observed at Askja, helping to protect the nation‚Äôs nearly 400,000 citizens and the hordes of foreign tourists who visit every year to enjoy Iceland‚Äôs world-renowned geothermal attractions.
‚ÄúIf it‚Äôs a big explosion, then it‚Äôs really dangerous for the people around here, the tourists.‚Äù
However, the team‚Äôs work has taken on broader significance. Last year, Parks and colleagues with the University of Iceland received government funding for a pioneering research project across 12 institutions to test a theory that could have dire implications not just for Iceland, but for every person on the planet: Whether the rapid retreat of glaciers as a result of human-caused climate change will trigger increased volcanic activity.
The basic process underpinning the idea is simple. The tremendous weight of glaciers and ice sheets can tamp down volcanoes. When the ice retreats, the downward pressure on the planet‚Äôs thin outer crust and much thicker underlying mantle¬†eases, allowing the ground to rebound. This change in pressure spurs dynamic forces beneath volcanoes to produce more magma and alter its movement, influencing eruptions.
‚ÄúIceland is essentially one of the best places in the world to study this ‚Ä¶ because we have both volcanism¬†and glaciers,‚Äù Parks said. ‚ÄúAt the end of the day, what we‚Äôre aiming for with this project is a much bigger picture. It‚Äôs the future of volcanic eruptions. How large can they be? ‚Ä¶ And what‚Äôs in store for us in the future, not only in Iceland but for the rest of Europe and potentially farther afield.‚Äù
Ground surface inflation at Olafsgigar monitoring station
Jan 2021
Source: Icelandic Meteorological Office with data processing by Sigr√∫n Hreinsd√≥ttir
In July 2021, one of the observation stations at the vast Askja volcanic system showed that the ground there was uplifting ‚Äî a process called inflation ‚Äî indicating that magma was accumulating under the volcano after decades of deflation. The sudden reversal coincided with a burst of seismic activity in the area.
The rapid uplift continued through September 2021 and then gradually slowed as earthquakes persisted.
As of September 2024, Askja was still expanding, having inflated by about 80 cm in just three years. Scientists fear an eruption could be increasingly likely, but the timing and intensity remain uncertain.
Uncorking cataclysm
What scientists already know of Iceland‚Äôs eruptive history supports the theory.
When the thick glaciers and ice caps that had covered the North Atlantic island during the last major Ice Age receded between about 15,000 and 10,000 years ago, underlying volcanoes responded with fury. In 2002, scientists calculated changes in Iceland‚Äôs volcanic activity over time by analysing¬†the chemical composition of lava rock samples. They found that eruption rates surged an estimated 30 to 50 times during and shortly after the ice loss compared to the preceding Ice Age and recent times.
It was likely a cataclysmic scenario, with a ‚Äúridiculous amount of eruptions,‚Äù Parks said, as rivers of lava reshaped the island and ash rained into surrounding seas. Askja, too, registered a major explosive eruption during this time.
Once again, scientists say, the elements needed to set off another surge in eruptions are converging. Glaciers now cover just 10% of Iceland, but that ice still weighs on more than half the nation‚Äôs 34 active volcanic systems, and it is rapidly melting as global temperatures climb. In the past 130 years, Iceland‚Äôs glaciers have lost about 16% of their volume, with half of that in just the past three decades. Scientists predict roughly half of the remaining volume will be gone by this century‚Äôs end.
Already, the magma chambers beneath Iceland may be responding to the loss of ice, and not just those directly under glaciers. Askja, which has been free of ice for 10,000 years, and much of Iceland are rebounding because pressure changes from glacial retreat affects large parts of the Earth‚Äôs crust and mantle.
Over the last three decades, magma has been produced beneath Iceland at a rate 2 to 3 times what it would have been without the ice loss, according to preliminary modelling¬†results from Parks‚Äô project shared exclusively with Reuters. ‚ÄúMore magma is coming in underneath Iceland, and we just don‚Äôt need it,‚Äù Parks said. ‚ÄúWe‚Äôve got enough.‚Äù
Scientists first theorised¬†in the 1970s that melting ice might impact volcanic eruptions. But only recently have they begun to understand the scale of the potential threat. Four years ago, volcanologists compiled the first comprehensive global database of volcanoes under ice or within five km (three miles)¬†of it, publishing their findings in the journal Global and Planetary Change. They found that some 245 active or potentially active volcanoes around the world met the criteria, from the Andes to North America‚Äôs Cascade Mountains and Alaska, to Russia‚Äôs Kamchatka Peninsula and Antarctica.
The same study also found that about 160 million people live within 100 km (62 miles) of at least one of those volcanoes and that 20,000 people are within the immediate vicinity. Numbers like that underscore the threat if glacial melt were to push more volcanoes to pop off, unleashing deadly floods and mud flows and spewing vast clouds of ash and deadly gases¬†into the atmosphere.
A global concern
A 2020 study identified 245 volcanoes within a 5 km radius of a glacier.
Volcanoes near or under ice
Iceland
Fault lines
ICELAND
Atlantic Ocean
0
300 km
CHILE
Pacific Ocean
0
300
Arctic Ocean
Alaska
RUSSIA
Atlantic Ocean
0
1,000
A 2017 study identified 138 suspected volcanoes beneath Antarctica
ANTARCTICA
Sources: ‚ÄòGlobal Mapping of Future Glaciovolcanism‚Äô Global and Planetary Change, December 2020; 'A new volcanic province: an inventory of subglacial volcanoes in West Antarctica' , Geological Society, London,¬†May 2017
The effects wouldn‚Äôt end there. Eruptions rich in carbon dioxide, a greenhouse gas, could exacerbate global warming, outlasting the sulphur¬†aerosols in the atmosphere that can cause initial periods of cooling. And the volcanoes now known to sit under the West Antarctic Ice Sheet could help speed ice melt into the ocean, raising¬†sea levels.
Evidence suggests that what happened in Iceland after the last Ice Age was duplicated to a lesser extent around the world. Eruptions were between 2 and 6 times higher globally between 12,000 and 7,000 years ago owing to more frequent eruptions in areas that were losing their ice cover, according to a 2009 study in the journal Earth and Planetary Science Letters.
A violent history
Iceland is uniquely primed for frequent volcanic eruptions because of its location at the juncture of two tectonic plates that are pulling apart over an upwelling of anomalously hot material in the Earth‚Äôs mantle. Many smaller eruptions have little impact beyond their immediate vicinity, though they are still able to put on spectacular shows. Others can have lethal effects far beyond Iceland.
When Laki, southwest of Askja, erupted in 1783-84, the fluorine it expelled contaminated the island‚Äôs plants and water sources, killing more than half of Iceland‚Äôs livestock. This agricultural collapse led to a famine that killed around a fifth of the island‚Äôs human population, while the resulting haze of sulphurous¬†fog that later reached Europe may have contributed to the deaths of thousands more people. And as far away as Africa and Asia, the eruption‚Äôs effects weakened monsoons, initiating more famines.
Near the southern coast under a small ice cap lies Katla, considered one of the world‚Äôs most dangerous volcanoes under ice. Katla has racked up more than 20 eruptions since the Middle Ages, averaging one every 60 years, and it is past due for another, having last erupted in 1918. During that eruption, heat pouring from Katla‚Äôs caldera rapidly melted the ice atop the volcano, releasing a torrent of water greater than the combined discharges of the Amazon, Mississippi, and Yangtze rivers at its peak. And, like Laki, Katla has explosive potential.
‚ÄúThe time for Katla to erupt is coming close ... It is high time for European governments and airline authorities all over Europe and the world to start planning for the eventual Katla eruption," then-Icelandic President Olafur Ragnar Grimsson told BBC News in 2010, following the eruption of Eyjafjallaj√∂kull. The ash cloud from that eruption caused ‚Ç¨1.3 billion (US$1.4 billion) in losses from cancelled¬†flights during a six-day European airspace ban, making it the world‚Äôs costliest eruption in the last century.
The Icelandic government has emergency plans in place for a Katla-style eruption and works with local police districts to create near-term risk assessments for other volcanoes.
But the government has also tapped its fiery foundation to its benefit, using it to heat homes and businesses and draw big-spending tourists to rejuvenating geothermal baths. After the Eyjafjallaj√∂kull eruption, the Icelandic government made volcanoes a pillar of a now multibillion-dollar tourism industry. Souvenir shops in the capital, Reykjavik, sell lava rocks from a recent eruption on the Reykjanes Peninsula for 2,000 Icelandic krona (US$14) apiece.
‚ÄúWatching your friends disappear‚Äù
Like many Icelanders, 29-year-old Iris Ragnarsdottir Pedersen and her father, 62-year-old Ragnar Frank Kristjansson, have an intimate knowledge of the extremes of their land of ice and fire. Along the south coast in Svinafell, Ragnarsdottir Pedersen, a mountain guide, lives with her husband and their Icelandic sheepdog, Blika. Her father, a retired national park manager, has a small turf-roofed summer house next door. Towering over them is a large cliff, beyond which lies Vatnajokull ice cap and, under it, the volcano Oraefajokull.
After Oraefajokull erupted in 1362, sailors reported seaborne pumice floating ‚Äúin such masses that ships could hardly make their way through it.‚Äù
Father and daughter both recall joining fellow Icelanders over the years to marvel at the glowing fountains and rivers of lava produced by eruptions. They also are familiar with the impact of climate change.
For 25 years, Kristjansson has volunteered as a glacier monitor with the Icelandic Glaciological Society, tracking the retreat of two glaciers that flow out of the Vatnajokull ice cap, the nation‚Äôs largest. Every autumn, he treks for hours to one of the glaciers, Skeidararjokull, to take measurements that he sends to the Icelandic Meteorological Office. ‚ÄúIt's a lonely walk toward the glacier ‚Äî 15 km in the black sand,‚Äù he said.
Kristjansson used to be able to stride right up to the edge of Skeidararjokull. But as it has rapidly shrunk over the past decade, a lake has formed at the foot of the glacier, blocking his way. He now has to use special binoculars to measure the distance. This year, he said, one point along the glacier‚Äôs edge showed a retreat of 300 metres¬†(984 feet), which is the greatest he has recorded.
Ragnarsdottir Pedersen has witnessed the retreat from year to year on treks to the glaciers with her father and by simply looking out her windows. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs just devastating to see,‚Äù she said. ‚ÄúI‚Äôve sometimes said to people, ‚ÄòIt‚Äôs like watching your friends disappear.‚Äô ‚Äù
As a child, she knew Oraefajokull lurked under the Vatnajokull ice cap. But it only became a concern when the volcano started rumbling in 2017, just as she and her husband began planning to build their home near it. When she felt the earthquakes and caught the smell of sulphur¬†from the rivers, she said, ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre like, ‚ÄòOh yeah, we actually have this powerful, quite dangerous volcano right above our home.‚Äô ‚Äù
Ultimately, she and her husband weren‚Äôt concerned enough to cancel the move, having faith that scientists would closely monitor the volcano for any danger. ‚ÄúPeople have lived in Svinafell since the settlement of Iceland,‚Äù she said.
A scientific mecca
Oraefajokull is one of six active volcanoes covered by the Vatnajokull ice cap. As Vatnajokull has thinned and retreated, some of the volcanoes beneath it, as well as nearby Askja, have become agitated. Oraefajokull has calmed since its 2017 awakening. Grimsvotn and Bardarbunga, two volcanoes under the ice cap that are being assessed in Parks‚Äô¬†project, have together notched five eruptions in the past 30 years, compared with just one under the ice cap in the preceding 40 years.
Parks and her scientific colleagues said it‚Äôs too early to conclude that the increased activity under Vatnajokull is linked to rapid loss of ice cover. Other scientists have established that clusters of eruptions can occur in natural cycles under the ice cap. But to determine whether climate change may also be playing a part, they will have to collect more data on ice retreat and magma generation and feed it into complex computer models.
Because of its continuing unrest, Askja is the main draw for scientists, who flock to its enormous calderas when Iceland‚Äôs brief summer allows for field research. Like most of Iceland‚Äôs volcanoes, Askja does not conform to the stereotypical cone shape of volcanoes such as Japan‚Äôs iconic Mount Fuji. Instead, fissures snake across a sprawling Mars-like landscape carpeted with lava rocks and pockmarked with craters from past eruptions.
To reach the centrepiece¬†of the Askja system, an 11-square-km (four sq miles) caldera lake called Oskjuvatn, scientists journey hours across the highlands in Super Jeeps and Land Rovers balanced on 35-inch tires. Oskjuvatn was formed in an explosive 1875 eruption that shot out huge volumes of airy pumice, much of it still covering the area around Askja, with fist-sized chunks tossed about by strong winds.
Above the magma reservoir near the western side of the lake is where Askja has been inflating fastest, a lure for researchers. On a visit in August, Reuters encountered a team of three scientists from the University of Geneva at the lake‚Äôs edge, loading equipment into an inflatable dinghy. Their aim: to measure carbon dioxide concentrations in the lake and collect water samples from its deepest points.
The data is not easy to get. The numbingly cold and often tempestuous waters are dangerous. Landslides on the caldera‚Äôs steep scarp can launch tsunamis big enough to swamp neighbouring¬†Viti crater. In 1907, two German scientists set off onto the lake and were never seen again. The Geneva team‚Äôs boat would be the first on the lake in nearly a decade. The University of Geneva researchers suited up in thick thermal gear, just in case.
Weight of glacier
Increased volatility from below
Magma migration pathways change
More magma is generated, and existing magma bodies may be destabilised
The great weight of glaciers places downward pressure on the Earth‚Äôs crust and mantle, in effect dampening upward force from magma chambers beneath volcanoes.
As the ice cover melts, this downward pressure eases, enabling more rock in the mantle to melt and form magma.
The rebound of the crust can also affect stress fields in the Earth, altering the movement of magma and creating new fractures underneath a volcano. Magma reservoirs can become unstable and move closer to eruption.
As magma pushes upward, it can cause the ground to expand and trigger swarms of small earthquakes. When the pressure is high enough, an eruption can burst through the surface.
One of them, geologist Nicolas Oestreicher, said the work was important given both Askja‚Äôs explosive capabilities and the increased activity around the lake. ‚ÄúIf it‚Äôs a big explosion, then it‚Äôs really dangerous for the people around here, the tourists,‚Äù Oestreicher said.
His team, which later returned safely from their boat trip, was one of several from Iceland and abroad that day racing to figure out what was going on at Askja. University of Iceland geophysicist Freysteinn Sigmundsson, co-head with Parks of the government-funded project, was there, too. ‚ÄúToday may be the richest day in all of Askja‚Äôs¬†history in terms of the number of scientists here,‚Äù he said.
Sigmundsson has been coming to the volcano almost every year since 1990 and he knows the terrain well. Shouldering a surveying tripod, he clambered with sure-footed expertise across beds of jagged, crunchy lava, searching for round metal markers that scientists anchored in the area in the 1960s and 1970s. These would tell him where to set up his equipment to check how much Askja had grown or shifted over the past year.
The measurements Sigmundsson and his colleagues took in August showed 12 cm (five inches) of uplift at Askja since the previous year, confirming that the volcano was still in a state of unrest and could erupt at any time.
From the Andes to Antarctica
Regardless of what they ultimately find, the interplay between volcanoes and ice will remain a chief worry among volcanologists. The most deadly eruption in the last 100 years was that of Nevado del Ruiz in the Colombian Andes in 1985. Some 23,000 people were killed after a superheated amalgam of ash, lava fragments and gases¬†known as a pyroclastic flow melted snow and ice near the volcano‚Äôs summit, sending massive rivers of mud and debris rushing down the volcano‚Äôs flanks.
‚ÄúAny eruption where there is a possibility of (snow or ice) interacting with the hot stuff adds to the elements of danger,‚Äù said Ben Edwards, a volcanologist at Dickinson College in Pennsylvania and lead author of the 2020 study that produced the database of ice-clad volcanoes.
Edwards and his fellow researchers drew up a list of the world‚Äôs most dangerous volcanoes that lie under glaciers,¬†based on the volume of ice on or near each one, the frequency of past eruptions, and the population living within 30 km (19 miles). Seven of the top 10, they found, are in the Andes. The most dangerous is Villarrica¬†in Chile, with more than 35,000 people living in its shadow. Edwards is in the middle of a five-year study seeking to understand how Villarrica responded to glacial retreat at the end of the last Ice Age.
The small number of studies to date of the interaction between retreating ice and volcanoes in other parts of the world, scientists said, mean the research underway in Iceland will help create a template for what could happen elsewhere. It‚Äôs not a perfect comparison: The underlying plate tectonics in Iceland differ from those in the Andes. Magma reservoirs also lie much deeper in the crust in the Andes than in Iceland, Edwards said.
‚ÄúWe don‚Äôt have a very good physical understanding of all the processes that control melting (of rock into magma) deeper in the mantle,‚Äù said David Pyle, a volcanologist at the University of Oxford who studies Chilean volcanoes. ‚ÄúWhereas in Iceland, the conceptual model is much better developed because in a way it‚Äôs geologically simpler.‚Äù
Antarctica, where both ice and volcanoes are abundant, has become an area of concern in recent years. While a handful of volcanoes were known to reside under the vast West Antarctic Ice Sheet, in 2017 scientists reported detecting another 91 possible volcanoes hidden there.
How many of them are active or have the potential to awaken is difficult to ascertain. ‚ÄúThere are probably two or three which are certainly active. But there may be as many as 100 or 150,‚Äù said John Smellie, a former senior volcanologist with the British Antarctic Survey who has completed 27 field seasons on the continent and won two Polar Medals from the British royals.
If climate change kicks off a new era of eruptions in the coming decades, Smellie said, it would hasten the already rapid loss of the continent‚Äôs ice cover due to climate change, adding to rising sea levels and swamping the world‚Äôs big coastal cities. Antarctica is shedding more than 150 billion metric tons¬†of ice a year, according to satellite measurements, and scientists believe that as oceans continue to warm, the West Antarctic Ice Sheet is fast approaching a tipping point beyond which the melt cannot be stopped.
‚ÄúIf an eruption adds even a small amount to global sea level, it will exacerbate effects already happening due to climate change,‚Äù Smellie said.
Under the worst-case scenario, rapid melting of the West Antarctic Ice Sheet would ease the pressure on buried volcanoes, triggering eruptions. This in turn would speed up ice loss, awakening even more volcanoes that melt more ice, and so on, leading to global catastrophe. However, Smellie said, that is unlikely to happen because thousands of nearly simultaneous eruptions would be needed to melt even a small proportion of the ice sheet.
In the danger zone
Few people make their home near Askja. The closest village lies about 60 km (37 miles) away. But some 13,000 people visit the volcano during the summer months, when Askja is more accessible, according to data from the national park authority. Often, tourists climb down into Viti crater to swim in the Instagram-worthy lake, despite signs that warn them about acidic water, which can irritate skin, and falling rock.
After a week of field work at and around Askja last August, the volcanologists gathered park rangers and local police and lodging staff in a nearby ranger hut one evening for a safety briefing on the state of the volcano.
Related content
In the Andes, mountain guides bear witness to shrinking glaciers
Climate change thaws world‚Äôs northernmost research station
Iceland builds defences for a future of increased volcanic activity
Iceland volcano erupts near town after weeks of quake activity
Parks hovered over her laptop displaying a series of charts and graphs on her most recent findings that showed Askja was still inflating. Sigmundsson, standing in the doorway, explained to the rangers and police that predicting eruptions is an imprecise science; they may not necessarily have weeks of seismic activity as a warning of an imminent eruption. ‚ÄúThe time scale can be quite short,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúHours, maybe ‚Ä¶ You would want to have a plan if something happens to quickly evacuate the caldera.‚Äù
One of the greatest risks to tourists is a phreatic explosion ‚Äî a blast of hot steam, ash and rocks that comes with little warning. That‚Äôs what formed Viti crater nearly 150 years ago. In 2019, a phreatic explosion at New Zealand‚Äôs White Island volcano killed 22 people who were visiting the island at the time.
At Sigmundsson‚Äôs words, the room fell quiet. One of the police officers then piped up with a plan: ‚ÄúWe just have to hope that it blows up in the middle of the winter.‚Äù
By Gloria Dickie
Graphics: Sam Hart and Anurag Rao
Photography: Stoyan Nenov
Video: Kuba Stƒô≈ºycki
Video production: Lauren Cook, Holly Murtha and M√≠a Womersley
Photo editing: Maye-E Wong and Eve Watling
Art direction: Eve Watling
Visual editing: Feilding Cage
Edited by Katy Daigle and John Blanton
Amid Israel‚Äôs military assault, the world‚Äôs hunger monitor has struggled to access key data to determine whether conditions in Gaza constitute a famine.
OnlyFans is among the world's most successful social media platforms ‚Äì but a secretive one. Reuters traces its journey from an obscure, porn-free site to an adults-only phenomenon turbocharged by erotic performers and influencers.
It‚Äôs a simple but brutal equation: The number of people going hungry or otherwise struggling around the world is rising, while the amount of money the world‚Äôs wealthiest nations are contributing toward helping them is dropping.
Multiple OnlyFans accounts featured suspected child sex abuse, investigator reports.
Reuters, the news and media division of Thomson Reuters, is the world‚Äôs largest multimedia news provider, reaching billions of people worldwide every day. Reuters provides business, financial, national and international news to professionals via desktop terminals, the world's media organizations, industry events and directly to consumers.

item.title: 'Sustainable' logging operations are clear-cutting Canada‚Äôs climate-fighting forests
item.description:
Nonprofit environment watchdogs put their stamps of approval on countless wood products that get touted as responsibly produced. But Reuters found that the timber firms these groups certify are harvesting large swaths of Canada‚Äôs older forests, which are critical to containing global warming.
By CHRIS KIRKHAM , GRANT SMITH and JESSICA DINAPOLI Photographs by CHRIS HELGREN and DAVID JACKSON
Filed Sept. 7, 2024, 10 a.m. GMT
With its vast expanses of forest, Canada has the most ‚Äúcertified‚Äù sustainable timber operations of any nation, according to the nonprofit organizations that attest to the environmental soundness of logging practices.
Such forestry-standards groups were born in the 1990s out of rage¬†over tropical rainforest destruction. Today, they put their leafy seals of approval on toilet paper, two-by-fours and other wood and paper goods to assure eco-conscious consumers and investors they were responsibly produced.
Yet research shows Canadian forests have seen some of the world‚Äôs largest declines in ecologically critical primary and old-growth woodlands over the last two decades, even as sustainability-certification programs grew to include nearly all of Canada‚Äôs logging.
To track destruction of older woodlands in these certified zones, Reuters analyzed forestry data in Ontario, a major logging province. The analysis found that about 30% of the certified boreal forests harvested from 2016 to 2020 were at least 100 years old. That resulted in the loss of 377 square miles of these older forests, an area the size¬†of New York City and Washington D.C. combined, the analysis found.
Canada‚Äôs forests ‚Äì accounting for 9% of the world‚Äôs total ‚Äì are considered critical to containing global warming. Environmental advocates have long pushed to end logging in primary or old-growth forests, which soak up far more climate-damaging carbon than logged-and-replanted areas. Primary forests are those that show no sign of previous harvesting. They can include old-growth areas ‚Äì some with trees hundreds or thousands of years old ‚Äì but also relatively newer woodlands that, for instance, might have regrown after wildfires.
Forest-certification nonprofits have chosen to allow logging of older forests through a host of concessions to industry. The harvesting of such areas in Ontario came despite the fact that 94% of the province‚Äôs managed forests are certified by one of the two dominant environmental-certification¬†organizations in Canada, the analysis found. Reuters analyzed satellite-derived logging data, government forest-age estimates and forest-certification maps to estimate the harvest of forests at least 100 years old in Ontario‚Äôs certified zones.
‚ÄúWhy the heck are they allowing logging ‚Äì certified logging ‚Äì in primary forests that are over 100 years old?‚Äù asked Dominick DellaSala, a conservation biologist with environmental group Wild Heritage who studies Canadian logging impacts.¬†‚ÄúFor Canada to claim that it‚Äôs doing sustainable management, it‚Äôs laughable. To put a certification seal of approval on it is more alarming.‚Äù
The rapid loss of older Canadian forests¬†highlights the flaws of certification programs that have come under heavy influence of the logging and forest-products¬†industries, a Reuters investigation has found. The damage has come under the watch of the Forest Stewardship Council (FSC), the world‚Äôs first such certification organization, founded in 1993 with environmentalist support; and the Sustainable Forestry Initiative (SFI), a rival founded by a timber¬†and forest-products¬†trade group the following year.
This account is based on the Reuters analysis of Ontario forests, a review of hundreds of pages of FSC and SFI audits, along with policy and strategy documents, and interviews with 20 current or former FSC employees or members and more than a half-dozen¬†researchers who study the environmental effects of Canadian logging.
In a statement, FSC said it has not wavered from its original commitment to responsible forest¬†management and¬†that its certification standards are ‚Äúrobust and credible.‚Äù SFI said its standards are ‚Äústrong and continuously improving‚Äù and that its certification has become a ‚Äúhighly trusted solution‚Äù to the growing demand for products from sustainably managed forests.
Neither organization commented on the Reuters analysis or on whether they considered harvesting large sections of century-old¬†forests to be sustainable.
The FSC and SFI certify logging companies‚Äô practices in specific forests and examine consumer-product supply chains. Their seals of approval ‚Äì a leaf insignia¬†for SFI, and a tree with a checkmark for the¬†FSC ‚Äì have become essential to timber and forest-products¬†firms amid rising pressure for environmental stewardship.
But these companies hold immense leverage over the big forest-certification nonprofits, which depend heavily on the industry for funding through certification fees, Reuters found. And since its inception, the FSC has watered down its forestry standards in response to the competitive threat posed by SFI and other industry-friendly certifiers, according to environmentalists and more than a dozen current and former FSC staffers and members, who advise the organization on policy and strategy.
Companies are free to choose which certifier to use, allowing them to avoid those with stricter standards and giving them clout to lobby all certifiers for permissive policies, said the FSC staffers and members.
‚ÄúIt‚Äôs easy to pull the wool over people‚Äôs eyes about what is good forestry.‚Äù
Widespread certification of British Columbia timber operations over the past two decades hasn‚Äôt stopped the disappearance of more than half of the province‚Äôs old-growth woodlands over that period. Logging caused the vast majority of the declines in the biggest old-growth trees storing the most carbon, according to one 2021 study in the Canadian Journal of Forest Research and another last year in the journal Frontiers in Forests and Global Change.¬†Studies in 2009 and 2017 examined areas of Quebec woodlands and found¬†sections¬†of forests dominated by trees more than a century old had dwindled to between 13% and 28% of the forest amid heavy logging.
